-- 17 of 437 --

1- PV bleeding in pregnancy =
Threatened miscarriage
- Patient: Aimee Drayton, 28yo
female
- PMH: asthma, normal vaginal
delivery 2 yrs ago
- DH: Salbutamol inhaler (PRN)
- Main concern: vaginal bleeding, ~11
weeks pregnant
- Details given: light bleeding for 2
days, no pain, no clots, no dizziness,
repeated pregnancy test still positive
- Social: lives with husband (away in
France for work), 2yo son at home,
smokes ~5/day, no alcohol, on
universal credit, no work
- ICE:
• Ideas → worried it’s a miscarriage
• Concerns → unsure if needs ED visit,
worried something is wrong
• Expectations → GP advice + what to
do next
- Red flags: ectopic (pain, shoulder
tip pain, dizziness), heavy PV bleed,
haemodynamic compromise,
miscarriage
Clarify bleeding: when started, how much, clots, fresh or old blood,
any discharge.
- Pregnancy history: LMP (11wks ago), parity (1 son, normal
delivery), any scans yet, booked antenatal?
- Associated symptoms: abdo pain, cramps, shoulder tip pain,
dizziness, syncope, urinary symptoms.
- Anaemia screen: SOB, palpitations, lightheadedness.
- Contraception history: any IUCD/Mirena use at conception.
- Lifestyle: smoking, alcohol, support at home.
Empathy phrases:
- “I can completely understand why this has made you anxious —
any bleeding in pregnancy can be worrying.”
- “It’s good that you came quickly so we can make sure everything is
safe.”
- “I can see you’re trying to manage this on your own while your
husband is away — that must be really stressful.”
Dx explained simply:
“From what you’ve told me, the most likely explanation is something
we call a threatened miscarriage. That means there’s some bleeding,
but the pregnancy can still continue normally — many women in your
situation go on to have healthy babies. We do, however, need to
check other things such as ectopic pregnancy, which is when the
pregnancy develops outside the womb. That’s rare, but it can be
serious, so we don’t want to miss it. To be safe, I’d like you to be
checked in the Early Pregnancy Unit today.”
- Immediate referral: Urgent same-day referral to Early Pregnancy
Assessment Unit (EPAU) → pelvic scan + β-hCG.
- Safety netting: urgent help (999/A&E) if: bleeding becomes heavy,
develops abdo pain or shoulder tip pain, feels faint/dizzy, or feels unwell.
- Lifestyle advice: Smoking cessation support offered → “Stopping
smoking reduces miscarriage risk and is one of the best things you can do
for the baby. We can link you to support services if you’d like.”
- Antenatal care: register with midwife if not already; folic acid (400 mcg
OD until 12 weeks).
- Vaccinations in pregnancy: COVID + flu (any time), Pertussis (16
weeks), RSV (28 weeks).
- Support/social issues: explore help with childcare (mum, friend,
neighbour); liaise with EPAU about timing if needed.
- Offer hospital social services: they can sometimes help with
childcare or transport.
- Signpost to Home Start: https://www.home-start.org.uk/ and
emergency childcare: https://www.emergencychildcare.co.uk/
Good SCA phrases:
- “The good news is that many women with light bleeding like yours
continue to have healthy pregnancies.”
- “I do think it’s important we get you checked today, just to rule out
anything more serious like an ectopic pregnancy.”
- “If it’s tricky to arrange childcare, I can explain to the hospital about your
circumstances — but I’d encourage you to ask your mum or a trusted
friend to help, just for today.”
- “If at any point you feel faint, develop pain, or the bleeding gets heavier,
please don’t wait — call 999 straight away.”
“I know this is overwhelming, especially with caring for your child alone.
Because miscarriage can sometimes lead to complications, it’s safest to
be seen in hospital. We can explore practical support for getting there and
childcare, and hospital staff can guide you through treatment and provide
emotional support.”

2- Abnormal LFTs = NAFLD
- Patient: Andy Goldman, 40yo male
- Setting: Routine NHS Health Check
→ abnormal LFTs
- PMH/DH: Nil
- Vitals (from HCA check): BP
135/85, Pulse 98, BMI 35.2
- Bloods: ALT 91↑, GGT 98↑,
ALP/Bilirubin normal, rest normal
- Social: Occasional alcohol
(monthly, small), no smoking,
accountant (WFH), eats poorly (lazy
to cook), unmarried, no partner
- ICE:
• Ideas → surprised anything wrong
• Concerns → not worried, feels well
• Expectations → just wants
explanation + advice
- Red flags to ask: jaundice, pruritus,
RUQ pain, tiredness, stool/urine
colour change, fever, weight loss,
travel, tattoos, transfusions, IV drugs,
OTC/herbal meds
- Symptom screen: jaundice (eyes/skin), itch, RUQ pain, tiredness,
stool/urine colour, fever, weight loss.
- Risk factors: tattoos, transfusions, IV drug use, new meds/herbals.
- Lifestyle: diet, alcohol intake, exercise, BMI (already high), smoking
(nil).
- Social: living alone, home situation, relationship history (hepatitis
risk).
Empathy phrases:
- “I can imagine it’s a bit unexpected to be told something looks
abnormal when you feel completely fine.”
- “The good news is, you’re not unwell, and we’ve picked this up early
— which means we can do something about it before it ever causes a
problem.”
Diagnosis explained simply:
“Andy, one of your liver blood tests, ALT, was a bit higher than we’d
like, and another marker called GGT was also raised. This often
suggests the liver is under a bit of strain. Since you don’t drink much
alcohol, the most likely cause is something called fatty liver. That
means fat is building up inside the liver cells. It’s very common,
especially if weight is on the higher side. The important thing is that
we’ve spotted it now, before it’s caused any damage — and with the
right lifestyle changes, the liver can heal.”
- “We can calculate your Fib-4 score, which helps estimate your risk
of scarring in the liver.”
- Further investigations: Hep B & C screen, autoimmune liver screen, AST
(for NAFLD score), lipid profile.
- Arrange imaging: Abdominal ultrasound.
- Risk scores: Calculate NAFLD fibrosis score (to assess progression
risk), Q-Risk for CVD (since BMI + age).
- Lifestyle advice:
• Weight loss through diet + exercise (gradual, sustainable).
• Cut processed/fatty foods, cook simple meals or use meal kits
(HelloFresh etc).
- Offer hello fresh/ Gousto who offer fresh healthy food
• Avoid alcohol excess (even though current intake is low).
• Daily activity e.g. 30min brisk walk.
- Consider off label pioglitazone
- Safety netting: If yellow eyes/skin, dark urine, pale stool, persistent
itching, abdo pain, tremors → seek urgent review.
- Follow-up: 1 week → review bloods/scans. Long-term → monitor 3-yearly
if low risk.
- Signpost to British Liver Trust
Good SCA phrases:
- “The most important thing is, this is reversible with the right changes.”
- “It’s common to see this in people with a higher BMI, and catching it early
means we can stop it causing future liver damage.”
- “Small, consistent changes like walking daily and swapping out
takeaways for simple home-cooked meals can make a big difference to
your liver health.”
- “If we find your risk is high, we’ll get specialist liver doctors involved — if
low, we’ll just keep an eye on it every few years.”
- “Your liver shows signs of fat accumulation, which is common in people
with weight or diabetes issues.”
- “Most people do well if we make lifestyle changes to reduce fat in the
liver.”
- “We’ll check your bloods and may do a scan to see if there is any
scarring.”
- “I’ll help you make a realistic plan for diet, weight loss, and exercise to
protect your liver.”
- “If you notice yellowing of your skin/eyes, swelling, confusion, or
vomiting blood, seek help urgently.”

boy

• Missed 4-in-1 preschool booster

discharged back to GP

(limited ability, caring for grandfather
w/ dementia). Father not involved.
Has 8-yo sibling (well).
- Presenting: Ear pain (R), fever,
discharge 2 days,
eating/drinking/passing urine fine.
- ICE:
• Ideas → thinks ear infection
• Concerns → worried not improving,
HV said may need antibiotics
• Expectations → wants antibiotics
Red Flags – urgent review:
- < 3months or 3-6months with temp
of 39 or more
- Headache/ Nystagmus or blurred
vision/ Nausea or vomiting/
Photophobia/ Vertigo/ Fever/
Labyrinthitis/ Facial paralysis.
- Swelling/erythema/tenderness
behind the ear.

• Pain worse when pulling tragus (→ otitis externa)?
• Duration/onset of pain?
• Nature/colour of discharge?
• Fever?
• Hearing loss/changes?
• Redness/swelling behind ear (mastoiditis)?
• Neck stiffness, drowsiness, headache, balance problems (serious
red flags)?
- Associated: runny/blocked nose, sore throat, rash, vomiting (NO)
- PMH: Recurrent ear infections? Swimming/bathing a lot?

Development?
- Social history: Missed vaccines/physio → why? Coping as single

- “Siobhan, I know it must be really hard juggling work, two children,

- “It’s stressful when your little one is in pain — you’ve done well to
give him Calpol and seek advice.”
Diagnosis explained simply:
“Connor’s ear pain, fever, and the yellow discharge suggest he’s got
a middle ear infection — otitis media. Sometimes the pressure in the
ear builds and the eardrum bursts a little, which causes the
discharge. It actually helps release the pressure and pain, and
usually heals on its own. Because it’s lasted more than 2 days and
there’s discharge plus fever, antibiotics are the right step.”
- Antibiotics: Oral amoxicillin course (meets NICE criteria: (< 2 yrs +
bilateral AOM OR otorrhea OR no improvement after 3 days or
systemically unwell). Could offer delayed script if mild, but here
immediate Rx is justified.
- Symptom relief: Paracetamol/ibuprofen for pain & fever. Encourage
fluids.
- Safety net: If worsening, seek urgent help:
• Redness/swelling behind ear (mastoiditis)
• Severe headache, stiff neck, drowsiness, imbalance
• Not drinking/passing urine
- Follow-up: Review in 2–3 days (phone or F2F if possible). Arrange face-
to-face if deteriorates.
- Address missed care:
• Re-refer to physiotherapy for in-toeing
• Book 4-in-1 preschool booster
- Safeguarding awareness: Empathise with her challenges but gently
highlight importance of attending health appointments. If repeated
missed care → consider safeguarding referral.
Good SCA phrases:
- “The good news is this is very common in children, and usually settles
quickly with antibiotics and pain relief.”
- “You’ve done the right things so far — Calpol, fluids, and getting advice
early.”
- “I’ll send the prescription today — would it be easier for you to collect it
at the pharmacy near work, or from the surgery?”
- “I completely understand how busy things must be for you — we’ll help
with rebooking Connor’s vaccine and physio so that’s taken off your
plate.”

4- Bloods suspecting Multiple
myeloma
- Patient: Thomas Hansford, 75,
retired pilot
- PMH: HTN, Hypercholesterolaemia,
OA (knee + wrists)
- DH: Amlodipine, Atorvastatin, Co-
dydramol PRN
- Recent history:
• 5w ago: Physio – MSK pain, safety
netted • 2w ago: GP – persistent back
pain, night waking, weight loss,
tiredness → bloods
- Bloods: Hb 90 (low), WCC 3.1 (low),
Plt 100 (low), CRP 18 (↑), PV 6.8 (↑).
U+E & bone profile normal.
- Current concerns: Back pain x6w,
wakes him up at night, ongoing
despite analgesia, weight loss,
fatigue. - Extra symptom: OTC
ibuprofen helps a bit but causes
reflux → wants antacid.
- Social: Lives alone, no
smoking/alcohol, ex-pilot.
- ICE:
• Ideas: Thinks back pain is from
years of sitting as a pilot.
• Concerns: Losing weight + fatigue →
worried something serious.
• Expectations: Wants results
explained. Will be shocked if cancer
is mentioned.
Red Flags – urgent referral:
- Suspected spinal cord
compression.
- Unexplained fracture.
- Severe hypercalcemia.
- Rapid renal deterioration.
- Clarify pain: Site, onset, severity, radiation to legs? Neuro
symptoms? (weakness, numbness, tingling, bowel/bladder).
- Red flags: weight loss, loss of appetite, night sweats
- Think CRAB mnemonic:
• Calcium ↑ (thirst, confusion, constipation)
• Renal impairment (reduced urine, swelling)
• Anaemia (fatigue, pallor, SOB, dizziness)
• Bone pain / fractures
- Social: Living alone, supports? Coping?
Empathy phrases:
- “I can see this has been really tough, Thomas, especially with your
energy levels being so low.”
- “It’s completely understandable you’d be worried about your
weight loss and tiredness.”
Explaining Dx (bad news):
“Your blood results show that your red cells, white cells and
platelets are all lower than expected, and your inflammation markers
are raised. With your back pain, tiredness and weight loss, I’m
concerned this could be due to a condition called multiple myeloma.
That’s a type of cancer that affects the bone marrow — the part
inside your bones that makes blood cells. This would explain your
anaemia, tiredness, and pain. I know this is difficult to hear, and I
want to reassure you that we’ll urgently arrange further tests and
specialist input to confirm what’s going on and get you the right
treatment.”
- “Sometimes the bone marrow can produce too many abnormal
cells, which can cause bone pain, tiredness, kidney problems, or
make you more prone to infections.”
- “This condition is called myeloma, a type of blood cancer. It is
treatable, and we’ll need to refer you urgently to hospital
specialists.”
- “I want to check for complications like weak bones, kidney strain,
or high calcium levels that may need quick treatment.”
- Investigations:
• Urgent serum protein electrophoresis + urine Bence Jones protein
• Urgent spine X-ray (looking for lytic lesions/fracture)
- Referral: 2WW urgent haematology referral (suspected myeloma).
- Symptom control:
• Continue co-dydramol for pain.
• If NSAID needed → prescribe with PPI (e.g. omeprazole) for reflux.
- Safety netting:
• New neuro symptoms (leg weakness, numbness around
bottom/genitals, bladder/bowel incontinence) → 999/A&E (suspected
cauda equina).
• Symptoms of high calcium (thirst, confusion, constipation, excessive
urination) → urgent review.
• If no contact from specialist within 2w → call practice.
- Follow-up: Arrange review in 2 days to check on symptoms + discuss
next steps.
Good SCA phrases:
- “We don’t have all the answers today, but we do know your blood results
and symptoms are concerning, and we need urgent specialist tests.”
- “This must be a huge shock — take your time to process it, and please
ask me anything that’s on your mind.”
- “You’re not alone in this, Thomas — we’ll support you through the next
steps and make sure you’re seen quickly.”
- “I’ll arrange urgent blood tests and a referral to hospital specialists for
further scans and treatment.”
- “If you develop sudden weakness in the legs, problems controlling your
bladder/bowels, or severe worsening pain, call 999 — this could mean
urgent pressure on the spinal cord.”
- “If you feel suddenly more confused, very thirsty, or pass less urine, you
must seek urgent care — these can be signs of high calcium or kidney
strain.”
- “We’ll work together with hospital doctors to confirm the diagnosis and
discuss treatments to control the condition.”

- Safety netting:
“If you develop chest pain, confusion, severe drowsiness, worsening
breathlessness, or your oxygen level drops below 90%, please call 111

• Vaccination status (flu, pneumococcal, COVID).

- “You’ve done well to take all your tablets, but I can see this HbA1c

- “Let’s look at what other options we have, especially as I hear you
really want to avoid injections.”

- “Your diabetes is not well controlled — the HbA1c has gone up
from 81 to 83. This explains your thirst and needing to pass urine
more often. If sugars stay high long term, there’s a risk to your eyes,
kidneys, heart, and feet. The good news is we still have other tablet
options we can try before insulin.”
- Investigations / Baseline checks:
• Urine ACR, Lipids, Q-risk.
• Annual retinal and foot screening.
- Pharmacological options: GIVE options
• Add SGLT2 inhibitor (empagliflozin) → lowers glucose, protects
heart/kidneys.
• Add DPP-4 inhibitor (linagliptin/sitagliptin) if not tolerated.
• GLP-1 RA (e.g. semaglutide, tirzepatide) → possible due to BMI 37 +
occupational insulin concern → usually with specialist input.
• Avoid dapagliflozin + pioglitazone (not licensed).
- Lifestyle advice:
• Weight reduction, reduce carbs/sugar.
• Encourage physical activity (adapted to job).
- DVLA advice:
• Group 2 drivers must inform DVLA about diabetes diagnosis regardless
of insulin use.
• If on insulin → stricter licensing rules.
- Vaccinations: Flu, Pneumococcal, COVID.
- Safety net:
• Side effects of empagliflozin (UTIs, thrush, rare Fournier’s gangrene).
• DKA symptoms → “If you feel very unwell, sick, abdominal pain, fruity
breath → seek urgent help.”
- Follow-up: 3 months with repeat HbA1c, foot check, medication review.
Good SCA phrases:
- “I completely understand your concern about injections and your licence
— it’s important we find a treatment plan that works for you and keeps you
safe at work.”
- “The next best step is adding another tablet called empagliflozin — it
lowers sugars and also protects your heart and kidneys.”
- “Even though insulin is an option in the future, right now we have good
alternatives to try first.”
- “We’ll work as a team with the diabetes nurse so you feel supported.”

- Patient: Elizabeth (Lizzy) Newman,
49yo female.
- PMH: Depression.
- Meds: Sertraline 50mg OD.
- Presenting problem: Persistent
tiredness for ~1 month, after viral
infection.
- Blood results: All normal except
TSH 4.9 (slightly ↑), free T4 20
(normal).
- Lifestyle: Medical secretary,
married, 2 children. Non-smoker,
non-drinker.
- ICE:
• Idea → thinks fatigue = vitamin
deficiency.
• Concern → affecting job
performance, manager worried.
• Expectation → wants explanation +
solution.
- Red flags to consider: Anaemia,
diabetes, thyroid disease,
malignancy, depression relapse,
sleep disorders.
Opening: “Thanks for coming Lizzy, I’ve reviewed your recent results.
Before we go through them, I’d like to understand how you’ve been
feeling.”
- Symptom history:
• Onset, duration, course (better/worse/unchanged).
• Post-viral fatigue features.
• Hypothyroid screen → cold intolerance, dry skin, hair loss,
constipation, weight gain, periorbital puffiness, concentration
problems.
• Menstrual history → regular.
• Mood check → any change in depression.
- Impact: daily activities, work performance, relationships, sleep.
- Other systems: appetite, weight, bowel habits, pain, night sweats.
- Good phrases:
• “I can see how worrying this must be, especially as it’s affecting
your work.”
• “Let’s explore your symptoms carefully so we don’t miss anything
important.”
- Diagnosis explained (simple):
“Your thyroid blood test shows that one level (TSH) is slightly higher,
but the hormone your body actually uses (T4) is still normal. This is
called subclinical hypothyroidism. It often happens after a viral
illness and can return to normal by itself. Right now, it doesn’t need
treatment, but we’ll keep an eye on it with repeat bloods. The
tiredness is likely a combination of your recent virus and this
borderline thyroid result.”
- Investigations/plan:
• Repeat thyroid function in 3 months (or sooner if worsening).
• Monitor symptoms with diary.
• Consider sick euthyroid/post-viral fatigue as cause.
- Symptom management:
• Pacing/energy management: balance rest with gentle activity.
• Daily symptom diary to track fatigue patterns.
• Encourage gentle exercise (short walks) to rebuild stamina.
- Work support: offer fit note for phased return/reduced duties if needed.
- Safety-netting:
• Seek review if weight gain, low mood, constipation, feeling unusually
cold, worsening fatigue.
- Follow-up: GP review + repeat bloods in 3 months.
- Good SCA phrases:
• “The reassuring news is that all your other tests — blood count, iron,
vitamins, sugar, kidneys, and liver — are completely normal.”
• “Your thyroid looks slightly underactive on paper, but not enough to
need tablets right now.”
• “Sometimes this happens after a viral illness, and the body gradually
resets itself.”
• “The best step is to monitor closely, support your energy levels, and re-
check your bloods in 3 months.”
• “If things get worse, please don’t wait — come back sooner.”

8- Hair loss = Telogen effluvium
- Patient: Sabrina Khan, 28yo female.
- PMH: Nil.
- Meds: None.
- Presenting problem: Diffuse hair
shedding for 2–3 months.
- Associated symptoms: Tiredness,
heavy periods over past 6 months.
- Menstrual cycle: 28-day cycle,
currently on period, bleeding lasts 7
days.
- Social: Lives with partner,
occasional alcohol, non-smoker.
Model → concerned about
appearance.
- Idea: Unsure of cause.
- Concern: Losing hair, confidence
affected.
- Expectation: Stop hair loss,
dermatology referral if needed.
- Red flags:
• Scarring alopecia, patchy loss,
sudden shedding
• Symptoms of anaemia: dizziness,
palpitations, SOB
• Systemic illness: weight loss, fever,
fatigue beyond post-viral
• Thyroid or nutritional deficiencies
- Opening: “Thanks for coming, Sabrina. Let’s talk about your hair
loss and how it’s affecting you.”
- Hair history:
• Onset, duration, pattern (diffuse vs patchy), shedding vs thinning.
• Scalp: redness, scarring, rashes?
• Hair care: new products, styling changes, heat treatments, tight
hairstyles.
• Family history of hair loss.
• Medications affecting hair growth.
• Nutritional/dietary factors, recent stress.
• Recent illness, pregnancy, post-partum.
- Menstrual history: Heavy periods, duration, spotting,
contraception use.
- Symptoms of anaemia: Fatigue, palpitations, SOB, dizziness.
- Other endocrine: Ask about acne, hirsutism, thyroid symptoms.
- Impact: Confidence, social life, work (modelling).
- Diagnosis explained simply:
“Sabrina, your hair shedding and tiredness may be linked to iron
deficiency from heavy periods. When iron is low, your hair enters a
resting phase and falls out more than usual, a condition called
telogen effluvium. This is usually temporary and often improves once
iron levels are corrected.”
- Investigations:
• Bloods: FBC, ferritin, iron studies, thyroid function, B12, folate, vitamin
D, LFTs, U+Es, coagulation.
- Examination: Face-to-face scalp check (hair pull test, look for
scarring/patches), vitals. Abdominal and speculum exam for heavy
periods; consider pelvic US.
- Treatment:
• Correct iron deficiency if present with supplements.
• Manage heavy periods: Tranexamic acid, Mefenamic acid, or
Norethisterone if appropriate.
• Temporary cosmetic options: styling, hairpieces.
- Referral: Dermatology if no improvement or underlying scalp pathology
suspected.
- Follow-up: 1 week to review blood results and plan treatment.
- Safety-netting: Seek urgent care if severe anaemia symptoms appear
(palpitations, fainting, SOB).
- Good SCA phrases:
• “It’s understandable to be worried — hair loss can affect confidence and
daily life.”
• “Checking your bloods first will help us target the cause and treatment.”
• “If iron deficiency is found and treated, your hair usually grows back
naturally.”
• “Referral to a dermatologist can be arranged if needed, but first we
should correct any reversible causes.”

• Adolescent vulnerability
• Risk of coercion or assault
- Opening: “Hi Chloe, I’m glad you called. This is a confidential
consultation, and I’m here to support you — nothing you say will get
you in trouble.”
- Incident history:
• Where and when it happened, who was present
• What drug was taken, how much, route of administration
• How she felt after taking it, how long she was unconscious
• Memory gaps, any injuries or pain, especially genital
• Any coercion or pressure
• Previous drug use
• Sexual activity and contraception use
- Safety assessment:
• Does she feel safe with her boyfriend?
• Has he pressured her in the past?
• Emotional impact, school and home support
- Explain risk sensitively:
“Passing out after taking a drug is serious because it can put you at
risk, and it’s important we make sure you’re safe and supported.”
- “Molly, or MDMA, is a type of party drug that affects the brain and
body.”
- “It can cause fast heartbeat, high temperature, sweating, mood
swings, and confusion.”
- “Sometimes people can feel low or anxious for several days
afterwards.”
- “The effects usually wear off, but it can be dangerous if too much
is taken or if mixed with alcohol/other drugs.”
- Immediate: If unwell now (palpitations, chest pain, confusion, collapse)
→ 999 / emergency referral.
- If currently stable: Observation, check vitals, urine, ECG if available.
- Safeguarding / child protection:
• Mandatory referral given age, blackout, limited parental supervision,
vulnerability
• Phrase clearly: “I need to involve the safeguarding team to ensure you’re
safe. This isn’t about punishment; it’s about protection and support.”
- Supportive care: Encourage hydration (water, isotonic fluids), rest, safe
environment.
- Substance misuse support: Offer referral to young people’s
drug/alcohol services.
- Education: “Most effects pass in a day or two, but it’s important to avoid
further exposure.”
• Discuss recreational drug risks
• Contraception advice and sexual health education
• Offer leaflets/resources
- Physical assessment:
• Only if concerns about sexual assault, injuries, or ongoing risks
• Consider STI screen, pregnancy test if indicated
Safety Netting (SCA phrases):
- “If you notice chest pain, feeling faint, seizures, or not making sense —
call 999 immediately.”
- “Come back if you feel persistently low, anxious, or notice ongoing heart
or sleep problems.”
- “I’ll also make sure you have support for your wellbeing and keep you
safe.”
- Follow-up: 2–4 weeks to ensure safety, support, and engagement with
safeguarding/sexual health services
- Good SCA phrases:
• “Thank you for trusting me with this, Chloe.”
• “You’re not in trouble; I just want to make sure you’re safe.”
• “Safeguarding is about support, not punishment.”
• “Passing out or memory loss after drugs can be serious — we need to
check you’re okay.”
• “We can provide advice about contraception and sexual health if you
want.”
• “I’ll help make sure you get the right support and follow-up.”

Triggers & Context:
• Stressors: divorce, financial pressure, work strain
• Social isolation, family support

• Work performance, daily routine, social activity
• Coping with Smoking, alcohol, recreational drugs, exercise?

function.”
- “It’s not a weakness, and with the right support and treatment most

lifestyle changes, talking therapies, and sometimes medication.”
- Management:

Psychological interventions: NHS Talking Therapies / CBT; consider
self-help resources/ CBT for insomnia
Lifestyle advice: Gentle daily exercise, reconnect with friends/family,
mindfulness/relaxation apps/ decrease alcohol as can interfere with
medication and worsen anxiety and depression
Medication: SSRI (e.g., sertraline) if indicated; explain onset 2–4 weeks,
continue ≥6 months after improvement; discuss side effects
Safety & Occupational concerns:
• Discuss firearm safety and occupational health referral; temporary
adjustments if needed
• Reinforce personal safety strategies
Follow-up: 1–2 weeks for review, side effects, ongoing support
Safety-netting: crisis team contacts, 111/999, immediate action if
thoughts worsen
Signpost to Mind, for bereavement (Cruse Bereavement Support,
AtaLoss), family support (Family Action or family counselling)
SCA phrases & examiner-friendly language:
- “I appreciate how openly you’ve spoken — it’s been a very tough time.”
- “These steps will help us support you safely and effectively.”
- “Even small changes, like gentle exercise or connecting with someone,
can make a real difference.”
- “We’ll keep a close eye on your safety and mood while we work on
treatment together.”
- “It’s understandable to feel this way given your recent stresses — there
is help available.”
- “I can see this has been really tough for you — thank you for sharing it
with me.”
- “Depression is a common illness, not a weakness, and with support
most people get better.”
- “We have a range of options, from talking therapies to medication,
depending on how severe your symptoms are.”
- “It’s important we check in on your safety — have you had any thoughts
of harming yourself?”
- “If things worsen, or if you feel you might act on unsafe thoughts, please

• “You mentioned weight loss — that’s important, can I check how

• “Because of your previous breast cancer, I’d like to ask whether

Diagnosis explained simply:
“Your blood results are mostly reassuring — thyroid, kidneys, liver,
and sugar are all normal. One test did stand out: your calcium is a bit
high. A slightly raised calcium can cause tiredness and sometimes
weight loss. Common causes include overactive glands in the neck
or vitamin D supplements, but in your case, given your history, I’m
also concerned this could mean your cancer has come back. I can’t
say for sure yet, but I think we need to get you checked urgently by
the breast team.”
Management plan:
Face-to-face: offer breast/armpit exam
2-week wait referral back to breast clinic (red flag due to breast ca
history + hypercalcaemia + weight loss)
Further bloods: PTH, Vit D
Stop vitamin D tablets (unlikely culprit but safest)
Diet: continue normal calcium foods (milk, yoghurt safe)
Hydration: keep fluids up
Safety-net: if worsening tiredness, confusion, nausea, constipation,
or urinary changes → call GP/111/attend urgently
Chase referral: if not heard within 2wks, call us to follow up
Good SCA phrases for management:
• “Thank you for doing the blood tests — they’ve given us some important
information.”
• “The calcium level is higher than we’d expect, and while sometimes this
is due to things like vitamin D, in your case I’m concerned about a
possible link to your past breast cancer.”
• “I don’t want to alarm you — it’s not definite — but I think it’s safest to
get you seen urgently by the breast team.”
• “Please stop the vitamin D for now, but you can continue with normal
foods like milk and yoghurt.”
• “If you develop new symptoms such as confusion, sickness,
constipation, or feeling very drowsy, please call us or 111 straight away.”
• “If you haven’t heard from the breast clinic within two weeks, please let
us know so we can chase it.”
• “How do you feel about what I’ve explained so far? Would it help if I
wrote this down for you?”

12- USS showing PCKD
Definition: A genetic condition
(autosomal dominant most common)
where multiple renal cysts cause
progressive kidney enlargement and
decline in renal function.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Family history of kidney disease,
aneurysm, sudden death.
- Hypertension at young age.
- Enlarged kidneys, abdominal mass.
- Recurrent UTIs, haematuria, flank
pain.
Name: Jeffrey Smith, 34M
Background: Newly diagnosed
hypertension, on Ramipril.
Reason: To discuss renal USS results
→ showed PKD.
ICE:
- Ideas: Doesn’t know why BP is high,
shocked as feels well.
- Concerns: Wants to get to the
bottom of it, worried about scan
result.
- Expectations: Explanation of results,
what it means for future, possible
treatment, family risk.
Focused History:
- Urinary: blood in urine, infections, frequency, pain.
- Pain: loin/abdominal pain, any palpable lumps.
- Swelling: ankles/legs.
- Neuro: thunderclap headache, visual loss, neck stiffness (berry
aneurysm/SAH).
- CVS: chest pain/back pain (aortic dissection).
- Family history: kidney disease, sudden unexplained deaths.
- Lifestyle: smoking, alcohol, NSAID use (ibuprofen).
- Social: wife + 2 kids (important for genetic discussion).
Red Flags to exclude:
- Haematuria, UTIs, flank/loin pain, abdominal mass
- Oedema (ankle swelling)
- Severe headache/visual changes (SAH)
- Chest pain/tearing pain (aortic dissection)
- Family history of kidney disease / sudden death
Good Phrases:
- “I want to check if you’ve had any symptoms that could suggest
complications of kidney cysts.”
- “Even though you feel well, it’s important I ask about some warning
features.”
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “Your scan shows you have polycystic kidney disease. That means
both kidneys have developed lots of small fluid-filled sacs called
cysts.”
- “These cysts are the likely cause of your high blood pressure. Over
time, they can affect kidney function, but this can vary a lot between
people.”
- “Because it’s usually inherited in a dominant way, each child has
about a 1 in 2 chance of inheriting it.”
Immediate Management:
- Continue Ramipril (good for kidneys + BP).
- Refer to nephrologist for ongoing care.
- Refer to genetics for confirmation + family counselling.
- Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen) — explain risk to kidneys.
- Avoid oestrogen in females – make it worse
- Advise avoid contact sports (cyst rupture risk).
- Discuss children: not routinely screened, but genetics referral if family
wants after age of 16 years to decide themselves to avoid extra anxiety
- Discuss new medications: Tolvaptan in selected cases by specialist
(used to slow down the growth of cysts, reducing overall kidney growth
and preserving kidney function for longer).
- Safety net: sudden headache/visual loss (subarachnoid haemorrhage)→
urgent medical help; severe abdominal pain → emergency.
- Fertility / pregnancy counselling (risk of pre-eclampsia).
- Lifestyle: low-salt diet, hydration, avoid smoking.
- Psychological support, CKD team involvement.
- Signpost to PCKD charity
Good Phrases:
- “There’s no cure for the cysts, but we can slow things down by keeping
your blood pressure well controlled.”
- “There is a newer medication called Tolvaptan that specialists
sometimes use, but it’s only started after assessment in hospital clinics.”
- “Your children don’t need routine screening at the moment — we’d
usually wait until they are adults, but if you and your wife want to explore it
sooner, we can link you with a genetics team.”
- “The important thing for you is good blood pressure control, regular
check-ups, and avoiding kidney-harming medicines.”
- “I can see this diagnosis feels overwhelming — let’s go step by step.”
- “The mainstay of care is controlling blood pressure, as this slows down
kidney damage.”
- “Not everyone progresses to dialysis — many live well with careful
monitoring.”
- “Because it runs in families, your children may need checks too — we
can arrange genetic counselling.”
- “If you notice blood in your urine or sudden pain, it’s important to get
urgent help.”

13- Tiredness + jaundice = Hepatitis A
Name: Rishi Punak, 26M
PMH/Allergies: Nil
Presenting: Unwell 2 weeks after trip
to Kerala, India.
Symptoms: Initially flu-like illness
(fever, tiredness, malaise), now
resolved → persistent tiredness, dark
urine, pale loose stools, itching,
yellow eyes (mum noticed).
Social: Chef in Indian restaurant,
lives with parents, no alcohol, no
smoking, not sexually active, no
tattoos/drugs/transfusions.
ICE:
- Ideas: Thinks malaria despite
prophylaxis.
- Concerns: Health + work fitness
(chef role).
- Expectations: Wants explanation +
advice on return to work.
Red flags to exclude:
- Confusion, headache, tremors
(hepatic encephalopathy).
- Severe abdominal pain (fulminant
hepatitis).
- Dehydration (vomiting, poor fluid
intake).
- Bleeding/bruising (coagulopathy).
Focused History:
- Timeline: onset, resolution of fever.
- Fever pattern: cyclical vs continuous (malaria vs viral hepatitis).
- GI: jaundice, pale stools, dark urine, nausea, vomiting, pruritus,
abdominal pain (esp. RUQ).
- Neuro: headache, confusion, tremors.
- Systemic: appetite loss, weight loss.
- Exposures: street food, water, mosquito bites, malaria prophylaxis.
- Risk factors: tattoos, blood transfusion, drug use, sex, travel
contacts.
- Work: chef → public health importance.
- Family/household contacts.
Good SCA Phrases:
- “I’ll ask about some warning signs that can happen when the liver is
inflamed.”
- “Even though the fever has gone, I want to check for symptoms that
can point us in the right direction.”
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “Your symptoms and history fit with a viral infection of the liver
called Hepatitis A. It’s caught through contaminated food or water,
which makes sense given your recent trip and eating street food.”
- “The yellow eyes, pale stools, and dark urine happen because your
liver is irritated and not processing waste normally.”
- “This usually gets better on its own, and most people recover fully.”
- “Malaria can cause fever, but it usually doesn’t settle on its own,
and jaundice is less common. In your case, Hepatitis A is more
likely.”
Investigations & Notifications:
- Arrange urgent face-to-face review for bloods: LFTs, hepatitis serology,
U+Es, FBC, CRP, malaria screen.
- Notify Health Protection Team (HPT) immediately (legal requirement).
Symptom Relief:
- Itching → antihistamine (chlorphenamine), loose clothes, cool
environment, avoid hot baths.
- Pain → paracetamol/ibuprofen if safe.
Public Health & Work:
- Stay off work until 7 days after jaundice or systemic symptoms started.
- Household contacts in food-handling jobs → also off work until HPT
advice.
- Emphasise strict handwashing and hygiene.
- No sexual activity (including oro-anal/oro-genital) until 7 days after
jaundice/symptom onset (if relevant).
Follow-up:
- Review in 1–2 weeks with repeat LFTs if abnormal.
- Safety net: seek urgent help if worsening tiredness, confusion,
abdominal swelling, bleeding, or vomiting.
- No need for vaccination in the future given long life immunity
Good SCA Phrases:
- “This is a notifiable infection — I’ll need to inform public health, but this
is routine and not your fault.”
- “You’ll recover fully, and the good news is once you’ve had Hepatitis A,
you don’t need the vaccine in the future — you’ll have natural immunity for
life.”
- “I know you’re worried about work. Because you handle food, it’s
important you stay off work until we know you’re no longer infectious. The
health protection team will support you with this.”
- “This sounds very much like hepatitis A, which is usually short-lived and
people recover fully.”
- “The liver needs rest—so avoid alcohol and unnecessary medicines until
blood tests confirm recovery.”
- “We’ll arrange blood tests to confirm the diagnosis and monitor your liver
function.”
- “Because this infection can spread, I’ll notify the public health team—
they can give advice and protect close contacts.”
- “If you develop confusion, heavy bleeding, or become very unwell,
please go to hospital immediately.”

- Sleepwalking
- Snoring/apnoea
- Neurodevelopmental issues
(ADHD/autism)
- Psychosocial stressors/abuse
- Parental mental health issues
Key questions / phrases:

(≥3 times/week, ≥3 mo = insomnia)

(pattern)
- “How many hours of sleep does he get in 24 hrs?”
- “Does he nap in the day?”
- “Has there been any big change at home, school or nursery?”
(psychosocial triggers)

RF)
- “Any nightmares, night terrors, or sleepwalking?”

- “What’s his bedtime routine like? Does he use a tablet or have fizzy

development → all normal
- “How are you coping? You mentioned being exhausted — have you
been feeling low or anxious yourself?”

“From what you’ve described, it sounds like Ciaran is struggling with
sleep because of his bedtime routine — things like screen time and
fizzy drinks can really affect children’s ability to switch off. This is
what we call poor sleep hygiene, and it’s very common at this age.”
Management (key exam points + phrases):
- No medication: “Sleeping tablets, including antihistamines like Piriton,
aren’t safe for children with sleep problems. They don’t teach children
how to fall asleep naturally and can even make things worse in the long
run.”
- Routine: “What will really help is a consistent bedtime routine — same
time every night, a calm wind-down with bath/story, no screens for an hour
before bed.” Avoid naps during day
- Environment: Quiet, dark room, night-light if afraid.
- Avoid stimulants: No fizzy/caffeinated drinks in evening.
- Behavioural: If he wakes and comes to your room → gently return to his
bed consistently, with brief reassurance.
- Sleep diary: “A diary will help us see patterns and measure progress.”
For 4 weeks then review
- Parent wellbeing: “I’m also mindful this is affecting your own sleep and
mood. Would you be open to a separate appointment just for you, so we
can support you too?” Recommend Headspace app and therapy
- Follow-up: Review in 4 weeks.
- Safety net: “If you notice him snoring heavily, stopping breathing at night,
or if he develops frequent nightmares or walking in his sleep — please let
us know straight away as that would need specialist input.”
Good SCA phrases:
“Let’s try to get him back into a consistent bedtime routine, ideally starting
earlier than 10pm.”
“A calm wind-down period before bed helps — a warm bath, a short story,
and no screens for at least an hour before sleep.”
“If he wakes at night and comes to you, gently return him to his bed and
offer reassurance — consistency helps him learn to settle.”
“Fizzy drinks or caffeine in the evening should be avoided, as they can
make it harder to sleep.”

Creatinine & eGFR stable

diet at end: “Do I still need to keep
following a low-salt diet?”

drowsiness, seizures, headache,
nausea/vomiting, palpitations, chest
pain, SOB, falls
Why blood test? → “Because of new BP tablet (Indapamide).”
Screen for hyponatraemia symptoms:
“Any new confusion, drowsiness, headaches, jerking
movements, blackouts?”
“Any nausea, vomiting, excessive thirst or drinking lots of water?”
“Any chest pain, palpitations, cough, breathlessness?”
“Any diarrhoea or recent illness?”
“Any change in passing urine?”
“Any weight loss or appetite changes?”
“Any recent falls or unsteadiness?”
Lifestyle & contributing factors:
• Low-salt diet → could worsen hyponatraemia.
• No alcohol/smoking.
• No OTC meds.
Diagnosis (plain words): “Your blood test shows your sodium (a salt
in your blood) is a bit low — likely due to the new tablet Indapamide.
You’re not unwell now, but in older adults it can increase risks like
tiredness, confusion or falls.”
- “The water tablet you are taking can sometimes lower the level of
salt in the blood.”
- “When salt gets too low, it can cause problems like feeling weak,
dizzy, confused, or even fits if severe.”
- “We have picked this up early on a blood test, which is good — but
it means we need to review your medication and keep you safe.”
Immediate:
Stop Indapamide (likely cause).
- Send to the hospital if unwell/ severe hyponatraemia < 125 or
symptomatic
Monitoring:
Repeat U&Es in 1 week.
If sodium still low → add TFT, FBC, LFT, urine/serum osmolality, BNP if
HF suspected.
Diet: Return to normal balanced salt diet (not restricted).
BP management:
Consider alternative (Bisoprolol / Doxazosin) at review.
Re-check BP when reviewing in 1–2 weeks.
Safety net (strong phrases):
“If you develop confusion, drowsiness, vomiting, severe headache or fits,
please call 999 immediately.”
“If you feel generally unwell or worried before your next test, contact us
sooner.”
Follow-up:
Arrange telephone/face-to-face in 1 week for repeat bloods + BP
review.
Good SCA phrases
- “It’s a recognised side effect of the new tablet – you’ve done the right
thing by having the test.”
- “Right now you’re well, but we don’t want to risk confusion or falls, so I’d
like to act early.”
- “I suggest we stop that tablet today and recheck your bloods in a week or
two.”
- “Please let us know urgently if you feel muddled, drowsy, sick, or if your
wife notices anything unusual.”
- “You asked about the salt diet – in fact, I’d recommend a normal
balanced salt intake from now.”
Closing phrase: “Thank you for raising the question about diet — yes, I’d
recommend going back to a normal balanced salt intake, as cutting salt
too low can make this problem worse.”

- Acute limb ischaemia (cold,

- Worsening pain despite opioids
- Infection (fever, erythema, swelling)
- Pulmonary embolism (shortness of

- Discolouration, temperature, pulses, sensation.

- Provide Daffodil Line / palliative care contact for urgent queries.
- Safety-net: contact GP/OOH if worsening pain, new confusion, or
distress.
Good Phrases:
- “We’ll take a careful stepwise approach, prioritising his comfort and
respecting his wishes.”
- “Even at home, we can adjust his medications and use all the resources

- Polyuria, polydipsia, blurred vision

- Neuropathy: tingling/numbness,

- Weight change unexplained
- Cardiovascular risk: chest pain, SOB

- Duration of tiredness, increased thirst, weight changes.
- Urinary: frequency, nocturia, infections.
- Vision: blurred vision or sudden changes.
- Feet: numbness, tingling, ulcers, non-healing wounds.
- Cardiovascular: chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations.
- Lifestyle: diet (fast food), activity, alcohol, smoking.
- Family history: diabetes (father).
- Housing/social: mobility, new location, follow-up plans.
Good Phrases:
- “Your symptoms and blood results suggest Type 2 Diabetes.”
- “This means your blood sugar is higher than it should be.”
- “With good control, most serious complications can be prevented.”
- “Type 2 diabetes happens when the body becomes resistant to
insulin, the hormone that controls sugar, or doesn’t make enough
of it.”
- “This means sugar builds up in the blood instead of being used for
energy.”
- “It’s common and can be well controlled with lifestyle changes
and medicines, which lower the risk of long-term problems like
heart, kidney, and eye disease.”
- Lifestyle first: “Healthy eating, regular activity, and weight control are
the most effective ways to start.” Refer to structured education
(DESMOND/Diabetes UK).
Immediate Management:
- Start Metformin: 500mg daily with breakfast, titrate weekly to max
1500mg/day with meals.
- Advise lifestyle changes: high-fibre carbs, fruits, vegetables, low-fat
dairy, oily fish; limit sugary drinks and processed food. Encourage physical
activity (walking/exercise).
- Diabetic Passport: portable summary of HbA1c, medications,
complications, and actions to take if hypo/hyperglycaemia.
- Offer pneumococcal, influenza, COVID vaccines; document in
Diabetic Passport.
- Recommend medical alert bracelet for emergencies.
- Advise register with new GP ASAP; transfer records electronically.
- Safety net: hypoglycaemia (shakiness, sweating, confusion), DKA
(vomiting, abdominal pain, deep breathing).
- Annual foot and eye checks at new GP.
- Very heavy safety netting – with providing glucometer + book a nurse
appointment for more advice
Good SCA Phrases:
- “Even though you feel well, these steps help prevent long-term
complications.”
- “Because you’re moving, we’ll make sure your care continues smoothly
with your new GP.”
- “The Diabetic Passport is a handy record to help any future healthcare
professional manage your diabetes safely.”
- “If you notice sudden weight loss, vomiting, tummy pain, or feeling
very drowsy — seek urgent help (999/A&E) as it could be severe diabetes
complication.”
- “Come back if you’re struggling with symptoms or side effects from

- “We’ll review you regularly to keep sugars safe and prevent

& urine culture negative

History to cover:
Onset, frequency, and duration of episodes
Colour: bright red vs brown/rust
Associated symptoms: pain (perineum, testicles, lower

Recent trauma to groin/pelvic area or high-risk activities (cycling,
horse riding)
Sexual history: partners, condom use, STI history
Medications: NSAIDs, anticoagulants
Bleeding elsewhere: gums, nose, rectum
Systemic symptoms (red flags): fever, chills, malaise, weight
loss, night sweats
Family history: prostate, testicular, urinary tract cancers
Anaemia red flags: headaches, dizziness, palpitations, chest
pain, shortness of breath
Red flags for urgent referral:
• Persistent or recurrent haematospermia
• Systemic symptoms: fever, weight loss, night sweats
• Painful ejaculation or perineal/testicular pain
• Abnormal findings on examination or investigations (PSA, DRE,
ultrasound)
• Age >40 with unexplained haematospermia
Good Phrases:
- - “Seeing blood in the semen can be worrying, but in most men it is
not dangerous and often settles on its own.”
- “It can happen after an infection, inflammation, or even after
vigorous sexual activity.”
- “In younger men it is usually harmless, but in older men or if it
keeps happening, we sometimes check for prostate or urinary tract
problems.”
- “Your STI screen was clear, so infection is unlikely.”
Immediate Management:
• Offer face-to-face review: examine abdomen, prostate, genitalia
• Arrange semen culture to rule out occult infection
• Blood tests: FBC, clotting profile, U+Es, LFTs
• PSA test: discuss and offer, especially given patient concern, even
though <40
• Advise stopping ibuprofen; use paracetamol for migraine
• Reassure: most cases in men <40 are benign and self-limiting, unlikely
to affect fertility and usually resolve in 4 weeks
• Safety-net: return if symptoms persist, worsen, or new symptoms
appear (pain, fever, weight loss)
• If haematospermia continues despite normal investigations → refer to
Urology
• Refer all patient >40 years if no identifiable cause
- Red flag referrals: suspected malignancy, persistent bleeding, abnormal
PSA/DRE, or recurrent episodes without cause
• SCA phrases:
– “I understand this is worrying; let’s take a thorough look to be sure
nothing serious is missed.”
– “Most cases in your age group resolve on their own and are not
harmful to fertility.”
– “Stopping ibuprofen may help prevent further bleeding, and we can
review migraine management safely.”
- “If the bleeding keeps coming back, or if you notice blood in urine, pain,
or difficulty passing urine — come back quickly.”
- “If you develop fever, severe pelvic pain, or feel very unwell, please seek
urgent help.”

• Medical abortion: suitable up to 10 weeks, involves two medications,
cramping/bleeding common, pain relief with ibuprofen/paracetamol
• Surgical abortion: procedure to remove pregnancy tissue, typically after

- Alternative: continuing pregnancy or adoption if desired
- Referral: BPAS (> 6 weeks), MSI, or NUPAS for support, treatment, and
follow-up. can have it at home <10 weeks
- Urgent referral if >20 weeks to ensure within legal timeframe.
- Safeguarding referral if under pressure, abuse, or not competent.
- Offer STI screening
- Discuss contraception options post-abortion (pill, implant, coil)
- Provide written resources/leaflets
- Safety net: urgent care if heavy bleeding, severe pain, fever, fainting
- Follow-up: repeat pregnancy test in 3 weeks
- Confidentiality: respect Gillick competency, Fraser guidelines
Good SCA Phrases:

- “We also provide contraception advice to help prevent future unplanned
pregnancies.”

- “I understand your concerns; it’s great that you’ve sought help.”

• Blood tests: thyroid function, HbA1c, lipids to rule out underlying issues

• SCA phrases:

– “Lifestyle changes are the safest and most effective first step at his

– “We want Adam to feel supported at school and at home while we

of the bed.”

(145/90 last visit)

Emotional impact – guilt, anxiety

• Arrange correct vaccination: “Ensure patient receives pneumococcal
vaccine safely.”
• Incident reporting: “Document in patient record and complete incident
form — this is a learning tool, not blame.”
• Reassurance: “Vitamin B12 is generally safe; side effects are usually
mild.”
• Emotional support: “Consider short time off, mindfulness apps, or GP
support if stressed.”
• Professional support: “You rarely lose your job for a single incident,
especially if managed transparently. Contact RCN or defence organisation
if concerned.”
• Financial support: “MoneyHelper or social prescriber can help with
financial stress.”
Key SCA Phrases:
- “Mistakes can happen, what matters is recognising them and taking
steps to prevent recurrence.”
- “The duty of candour means being open and honest with the patient,
which is professional and expected.”
- “Completing an incident form allows us to reflect, learn, and improve

- “It’s not a sign of weakness — it’s the brain’s way of trying to cope
after trauma.”
Management / SCA phrases:
• Screening: Trauma Screening Questionnaire (TSQ): “This helps confirm
the severity of PTSD symptoms and guide next steps.”
• Sleep hygiene:
– Set regular bedtime/wake time, avoid screens 1 hr before bed, step
away if unable to sleep, limit daytime naps
– Exercise earlier in the day
– SCA phrase: “Improving sleep habits can significantly reduce your
distress and improve daytime functioning.”
• Short-term medication: Zopiclone 7 tablets for 2 weeks, alternate
nights: “This is just to help you sleep temporarily while we address
underlying PTSD; it’s not a long-term solution.”
• Specialist referral: Veterans’ priority pathway for trauma-focused CBT
or EMDR: “This is the most effective treatment for PTSD and is tailored to
experiences like yours.”
• Consider antidepressant (SSRI/SNRI) if symptoms severe or awaiting
therapy: “Medication can help stabilise mood and reduce anxiety; we can
discuss options together.”
• Work support: Fit note / adjusted duties: “We want to make sure you
feel supported at work while recovering.”
• Safety-net: provide crisis team contact; review in 1–2 weeks: “If things
worsen or you feel unsafe, there are immediate supports available.”
• Veteran support groups: Combat Stress, Veterans UK: “Connecting
with others who’ve had similar experiences can be very helpful.”
• Good SCA phrases for management:
• “It’s completely understandable that you’re struggling; PTSD is a normal
response to extreme trauma.”
• “By combining therapy, sleep strategies, and support at work, we can
help you regain control and improve your daily life.”
• “You’ve already taken a courageous step by seeking help — that’s an
important part of recovery.”
• “Let’s make a plan together to support both your sleep and your mental
health safely.”

28- TATT= Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
PC: Persistent fatigue for 8–10 weeks,
worse with minor activity
Social: Accountant, lives with
husband and 2 children, non-smoker,
non-drinker
ICE:
• Ideas – unsure, thinks it might be
vitamin deficiency
• Concerns – struggling to manage
work and family
• Expectations – wants explanation,
investigations, and advice
Risk factors: Recent flu-like illness,
functional impact at work and home
When to Suspect
- ≥6 weeks of persistent fatigue in
adults, not caused by exertion,
significantly reducing daily
functioning.
- Post-exertional malaise (PEM):
delayed worsening after activity,
disproportionate and prolonged
recovery.
- Unrefreshing sleep or sleep
disturbance: flu-like exhaustion on
waking, shallow or disrupted sleep.
- Cognitive difficulties: brain fog,
memory or concentration problems.
- Often accompanied by muscle/joint
pain, headaches or orthostatic
intolerance.
Red Flags – urgent review:
- Features suggesting other
diagnoses: weight loss, fever, organ
symptoms. - Abnormal findings on
exam or bloods. - Rapid progressive
neurological symptoms
Focused History:
- Fatigue characteristics: duration, triggers, relief (or lack thereof).
- PEM: timing, delay, length of recovery.
- Sleep: unrefreshing, hypersomnia, broken sleep.
- Cognitive symptoms: concentration, memory, speech clarity.
- Associated symptoms: pain, dizziness on standing, headaches.
- Review investigations: rule out thyroid disease, anaemia, renal or
liver dysfunction.
- Impact & ICE: effect on work, mood, belief in illness origin.
Explanation in Lay Terms (mechanism included):
- “CFS is a condition where your body and brain become unusually
sensitive to everyday activity — even small tasks can make you feel
overwhelmingly tired.”
- “It often follows an infection and affects how your energy, sleep,
and thinking work. Rest may not help, and recovery after activity can
be slow and delayed.”
- “We don’t yet understand the exact cause — it may involve immune
system changes, autonomic dysfunction, or altered metabolism.”
Good SCA phrases:
• “Thank you for explaining how this fatigue is affecting your work
and family — that helps me understand the impact on your daily
life.”
• “I’d like to ask a few questions about your sleep, energy levels, and
any other symptoms to rule out anything serious.”
• “It’s important to explore both physical and mental health factors
so we can provide the most effective support.”
• “It’s important not to push through the fatigue with unstructured
exercise, as that can make symptoms worse.”
“Many people use a diary to track how much activity they can
manage before symptoms worsen. This helps identify your personal
limits.”
“Small, regular rests are better than pushing through and collapsing
later.”
“On good days, it’s tempting to do more, but pacing means keeping
within safe limits even then, to prevent setbacks.”
• “We want to make sure nothing treatable is being missed, so we’ll do a full
set of blood tests first.”
Your fatigue is real and valid, and with support we can help you gradually
regain energy for work and family life.” “Over time, pacing helps stabilise your
symptoms and may allow gradual, safe improvement.”
Explain that a diagnosis of ME/CFS can only be confirmed after 3 months
of persistent unexplained symptoms
• Investigations: if > 6 weeks: blood tests (FBC, U&Es, LFTs, ESR, CRP,
TFTs, HbA1c, Vitamin D, B12, Ferritin, Folate, Coeliac screen, CK). “This
help rule out other causes and ensure we don’t miss something
treatable.”
• Referral: If tests normal and symptoms persistent, refer to specialist
CFS service for multidisciplinary support. “They can offer structured
programmes including physiotherapy and CBT tailored for fatigue.”
• Energy management & pacing: “Think of your energy like money in a
bank — you have a limited daily allowance. If you overspend, you go into
‘debt’ and feel much worse the next day. Pacing means planning activities
so you never fully run out.” “We call this the ‘energy envelope’ — try to
stay within the safe limits of what your body can manage. Overdoing it one
day can trigger a flare-up of symptoms.” “You’ll find it useful to spread
tasks across the week instead of cramming them into one day.”
“It’s about balancing activity and rest. For example, if you’ve done
something physically or mentally demanding, build in recovery time
straight after.”
• Symptom diary: Encourage recording daily energy levels and triggers.
“This helps identify patterns and plan activity safely.”
• Sleep hygiene: Encourage regular sleep schedule, relaxing bedtime
routines. “Good sleep habits can improve energy and overall wellbeing.”
• Talking therapy/CBT: Optional referral for coping strategies even if mood is
currently stable. “This helps manage the emotional and functional impact of long-
term fatigue.”
Support for Work and Education:
• Workers: Fit notes, phased return to work, reduced hours, flexible/remote
working, workplace adjustments under Equality Act.
• Students: Individual education plan, reduced timetable, rest breaks at
school/college, flexibility with exams/deadlines, liaison with teachers/university
support services.
• “We can write supportive letters for your employer or school to explain
adjustments needed.”
• “These adjustments are your right — it’s about making work or study possible, not
pushing through illness.”
Good SCA phrases for management:
• “I appreciate how challenging this has been for you — we’ll work together to
manage it safely.”
• “There’s no single cure, but structured support and pacing can significantly
improve daily functioning.”

29- Iron deficiency anaemia
Patient: Ameera Khan, 67F
PMH: MI 5 yrs ago, hypertension,
hypothyroidism
Meds: Aspirin 75mg OD, Ramipril
5mg OD, Bisoprolol 2.5mg OD,
Omeprazole 20mg OD, Levothyroxine
50mcg OD
PC: Review of blood results (FBC
shows Hb 109 g/L, MCV 72 fL, MCHC
22 pg)
Duration: Routine annual review
Other symptoms: None reported
Social: Lives alone, vegetarian diet
mainly packaged meals, lost
motivation to cook after husband’s
death
Ideas: Unsure what results mean
Concerns: Wants to understand
results
Expectations: Explanation of blood
test results
Risk factors: Age >65, poor dietary
iron intake, post-menopausal
Red Flags – urgent review:
- Symptomatic anaemia (chest pain,
syncope).
- Hb <70 g/L.
- GI bleeding symptoms (melaena,
haematemesis, PR bleeding).
- Weight loss, change in bowel habit,
family history of bowel cancer.
History – what to cover:
Reason for blood tests: routine review or specific concerns
Symptoms of anaemia: fatigue, dyspnoea, light-headedness,
dizziness, palpitations, chest pain
Bleeding history (red flags): haematemesis, melaena, rectal
bleeding, haematuria, epistaxis, gum bleeding, unexplained bruising,
heavy vaginal bleeding
GI symptoms (red flags): abdominal pain, dysphagia,
unintentional weight loss, appetite changes, persistent heartburn
Diet: low iron intake, recent changes, vegetarian/vegan diet
Medications: aspirin/anticoagulants (increase bleeding risk)
Social: support at home, ability to prepare meals Systemic:
fatigue severity, shortness of breath, pallor, weakness
Past medical & family history: GI malignancy, coeliac disease,
chronic illness
Good SCA phrases:
• “Thank you for booking this consultation — I can see you’d like to
understand your results better.”
• “I’d like to ask a few questions about your general health and any
bleeding or tummy symptoms, to ensure we don’t miss anything
serious.”
• “It’s important we consider all possibilities, but there’s no need to
panic — we’ll investigate thoroughly.”
Simple Dx explained:
“Your blood tests show microcytic anaemia, which usually points to
iron deficiency. In older adults, we always consider both dietary
causes and the possibility of slow blood loss in the gut, so we want
to investigate this safely.”
- “Your blood test shows you have anaemia, which means your red
blood cells are lower than normal.”
- “In your case, it’s likely due to low iron — iron is needed to make
haemoglobin, the part of blood that carries oxygen.” - “This isn’t
usually cancer, but because bleeding from the gut can be one cause,
we sometimes need to arrange tests to rule that out.”
Management / SCA phrases:
• Investigations:
– Ferritin and other haematinics (B12, folate)
– Coeliac screen
– Urinalysis for hidden blood
– FIT test (stool test for occult blood)
“We want to confirm whether the anaemia is due to iron deficiency and
rule out other causes before starting treatment.”
• Face-to-face review: To examine abdomen and assess for sources of
bleeding. “Seeing you in person allows us to examine your tummy and
ensure we don’t miss anything important.”
• Specialist referral: If iron deficiency confirmed, refer to
gastroenterology for possible gastroscopy/colonoscopy. “These tests are
safe and help us identify any hidden causes of blood loss.”
• Dietary advice: Encourage iron-rich foods (dark leafy greens, fortified
cereals, apricots, prunes). Consider dietitian referral. “Even simple
changes to your diet can help, and a dietitian can make it easier to plan
meals.”
• Iron supplements: Only start after confirming iron deficiency. “Iron
tablets are effective, but we need to confirm deficiency first; we’ll monitor
for side effects like constipation or dark stools.”
• Safety-net advice (red flags): Seek urgent care if new bleeding occurs
(urine, stool, gums), chest pain, shortness of breath, dizziness,
palpitations. “If you notice any of these symptoms, please contact us
immediately or call 111.”
• Follow-up: Review in 2–3 days after additional tests. “We’ll go over the
results together and plan next steps to manage your anaemia safely.”
Good SCA phrases:
• “Your blood results show something we can investigate and manage; it’s
important to be thorough at your age.”
• “We’ll work together to find the cause and support you with diet and
treatment if needed.”
• “Our goal is to treat the anaemia safely while making sure nothing more
serious is missed.”

30- Dizziness = Vestibular Neuronitis
Patient: Chioma Eze, 34F
PMH: Normal vaginal delivery 7
months ago
Meds: None
PC: Dizziness/vertigo for 4 days
Duration: 4 days
Other symptoms: Nausea, vomiting;
no hearing loss or tinnitus
Social: Single parent with 2
dependent children, lives alone, on
maternity leave, drives but not
currently
Ideas: Worried about brain tumour
Concerns: Safety with children,
impact on daily functioning
Expectations: Advice, medication,
possibly scan
Risk factors: Post-viral, postpartum,
caring for dependent children
History – what to cover:
Characterise dizziness: vertigo vs. light-headedness
Onset, duration, progression
Pattern: constant vs. intermittent (constant in vestibular
neuronitis; triggered in BPPV)
Exacerbating factors: head movements worsen vertigo
Associated symptoms: nausea, vomiting, hearing loss, tinnitus
(hearing loss absent – supports vestibular neuronitis)
Preceding viral illness (flu-like symptoms 3 days prior)
Red flags / neurological: headache, visual changes, slurred
speech, weakness/numbness, falls
Impact: difficulty caring for children, ability to drive
Lifestyle & social: support at home, work, alcohol, smoking
Good SCA phrases:
• “Thank you for calling — I understand this dizziness has been
distressing and is affecting your ability to care for your children.”
• “I’d like to ask some questions to understand exactly what’s
happening and to make sure we don’t miss anything serious.”
• “It’s reassuring that you haven’t noticed any weakness, visual
changes, or speech problems — these would be red flags for more
serious conditions.”
Simple Dx explained:
“Based on your history — constant spinning dizziness after a viral
illness, worsened by head movement but without hearing loss — this
sounds like vestibular neuronitis. This is an inflammation of the
nerve in your inner ear that helps with balance. It’s usually self-
limiting, and most people recover in a few weeks.”
Management / SCA phrases:
• Face-to-face assessment: Examine ears, BP, cranial nerves, peripheral
nerves, head impulse test. “Seeing you allows me to rule out serious
neurological problems and examine your balance safely.”
• Symptomatic treatment: Prescribe short course antiemetic (cyclizine
or cinnarizine) for 3 days max. “This will help with nausea, but only for a
short period to allow your body to naturally recover.”
• Breastfeeding advice: Small amounts pass into milk; monitor baby for
drowsiness/lethargy; express milk if concerned. “The risk is very low, but
we’ll keep an eye on your baby.”
• Hydration & rest: Encourage fluids and rest. “Staying hydrated and
resting helps your recovery.”
• Support at home: Ask about friends, relatives, neighbours, or paid
childcare; consider social prescriber if needed. “It’s important to have
help looking after your children while you recover safely.”
• Activity precautions: Avoid driving or operating machinery. “This is
important to keep both you and your children safe.”
• Follow-up: Review in 1 week to monitor recovery. “We’ll check how
you’re doing and adjust care if needed.”
• Safety-net / red flags: Seek urgent medical attention if new/worsening
symptoms occur: headache, blurred vision, slurred speech, weakness,
numbness, persistent vomiting. “If you notice any of these, don’t wait —
contact 111 or go to urgent care immediately.”
Good SCA phrases:
• “This condition is usually self-limiting, but we’ll support you to feel more
comfortable while your body recovers.”
• “Using medication short-term can relieve symptoms without slowing
your recovery.”
• “We’ll put a plan in place to keep both you and your baby safe while you
recover.”

Condition /
Cause
Key Features / Focused History Lay Explanation (SCA
phrasing)
Management & SCA Phrases Red Flags / Safety Netting
BPPV (Benign
Paroxysmal
Positional
Vertigo)
- Sudden, brief vertigo (<1 min), triggered
by head movement (rolling over, looking
up)- Nausea; no hearing loss- No
neurological deficits
“Tiny crystals in your inner
ear have shifted, causing
brief spinning sensations
when you move your head.”
- Epley maneuver in clinic- Reassure: self-
limiting- Advise gradual head movements to
prevent falls- SCA phrases: “This vertigo is
usually harmless and improves with
repositioning maneuvers.”
- Neurological symptoms (weakness,
numbness, diplopia, dysarthria, ataxia)-
Persistent vertigo not positional- Falls or
inability to walk safely
Meniere’s
Disease
- Recurrent vertigo episodes (20 min–24 h)-
Unilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, aural
fullness- Nausea, vomiting- Progressive
hearing loss over months
“Fluid builds up in your
inner ear, causing vertigo,
ringing in the ear, and
hearing changes.”
- Acute attacks: vestibular suppressants (short-
term), antiemetics- Long-term: low-salt diet,
diuretics- ENT referral for specialist
management- SCA phrases: “Attacks are
temporary, but hearing may gradually change.”
- New or bilateral hearing loss- Severe
tinnitus- Persistent severe vertigo-
Neurological deficits (stroke signs)
Labyrinthitis - Sudden, severe, continuous vertigo
(hours–days)- Hearing loss present
(unilateral)- Nausea, vomiting, gait
instability- Often post-viral
“A virus has inflamed your
inner ear, affecting both
balance and hearing.”
- Supportive care: hydration, short-term
vestibular suppressants- Vestibular
physiotherapy- Usually self-limiting;
improvement over days to weeks- SCA
phrases: “This usually improves with time and
exercises, but we monitor your hearing.”
- New or worsening hearing loss-
Neurological deficits- Fever, headache,
photophobia, neck stiffness (possible CNS
infection)- Post-trauma vertigo- Falls or
inability to walk safely
Vestibular
Neuritis
- Sudden, severe, continuous vertigo
(hours–days)- No hearing loss- Nausea,
vomiting, gait instability- Often post-viral
“A virus has inflamed the
balance nerve in your ear,
causing intense spinning.”
- Supportive care: hydration, short-term
vestibular suppressants- Vestibular
physiotherapy to aid compensation- Usually
self-limiting; improvement over days to weeks-
SCA phrases: “Symptoms are severe initially
but improve with exercises and time.”
- Neurological deficits (weakness, diplopia,
dysarthria, ataxia)- Persistent severe vertigo
not improving- Falls or inability to walk
safely- Fever or signs of CNS infection
Central Vertigo - Often gradual or sudden vertigo, may be
constant- Neurological deficits common:
diplopia, dysarthria, limb weakness,
numbness, ataxia- Vertical or direction-
changing nystagmus, not inhibited by
fixation- No hearing loss usually
“This type of dizziness is
caused by the brain or
brainstem, not the inner ear.
It can be more serious and
needs urgent evaluation.”
- Urgent referral / hospital assessment
(stroke, tumor, MS, posterior circulation
issues)- Imaging: MRI brain / CT if acute-
Supportive care as per underlying cause- SCA
phrases: “We need urgent evaluation to rule
out serious causes affecting the brain.”
- Sudden neurological deficits (weakness,
numbness, facial droop, dysarthria,
diplopia)- Severe imbalance or inability to
walk- Sudden headache, vomiting-
Persistent vertigo without positional
triggers- Any signs suggesting stroke,
tumor, MS

- “My job is to keep you safe — that means avoiding sudden

- “Jason, you’ve developed a reliance on these strong pain and sleep

with support.”

- Medication:
• Option 1: Prescribe a short-term supply of pregabalin, tramadol, and

• Option 2: Do not restart long-term; instead arrange supervised
reduction with pharmacist input.

- Pain:
• Option 1: Refer to physiotherapy for long-term back pain support.

contraindications) for flare-ups.
- Sleep:
• Option 1: Explore sleep hygiene (routine, no screens, activity).
• Option 2: Refer to talking therapy for chronic insomnia.
- Social support: Refer to social prescriber for housing/employment
support.
- Charities: Signpost to Nacro, Prison Reform Trust, Hardman Directory,
Shelter, Unlock to support people transitioning out of prison
- Safety net: Contact GP if new red flag back symptoms, worsening
withdrawal, suicidal thoughts, or housing crisis.
- Driving: advice against driving
Good Phrases:
- “I can give you a small supply today to keep you safe, but we’ll need to
taper gradually.”
- “Stopping suddenly could be dangerous — so we’ll reduce step by step,
together.”
- “For your back pain, we’ll explore physio and safer pain relief options.”
- “We’ll get support for your housing and link you with services that help
people after prison.”
- “Let’s book a review in 2 weeks, but if anything changes before then —
especially new pain or weakness — please reach out sooner.”

Reason: Telephone consultation to
request DNACPR.
Trigger: Recently saw brother
resuscitated → survived with severe
brain injury → now in vegetative state.
ICE:
- Idea: Understands CPR ≠
guaranteed recovery.
- Concern: Fear of survival with poor
quality of life (“becoming a
vegetable”).
- Expectation: Wants GP to complete
DNACPR form.
Red Flags to exclude:
- Low mood / depression or suicidal
intent.
- Misconception that DNACPR =
refusal of all care.
- Family unaware (potential for future
conflict).
Focused History:
- What prompted this now? (explore impact of brother’s story).
- How is he coping emotionally? (screen for trauma/depression).
- What does he understand about CPR (procedure, survival rates,
risks)?
- Clarify misconceptions: DNACPR only applies to CPR, not other
treatments.
- Any other health worries or recent hospital admissions?
- Has he spoken to family about this?
- Would he want to explore Advance Decision to Refuse Treatment
(ADRT) or Advance Care Plan (ACP)?
Good Phrases:
- “I’m really sorry to hear about what happened to your brother — I
can see how much that’s affected you.”
- “CPR can sometimes save lives, but not always in the way people
hope — and the risks of brain injury or poor recovery are real.”
- “A DNACPR only applies to resuscitation, it doesn’t mean we’d stop
treating you for other things like infections or pain.”
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “I understand you had a distressing experience with your brother,
and it’s completely reasonable to have worries about CPR.”
- “DNACPR means that if your heart or breathing stops, CPR will
not be attempted, but all other care will continue.”
- “We can discuss your wishes, and I want to make sure you fully
understand what this means, so your decision is informed and
respected.”
Immediate Management (with options):
- Capacity & understanding: Confirm he has capacity, understands CPR,
and that DNACPR ≠ refusing other treatments.
- Discussion & documentation:
• Option 1: Complete DNACPR form today (document in GP record,
summary care record, and provide copy for home).
• Option 2: Arrange face-to-face appointment to finalise decision and
ensure form signed correctly.
- Family involvement: Encourage discussing with wife/sons to avoid
future confusion. Clarify decision is his alone.
- Legal aspects:
• Explain DNACPR is not legally binding, but strongly respected
clinically.
• Option: If he wants legally binding refusal → explore ADRT (Advance
Decision to Refuse Treatment).
- Advance planning: Offer written info, consider Advance Care Plan
and/or Lasting Power of Attorney for health & welfare.
- Offer psychological support if trauma affects decision-making.
- Review regularly — decisions can be revisited as circumstances or
preferences change.
- Safety net: He can change his mind anytime; advise to contact surgery if
wishes change.
Good Phrases:
- “This decision is yours — and it will be respected.”
- “A DNACPR only stops CPR; it doesn’t stop us giving you oxygen,
antibiotics, fluids, or admitting you to hospital if you need it.”
- “The form guides doctors, but if you want your wishes to be legally
binding, we can look at an Advance Decision.”
- “I’d encourage you to share this with your family so they’re not left with
uncertainty later.”
- “If at any point you change your mind, just let us know and we’ll update
the plan.”
- “We will review this regularly to make sure your wishes continue to be
respected safely.”

34- Epilepsy
Name: Michelle Williams, 23F
Background: Epilepsy since 17,
usually on Tegretol PR 400mg BD,
stopped 7 months ago.
Reason: Urgent telephone
appointment after suspected
nocturnal seizure (incontinence +
bitten tongue).
ICE:
- Ideas: Unsure why seizure returned,
thought she was “cured.”
- Concerns: Worried seizures may
return.
- Expectations: Wants to restart her
epilepsy medication today.
Red Flags to Exclude:
- New neuro symptoms: persistent
headaches, visual disturbance, limb
weakness.
- Signs of CNS infection: fever,
photophobia, neck stiffness.
- Head injury, prolonged confusion.
Focused History:
- Seizure episode: when, how she noticed (bitten tongue,
incontinence), recovery phase.
- Type: Usually tonic–clonic with aura (light-headedness), LOC, jerks,
post-ictal sleep.
- Last seizure: 9 months ago.
- Triggers: stress, sleep, alcohol, flashing lights, infection.
- Medication: stopped Tegretol 7 months ago (own decision, no side
effects) – ask about reasons
- Past neurology FU: missed 3 appointments.
- Social: Drives daily, works as accountant, sexually active (new
partner 3 weeks), and exclude pregnancy
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “This was likely a breakthrough seizure due to stopping your
epilepsy medication.”
“Epilepsy means that the brain’s electrical activity can become
overactive or misfire, which causes seizures. Seizures can look
different for different people — some involve shaking, others involve
staring or confusion. It doesn’t mean brain damage each time, but it
does mean the brain is more prone to these bursts of activity.”
Good Phrases:
- “Thank you for explaining so clearly — it helps me understand what
happened.”
- “From what you’ve described — tongue bite and wetting yourself —
this does sound like a seizure.”
- “I can see why you felt well and stopped the tablets, but seizures
can come back suddenly if we don’t keep them controlled.”
- Why medication matters: “The medicines you take keep the
electrical activity in the brain steady. If doses are missed, the risk of
seizures increases very quickly.”
- “The important thing is to get back on track with your medication
and avoid further episodes.”
Immediate Management Options:
- Restart medication: Resume Tegretol 400mg BD (her previous effective
dose).
- Bloods + baseline checks: FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT, electrolytes, drug levels
if indicated.
- Neurology referral: Urgent referral back to epilepsy clinic.
- Driving advice: Must stop driving and inform DVLA after any seizure.
- Contraception: Carbamazepine reduces effectiveness of some
contraceptives (esp. COCP); risk of fetal harm in pregnancy. Interim
condom use; arrange contraception review.
- Lifestyle safety: No swimming alone, baths, heights, or heavy machinery
until stable.
- Medical ID + seizure alarm if living alone.
- Patient education: Do not stop AEDs without medical advice. Explain
SUDEP (gently, if asked).
- Safety net: Call GP/ED if recurrent seizure, new headaches, weakness,
vision change, or prolonged confusion.
Good Phrases:
- “It’s really important to restart your epilepsy medication so we can get
your seizures back under control.”
- “I’ll also send an urgent referral so you have specialist follow-up as soon
as possible.”
- “Because you’ve had a seizure, you must stop driving for now — I know
that’s hard, but it’s a legal requirement to keep you and others safe.”
- “Carbamazepine can make some contraceptives less effective, so let’s
arrange a review soon and use condoms in the meantime.”
- “Our goal is to keep you safe and seizure-free, and we’ll work together
step by step.”
