35- TAAT + low platelets =
Thrombocytopenia
Name: Michael Hollaway, 39M
Background: No PMH, no meds, no
allergies.
Reason: Phone call to discuss blood
test results (done for bruising +
fatigue).
Bloods: Hb 14 (normal), WBC 5.5
(normal), Platelets 80 (low).
ICE:
- Ideas: Thinks it might be leukaemia
(sister had it, passed away).
- Concerns: Very worried about same
diagnosis.
- Expectations: Wants a clear
explanation of the results.
DDx: Liver disease, medications,
alcohol, infections (HCV, HIV, H.
Pylori, TB)
Red Flags to Exclude:
- Bleeding (nosebleeds, gums,
haematemesis, melaena,
haematuria).
- Systemic symptoms: fever, night
sweats, weight loss, bone/joint pain.
- Infections, lumps/bumps,
abdominal pain/swelling.
- Platelets < 50
History to Cover:
- Presenting complaint: Tiredness + unexplained bruising for 5
weeks, worsening.
- Bleeding screen: Nosebleeds? Gum bleeding? Blood in
urine/stool/vomit? Heavy bleeding with minor cuts?
- Systemic screen: Night sweats, weight loss, fevers, bone/joint
pains.
- Abdominal: Pain, swelling, palpable lumps.
- Past infection: Sore throat 7 weeks ago (resolved).
- Meds: Any OTC (esp. NSAIDs)? No.
- FHx: Sister died of leukaemia.
- Social: Married, 3 kids, cloud engineer, non-smoker, no alcohol.
Good Phrases:
- “Thanks for explaining — I can hear this has been worrying you.”
- Addressing cancer worry: “I understand you’re worried this could
be blood cancer. From what we see so far, your red and white blood
cells are normal and only your platelets are affected — that pattern
is more in keeping with ITP, not blood cancer.
- “Let’s go through your results together step by step.”
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “Your blood test shows a low platelet count — that’s the part of
blood that helps with clotting. That explains the bruising you’ve
noticed.”
- “One possible cause is something called immune
thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) — where the body, sometimes after
an infection, mistakenly attacks platelets.”
- “I also take your concern about leukaemia seriously, but I want to
reassure you that your other blood cells (red and white) look normal,
which is reassuring.”
Next Steps (Management Plan):
- Examination: exclude splenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, bruising
- Investigations: Arrange urgent blood film, plus LFTs, U&Es, clotting
profile, B12, folate, ferritin, viral screen (HIV, Hepatitis if risk factors).
- Monitor: Book F/U in 1 week for results.
- Referral: If platelets drop further or film abnormal → urgent haematology
if < 50 and emergency if < 20
- Safety advice: Avoid contact sports/trauma, be cautious with sharp
objects, seek urgent help if severe bruising, bleeding (nose, gums,
stool/urine, vomiting blood) or new symptoms (dizziness, SOB, chest
pain). Avoid NSIADs
- Reassurance: Not everyone with low platelets has leukaemia. Many
cases are temporary and treatable.
Good Phrases:
- “I want to reassure you that your red and white cells are normal, which
makes leukaemia less likely.”
- “The most likely cause in your case is the immune system reacting after
that sore throat, something we call ITP.”
- “I’ll arrange further tests and a blood smear to look at your cells more
closely.”
- “If things get worse — for example, heavy bleeding, passing blood, or
feeling faint — please seek urgent help.”
- “Our aim is to keep you safe and get to the bottom of this quickly.”
- “Avoid medicines like ibuprofen or aspirin as they thin the blood.”
- “In many cases, especially in children, this condition improves by itself,
but adults may need treatment to boost platelets.”

-- 53 of 437 --

Bruising DDx
Clinical Clues / Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing) Management / SCA Tips
Risk Factors /
Predisposing Factors
- Medications: Steroids, antiplatelets, NSAIDs, SSRIs, anticoagulants.
- Medical conditions: Liver disease, frailty, alcohol excess.
- Family history: Haemophilia (X-linked), Von Willebrand Disease (autosomal
dominant).
- Other: Domestic abuse (consider safeguarding).
- Take full medication and social history.- Ask about family history of
bleeding disorders.
Differential Diagnosis
(DDx)
- Haematological: ITP, Haemophilia, Von Willebrand Disease, Leukaemia.
- Vasculitic: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP).
- Other: Malignancy, liver disease, Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome, medication-induced
bruising.
- Consider pattern, location, size, and frequency of bruises.- Assess for
systemic symptoms: fever, rash, joint pain, fatigue, weight loss.
Red Flags - Non-accidental injury (child abuse).
- Unexplained petechiae.
- Hepatosplenomegaly.
- FBC suggestive of leukaemia (low/high WCC, low platelets).
- Periorbital bruising in children.
- Bruises without trauma, or on unusual sites (torso, ears, neck).
- Any child with red flags requires urgent paediatric assessment.- Adults
with unexplained bruising or abnormal labs require haematology referral.
Referral Guidance –
Children
- Periorbital bruising: Urgent specialist assessment within 48 hours if accompanied by:
• Palpable abdominal mass
• Unexplained organomegaly (e.g., liver, spleen).
- All other children: Liaise with paediatrician or paediatric haematologist for
venepuncture & investigations.
- Investigations: FBC, clotting screen, liver function, coagulation factor
assays, urinalysis if HSP suspected.- Safeguarding if abuse suspected.
Referral Guidance –
Adults
- Immediate referral: Suspected leukaemia (pancytopenia, systemic symptoms).
- Routine referral: Low platelet count or abnormal clotting screen.
- Investigations: FBC, coagulation screen (INR, aPTT), liver function,
peripheral smear.
- Review medications and alcohol use.
- Haematology referral for confirmed or suspected bleeding disorder.
Lay Explanation (SCA
phrasing)
- “Bruising occurs when blood leaks under the skin, which can happen more easily due to
certain medications, medical conditions, or inherited bleeding disorders.”
- “Most bruises are minor, but unusual bruising, repeated unexplained bruises, or bruises
in unusual areas need further assessment.”
- Reassure if minor and explain risk factors.- Educate about reporting
abuse, avoiding trauma, and monitoring new bruises.
SCA Exam Tips:
• Location & pattern of bruises are key: limbs = common, torso/periorbital = red flag.
• Medications & family history are crucial clues.
• Urgent paediatric referral for periorbital bruising with abdominal mass/organomegaly.
• Adults: Consider haematology referral for abnormal labs.
• Always consider safeguarding in children.

-- 54 of 437 --

Condition When to Suspect (Primary
Care Entry Point)
Key Features & Focused History Red Flags Lay Explanation (SCA
phrasing)
Management & SCA Phrases
Immune
Thrombocytopenic
Purpura (ITP)
- Isolated thrombocytopenia on
FBC with normal Hb/WCC. -
Sudden onset bruising/petechiae
without trauma. - Child post-
viral illness with bleeding
tendency.
- Sudden bruising, petechiae, nose/gum
bleeds. - Often after viral illness. - No
lymphadenopathy/splenomegaly.
- Major bleeding. -
Neurological symptoms
(headache, seizures). -
Platelets <30 ×10⁹/L.
- “Your immune system is
attacking the platelets —
the cells that stop
bleeding.” - “This makes
bruising and bleeding
more likely, but it is not
usually cancer.”
- Repeat FBC, blood film. -
Urgent haematology if <30
×10⁹/L or bleeding. -
Steroids/IVIG if severe; often
resolves in children. - Phrase:
“We’ll involve the blood
specialists to confirm and keep
you safe.”
Lymphoma
(Hodgkin / Non-
Hodgkin)
- Persistent painless lymph node
>6 weeks. – unexplained
splenomegaly - Unexplained
fever, night sweats, weight loss.
- Generalised itching/fatigue.
- Painless lymphadenopathy (neck, axilla,
groin). - “B symptoms”: night sweats,
fever, weight loss. - Alcohol-induced
node pain (Hodgkin’s).
- Rapidly enlarging
nodes. - Airway
compromise. - Severe
systemic “B” symptoms.
- “Lymphoma is a type of
blood cancer that starts in
the lymph glands.” - “It
causes swollen glands,
night sweats, weight loss,
and tiredness.”
- 2WW referral (within 48
hours for children) for
excision biopsy. - Bloods,
CXR, LDH. - Phrase: “I’ll
urgently refer you to specialists
to confirm the diagnosis and
start treatment if needed.”
Leukaemia
(Acute/Chronic)
- Abnormal FBC with
blasts/pancytopenia. -
Unexplained bruising, recurrent
infections, fatigue. -
Hepatosplenomegaly or
lymphadenopathy in child/adult.
- Fatigue, infections, bruising/bleeding. -
Gum swelling, bone pain (esp. acute). -
Often pancytopenia.
- Acute blast crisis: fever,
sepsis, bleeding,
confusion. -
Hyperleukocytosis with
neuro/resp symptoms.
- “Leukaemia is a cancer
of the blood cells where
the bone marrow makes
abnormal white cells.” -
“This crowds out normal
cells, causing tiredness,
infections, and bleeding.”
- Urgent bloods within 48
hours if suspected
- Same-day urgent
haematology admission if
bloods showing leukaemia -
Confirm with blood film +
bone marrow biopsy. - Phrase:
“If you develop fever, heavy
bleeding, or sudden confusion,
call 999 immediately.”

36- Bedwetting/2ry Enuresis due to
constipation
Name: Adam Leatherhead, 6M
Background: PMH: bronchiolitis age
1, now on Movicol 2 sachets daily for
constipation. No allergies. Up-to-date
with immunisations.
Reason: Mum (Ruby) concerned
about bedwetting for past 3 months.
ICE:
- Idea: Mum worried it may be
diabetes (dad had T1DM as child).
- Concern: Secondary enuresis,
something serious going on.
- Expectation: Wants tests for
diabetes + explanation.
Red Flags to consider/exclude:
- Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss
(diabetes)
- Daytime wetting/urgency (UTI,
bladder overactivity)
- Constipation (already present)
- Psychosocial stress (bullying, family
stressors)
Focused History:
- Clarify onset: was previously dry → now wetting again = secondary
enuresis.
- Frequency: 1–2x/week, 3 months.
- Night vs day: only at night, no daytime symptoms.
- Associated symptoms: thirst, frequency, weight change → not
present.
- Constipation still an issue: hard stools, straining, poor diet (junk
food/fizzy drinks).
- Social: happy, no bullying, sleeps in bunk bed near bathroom.
- Family history: Dad with T1DM.
Good Phrases:
- “You’ve done the right thing calling today — I can hear how much
you’re looking out for Adam.”
- “Bedwetting is common at this age, but because Adam was
previously dry, we call this ‘secondary enuresis’ and it’s important
we check for possible causes.”
- “One big factor may be his constipation — when the bowel is full, it
presses on the bladder and makes night-time accidents more likely.”
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “Bedwetting that starts again after a child has been dry for months
is quite common and usually has an underlying reason.”
- “Sometimes it’s due to things like constipation, urine infections, or
rarely diabetes.”
- “It can also happen when a child is under stress, like changes at
home or school.”
- “It’s not the child’s fault — they’re not being lazy or naughty. Their
body or mind is struggling with something.”
- “Stress or big life changes can sometimes cause this, and children
may not always talk about what’s worrying them.”
- “We’ll make a clear plan — starting with simple steps like fluid
management and reward charts, and if needed, we can look at
alarms or medicines.”
Immediate Management:
- Offer F2F review: check abdomen for constipation/impaction + general
wellbeing.
- Investigations: urine dipstick (infection, glucose, ketones), optional
blood sugar check for reassurance.
- Constipation plan: optimise Movicol dose until stools soft daily,
improve fibre + fluids, reduce fizzy drinks/junk food.
- Behavioural support: toilet before bed, easy access to toilet, avoid fluid
restriction, avoid caffeine and fizzy drinks, reward system about positive
behaviour not dry nights (stickers, praise for effort), toileting pattern
(regular time to empty bladder day and before sleep), advice about potty
training, waterproof mattress.
- Diary: 2-week record of fluid intake, stool pattern, and wetting episodes.
- Escalation options if persists: enuresis alarm which gives alarm when
wet to help the child to learn to wake up before accidents happen (>7yrs,
frequent enough), take time to work from weeks to months. It teaching
them to recognize the feeling of a full bladder and wake up to use the
toilet.
- Desmopressin if older/no response/ short term as in trips. Begin 1-2
weeks before the trip to assess response. Avoid water 1 hour before and
for 8 hours before taking it, so take at night. Avoid taking NSAIDs with it,
stop when vomiting and watch for side effects such as dizziness,
headaches and confusion. Caution with asthmatic child. Review after 3
months with 1 week trial of stopping.
- Signpost to ERIC charity for advice and alarm
- Reassurance: common, not Adam’s fault, most cases resolve.
- Follow-up: review in 2–3 weeks.
Good Phrases:
- “I understand your concern about diabetes — we can do a simple urine
and possibly blood test to give you peace of mind.”
- “Bedwetting is not Adam’s fault, and it often improves once constipation
is well managed.”
- “We’ll take this step by step — starting with the simple checks and
supporting Adam with positive routines.”
- “If things don’t improve, we can move to the next stage such as alarms or
specialist input.”

bad despite inhalers.

lets her play football without being
limited.

- Severe attacks, hospital/ICU
admissions
- Frequent night waking with
wheeze/cough
- Daily uncontrolled symptoms
- Chest pain, palpitations
Focused History:
- Symptoms: Wheeze + SOB with football → uses blue inhaler during
game, relief takes 30 mins. Occasional nocturnal cough. No daytime
symptoms otherwise.
- Impact: Interfering with football performance, affecting confidence
& opportunities.
- Reliever use: Regular use during matches.
- Adherence: Takes brown inhaler as prescribed.
- Triggers: Only exercise. No smoke, no pets/mould.
- Past severity: No admissions/ICS courses.
- PMH: No eczema/hay fever. No NSAID use.
- Vaccines: Not sure about flu/COVID.
Good Phrases:
- “Thanks for explaining so clearly — I can hear how much this is
affecting your football.”
- “What you’re describing fits with what we call exercise-induced
asthma, which is when exercise triggers narrowing of the airways.”
- “The fact you’re relying on your blue inhaler during games and it
takes time to work means your asthma isn’t fully under control right
now.”
Immediate Management:
- Offer F2F review today: listen to chest, check inhaler technique, vitals,
peak flow.
- Treatment switch: Offer MART regimen (e.g. Symbicort 100/6 or Fostair
100/6):
• 1 puff BD (maintenance)
• 1 puff PRN for symptoms (max 8/day)
• 1 puff pre-exercise if needed
→ replaces both current inhalers.
- Spacer: if pMDI (Fostair), prescribe with spacer; not needed if DPI.
- Asthma Action Plan: personalised, update to MART regimen, written
guidance on flare-ups.
- Option 2 is to use SABA before exercise and add Montelukast at night
and gradually came off it to check if controlled
- Lifestyle: carry inhaler at all times, inform coaches/teachers how to
help.
- Vaccinations: advise flu + COVID boosters.
- Follow-up: 4–8 weeks to review response.
- Safety net: if worsening breathlessness despite inhaler, severe attack,
or cannot complete sentences → seek urgent care/999.
Good Phrases:
- “The good news is we now have a simpler, updated approach to asthma
— one inhaler instead of two.”
- “You’ll be able to use this inhaler before football to stop symptoms
coming on.”
- “We’ll support you with a written plan so you know exactly what to do if
your asthma flares.”
- “Our aim is to get you back to playing football at your best, without
asthma holding you back.”

drinker, retired train driver.

something serious.
- Concerns: Very worried it could be

- Painless haematuria (malignancy)
- LUTS (frequency, urgency, nocturia,
weak stream, hesitancy, dribbling)
- Systemic symptoms (weight loss,
fever, night sweats, appetite loss)
- Flank pain (stones)
- Anaemia symptoms (dizziness,
palpitations, SOB, chest pain)
- Bruising/bleeding elsewhere
(coagulopathy)
- FH of urological malignancy

appetite.

- Medications (NSAIDs, anticoagulants).
- FH: bladder, kidney, prostate cancer.
- SH: occupation exposures (dyes, rubber, chemicals).
- Bleeding tendencies elsewhere.
- Sexual history (low risk here).

- “I can hear how unsettling it must have been to see blood in your

- “The reassuring thing is you don’t have pain, fever, or signs of
infection, but we still take this very seriously.”
- “We sometimes see blood due to infection or stones, but in your
case those seem unlikely.”
- “In people over 45, even one episode of visible blood can be a sign
of something serious like bladder or kidney cancer, so we always
investigate thoroughly.”
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “The most likely explanation isn’t clear yet — it could be something
minor, but we must rule out important causes like bladder or kidney
cancer. That’s why I need to arrange urgent specialist tests.”
Immediate GP Management:
- Arrange face-to-face review today if possible (abdo + flank exam, DRE
for prostate).
- Repeat urine dipstick and send MSU for culture.
- Arrange blood tests: PSA, U+Es, FBC, LFTs, CRP, clotting, bone profile.
- Urgent 2WW referral to urology (criteria met: >45 + unexplained visible
haematuria).
Travel discussion:
- Ideally delay travel to allow investigations.
- If cannot delay, still send 2WW referral and inform urology of travel
dates.
- Advise comprehensive travel insurance.
Safety-netting:
- Seek urgent help if blood returns, pain, fever, or feeling unwell.
- Arrange GP follow-up after return for results and planning.
Good Phrases:
- “I’d like to see you in person today to do some important checks,
including your tummy and prostate.”
- “Because you’ve had visible blood in your urine and you’re over 45,
national guidelines recommend urgent referral to the urology team.”
- “I’m not saying this is cancer, but we can’t ignore it — early detection
makes a big difference.”
- “Ideally, it would be best if you could delay your holiday until after these
urgent checks — but if that’s not possible, I’ll still send the referral and
notify the hospital.”
- “While you’re abroad, if blood returns or you feel unwell, please get
checked straightaway and let us know as soon as you’re back.”

39- Recurrent fractures = Relative
Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S) =
Female athlete triad
Definition: A syndrome in athletes
due to low energy availability,
affecting menstrual function, bone
health, and metabolism. RED-S is
broader than the female athlete triad
and includes endocrine,
cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, and
psychological effects.
When to Suspect:
- Female athlete with missed periods
(oligomenorrhoea/amenorrhoea).
- Fatigue, poor performance, stress
fractures.
- Low BMI or rapid weight loss.
Name: Emily Johnson, 21F
Background: Competitive gymnast,
multiple fractures in the past year
(elbow, humerus, wrist requiring
ORIF). BMI 16.5.
Reason: Wants to discuss ortho
letter recommending GP review for
recurrent fractures.

bones/underlying condition.
- Concerns: Will it stop her competing
at Olympic level?
- Expectations: Wants tests, advice to
prevent more fractures.
Red Flags:
- Multiple low-impact fractures
- Amenorrhoea (6–7 months, only if
asked) - Restrictive diet (low carbs,
BMI <18.5)
- High exercise load (3–4h/day)
Focused History:
- Mechanism of each fracture: low-impact vs high-impact.
- Menstrual history → amenorrhoea 6–7 months.
- Diet: protein heavy, avoids carbs, calcium/vit D intake.
- Exercise routine: 3–4h/day, intensity, recovery time.
- Eating behaviours: screen gently for disordered eating (SCOFF).
- Family history of osteoporosis/fragility fractures.
- Social: smoking, alcohol (none).
- Symptoms: fatigue, weight loss, stress, laxative use (denies).
The classic Female Athlete Triad includes:
1. Low energy availability (with or without disordered eating).
2. Menstrual dysfunction (oligomenorrhoea or amenorrhoea).
3. Low bone mineral density (osteopenia or osteoporosis).
Good Phrases:
- “It’s impressive how committed you are to your sport, but I can see
how this has been a difficult year for you with so many injuries.”
- “When the body isn’t getting enough energy, it can start affecting
periods, bones, and overall health.”
- “You’ve done the right thing coming today — this isn’t about
stopping your sport, but supporting you to train safely and avoid
more fractures.”
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “What you’re describing fits with a condition called Relative Energy
Deficiency in Sport, or RED-S. - “Your body may not be getting
enough calories to match the energy you use in training.”
- “This can affect your periods, bones, heart, hormones, and
mood, not just your athletic performance.”
- “Missing periods or having stress fractures is a sign your body is
under too much stress and needs more energy and rest.”
- “If left untreated, it can lead to permanent bone loss, fertility
problems, and long-term health consequences.”
Immediate steps:
- Order blood tests: FBC, U&Es, LFTs, TFTs, HbA1c, calcium & bone
profile, vitamin D, hormone profile (FSH, LH, oestradiol, prolactin).
- Arrange DEXA scan urgently (BMI <18.5 + multiple fractures).
Referrals:
- Dietitian → optimise nutrition, balanced energy intake.
- Endocrinology → if abnormal hormones/amenorrhoea persists.
- Rheumatology → if DEXA shows osteoporosis.
- Sports physio → adapt training plan to reduce injury risk.
- Signpost to Beat Eating disorder
https://www.beateatingdisorders.org.uk/
- Psychological support: CBT or specialist counselling if eating disorder
or body image concerns.
- Specialist eating disorder service if disordered eating is present.
- Sports endocrinology or bone health clinic if prolonged amenorrhoea,
stress fractures, or low bone density.
- Urgent referral if syncope, severe bradycardia, fractures, or severe
malnutrition.
Lifestyle/Advice:
- Encourage adequate calcium + vitamin D intake (diet + supplements if
deficient).
- Modify training → reduce intensity until bones recover.
- Avoid OCP just to ‘restart periods’ (may mask RED-S, not improve bone
health).
Good Phrases:
- “We’ll do some blood tests and a bone scan to understand what’s
happening.”
- “I’ll link you with a dietitian and physiotherapist so you can keep training
but also protect your body.”
- “Periods stopping is your body’s way of saying it’s under stress — we
need to help it get back into balance.”
- “Our aim is to keep you healthy and strong so you can return to sport
safely and perform at your best.”
Safety net:
- “If you have another fracture, new pain, or worsening fatigue, please get
in touch straight away.”
- Book follow-up after bloods + DEXA to review.

delay.

- Midwife will do bloods, urine, BP, weight, arrange scan.
- Vaccinations in pregnancy: flu, COVID any time; pertussis from 16
weeks; RSV from 28 weeks.
Safety net:

please seek urgent help.”
- Arrange GP follow-up in 2 weeks to ensure she’s linked into antenatal +
neuro services.
Good Phrases:
- “Stopping medication suddenly could trigger seizures, which may be
more harmful than the medication.”
- “We’ll get you specialist support quickly — you won’t be going through

41- Severe COPD
Name: Steve Hutchinson, 68M
Background: PMH: COPD,
osteoarthritis, chronic back pain,
hypertension, raised cholesterol.
Medications: Trimbow NEXThaler,
Co-codamol PRN, Amlodipine,
Ramipril, Atorvastatin. Allergic to
Azithromycin (GI).
Reason: Increasing breathlessness,
bedbound, wants to manage at home
without further hospital visits.
ICE:
- Idea: COPD is worsening; not sure
what can be done.
- Concern: Feeling like a burden to
wife, limited mobility.
- Expectation: Wants symptom relief,
clear options at home.
Red Flags to consider/exclude:
- Breathlessness at rest or minimal
exertion
- New/worsening cough, haemoptysis
- Fever, weight loss, night sweats →
infection or malignancy
- Fatigue, confusion, depression
- Dependence at home, carer strain
- Poor inhaler technique or adherence
Focused History:
- Breathlessness: how far can he walk (<100m), at rest, wheeze?
- Orthopnoea/PND: none reported → rule out heart failure.
- Cough: dry vs productive, sputum colour, haemoptysis, infection
symptoms.
- Fever, chills, night sweats, weight loss → not present.
- Daily function: washing, dressing, toileting, mobility.
- Inhaler use: adherence and technique.
- Exacerbation history: hospital admissions, antibiotics/steroids.
- Home support: wife coping, carers, social support.
- Mood: low, anxious, overwhelmed.
- Vaccinations: flu, pneumococcal, COVID.
- Advance care planning: ceilings of care, DNACPR.
Good Phrases:
- “I hear how difficult it’s been for you and your wife, and I’m here to
support you.”
- “Your symptoms suggest your COPD is more advanced, which we
sometimes call end-stage. This means our focus now is on comfort
and quality of life.”
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “Steve, your COPD is at a stage where we focus on keeping you
comfortable and managing breathlessness, rather than trying more
hospital-based treatments.”
mmediate Management:
1. Symptom relief & comfort:
- Non-pharmacological: cool room, fan, open window, positioning.
- Physiotherapy (home-based): breathing techniques, gentle activity,
standing briefly once an hour.
- Pharmacological: switch co-codamol → low-dose oral morphine (titrate
carefully) for breathlessness + pain; co-prescribe laxatives, consider
antiemetic.
- Benzodiazepines if anxiety contributes: lorazepam (short-term),
diazepam (ongoing), midazolam if severe/distress.
- Nebuliser trial if inhalers difficult to use.
- Oxygen only if hypoxaemia criteria met (SpO₂ ≤92%).
2. Cough management:
- Dry: humidifier, Simple Linctus, weak opioids → escalate to morphine if
needed.
- Moist: nebulised saline, carbocisteine 750 mg TDS (review 4w),
antibiotics if infection.
- Secretions: glycopyrronium/hyoscine cautiously; morphine if terminal &
distressing.
3. Home & social support:
- Home visit for Advance Care Planning: ceiling of care, DNACPR,
patient/family wishes.
- Frailty/OT assessment: home support, carers, equipment.
- SR1 form → financial support; involve social prescriber.
4. Medication review:
- Clinical pharmacist: deprescribe unnecessary medications, simplify
regimen.
5. Follow-up & safety net:
- Telephone review in 2 weeks.
- Advise urgent contact if worsening breathlessness, infection, or new
problems.
Good SCA Phrases:
- “We can focus on what matters most to you day-to-day, keeping you
comfortable at home.”
- “Small, gentle movements can actually help you breathe better and
maintain some strength.”
- “We’ll make sure your medications are as simple as possible so you
don’t feel overburdened.”
- “We’ll also look at extra support at home to help both you and your wife.”

42- High Blood pressure in A&E
Name: Andrew Coleman, 50M
Background: PMH: nil. Medications:
nil. Allergies: nil.
Reason: Found to have high BP at
A&E (160/100 & 162/98), wants
antihypertensive started.
ICE:
- Idea: Believes he has high blood
pressure.
- Concern: Family history—
grandmother had hypertension and
stroke.
- Expectation: Wants to start
antihypertensives immediately.
Red Flags to consider/exclude:
- Headache, visual changes
(hypertensive retinopathy)
- Chest pain, palpitations, SOB, ankle
swelling (heart involvement)
- Blood in urine (renal involvement)
- Neurological deficits (stroke
symptoms)
- BP ≥180/120 mmHg with end-organ
signs → emergency referral
Focused History:
- Symptoms of target organ damage: headache, blurred vision, chest
pain, SOB, palpitations, ankle oedema, haematuria, neurological
deficits → not present.
- Lifestyle: diet (junk food), physical activity, alcohol (none), smoking
(none), recreational drugs (none).
- Family history: grandmother hypertension + stroke.
- Injury: right wrist cut, healing, hand clinic follow-up in 2 days.
- Work: tree surgeon, physically active.
Good Phrases:
- “It’s understandable to be concerned about your blood pressure,
especially given your family history.”
- “Many people feel fine even when blood pressure is elevated, which
is why we confirm it before starting medication.”
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “Based on these readings, you may have high blood pressure, but
we need to check it over time to be sure before starting treatment.
This is sometimes called ‘probable essential hypertension.’”
Immediate Management:
1. Confirm diagnosis:
- Offer ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) or home BP
monitoring (HBPM) over 4–7 days.
- Arrange face-to-face review: repeat BP, cardiovascular exam,
fundoscopy (or high-street optician), ECG, routine bloods (U&Es, eGFR,
cholesterol, HbA1c), urine ACR.
2. Cardiovascular risk:
- Calculate QRISK2 score to guide treatment threshold.
3. Lifestyle advice:
- Reduce salt/caffeine, improve diet, increase physical activity.
4. Pharmacological:
- If hypertension confirmed, start Ramipril 1.25–2.5 mg; discuss side
effects (cough, dizziness).
- Targets: clinic <140/90 mmHg, home <135/85 mmHg.
5. Follow-up:
- Review ABPM/HBPM results in 4–7 days, check blood results.
6. Safety net:
- Seek urgent help if sudden weakness, stroke symptoms, chest pain,
SOB, blurred vision.
Good SCA Phrases:
- “We’ll confirm your blood pressure readings before starting medication
so we’re treating you appropriately.”
- “Lifestyle changes can make a real difference, and medications are only
started when we’re sure it’s needed.”
- “We’ll check your heart, kidneys, and eyes to make sure nothing has
been affected.”
- “If anything sudden or worrying happens, contact us straight away — it’s
better to be safe.”

initiated and child is supported.

future stroke, especially after friend

(weakness, numbness, speech
difficulty)
- Facial droop, vision changes,

- Chest pain, palpitations, shortness
of breath (possible AF)
- Blood pressure uncontrolled or
medication non-adherence
- Previous stroke/TIA
- Risk factors for vascular disease:

smoking, family history

for anxiety.

- “I understand why this episode was concerning; it’s important we
take these symptoms seriously even though they resolved.”
- “What you’ve described sounds like a Transient Ischaemic Attack,
or TIA — sometimes called a mini-stroke.”
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “You had a brief episode where part of your brain didn’t get enough
blood flow. Thankfully, it has resolved, but it is a warning that a full
stroke could happen if we don’t act quickly.”
Immediate Management:
1. Medication:
- Start aspirin 300 mg immediately, continue daily until specialist review
(don’t GIVE IF ALREADY ON ASPIRIN OR CONTRAINDICATION) + PPI
- FTF appointment to check pulse and neurological exam
2. Urgent referral:
- Refer to TIA clinic for assessment within 24 hours of symptom onset. (If
suspected TIA > 1 week, then referral within 7 days)
- Likely investigations: CT head, carotid Doppler (‘jelly scan’), ECG, blood
tests.
3. Driving advice:
- Advise patient not to drive until assessed by specialist.
- Explain Group 1 licence DVLA guidance: at least 4 weeks off driving post-
TIA (usually 1 year for Group 2 DVLA)
- Recommend contacting local licensing authority regarding return to taxi
work.
4. Safety net:
- Call 999 immediately if symptoms recur: weakness, speech difficulty,
vision loss.
5. Social support:
- Offer referral to social prescriber to discuss financial support during
driving restriction.
- Signpost to Stoke Associating UK
6. Follow-up:
- Review after TIA clinic assessment to discuss results and ongoing
prevention (managing RFs)
Good SCA Phrases:
- “Even though you feel fine now, a TIA is a serious warning — early action
reduces your risk of a full stroke.”
- “We’ll ensure you’re seen urgently, get the right investigations, and put a
plan in place to prevent this happening again.”
- “I understand this affects your work and family — we’ll provide support
and guidance while you’re unable to drive.”
- “For your safety, you must stop driving until the specialist team confirms
it’s safe to restart.”

- “Anaemia means your blood has fewer red cells, so you may feel
tired, dizzy, or short of breath.”
- “Vitamin B12 and folate are needed to make healthy red blood
cells.”
- “If low, it can also affect your nerves, causing tingling, numbness,

- “We can treat this safely once we know the cause.”

- If negative, consider gastroenterology referral

2. Treatment:
- Start B12 (Hydroxocobalamin) IM 1 mg three times a week for 2 weeks.
- Add folate 24–48 hrs after B12 course starts.
3. Monitoring:
- Repeat FBC & reticulocyte count 7–10 days after starting B12.
- Repeat after 8 weeks to monitor ongoing improvement.
4. Lifestyle:
- Continue B12 and folate rich diet.
5. Workplace support:
- Offer fit note or amended duties if fatigue affects work.
6. Safety net:
- Seek urgent advice if new neurological symptoms develop: tingling,
numbness, balance problems, memory changes.
Good SCA Phrases:
- “Your B12 is low despite tablets, which suggests your body may not be
absorbing it properly — this is why injections are more effective.”
- “We’ll check for pernicious anaemia and other causes to guide whether
treatment needs to continue long term.”
- “Starting B12 now should help improve your tiredness and prevent nerve
complications.”

46- TAAT + bloods showing suspected
Addison’s disease
Name: Laura Green, 26F
Background: PMH: nil. Medications:
none. Allergies: none.
Reason: Fatigue, malaise, weight
loss over 2 weeks. Blood tests: Na
130 mmol/L (low), K 5.8 mmol/L
(high), BP 90/59, pulse 99 bpm,
weight down from 63 to 57 kg. Other
labs normal.
ICE:
- Idea: Suspects low iron causing
tiredness.
- Concern: Fatigue and weakness
affecting work.
- Expectation: Wants explanation for
test results.
When to Suspect:
- Gradual onset: fatigue, weakness,
weight loss, nausea, dizziness.
- Hyperpigmentation, especially in
sun-exposed areas, scars, palmar
creases.
- Postural hypotension, salt craving,
hyponatremia, hyperkalemia.
Red Flags to consider/exclude:
- Hypotension, hyperkalaemia,
hyponatraemia (risk of adrenal crisis)
- Severe fatigue, dizziness, syncope
- Unexplained weight loss, malaise
- Skin hyperpigmentation (Addison’s
disease)
- Cardiac symptoms: palpitations,
chest pain (from electrolyte
disturbances)
Focused History:
- Fatigue: 2 weeks, associated with muscle weakness and
dizziness/light-headedness.
- Appetite/weight: reduced, unintentional weight loss (6 kg).
- Skin: new darkening noted.
- Cardiovascular: no palpitations, chest pain.
- Medications: none.
- Social: police officer, off work, lives with partner, non-smoker, non-
drinker, no recreational drugs.
- Family history: unknown relevant endocrine disorders.
Good Phrases:
- “I can see how tiredness and weight loss are affecting your life —
it’s good you called today.”
- “Your blood tests show low sodium and high potassium, along with
low blood pressure, which can indicate your adrenal glands aren’t
working properly.”
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “Your symptoms and blood tests suggest a condition called
Addison’s disease.
“Addison’s disease happens when your adrenal glands don’t make
enough hormones called cortisol and aldosterone.”
“Cortisol helps your body respond to stress, maintain energy, and
control blood sugar. Without enough cortisol, you can feel tired,
weak, dizzy, or faint, especially when standing up.”
“Aldosterone helps your body keep the right balance of salt and
water, so low levels can cause low blood pressure, salt cravings,
and dehydration.”
“Your skin may darken, particularly in areas exposed to the sun or in
scars — this is a sign your body is trying to compensate.”
“If untreated, it can lead to a life-threatening crisis if you get sick or
injured, but with hormone replacement therapy, most people can
live a normal, active life.”
Immediate Management:
1. Urgent care:
- Same-day admission via AMU or ED due to hypotension and abnormal
electrolytes.
- Ensure patient has someone to accompany her; advise not to drive.
2. In-hospital management:
- IV fluids to correct sodium and potassium.
- Further tests to confirm Addison’s disease.
- Initiate steroid replacement therapy if confirmed.
3. Follow-up:
- Book post-discharge follow-up at GP to review medications, results, and
ongoing management.
4. Safety-net:
- If symptoms worsen en route or at home, call 999 immediately.
- “If you develop severe vomiting, diarrhea, collapse, confusion, or low
blood pressure, go to A&E immediately — this could be an adrenal
crisis.”
- “Increase steroid dose during illness, fever, or surgery as instructed.”
Good SCA Phrases:
- “Because of your blood pressure and salt levels, it’s safest to be seen in
hospital today.”
- “If Addison’s is confirmed, steroid treatment can replace the hormones
your body isn’t making and make you feel much better.”
- “Once you’re discharged, we’ll go over everything here in the practice to
support your ongoing care.”
“We’ll teach you how to adjust your medication during illness (‘sick-day
rules’) and how to recognise warning signs early.”

- Bone pain: legs.
- Hydration: increased thirst, polyuria.
- Social: lives with wife (struggling to cope), retired bus driver, non-
smoker, non-drinker.
- Patient preferences: deferred to healthcare professionals; wife
aware.
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “Pete’s confusion, dehydration, and constipation are likely due to
high calcium from his prostate cancer spreading to the bones.”
Good Phrases:
- “Given his symptoms and the lack of an advance care plan,
hospital admission is appropriate to stabilise him and improve
comfort.”
- “We want to ensure he’s safe and comfortable while we treat his
symptoms.”
Immediate Management:
1. Hospital Admission:
- Admit due to symptomatic hypercalcaemia, confusion, and dehydration.
- Aim: comfort, symptom control, stabilisation.
2. In-hospital treatment:
- IV fluids to correct dehydration and hypercalcaemia.
- Possible bisphosphonates to treat high calcium.
- Liaise with acute medical/oncology team prior to arrival.
3. Pre-hospital care:
- Ambulance crew to consider IV fluids if within scope.
4. Post-discharge / community care:
- Home visit to discuss advance care plan with patient and wife.
- Involve community palliative care / Macmillan nurses.
- Occupational therapy assessment for home support.
- Social prescriber involvement and completion of SR1 form for benefits
and support.
Good SCA Phrases:
- “The goal here is comfort and dignity, not cure.”
- “Once stabilised, we’ll review his ongoing care needs and support his
wife in managing at home.”
- “Thank you for your assessment and care — you’re doing an excellent
job. Please keep us updated.”

48- Primary Axillary Hyperhidrosis
Name: Anita Bells, 26F
Background: No past medical
history, not on medications.
Presentation: Excessive sweating
under both armpits for ~2 years, daily,
stops at night.
Triggers: Suspects caffeine (3–5
cups/day).
Social: Fashion model, lives with
partner, non-smoker, non-drinker, no
recreational drugs.
ICE:
- Idea: Might be linked to caffeine.
- Concern: Sweating causes visible
patches affecting confidence and
work.
- Expectation: Wants to know about
iontophoresis and whether she can
be referred.
Red Flags:
- Night sweats (absent)
- Weight loss (absent)
- Lumps, fever, systemic symptoms
- Mood or anxiety issues (absent)
Focused History:
- Duration: 2 years
- Location: armpits only (bilateral)
- Frequency: most days, stops at night
- Triggers: caffeine suspected, stress, hot food, exercise, alcohol (all
negative except caffeine)
- Family history: none
- Psychological impact: affects confidence, no depression/anxiety
- Occupation: model, affected by visible patches
Diagnosis (easy words):
- - “Primary axillary hyperhidrosis is when your underarm sweat
glands are overactive because the nerves controlling them send
too many signals.”
- “This overactivity happens even when your body doesn’t need to
cool down, which is why you sweat more than others.”
- “It’s usually not caused by another illness, which is why it is
called ‘primary’.”
- “Sweating can interfere with daily life or cause embarrassment,
but it’s not dangerous.”
- “We have treatments that can reduce the sweating by calming
these overactive nerves or blocking signals to the sweat glands.”
Good SCA Phrases:
- “It’s common in young adults, not harmful, but can affect
confidence.”
- “Stopping caffeine may help reduce symptoms.”
Management:
1. Investigations (to rule out secondary causes):
- FBC, TFTs, U&E, LFTs, ESR/CRP, HbA1c
2. Lifestyle advice:
- Avoid triggers (caffeine, heat, stress)
- Loose, breathable clothing; prefer white or black
- Use underarm pads to protect clothes
3. First-line treatment:
- Topical 20% aluminium chloride hexahydrate
- Apply at night on clean, dry skin, wash in morning, every 1–2 days until
improved, then as required
- Avoid shaving the area and using hair removal products within 12 hours
of application, and do not bathe immediately before use.
- If skin irritation – try to use topical emollients or hydrocortisone or
decrease frequency
4. Specialist options if refractory:
- Iontophoresis: weak electrical current through water, blocks nerve
signals to sweat glands, often home-use devices, requires ongoing
maintenance
- Botulinum toxin injections
- Topical glycopyrrolate/ oral antimuscarinics such as oxybutynin
- Propantheline bromide is the only licensed medication for generalised
hyperhidrosis.
5. Follow-up:
- Monitor response to first-line measures after 6 weeks, adjust plan,
provide ongoing support
Good SCA Phrases:
- “This is primarily about improving confidence and quality of life.”
- “We start conservatively before considering specialist treatments like
iontophoresis or botulinum toxin.”

- Headaches: none

- “Based on what you’ve described, he may be showing early signs of
cognitive decline.”
- “Dementia is when the brain’s ability to remember, think, and
make decisions gradually declines.”
- “It happens because brain cells are damaged or lost, depending
on the type of dementia (e.g., plaques in Alzheimer’s, blood vessel
damage in vascular dementia).”
- “This causes memory problems, confusion, difficulty with daily
tasks, and sometimes changes in behaviour or personality.”
- “It’s usually progressive, but early diagnosis allows us to manage
symptoms and plan support.”
- “The accidental double dose is not usually harmful in small

- Home visit to assess cognition with 6 CIT

- Refer to memory clinic for formal assessment

- Resume regular medications next day
- Carers to monitor for concerning symptoms (bleeding, bruising, muscle
pain, dizziness, confusion)
- Use TOXBASE/BNF if unsure about specific medication overdose
3. Safety Measures:
- Carer-supervised administration of meds
- Provide written instructions to carers/next of kin
- Consider locking away medications with patient consent
- Liaise with pharmacy for early replacement supply
4. Support and Follow-up:
- Falls alarm for patient safety
- Referral to frailty or OT team to reassess care needs
- Inform son (next of kin) with patient consent
Good SCA Phrases:
- “We’ll monitor him closely and ensure he takes the correct doses.”
- “This is primarily about supporting his safety at home and assessing
cognition.”
Validation therapy: It is a gentle communication approach used for
patients with dementia, including those with visual hallucinations. Instead
of correcting or arguing about their experience, you acknowledge their
feelings and respond with empathy. For example, if a patient sees
something that isn't there, you might say, "That must feel strange. I'm here
with you, and you're safe."

50- Adolescent Migraine on COCP
Name: Amanda Walsh, 16F
Background: Migraine headaches;
recently started Microgynon 3 months
ago
Presentation: Right-sided, throbbing
headaches lasting 2–3 days, once
weekly, worsened by light, affecting
concentration. Started ~2 weeks after
COCP initiation. No aura, no neuro
symptoms.
Social: Sexually active, monogamous
relationship, no
smoking/alcohol/drugs, studying for
A-levels
Idea: Stress may be a trigger
Concern: Headaches increasing in
frequency, affecting daily function
Expectation: Wants GP to advise on
cause and management
Red Flags:
- Aura (none)
- Neurological deficits (none)
- Headache waking from sleep (none)
- Fever, neck stiffness, visual loss
(none)
Focused History:
- Location: right-sided
- Onset/duration: 2–3 days, weekly, 2 months
- Character: throbbing, worsened by light
- Triggers: stress, possibly COCP
- Medications: paracetamol/ibuprofen, sumatriptan previously
helpful
- Red flags: none (no aura, neuro deficit, visual change, vomiting,
fever)
- Impact: concentration affected, schoolwork affected
- Ask about relation to the menstrual cycle
Diagnosis (easy words):
- “A migraine is more than a headache — it’s a throbbing or pounding
pain, often on one side of your head, and can make you feel sick or
sensitive to light and sound.”
- “Migraines happen because the nerves in your head that usually
sense pain become overly sensitive. This means that things that
normally wouldn’t hurt — like light touch, bright lights, or sounds —
can feel painful.”
- “Some people also have warning signs called aura, like flashing
lights, zig-zag lines, tingling in your hands or face, or temporary
speech problems before the headache starts.”
- “Migraines can last a few hours to a couple of days and may be
triggered by stress, lack of sleep, certain foods, or your period.”
- “They are common in teenagers, especially girls, and are usually
not dangerous, but it’s important to manage them carefully,
especially if you are taking the combined pill.”
Good SCA Phrases:
- “It sounds like your headaches have been tough, especially with A-
levels.”
- “These are typical migraines, and the combined pill may be
contributing.”
Management:
1. Examination & Monitoring:
- Offer face-to-face appointment for full neurological exam & fundoscopy
- Keep headache diary for 8 weeks (frequency, duration, triggers, meds)
2. Medication & Treatment:
- Stop COCP temporarily; discuss alternative contraception (use condoms
in interim)
- Prescribe nasal sumatriptan for acute attacks; can combine with NSAIDs
if required – start when headache start not when aura start. Take as soon
as migraine starts, ideally within first hour. Can repeat after 2 hours if
headache returns (max 40mg per day). If the first dose of sumatriptan
does not help, do not take another dose for the same attack.
1. Blow your nose if it feels blocked or if you have a cold.
2. Place your finger over 1 nostril.
3. Spray the medicine into the other nostril and breathe in gently at
the same time. Each spray contains 1 dose
- Consider antiemetics
3. Lifestyle & Self-care:
- Stress management, screen time reduction, hydration, sleep, light
exercise
- Signpost to The Migraine Trust
4. Follow-up & Safety Netting:
- Review in 2 weeks with diary
- Advise urgent review if headache changes in character, worsens, or
develops neurological symptoms
5- Preventive treatment
- If children – refer to paediatrics
- If menstrual related: give Frovatripan or Zolmitriptan on days migraine is
expected or 2 days before to 3 days after bleeding starts
6. SCA Phrases:
- “We’ll track your headaches carefully and adjust treatment if needed.”
- “Stopping the pill is temporary; we’ll discuss safe alternatives to keep
you protected.”
- “Most migraines are not dangerous, but we want to manage them safely
and reduce their impact on your daily life.”
- “If you notice sudden changes in your headache or new neurological
symptoms, seek urgent help.”
- “Lifestyle measures and medication can help prevent attacks and make
them easier to manage.”

to shrink prostate

- Cauda equina syndrome

- Past medical history: urinary tract infections, prostatitis, BPH,

- Family history: prostate cancer, BRCA mutations.
- Medications: finasteride/dutasteride (affect PSA).
- PSA pre-test precautions: avoid ejaculation, vigorous exercise

- “Your PSA test is slightly/highly raised. PSA is a protein made by the
prostate, and raised levels can be due to several reasons, including
prostate enlargement, inflammation, or rarely cancer.”
- “A raised PSA does not automatically mean cancer, but we need to
investigate it properly to be safe.”
- “We usually repeat the test and consider imaging or referral to a
specialist if levels remain high.”
- False positives: “Sometimes PSA can be raised by infections,
recent catheter use, vigorous exercise, or procedures — so a single
high reading doesn’t automatically mean cancer.”
- False negatives: “Occasionally, PSA can be normal even if cancer
is present, especially in early stages, so we always consider your
age, symptoms, and family history.”
Good SCA Phrases:
- “I can see these waterworks symptoms are frustrating.”
- “Your PSA is higher than expected, which may be due to prostate
enlargement or something more serious.”
Management:
1. Urgent Referral:
- Two-week wait (2WW) urology referral for further investigation (MRI,
possible biopsy)
2. Symptom Relief:
- Do not start finasteride yet (can affect PSA results)
- Reassure patient about temporary waiting
3. Safety Netting:
- Advise to contact GP immediately if unable to pass urine, develops pain,
or haematuria
4. Follow-up:
- Review after urology assessment for ongoing symptom management
SCA Phrases:
- “We need to rule out anything serious before starting new medications.”
- “I understand this is uncomfortable; we’ll support you through the
referral process.”
- “Return urgently if you develop blood in urine or semen, new bone
pain, or urinary obstruction.”
- “Even if the PSA is only mildly raised, specialist review is important to
rule out cancer.”

52- Acute chest pain = suspected ACS
Presentation: Chest pain for past 11
hours; dull ache, central, lasted ~30
min, occurred at rest and with
minimal exertion (lifting files),
associated with shortness of breath
and palpitations. Currently pain-free.
Social: Accountant, sedentary, poor
diet, non-smoker, no alcohol, family
history: uncle MI at 42
Ideas: Thinks it might be muscle
strain or heartburn
Concern: Worried it could be serious,
wife anxious
Expectation: Wants to know if it’s
serious, needs tests/painkillers
When to suspect / Diagnostic
features:
- Central chest pain/pressure, may
radiate to jaw, neck, shoulder, or arm
- Sudden onset, often at rest or
minimal exertion
- Associated symptoms: diaphoresis,
nausea, vomiting, dyspnea,
palpitations
- Risk factors: age >40, male,
smoking, hypertension, diabetes,
dyslipidemia, family history of CAD
- ECG changes: ST elevation, ST
depression, T wave inversion
- Elevated cardiac biomarkers
(troponin I/T)
Focused History:
- Chest pain: onset, character (pressure, tightness, burning),
location, radiation, duration, precipitating/relieving factors.
- Associated symptoms: diaphoresis, nausea, vomiting, dyspnea,
palpitations, syncope.
- Past cardiac history: MI, PCI, CABG, angina.
- Medical history: hypertension, diabetes, CKD, hyperlipidemia.
- Medication history: antiplatelets, anticoagulants, nitrates, beta-
blockers.
- Family history: premature CAD.
- Social history: smoking, alcohol, physical activity.
Examination (SCA style):
- Vital signs: BP, HR, RR, O2 saturation, temperature.
- General: pallor, diaphoresis, distress.
- Cardiovascular: heart sounds (S4, murmurs), JVP, peripheral
pulses, signs of heart failure (edema, crackles).
- Respiratory: lung auscultation.
- Look for other causes of chest pain (PE, aortic dissection signs).
Risk Factors:
- Smoking, hypertension, diabetes, obesity
- Dyslipidemia, family history, previous CAD
Red Flags:
- Severe, crushing chest pain >15 min
- Hemodynamic instability (hypotension, shock)
- Pulmonary edema, arrhythmias, syncope
- Signs of ongoing ischemia on ECG
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Your symptoms suggest that your heart may not be getting enough
blood due to a blocked artery, which can be serious.”
- “We need to act quickly to confirm if this is a heart attack and treat
it to prevent damage to your heart.”
“I will arrange for an ambulance from your workplace.”
- “Please keep someone with you and let them know what’s
happening.”
- “After the hospital, we will review your medications, diet, and
exercise to protect your heart health.”
1. Immediate assessment & safety:
- Assess ABCs.
- Place patient in a comfortable position.
- Oxygen if hypoxic (<94%).
- Continuous monitoring if feasible.
- Aspirin 300 mg orally (unless contraindicated).
- patient must not drive themselves
- Chest pain within 12 hours then emergency. if 12-72 hours + pain free
now = same day admission. If pain > 72 hours + pain free = chest pain
clinic
2. Urgent escalation:
- Call 999 / emergency services immediately if suspected ACS.
- Prepare for ambulance handover: brief history, vitals, ECG if available,
medications.
- Do not delay transfer for routine investigations.
3. Pain relief / symptomatic management while waiting for
ambulance:
- GTN (sublingual) if not hypotensive, consider repeated doses every 5 min
as needed.
- Morphine only if severe pain and no contraindications, per local
protocol.
4. Investigations (if available but do not delay transfer):
- ECG: identify STEMI/NSTEMI patterns.
- Bloods usually deferred to hospital.
5. Safety netting / monitoring:
- Monitor vitals while waiting.
- Be prepared for resuscitation if collapse occurs.
6. SCA phrases:
- “Your chest pain is concerning for a heart attack; we need to call an
ambulance immediately.”
- “I’m giving you aspirin to help prevent your blood clot from getting
worse.”
- “We will monitor you closely until emergency services arrive.”
- “Do not try to drive yourself; we will make sure you get to the hospital
safely.”
- “It’s important to report if your pain worsens, you feel faint, or have
shortness of breath while we wait for help.”

- Regular check-ins with mother

- “If Peter becomes aggressive, suicidal, or in immediate danger, call 999

Condition / Scenario (with Dx Criteria) Focused History & Layperson-Friendly Explanation Management Plan (SCA phrases, Safety Netting & Referral
Criteria)
Wernicke-Korsakoff Syndrome Dx Criteria (classic
triad): - Wernicke’s (acute): confusion, ataxia,
ophthalmoplegia/nystagmus (need ≥2 for diagnosis). -
Korsakoff’s (chronic): memory impairment + confabulation.
- Usually in setting of alcohol misuse/malnutrition.
History: Alcohol use, poor diet, confusion, gait
disturbance, eye movement problems, memory loss,
confabulation. Lay Explanation: “Your brain isn’t
getting enough vitamin B1, which is vital for memory,
balance, and eye movement. Alcohol and poor
nutrition lower vitamin B1, damaging brain cells.”
Immediate: IV thiamine 500 mg TDS for 2–3 days before glucose,
then oral replacement. Supportive: Fluids, electrolytes, nutrition.
Referral: Addiction services, neurology if cognitive deficits persist.
Safety Netting: Urgent if worsening confusion, hallucinations, poor
intake.
Delirium Tremens (DTs) Dx Criteria: - Recent alcohol
cessation/reduction. - Onset 2–5 days after last drink. - Severe
withdrawal: agitation, tremor, hallucinations, confusion,
autonomic hyperactivity (sweating, tachycardia, hypertension,
fever).
History: Stopping alcohol suddenly, tremor, sweating,
hallucinations, agitation, disorientation. Lay
Explanation: “When alcohol is stopped suddenly, the
brain becomes overactive, causing confusion, shaking,
sweating, and hallucinations.”
Immediate: Admit to monitored unit. Pharmacological:
Benzodiazepines (chlordiazepoxide/diazepam). Supportive: Fluids,
electrolytes, nutrition. Referral: Addiction services, psychiatry. Safety
Netting: Emergency if seizures, severe agitation, collapse.
Alcohol-Related Seizures Dx Criteria: - Generalized tonic-
clonic seizure. - Within 6–48 hrs of last alcohol intake. - No
other seizure disorder. - Often with other withdrawal
symptoms.
History: First or recurrent seizure, timing after last
alcohol intake, associated tremor, sweating, agitation.
Lay Explanation: “Stopping alcohol suddenly can
make the nervous system overexcited, which may
trigger seizures.”
Immediate: ABCs, protect patient during seizure. Investigations:
Blood glucose, electrolytes, LFTs, drug screen. Pharmacological:
Benzodiazepines; anti-epileptics if recurrent. Referral: Neurology,
addiction services. Safety Netting: Call 999 if seizure >5 mins or
repeated.
Alcohol-Related Dementia Dx Criteria: - History of chronic
heavy alcohol use. - Progressive cognitive decline (memory,
executive function, daily functioning). - Must exclude other
causes (Alzheimer’s, vascular dementia, B12 deficiency).
History: Long-term alcohol misuse, memory loss,
difficulty with daily tasks, poor insight, social decline.
Lay Explanation: “Over time, alcohol can damage
brain cells, leading to problems with memory, decision-
making, and daily activities. Some problems may
improve with stopping alcohol.”
Assessment: Cognitive tests (MoCA/MMSE), labs (B12, folate, TFTs,
LFTs), imaging if needed. Management: Abstinence, thiamine,
nutrition, occupational therapy, caregiver support. Referral: Memory
clinic, neurology, addiction services. Safety Netting: Urgent review if
sudden confusion, falls, infections, or fast deterioration.

3. Follow-up:

- Gender identity clinic follow-up.
- Safety net: seek urgent help if thoughts of self-harm develop (111, crisis

57- Primary Headache Related to
Sexual Activity (PHSA)
Name: Michael Stevenson, 32M
Background: Migraine history; no
other comorbidities
Presentation: Headaches during
sexual activity over the past 2–3
weeks. Pain described as pressure-
like, sometimes severe pounding at
orgasm. Lasts 30–60 mins, mild
lingering discomfort after. Not
relieved by ibuprofen or paracetamol.
No associated nausea, vomiting,
photophobia, phonophobia, visual
changes.
Social: Lives with girlfriend, works as
financial analyst, non-smoker, non-
drinker.
Ideas/Concerns/Expectations:
Thinks migraine is worsening; wants
naproxen prescription.
Red Flags: Sudden onset, severe
intensity, first-time headache—
requires urgent exclusion of
subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH).
- Thunderclap headache (sudden,
“worst ever”).
- Neurological deficits, loss of
consciousness, seizures.
- New pattern in older adults (>50
years), immunosuppressed, or with
systemic symptoms.
Focused History:
- Onset: 2–3 weeks ago, during sexual activity
- Location: bilateral, pressure-like, or pounding
- Nature: sudden peak at orgasm
- Duration: 30–60 minutes, mild residual
- Associated: no nausea, vomiting, visual symptoms, photophobia,
phonophobia
- Triggers: sexual activity, both masturbation and intercourse
- Previous similar headaches: none
- Day-to-day impact: avoidance of sexual activity, distress
When to Suspect / Diagnostic Pointers:
- Sudden onset, severe, bilateral or occipital.
- Occurs just before or during orgasm (pre-orgasmic), or at orgasm
(orgasmic).
- Headache resolves within minutes to hours.
- No focal neurological deficits.
- Often first occurs in adulthood.
Working Diagnosis: - “Some people get headaches triggered by
sexual activity or orgasm. Usually, these are not dangerous and are
different from a stroke or brain bleed.”
- “They are thought to be due to temporary changes in blood
pressure and blood flow in the brain during sexual excitement.” -
“The headache is usually brief, resolves after the activity, and does
not leave lasting problems.”
SCA Phrases:
- “The headaches you describe are known as sexual headaches.
They are usually harmless but need careful assessment as this is
your first episode.”
- “We must rule out rare but serious causes, such as a subarachnoid
haemorrhage, before starting preventive treatment.”
Management:
1. Urgent Investigation:
- Refer to acute medical unit / ED for urgent CT head ± lumbar puncture to
exclude SAH
2. Treatment (once serious causes excluded):
- Indometacin 25–75 mg 60 minutes before planned sexual activity
- Triptans if indometacin is unsuitable
- Propranolol (40–240 mg daily) for prophylaxis
3. Symptomatic/Lifestyle Advice:
- Avoidance/reduction of intensity during intercourse
- Maintain healthy weight, regular physical activity
4. Follow-up:
- Review after hospital assessment to initiate prophylactic therapy as
appropriate
5. Safety-net:
- If worsening symptoms or new neurological features develop en route to
hospital, call emergency services; avoid self-driving
SCA Phrases:
- “Although most sexual headaches are not dangerous, it’s safest to rule
out anything serious first.”
- “Once we confirm everything is safe, we can discuss preventive
treatments to reduce the frequency and severity.”
- “Simple painkillers can help, and preventive medications are rarely
needed unless headaches are frequent.”
- “You don’t usually need to stop sexual activity, but be aware and take
precautions if headache occurs.”

58- Suspected first seizure/epilepsy
Name: Sarah Collins, 28F
PMH: Asthma
PC: Two episodes of collapse:
1. Witnessed 3 weeks ago at home –
unresponsive for 3–4 min, shaking,
urinary incontinence.
2. Unwitnessed 10 days ago – found
on floor, urinary incontinence, no
memory of event.
Associated Symptoms: No warning
signs (dizziness, palpitations, chest
pain), no confusion or prolonged
drowsiness, no tongue biting, visual
disturbances, or headache.
Social: HCA, drives to work, non-
smoker, non-drinker, stable partner,
active job.
Ideas/Concerns/Expectations:
Uncertain of cause, concerned about
further episodes at work, wants invx.
Dx Criteria (based on NICE):
- ≥2 unprovoked seizures, or one
seizure with high recurrence risk.
- Features suggestive of seizure:
sudden loss of consciousness, tonic–
clonic movements, post-ictal
confusion, tongue biting,
incontinence, amnesia.
- Exclude mimics: syncope, TIA,
hypoglycaemia, non-epileptic
attacks.
- Red flags: prolonged seizure (>5
min), status epilepticus, new
neurological deficit, first seizure in
pregnancy, head trauma, CNS
infection.
History to Clarify:
- Onset, duration, witness accounts
- Pre-collapse symptoms: dizziness, palpitations, chest pain, light-
headedness
- During: position, duration, movements, tongue biting, incontinence
- Post-episode: drowsiness, confusion, amnesia
- Past similar episodes, recent illness, head trauma, new
medications
- Family history of epilepsy
- Occupational risks and driving
- Hobbies and sport
- Pregnancy and contraception
Working Diagnosis: Suspected seizure (possible first epileptic
event)
“Epilepsy means someone has a tendency to have repeated
seizures.”
- “A seizure happens when the brain’s electrical activity suddenly
becomes disorganised, leading to loss of awareness, shaking, or
unusual movements.”
SCA Phrases:
- “Based on your description, it’s possible you’ve had seizures,
which can cause collapse, shaking, and sometimes incontinence.”
- “Because this has happened more than once, we need to
investigate further for epilepsy.”
- “You’ve had what sounds like a seizure, which is when the brain’s
electrical activity becomes overactive for a short time.”
- “There are many possible causes — sometimes it’s epilepsy, but it
can also be due to things like low blood sugar, infection, or fainting.”
- “We need to arrange further tests to confirm the cause and keep
you safe.”
- “Your episode may have been a seizure, but we need to do tests to
confirm the cause.”
- “Because of safety concerns, I must advise you not to drive until
you’ve been reviewed by a specialist.”
Management:
- Do not diagnose epilepsy in primary care.
1. Face-to-face Assessment:
- Full neurological and cardiovascular examination
- ECG and routine bloods
2. Referral:
- First Seizure Clinic (brain scan, EEG)
- Aim: seen within 2 weeks
3. Driving & Safety:
- Stop driving until reviewed and diagnosed; notify DVLA (usually after 6
months for Group 1 and 5 years for group 2 of seizure free without
medication)
- Inform employer and arrange occupational health assessment for work
adjustments
- Avoid triggers (avoid alcohol/ drugs/ dehydration/ poor sleep/
overexertion)
4. Education & Safety:
- Provide leaflets for emergency measures – Seizure diary
- Advise colleagues on how to respond if a seizure occurs (ensure safety,
don’t restrain, don’t put anything in mouth, place in recovery position,
protect the head with cushion)
- Avoid high-risk situations (working at heights, swimming alone, operating
machinery)
- Consider wearing a medical alert bracelet
- Signpost to Epilepsy Action
5. Safety Net:
- If a seizure lasts >5 minutes, call 999 immediately
SCA Phrases:
- “We’ll refer you to a clinic to investigate further and manage safely.”
- “These precautions are to keep you and others safe while we find out
what’s going on.”

- Avoid travelling to Albania until assessed
- Provide safety net: seek urgent medical attention if symptoms worsen,

- “The itching can be very uncomfortable, but treatment helps and serious
complications are uncommon with monitoring.”
- “We monitor your liver function and your baby closely to reduce risks.”
- “Delivery may be recommended a little early if your bile acid levels are

- “Symptoms usually resolve after delivery, but there is a risk of recurrence

61- Palpitations = Suspected
hyperthyroidism
Name: Amanda Bush, 47F
PMH: Generalised Anxiety Disorder
(managed with CBT)
Presentation: 2-month history of
excessive sweating (day and night),
persistent fatigue, unintended weight
loss (~5 kg), intermittent palpitations.
Menstrual & Hormonal: On
combined oral contraceptive pill
(Rigevidon), periods regular.
Social: Teacher, non-smoker, non-
drinker.
Ideas/Concerns/Expectations:
Thinks it might be menopause;
worried about early menopause like
mother; wants blood tests to check.
Red Flags: Unexplained weight loss,
night sweats, palpitations, fatigue,
potential lymphadenopathy.
History to Clarify:
- Onset and progression of sweating, fatigue, and weight loss
- Fever or infectious symptoms (none reported)
- Palpitations, feeling hot, diarrhoea, neck swelling or discomfort
(hyperthyroidism)
- Lumps, joint pains, or swellings (rule out malignancy, particularly
lymphoma)
- Impact on daily functioning and work
- Family history of thyroid disease or early menopause
Working Diagnosis: Likely hyperthyroidism (thyrotoxicosis), - “Your
thyroid gland is producing too much hormone, which speeds up your
body’s metabolism.”
- “This makes you burn energy faster, so you may lose weight even if
you eat normally.”
- “It can also make your heart beat faster, cause trembling, sweating,
anxiety, and digestive changes like diarrhoea.”
- “If you have eye symptoms like bulging or double vision, this may be
related to the thyroid condition.”
- “The good news is that there are effective treatments to bring your
hormone levels back to normal and relieve your symptoms
SCA Phrases:
- “Because you’re on the combined pill, it’s unlikely these symptoms
are due to menopause.”
- “One possibility is that your thyroid is overactive, which can explain
sweating, fatigue, weight loss, and palpitations.”
- “We also need to rule out more serious causes, like lymphoma,
while investigating your symptoms.”
Management:
1. Face-to-Face Assessment:
- Physical examination: pulse, BP, thyroid palpation, check for
lymphadenopathy
- ECG if palpitations to rule out arrhythmia (esp. atrial fibrillation)
2. Investigations:
- TFTs: TSH (suppressed), free T4/T3 (raised).
- Thyroid antibodies (TSH receptor antibodies) if Graves’ suspected.
- Ultrasound or nuclear scan if nodular goitre suspected.
- ECG if palpitations/arrhythmia suspected.
- Consider bone density if prolonged hyperthyroidism.
3. Symptom control:
- Beta-blockers (e.g., propranolol) for palpitations, tremor, anxiety.
4. Definitive therapy:
- Antithyroid drugs: carbimazole or PTU (PTU in first trimester pregnancy).
- Radioactive iodine ablation or surgery if indicated (toxic nodules,
uncontrolled Graves’).
- Monitor for hypothyroidism after definitive treatment.
5. Special considerations:
- Pregnancy: avoid radioactive iodine; use pregnancy-safe medications.
- Graves’ orbitopathy: ophthalmology referral if eye signs present.
6. Safety netting:
- “Seek urgent help if you develop chest pain, severe palpitations,
confusion, fever, vomiting, or extreme agitation – these could be signs of
thyroid storm.”
- “Report any worsening eye symptoms such as pain, double vision, or
bulging.”
SCA phrases:
- “Your thyroid is overactive, which explains your symptoms of weight
loss, palpitations, and tremor.”
- “We can start medication to control your hormone levels and relieve your
symptoms.”
- “Beta-blockers can help with palpitations and tremor while the main
treatment takes effect.”
- “We’ll monitor your blood tests closely and adjust treatment as needed
to prevent complications.”

radiating to anterior abdomen, worse
at night, disrupting sleep
Symptoms: Pain is burning,
occasionally triggered by touch
(allodynia); no new rashes, trauma,
urinary symptoms, fever, nausea,
vomiting, or weight loss
Social: Car mechanic, moderate
physical activity, non-smoker, no
alcohol, lives with wife
Ideas/Concerns/Expectations:
Unsure of cause, feels similar to
shingles pain, wants relief and
explanation
Red Flags / Urgent Referral:
- New rash suggesting recurrence or
secondary infection.
- Severe, uncontrolled pain affecting
daily function or sleep.
- Suspected immunocompromise or
systemic illness.
History to Clarify:
- Onset, duration, nature (burning), location, radiation, triggers,
relieving/aggravating factors
- Presence of rash or blisters
- Urinary symptoms (haematuria, dysuria)
- Constitutional symptoms: fever, weight loss, night sweats
- Trauma history
- Impact on daily life, sleep, and mood
Working Diagnosis: Post-herpetic neuralgia (persistent neuropathic
pain following shingles)
- “After shingles, some people continue to have nerve pain in the
same area even when the rash has healed.”
- “This is called post-herpetic neuralgia and is due to irritation and
damage of the nerves by the virus.”
- “It can be uncomfortable or even severe, but there are treatments
to help manage the pain.”
- “The pain usually improves over time, but sometimes it may persist
for months.”
SCA Phrases:
- “Even though the rash has resolved, the nerve can remain irritated
and send pain signals.”
- “The pain you’re experiencing is real and not imagined.”

- Examine area for nerve-related tenderness, allodynia, hyperalgesia
2. Self-care Advice:
- Soft clothing, protect skin with barrier dressings or cling film if sensitive
- Regular skin care, gentle clothing, apply cold compresses (if helpful) to
reduce allodynia.
- Psychological support for chronic pain (CBT, mindfulness).
- Signpost to Singles Support Society and Pain Concern
3. Neuropathic Pain Relief:
- Amitriptyline (first choice, especially with sleep disturbance)
- Alternatives: gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine
- Topical lidocaine 5% plaster, applied once daily up to 12h
- TENS device to help blocking pain signals
4. Follow-Up:
- 2–4 weeks to assess response and adjust treatment
5. Safety-Netting:
- Seek medical attention if pain worsens, spreads, or significantly affects
daily life
SCA Phrases:
- “Treatment is tailored to the individual, considering both physical
symptoms and quality of life.”
- “Topical capsaicin is currently unavailable, so we focus on available
alternatives like lidocaine plasters.”
- “This nerve pain is common after shingles and usually improves
gradually.”
- “We can use medications and patches to reduce the burning or stabbing
sensations.”
- “Seek urgent care if you notice a new rash, signs of infection, or sudden
worsening pain.”
- “Pain may persist for weeks to months, but with treatment it can be
controlled, and quality of life improved.”

Atorvastatin 20 mg OD
Allergies: NKDA
SH: Lives alone, retired teacher, non-

and housework
FH: No significant cardiac history
Presenting Complaint: 2-week
history of light-headedness/dizziness
on standing
Symptoms Reported:
- Light-headedness when standing up
quickly or prolonged standing
- Brief episodes (~30 seconds)
- No falls, blackouts, palpitations,
chest pain, or SOB
- Previously had palpitations
(resolved)
Red Flags to Explore (must ask):
- Syncope/falls with injury
- Chest pain, palpitations, SOB
- Headache, blurred vision,
neurological deficits
- Recent medication changes
- Hydration and nutrition status
Focused History (what examiner looks for):
- Onset and duration: 2 weeks, brief episodes
- Character: light-headedness (not vertigo)
- Triggers: standing up suddenly, prolonged standing
- Frequency: several times per week, not worsening
- Associated symptoms: no chest pain, palpitations, or SOB
- History of falls or injuries: none
- Medication review: bisoprolol recently started for palpitations
(likely no longer needed), ongoing amlodipine and ramipril
- Hydration: sometimes forgets fluids
- Lifestyle impact: gardening, housework
- Psychosocial: lives alone, supportive network, no alcohol or
smoking
Ideas: Unsure of cause
Concerns: Worries about safety and independence; impact on
housework/gardening
Expectations: Wants explanation and advice to continue normal
activities safely
Diagnosis Explanation (Layperson):
- “Mr Sharma, from what you’ve told me, this sounds like postural
(or orthostatic) hypotension, which means your blood pressure
drops a bit when you stand up. This can make you feel dizzy or light-
headed for a short time.”
- “It’s common in older adults, especially if you’re taking
medications like amlodipine, ramipril, or bisoprolol, which can
contribute to this.”
- “The good news is that your symptoms are brief, you haven’t fainted
or hurt yourself, and there’s no evidence of heart problems.”
- “We can take some simple steps to make you feel safer and
continue your daily activities like gardening.”
1. Assessment:
- Face-to-face review to check lying and standing BP, pulse rate, and
rhythm.
- Review recent blood tests (FBC, U&Es, electrolytes, kidney function).
- ECG if needed.
2. Medication Review:
- Stop bisoprolol (palpitations resolved, no longer required).
- Assess need for adjusting amlodipine or ramipril if contributing to
dizziness (based on BP readings).
3. Lifestyle & Safety Advice:
- Stand up slowly from sitting or kneeling.
- Stay well-hydrated and avoid skipping meals.
- Take breaks during gardening or housework.
- Consider small snack before physical activity.
- Falls prevention: arrange falls alarm if concerned living alone.
4. Follow-Up:
- BP review in 2 weeks after any medication changes.
5. Safety-Netting:
- Seek urgent medical attention if chest pain, palpitations, collapse, or
worsening dizziness occurs.
6. Reassurance / Support:
- Encourage continuation of safe gardening and activities with
precautions.
- Reassure patient: symptoms are common, manageable, and not
indicative of serious heart disease.
Learning Point (Examiner):
- Orthostatic hypotension = fall in SBP ≥20 mmHg or DBP ≥10 mmHg
within 3 minutes of standing.
- Common in older adults, exacerbated by antihypertensives.
- Management = adjust medications, lifestyle measures, hydration, falls
prevention, assess fitness to drive.
- Emphasis on independence and quality of life.

64- Dizziness = Postural Orthostatic
Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS)
Patient Details & Background
Name: Emily Taylor, 15F
SH: Secondary school student, lives
with family, non-smoker, no alcohol
or drugs.
FH: Not relevant
Presenting Complaint: 6-month
history of dizziness, fainting (x6),
palpitations when standing.
Symptoms Reported:
- Episodes of dizziness/
lightheadedness, esp. on standing or
prolonged standing.
- Palpitations (HR up to 125 bpm on
smartwatch).
- Six faints, brief, rapid recovery, no
confusion.
- Worse with stress (exam prep).
- No chest pain, no SOB, no exertional
syncope.
Red Flags to Explore (must ask):
- Exertional syncope (during sports →
risk of cardiac pathology).
- Chest pain, palpitations at rest, or
SOB with fainting.
- Family history of sudden cardiac
death.
- Prolonged confusion post-collapse
(→ seizure).
- Scalp/scarring rash (→ secondary
cause).
- Menstrual history + chance of
pregnancy.
Focused History (what examiner looks for):
- Onset: 6 months, gradual.
- Character: Lightheadedness/dizziness (not spinning vertigo).
- Triggers: Standing up suddenly, prolonged standing.
- Frequency: Occurs regularly, 6 faints.
- Associated symptoms: Palpitations, no chest pain, no SOB.
- t is also associated with comorbidities such as migraine
headaches, irritable bowel syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome,
chronic fatigue syndrome, and autoimmune diseases such as
Hashimoto's thyroiditis and coeliac disease.
- After episodes: Quick recovery, no post-ictal confusion.
- Menstrual Hx: Regular, LMP 1 week ago, no chance of pregnancy.
- Psychosocial: GCSE stress, otherwise well, supportive family.
- Lifestyle: Hydration, diet, exercise, no smoking/alcohol/drugs.
Ideas: Wonders if it’s anxiety (mum’s suggestion).
Concerns: GCSEs coming up, worried it’ll affect performance.
Expectations: Wants explanation + management.
Diagnosis Explanation (Layperson):
- “Emily, from what you’ve described, this sounds like a condition
called Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome, or PoTS for
short.”
- “In PoTS, when you stand up, your heart beats much faster than
normal. This can make you feel dizzy, lightheaded, and sometimes
faint. It happens because your body’s circulation takes a while to
adjust to being upright.”
- “It’s not dangerous in terms of damaging your heart, but it can
interfere with daily life and school, which I know is worrying with your
exams.”
- “It’s different from anxiety or being unfit — it’s a recognised
condition we see in teenagers, especially girls.”
Management Plan:
1. Immediate Actions:
- Offer face-to-face review for lying/standing BP and HR + cardiovascular
exam.
- ECG to rule out arrhythmia.
- Blood tests: FBC, TFTs, U&Es, LFTs, cortisol, ferritin.
2. Lifestyle & Self-help (first-line):
- Increase fluid intake (2–2.5L/day).
- Adequate salt in diet (unless contraindicated).
- Avoid prolonged standing (e.g. assemblies).
- Rise slowly from sitting/lying.
- Gentle regular exercise (walking, swimming).
- Stop activity immediately if dizziness occurs during exertion.
- Compression stockings if symptoms troublesome.
3. Longer-term / Referral:
- If lying/standing test + ECG support PoTS → refer to paediatric
cardiology.
- They may consider medications (fludrocortisone, midodrine, SSRIs) if
lifestyle changes not enough.
4. Supportive Measures:
- Offer school support letter for GCSEs (e.g. seating at front in assemblies,
breaks if dizzy).
- Validate concerns: “I know exams are stressful already, so we’ll help you
manage this.”
5. Safety-netting:
- Seek urgent help if: chest pain, fainting during exercise, fainting with
SOB, prolonged blackouts, or worsening symptoms.
Learning Point (Examiner):
- PoTS = sustained rise in HR ≥40 bpm (12–19y) within 10 min of standing,
without postural hypotension.
- Very common in adolescent girls, often misattributed to anxiety.
- Management is lifestyle first, with referral for persistent/severe cases.

regularly

- Duration: few hours up to 1 day

- Associated mild nausea, no

- No changes in bowel habits, no PR
bleeding, no fever, no weight loss
Ideas: Concerned it might be
abdominal migraine
Concerns: Child in pain, may need
treatment
Expectations: Confirmation of
diagnosis, guidance on management
