- Side affected: Left, involving forehead (cannot raise
eyebrow/wrinkle), eye, mouth.
- Eye symptoms: Cannot fully close left eye, dryness risk.
- Other neurological symptoms: None (no slurred speech, limb
weakness, dizziness, visual loss).
- Associated symptoms: No facial pain, earache, rash, or systemic
symptoms.
- Social / ICE: Concerned it may be stroke, worried about
permanency, safety at work and driving.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
“Daniel, from your description—the drooping mouth, inability to
close your eye, and forehead involvement—this sounds like Bell’s
Palsy. It occurs when the nerve controlling one side of your face
becomes inflamed or irritated. This is different from a stroke, as you
have no limb weakness, speech problems, or balance issues. Early
treatment can improve recovery. We’ll need to see you in person to
confirm, but most people recover fully within a few weeks to 3
months. In the meantime, we can start treatment to help the
recovery and protect your eye.”
1. Face-to-Face Assessment
- Examine facial muscles, confirm lower motor neurone pattern, exclude
red flags.
2. Medication
- Prednisolone 50mg once daily for 10 days (within 72 hours of onset).
3. Eye Care
- Lubricating eye drops during the day, eye ointment at night.
- Tape eye closed at night if it does not fully close.
- Sunglasses outdoors to protect the eye.
4. Driving / Lifestyle Advice
- Can drive if vision unaffected; advise informing DVLA.
5. Follow-Up
- Review in 2–3 weeks to assess recovery.
- Refer to specialist (ENT/neurology) if no improvement after 3 weeks or
incomplete recovery by 3 months.
6. Safety Netting
- Urgent review if sudden worsening, new limb weakness, slurred speech,
or balance issues.
- Seek immediate care if eye becomes red, painful, or vision changes.
SCA Phrases:
- “Your facial weakness involves the whole side, including the forehead,
which suggests Bell’s Palsy rather than stroke.”
- “Early steroid treatment within 72 hours improves chances of full
recovery.”
- “Eye care is crucial when you cannot close your eye fully; this prevents
dryness or injury.”
- “Most people recover fully within weeks to months, but we’ll review you
to ensure progress.”

ability to look after themselves

5. Lifestyle / Cognitive Support

6. Driving Advice

drive but requires assessment.
7. Safety Netting / Follow-Up

- Advise patient and spouse to report new or worsening memory
problems, safety incidents at home, or mood changes promptly.

Name: Stanislaw Kowalski

occasional alcohol, no recreational
drug use
Presenting Complaint: Requests
codeine for chronic low back pain
following RTA >1 year ago
Red Flags / Concerning Symptoms
to Exclude:
- Leg weakness, numbness, or
radicular pain (sciatica)
- Bowel/bladder dysfunction (cauda
equina)
- Fever, weight loss, night sweats
(infection/malignancy)
- Progressive neurological deficits
Focused History:
- Onset & duration: Persistent low back pain >1 year since RTA.-
Location & character: Low back, non-radiating; no leg pain.
- Red flags: None present (no numbness, weakness, bowel/bladder
issues, fever, or night sweats).
- Impact on daily life: Pain affects work and quality of life; sleep
affected.
- Previous management: Physiotherapy in Poland; codeine use 7–
10 tablets of 30mg daily; last prescription 3 months ago.
- Effect of missed dose: Not specifically stated; persistent pain.
- Social context: Newly registered, unfamiliar with NHS; settling into
UK life.
Explanation to Patient:
“Stanislaw, I can see how living with this back pain for over a year
has been very frustrating, and I understand why you want codeine to
manage it. Long-term pain like yours can make everyday life really
difficult. Codeine can help, but when used regularly, the body can
become dependent, and higher doses may be needed over time,
which carries risks to your liver, bowel, mood, and sleep. Stopping
suddenly can also cause withdrawal symptoms. Because of this, the
safest approach is to provide a small supply today, and create a
clear plan to gradually reduce it. Alongside this, there are other ways
to manage your pain:
- Physiotherapy tailored to you, possibly different from your previous
sessions in Poland
- Non-opioid pain relief like naproxen or ibuprofen, if safe for your
stomach and asthma status
- Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) to help cope with chronic pain
- Social prescribing support to help you navigate the NHS and local
community resources.
We’ll review your progress in 3–4 weeks and adjust the plan as
needed. This approach helps manage your pain safely while reducing
the risk of long-term opioid dependence. Does that sound okay?”
1. Assessment & Face-to-Face Review
- Arrange review of back pain including neurological exam (power,
reflexes, sensation) and use STarT Back questionnaire to stratify risk and
tailor interventions.
2. Medication Management
- Prescribe small, limited supply of codeine with a plan to gradually
reduce over time.
- Educate patient on risks of long-term codeine use (dependence,
tolerance, liver, bowel, mood).
- Offer NSAIDs (naproxen/ibuprofen) PRN, after screening for GI or
asthma contraindications; provide PPI if needed.
3. Non-Pharmacological Support
- Refer to physiotherapy with individualized program.
- Offer CBT for chronic pain coping strategies.
- Provide access to social prescriber for local services and support in
NHS navigation.
4. Safety-Netting
- Advise urgent review if: leg weakness/numbness, bowel/bladder
problems, sudden worsening of pain, or new neurological symptoms.
5. Follow-Up
- Schedule review in 3–4 weeks to assess pain, medication use, and
adherence to non-drug strategies.
SCA Phrases:
- “We can provide a small supply of codeine today, with a plan to gradually
reduce it to reduce risk of dependence.”
- “Other options like physiotherapy, NSAIDs, CBT, and social support can
help manage your pain safely.”
- “We’ll review your progress in a few weeks and adjust the plan as
needed.”
- “If you notice any new weakness, numbness, or bowel/bladder changes,
seek urgent medical attention immediately.”

3. Safeguarding
- Make a child safeguarding referral due to methadone spill in household

Red Flags / Concerning Symptoms
to Exclude:
- Sleep problems
- Toileting issues
- Learning difficulties
- Social difficulties
- Behavioural concerns reported by
school or nursery
- Concerns about safety
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
Pointers:
- Inattention: easily distracted,
difficulty sustaining attention,
forgetful, poor organisation.
- Hyperactivity: fidgeting, inability to
stay seated, constantly “on the go.”
- Impulsivity: blurting out answers,
interrupting, difficulty waiting turn.
- Present in ≥2 settings (home and
school).
- Symptoms present for ≥6 months,
started before age 12, and impact
daily life.
Focused History:
- Behaviour: Energetic, hyperactive at home; jumps and moves
around a lot.
- Attention: Can maintain attention during structured tasks; no
significant inattention reported.
- Hyperactivity/Impulsivity: No concerning impulsive behaviour

- Social / Peer Interaction: Gets along well with friends; no issues
reported at school.
- Home environment: Lives with parents and older brother; positive
family dynamics.
- PBIND: Normal pregnancy, birth, immunisations, nutrition, and
developmental milestones.
- Family history: No ADHD or autism.
Explanation to Parent:
“Thanks, Tina, for sharing your observations. From what you’ve
described, Leo is a bright, lively 6-year-old, and it’s reassuring that
school hasn’t raised any concerns. When assessing for ADHD, we
look for persistent difficulties that occur in multiple settings — home
and school — and have a significant impact on learning,
relationships, or daily functioning. At this stage, Leo’s behaviour
appears within the normal range for his age. It’s also normal for
children in the same family to behave differently — being more active
doesn’t automatically mean something is wrong. For now, we
recommend watchful waiting and a review in about 10 weeks to see
how things progress. In the meantime, we can provide resources on
child development and behaviour and offer behavioural strategies to
help manage his energy positively.”
- “ADHD is a condition where the brain works a little differently,
especially in areas that control attention and self-control.”
- “It’s not caused by bad parenting or too much screen time — it’s to
do with brain wiring and chemistry, particularly how brain cells use a
chemical messenger called dopamine.”
- “That’s why children with ADHD can struggle to focus on things they
find less interesting, but may hyperfocus on things they enjoy.”
- “It usually shows up at school age because children are expected
to sit still and concentrate for longer.”
1. Reassurance & Education
- Explain that Leo’s current behaviour is within the normal range for his
age.
- Validate parental concerns and acknowledge comparison with older
sibling.
- Emphasise ADHD requires persistent symptoms across multiple
settings with impact on functioning.
2. Watchful Waiting
- Schedule a follow-up review in ~10 weeks to reassess behaviour and
development.
- Encourage communication with school, asking them to report any
concerns. Speak to SENCO at school
- Consider referral to CAMHS if waiting is not acceptable or persistent
symptoms > 10 weeks or severe symptoms or sleep problems for
melatonin consderaiton
3. Parenting Support
- Offer referral to a group-based parenting programme (ADHD-focused
or positive parenting support). Signpost to Triple P, NSPCC, family Lives
- Provide resources on child development and behavioural strategies
for energetic children (ADHD Foundation Charity/ Adult Attention Deficit
Disorder UK)
- Advice about setting clear and consistent boundaries with positive
reinforcement to reward desired behaviour
- Limit caffeine, fizzy or enery drinks,
4. Safety Net Advice
- Advise parent to return sooner if new concerns arise:
• Learning difficulties
• Attention issues
• Behavioural challenges
- Ensure support is available if school begins to notice difficulties.
SCA Phrases:
- “Leo’s behaviour appears within the normal range for his age, especially
as school hasn’t raised concerns.”
- “Children develop differently; being more active than a sibling doesn’t
mean there’s a problem.”
- “We’ll review him again in about 10 weeks to see how he’s progressing.”
- “We can provide resources and parenting support programmes if you’d
like practical strategies and reassurance.”
- “If any new concerns arise, especially at school, let us know sooner and
we can reassess.”

difficulty swallowing, or voice changes — this does not appear
concerning for cancer at this stage. What you’re likely experiencing is
called globus sensation, where a lump is felt in the throat but no
physical obstruction exists. Stress and anxiety often contribute to
this. A face-to-face appointment is still important so we can examine
your neck and throat to ensure nothing is missed. Most cases
improve with reassurance and stress management.

- Offer face-to-face appointment to examine neck and throat.
- Reassure patient: absence of red flags suggests benign globus
sensation.
- Arrange review in 3–4 weeks to assess progress and response.
2. Symptom & Lifestyle Management
- Advise avoid habitual throat clearing; sip chilled carbonated water
when urge arises.
- Recommend stress management strategies: mindfulness, CBT, gentle
exercise, yoga, relaxation apps (e.g., Headspace).
- Offer short-term fit note if work-related stress affects performance.
3. Safety-Net / Escalation
- Advise urgent review if new symptoms develop: dysphagia,
odynophagia, persistent hoarseness, neck lumps, unexplained weight
loss.
- Consider ENT referral if symptoms persist, become constant, or red
flags emerge.
SCA Phrases:
- “From what you’ve told me, this does not suggest anything serious at this
stage.”
- “This sounds like a common condition called globus sensation.”

be thorough.”
- “Stress and anxiety can contribute to this sensation, and there are
strategies to help manage it.”
- “If any new concerning symptoms appear, contact the practice urgently
or attend A&E.”

LDL (3.9 mmol/L), but your overall 10-year cardiovascular risk
(QRISK3) is low at 4%, meaning about 4 in 100 people like you are
expected to have a heart attack or stroke in the next 10 years. This is
reassuring. The most effective way to reduce your risk further is
through lifestyle changes: healthy diet (5-a-day, reduced saturated
fat), regular aerobic activity (~150 minutes/week), and smoking
cessation. Statins are effective and safe but are not routinely
recommended for someone with a low QRISK3 unless you prefer
treatment after informed discussion. Would you like to discuss
starting a statin?”

- Advise smoking cessation referral/support; highlight cardiovascular
risk reduction.
- Recommend dietary changes: increase fruits/vegetables, reduce
saturated fats, incorporate unsaturated fats (olive oil, nuts, oily fish).
- Encourage regular exercise: aim for 150 min/week of moderate activity;
even brisk walking daily is helpful.
- Offer referral to Health & Wellbeing Coach or lifestyle support services.
2. Statin Therapy (Shared Decision)
- Discuss statin benefits, side effects (muscle aches, GI upset, liver
effects).
- If patient opts for treatment, prescribe statin and repeat lipid profile in 3
months to monitor response.
- If patient chooses lifestyle alone, repeat lipid profile in 3 months to
evaluate effectiveness.
3. Safety-Net / Escalation
- Advise urgent review if develop chest pain, shortness of breath,
palpitations, focal neurological deficits.
4. Follow-up
- Schedule follow-up in 3 months to review progress, lifestyle adherence,
and blood tests.
SCA Phrases:
- “Your 10-year risk of a heart attack or stroke is low at 4%, which is

- “Lifestyle changes are the most important step to reduce your

- Other medications: Paracetamol ongoing; started ibuprofen
yesterday.
- Missed monitoring: Explained busy work schedule; understands
methotrexate stopped per safety protocol.
- Systemic/extra-articular symptoms: None reported (eyes, lungs,
heart, skin).
- Infection symptoms: None.
- Social: Lives with wife and 2 kids; non-smoker, no alcohol.
Explanation to Patient:
“Thomas, your symptoms are consistent with a RA flare, likely
triggered by stopping methotrexate. I understand your work is
demanding and this is affecting you significantly. Methotrexate
cannot be safely restarted until your blood tests are up-to-date,
because it can affect your liver, kidneys, and blood counts, and
increase infection risk. We can arrange urgent blood tests today.
Once the results are normal, we can restart your methotrexate
promptly. In the meantime, continuing NSAIDs like ibuprofen
regularly with food can help, and we can consider stronger NSAIDs
or a short course of oral steroids if needed. I’ll also inform your
rheumatology team so they are aware and can provide input. Going
forward, we can set reminders or schedule your monitoring in
advance to prevent missed bloods.”
RA: It is a long-term condition where the body's immune system
mistakenly attacks the lining of the joints, causing pain, swelling,
and stiffness. Over time, this can lead to joint damage and deformity
1. Monitoring & Safe Prescribing
- Arrange urgent blood tests: FBC, U&E, LFTs, CRP.
- Reinforce importance of routine methotrexate monitoring per shared
care / NICE guidelines AND to stop if red flags on monitoring
- Liaise with Rheumatology team to inform them of flare and plan.
- Need at least monitoring every 12 weeks and more frequently when
restarting it or decrease dose every 2 weeks until stable for 6 weeks
2. Symptom Management
- NSAIDs: Continue ibuprofen regularly with food; consider naproxen +
omeprazole if stronger anti-inflammatory needed.
- Oral steroids: Consider short course if NSAIDs insufficient (e.g.,
Prednisolone 10 mg OD for 7 days → 5 mg OD for 7 days).
3. Future Compliance & Support
- Discuss practical strategies to prevent missed blood tests (phone
reminders, calendar scheduling, GP reminders).
- Offer pneumococcal and annual influenza vaccination to reduce
infection risk while on immunosuppressive therapy.
- Ensure regular Folic acid 5mg
- Offer apt in extended hours
- Explain OOH services and clinic over the weekend
4. Safety-Net / Escalation
- Advise urgent review if joints become hot, swollen, immobile, or if
patient develops fever or systemic symptoms, suggesting possible
infection.
5. Follow-up
- Review blood results and restart methotrexate once safe.
- Arrange follow-up consultation (phone or face-to-face) after results to
reassess symptoms and adjust treatment.
SCA Phrases:
- “We need up-to-date blood tests before restarting methotrexate to
ensure it’s safe.”
- “Your flare is likely due to methotrexate being stopped.”
- “Continuing NSAIDs regularly and considering steroids can help control
symptoms until methotrexate is restarted.”
- “We’ll liaise with Rheumatology to keep your care coordinated.”
- “If your joint becomes hot, swollen, or you develop a fever, seek urgent
review immediately.”

170- Flight anxiety
Patient Details & Background / Red
Flags
Name: James Nolan
Age: 40M
PMH: Newly registered, no prior
medical issues
Current Medications: None
Allergies: None
Presenting Complaint: Requests
diazepam for flight anxiety
Red Flags / Concerning Symptoms
to Exclude:
- Signs of generalized anxiety
disorder, panic disorder, or severe
depression
- Substance misuse history
- Suicidal thoughts or self-harm
- Sedation risk or polypharmacy
Why not prescribe diazepam for
flights?
- Risk of dependence/addiction.
- Side effects: drowsiness, slowed
reaction time, confusion.
- Safety concerns: reduced ability to
respond in emergencies during flight.
- Can worsen hypoxia at altitude,
especially in patients with respiratory
disease.
- Legal restrictions in some countries.
Focused History:
- Anxiety onset: Fear of flying began 5 years ago after severe
turbulence on a flight to Singapore.
- Flight symptoms: Panic during take-off, landing, or turbulence;
palpitations, sweating, tingling in lips/fingers.
- Past management: Diazepam previously prescribed by former GP;
helps with symptoms.
- Other anxiety: No anxiety outside flying situations.
- Other medication or substance use: None.
- Social & work: IT consultant, lives with wife.
- Current location: Netherlands; returning to UK tomorrow; long-
haul flight to US in 5 days.
- Non-drug strategies: Tried breathing exercises and calming music;
minimal benefit.
- Mood & functioning: Otherwise well; no sleep or appetite
disturbance.
Explanation to Patient:
“James, I can hear how distressing flying has been for you and how
much you rely on diazepam. However, current guidance advises
against prescribing diazepam for flight anxiety because it can cause
sedation, confusion, breathing problems, increase risk of DVT on
long flights, and reduce your ability to respond in an emergency. It
may also be illegal in some countries, creating additional legal risks.
I understand this may be disappointing, but there are safer
alternatives that can help you manage anxiety effectively.”
1. Patient Education & Safety:
- Explain risks of diazepam during air travel: sedation, respiratory
depression, DVT, impaired response in emergencies, legal issues abroad.
- Emphasise that prescribing is not appropriate for flight anxiety per
current medical guidance.
2. Short-term Management:
- Offer non-drug strategies: structured breathing exercises, distraction
(music, podcasts, films, puzzles), mindfulness or relaxation apps (e.g.,
Headspace).
- Discuss preparing for flights with planning, arrival time, and comfort
measures.
3. Long-term Management:
- Offer referral to talking therapy or CBT for anxiety management.
- Offer referral to structured airline fear-of-flying courses (e.g., British
Airways, EasyJet).
4. Follow-up & Safety-Netting:
- Arrange follow-up appointment after returning to the UK to review
anxiety and support progress.
- Advise urgent contact if anxiety worsens significantly, or if suicidal
thoughts develop.
SCA Phrases:
- “Current guidance no longer supports prescribing diazepam for flight
anxiety because of safety risks.”
- “There are safer, evidence-based strategies to help you manage flying
anxiety effectively.”
- “We can explore long-term support such as CBT or structured fear-of-
flying courses once you’re back in the UK.”
- “If your anxiety worsens or you feel unsafe, please contact us urgently.”
- “Airlines run excellent courses where you can learn techniques with a
pilot and psychologist on board — many people find them life-changing.”
- “For the physical side — things like shaking or palpitations — propranolol
can sometimes help.”

- Social & lifestyle: Sedentary IT work, minimal exercise, diet high in
fast food/snacks, lives alone, non-smoker, no alcohol.
- Blood test results: HbA1c 46 mmol/mol (pre-diabetic), total
cholesterol 5.8 mmol/L, LDL 3.9 mmol/L, HDL 1.3 mmol/L,
triglycerides 1.4 mmol/L, BMI 30, QRISK3 score 5.5% (low).
- ICE: Patient wants results explained; asks about medications to
prevent progression to diabetes.
Explanation to Patient:
“Alan, your routine blood tests show that most results are normal,
which is good news. Your HbA1c is in the pre-diabetic range, which
means your blood sugar is higher than normal but not high enough to
be classified as diabetes. Your cholesterol is slightly raised, and your
BMI is above the recommended range. Overall, your 10-year
cardiovascular risk (QRISK3) is low at 5.5%, so there’s no immediate
danger. These results are an early signal to make some lifestyle
changes to reduce your risk of developing diabetes or heart disease.
Are you following so far?”
- “It’s a warning sign – without changes, many people go on to
develop diabetes.”
- “The good news is that with lifestyle changes, this can often be
reversed, and your risk of diabetes greatly reduced.”
1. Lifestyle Advice:
- Encourage regular physical activity: ≥150 minutes moderate-intensity
exercise/week (e.g., brisk walking, cycling).
- Promote healthy diet: reduce saturated fat, increase fibre and
vegetables, reduce fast food/snacks, consider meal planning.
- Weight management: aim for gradual reduction in BMI to improve blood
sugar and cholesterol.
2. Structured Programmes:
- Refer to NHS National Diabetes Prevention Programme (digital or face-
to-face) for evidence-based support.
3. Monitoring & Follow-Up:
- Repeat HbA1c and cholesterol monitoring at regular intervals (e.g.,
annually).
- Review lifestyle changes in 3 months, discuss further interventions if
needed.
- Medication for pre-diabetes (e.g., metformin) not routinely indicated
unless lifestyle changes fail or risk increases.
4. Safety-Net Advice:
- Seek medical review if symptoms of diabetes develop: increased thirst,
urination, fatigue, unexplained weight change.
SCA Phrases:
- “Your blood sugar is in the pre-diabetic range, but this can often be
reversed with lifestyle changes.”
- “Your cholesterol is slightly elevated, but your overall cardiovascular risk
is currently low.”
- “The most effective approach right now is focusing on diet, exercise, and
weight management. Medication is not usually needed at this stage.”
- “We’ll monitor your progress with follow-up tests and review in 3 months
to see how you’re getting on.”
- “This is a chance to act early before diabetes develops.”
- “Lifestyle changes can actually reverse this condition.”
- “We’ll support you with weight loss and activity programmes.”
- “If lifestyle alone isn’t enough, we can consider medication such as
metformin to help.”

- Red flags: night pain, functional
impact, risk of fracture, rare risk of

days ago and had an X-ray arranged. Thanks for booking in to go
through the results.”
- Clarify pain: worse, better, same? waking at night?
lumps/bumps/swelling? stiffness?
- Function: mobility, can he walk, is daily activity or caring role

- Other sites: any other bone/joint pains? skull involvement (hearing

- Systemic red flags: fever, sweats, weight loss, appetite loss.
- FHx: bone disease?
- Social impact: sole carer → strain, support available?

- “I can see how this must be worrying, especially since you are the
main carer for your wife.”
- “You’re doing an incredible job supporting her, and we’ll look at
how we can support you too while we sort this out.”
Dx explained simply: “Your X-ray shows that this isn’t arthritis.
Instead, the changes are due to something called Paget’s disease
of bone. This means that part of the bone is being broken down and
rebuilt too quickly in a way that isn’t normal. Over time, this can
cause pain and make the bone weaker. It is not cancer, but we do
keep an eye on it closely because it can very rarely cause
complications.”
- “This condition isn’t cancer, but the way the bone is behaving is
abnormal and can sometimes weaken it.”
- “The good news is we do have effective treatments that can help
reduce the pain and protect your bone.”
Bloods: ALP, LFTs, calcium, vitamin D, PTH.
- Referral: rheumatology (for confirmation + treatment plan).
- Treatment: rheumatology may offer bisphosphonates (reduce
abnormal bone turnover, improve pain).
- Analgesia: continue ibuprofen if helpful + add PPI for gastric protection
(age risk).
- Support: referral to social prescriber → explore home help, carer
support, benefits.
- Lifestyle advice
- Maintain mobility, safe exercise, good posture
- Adequate calcium and vitamin D intake.
- Fall prevention strategies.
- Avoid high impact activities
- Safety-netting: seek urgent review if:
• sudden worsening pain,
• inability to walk/use limb,
• new lumps/swelling,
• systemic symptoms (weight loss, fevers).
- Monitoring: watch for hearing loss/tinnitus (skull involvement).
Good SCA phrases:
- “Because you are caring for your wife, I’d like to link you with our social
prescriber to see what support can be put in place, even if just
temporarily.”
- “If you notice sudden worsening pain, problems walking, or any new
lumps, I’d want you to seek urgent medical review.”
- “Your symptoms and X-rays suggest Paget’s disease, which is a problem
with how bones remodel themselves.”
- “We usually treat with medications called bisphosphonates to slow
down bone turnover and relieve pain.”
- “It’s important to maintain mobility and prevent falls to reduce fracture
risk.”
- “We will monitor blood tests and X-rays to track your condition over
time.”
- “If you notice sudden pain, swelling, or weakness, you should seek
medical attention promptly.”

- Red flags: daytime sleepiness +

- Risk factors: weight, alcohol, diet, exercise.

- Impact: relationships (wife sleeps separately), work.

- “Because you’re a professional driver, we need to take your
sleepiness very seriously for your safety and others on the road.”
Dx explained simply: “From what you’ve told me, I’m concerned
about a condition called obstructive sleep apnoea. That’s when the
throat relaxes too much during sleep, blocking airflow. It causes
snoring, pauses in breathing, and poor-quality sleep. This explains
your tiredness and headaches.”
Management plan:
- Face-to-face review: Examine neck, oropharynx, nostrils, BP.
- Epworth Sleepiness Questionnaire → send electronically or complete in
clinic.
- Refer to sleep clinic (urgent, within 4 weeks given Group 2 driver status).
- Explain sleep study: usually at home with a small monitor checking
breathing, oxygen, sleep position.
- If OSA confirmed: CPAP device at night to keep airway open.
- Driving advice: MUST stop driving immediately due to excessive daytime
sleepiness (DVLA rules for Group 2). Provide fit note.
- Support: Refer to social prescriber for financial/work support.
- Lifestyle advice: weight loss, healthier diet, reduce alcohol (alcohol
worsens OSA), regular activity, avoid back sleeping.
- Follow-up: after sleep clinic for review and ongoing support.
- Signpost to British Snoring & Sleep Apnoea Association:
https://britishsnoring.co.uk/ Or Sleep Apnea Trust
Good SCA phrases for management:
- “Given your symptoms and your job as a lorry driver, my main concern is
safety — for you and others on the road.”
- “I need to advise you to stop driving until the sleep clinic has assessed
you. I’ll provide a fit note and support with any financial concerns.”
- “The sleep study is simple — a monitor records your breathing and
oxygen overnight at home.”
- “If sleep apnoea is confirmed, the usual treatment is a CPAP machine,
which helps keep the airway open and usually transforms people’s energy
levels.”
- “Cutting back on alcohol and working on weight loss can make a big
difference, and we’ll support you with that.”
- “We’ll review things after your sleep clinic appointment to check on
progress and next steps.”

quickly, relieve pain.
• Expectations → hopes for additional

wants prevention advice.
- RFs: age >60, male, hypertension,
high red meat intake, occasional
alcohol, first gout episode.

- Pain: severity, ongoing swelling, redness, mobility, effect on daily

- Previous episodes: confirm first flare.
- Medication side effects: diarrhoea, nausea, vomiting, abdominal

- Occupational impact: days off work, ability to perform duties.

and work.”
- “Thank you for explaining the side effects you experienced from
colchicine — it’s important we consider this when planning further
treatment.”
Diagnosis explained simply:
- “It sounds like your gout flare is improving, which is encouraging.
Gout happens when uric acid crystals form in the joint, causing pain
and swelling. You’ve already had the recommended course of
colchicine, and your symptoms are gradually settling.”
Management plan:
- Do not repeat colchicine: maximum safe dose reached (500 mcg 2–
4x/day, max 6 mg/course), narrow therapeutic index.
- Alternative pain relief: NSAID (e.g., naproxen) if no contraindications;
consider PPI for gastric protection (age >55).
- Steroid option: short-course oral prednisolone (30 mg OD for 3–5 days)
if NSAID contraindicated.
- Self-care: rest, elevate foot, ice packs, avoid tight footwear.
- Lifestyle advice: reduce red meat, alcohol; maintain hydration.
- Preventative therapy: discuss allopurinol once flare resolved to lower
uric acid and reduce recurrence.
- Work advice: self-certify up to 7 days; fit note if unable to return.
- Safety-netting: urgent review if joint becomes red, hot, swollen, or
systemic symptoms develop.
- Follow-up: 2–3 weeks to review blood tests and discuss long-term
management.
- If patient on thiazide, stop it and change to alternative (losartan is more
beneficial)
Good SCA phrases for management:
- “You’ve completed the safe course of colchicine, so we won’t repeat it
as it could be harmful.”
- “Because you’re still in some pain, we can use naproxen to help reduce
inflammation and discomfort.”
- “Simple measures like elevating your foot and applying ice can really
help recovery.”
- “Once your flare settles, we can discuss starting allopurinol to prevent
future attacks.”
- “If you notice your joint becoming hot, red, or swollen again, or if you
develop a fever, please contact us urgently.”

work — we need to act promptly.”
Diagnosis explained simply:
- “Based on your symptoms — tiredness, excessive thirst and
urination, rapid weight loss, and your home blood sugar reading —
there is a strong possibility that you have type 1 diabetes. This is
where your body is not making insulin, which helps control sugar
levels. It can get serious quickly, so the safest step is to go to
hospital today for urgent treatment and confirmation.”
Management:
- Immediate same-day referral/admission: via Acute Medical Unit for
suspected type 1 diabetes.
- Transport advice: do not drive; arrange for wife or someone else to take
him safely.
- Hospital care: confirmation of diagnosis, start intravenous fluids and
insulin if required.
“The FreeStyle Libre is a small sensor you wear on the back of your arm for
up to two weeks. It continuously measures your sugar levels and you can
check them anytime by scanning the sensor with your phone. It helps spot
patterns, so you can manage your diabetes more easily. Sometimes you’ll
still need finger-prick checks, especially if your sugar is changing quickly
or if you don’t feel right, but most of the time it saves you from frequent
finger-pricking.”
- Urgent warning: if symptoms worsen en route (vomiting, severe
tiredness, confusion, shortness of breath) → call 999 immediately.
- Follow-up: after discharge, book GP appointment to discuss long-term
blood sugar control, lifestyle, diet, and education.
- Signpost to DAFNE: https://dafne.nhs.uk/ for more info about diet
Good SCA phrases:
- “Waiting even one more day could be risky — your body is struggling to
cope without insulin.”
- “At the hospital, they can bring your blood sugar under control safely
with insulin and fluids.”
- “Once stable, I will support you with diet, lifestyle advice, and follow-up
care to manage your diabetes long-term.”
- “Do not drive yourself — your wife or someone else should bring you, and
if you feel worse on the way, call 999 immediately.”

- Social: lives with parents and

• Ideas → knows MSM at higher risk.
• Concerns → worried he has HIV.
• Expectations → wants confidential
HIV test.
- Red flags:
under 18, MSM, unprotected anal
sex?, partner unknown status,
safeguarding (consent, coercion,
grooming, home safety).
PCP (dry cough with drop in oxygen
with exertion)/ meningitis/
lymphoma/ Kaposi’s sarcoma (dark
purple intradermal skin lesion like a
firm bruise)/ cervical cancer
Start with empathy & confidentiality:
- “Thanks for coming in — it shows you’re taking responsibility for
your health.”

serious harm, and I’d explain before sharing.”

- “Is it okay if I ask some personal questions to give the best care?”
- First time sexually active? When did it start?
- When was last encounter? (PEPSE if <72h).
- Protected vs unprotected? (condoms).
- Partner’s HIV/STI status? (unknown).
- Any other partners for either of you?
- Was it always consensual? Any pressure, coercion, or grooming?
Symptoms:
- Explore flu-like illness → query acute HIV.
- Ask about rash, ulcers, dysuria, discharge, weight loss, diarrhoea,

- Does family know about sexuality?
- Would disclosure put him at risk at home/school?
- Support network? Exam stress? Mental health.

- “You’ve done the right thing by asking for help so soon.”
- “Flu-like symptoms are very common, but I understand your
concern — only a test can confirm.”
- “This is about keeping you safe, not about judging you.”
Simple explanation:
- “Because your partner’s status isn’t known and you’ve had anal
sex, you are at some risk of HIV. Flu-like illnesses are common, but
they can sometimes be an early sign of HIV. A blood test is the only
way to know.”
Investigations / next steps:
- Offer HIV test today (4th-gen test available in GP).
- Explain repeat at 12 weeks due to window period.
- Offer full STI screen (chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis, Hep B/C).
PEPSE (if <72h since last sex):
- “If your last unprotected sex was within the last 3 days, there’s a tablet
called PEP that can reduce the chance of infection — we’d arrange this
urgently through sexual health.”
Pre-exposure prophylaxis = two antiretroviral drugs that are taken before
and after sex to reduce the risk of HIV acquisition for high-risk HIV
negative people
Prevention / education:
- Safe sex → consistent condom use.
- Partner should also be tested.
- PrEP available from sexual health clinic to reduce risk in future.
- HIV treatment now = highly effective → people live normal lifespan.
- U=U (undetectable = untransmittable).
Safeguarding:
- He is over 16, can consent, but check coercion/grooming.
- If concerns → safeguarding referral.
Confidentiality reassurance:
- “Your results won’t be shared with parents without your consent.”
- If new Dx and stable = refer urgently to HIV clinic within 2-14 days for
starting ART + encourage safe sex with condoms + signpost to Aidsmap/
BASHH
Follow-up & safety net:
- Review results in 1 week.
- Repeat HIV test at 12 weeks.
- Return urgently if new symptoms like diarrhoea, fever, weight loss, rash.
Good SCA phrases for management:
- “We can do the HIV test here today.”
- “Because the virus can take time to show, we repeat it in 12 weeks.”
- “If positive, treatment is so effective now that people can live long,
healthy lives.”
- “There’s a prevention tablet called PrEP — you can talk about this at a
sexual health clinic if you’re interested.”
- “You’ve done the right thing by coming early — this gives you the best
protection.”

182- Proctalgia Fugax
Patient Details & Red Flags
- Sudden, severe rectal pain lasting
seconds–minutes, resolving
spontaneously
- No relation to defecation
- Episodes occur intermittently, often
at night
Red Flags to Exclude (must ask):
- Rectal bleeding
- Change in bowel habit
- Weight loss
- Anaemia symptoms
- Family history of bowel cancer
- Persistent pain > 30 minutes
(consider levator ani syndrome,
fissures, cancer, abscess, IBD)
Focused History:
- Pain characteristics: Onset, duration (few seconds to minutes),
frequency, severity, relation to bowel movements
- Associated symptoms: Bleeding, mucus, change in stool,
incontinence, tenesmus
- Red flag questions (cancer/IBD symptoms as above)
- Triggers: Stress, anxiety, bowel irregularity
- Past history: Anorectal disease (fissures, haemorrhoids, IBS)
Explanation to Patient:
“From what you’ve described — sudden, short-lived, sharp rectal
pain that comes and goes — this fits with a condition called
proctalgia fugax. It literally means ‘fleeting pain in the rectum’. It’s
caused by brief spasms of the muscles around the anus. It can feel
severe but is not dangerous. The pain usually lasts less than 20
minutes and disappears on its own. It’s not linked to cancer and
does not cause long-term harm. We still need to keep an eye on any
red flag symptoms like bleeding or change in bowel habits, but from
your story this sounds like benign spasm pain.”
Exam Tips (SCA focus):
1. Reassurance is key → patient anxiety can be significant
despite benign nature.
2. Differentiate from red-flag anorectal conditions →
fissures, abscesses, malignancy.
3. Symptom relief advice → warm baths, high-fibre diet,
relaxation.
4. Investigations usually unnecessary → only if atypical
features present.
5. Safety net clearly → prolonged pain, bleeding, systemic
symptoms require review.
1. Reassurance & Education:
- Harmless, benign muscle spasm
- Episodes self-limiting
2. Lifestyle / Self-care:
- Keep bowels regular (diet, fibre, hydration)
- Warm baths, relaxation techniques
- Avoid known triggers (stress, constipation)
3. Medical Management (rarely needed):
- Inhaled salbutamol (off-label, sometimes used for acute spasm)
- Local anaesthetic gel if severe episodes
• Topical nitroglycerin or calcium channel blockers for severe/recurrent
cases (specialist referral).
- Refer to gastroenterology if red flags or persistent pain >20 min (consider
levator ani syndrome, fissure, malignancy, abscess)
4. Safety Netting / SCA Phrases:
- “The good news is this is not dangerous, and many people find it settles
with reassurance and simple measures.”
- “Please let me know urgently if you notice bleeding, weight loss, change
in bowel habits, or pain lasting longer than 30 minutes.”
- “If symptoms become frequent or disabling, we can consider referral to a
specialist.”
- “The sharp rectal pains you experience are called proctalgia fugax and
are harmless.”
- “They usually last a few seconds to minutes and stop on their own.”
- “Warm baths and relaxation techniques may help when episodes
occur.”
- “There’s no evidence of infection or serious disease, but let us know if
you notice bleeding, discharge, or persistent pain.”
- “These episodes are common and do not cause long-term problems.”

183- Levator Ani Syndrome
Patient Details & Red Flags
- Dull, aching pain/pressure in the
rectum or pelvic floor
- Worse when sitting, better when
standing/lying
- Often chronic/recurrent (>3 months)
Red Flags to Exclude:
- Rectal bleeding
- Change in bowel habit
- Weight loss
- Anaemia symptoms
- Family history of bowel cancer or
IBD
- Severe, acute pain (rule out fissure,
thrombosed haemorrhoid, abscess)
Focused History:
- Pain: Onset, duration (>30 min suggests levator ani, <20 min =
proctalgia fugax), relation to posture/activity
- Exacerbating/relieving factors: sitting, defecation, stress
- Bowel habits: constipation, diarrhoea, straining
- Associated symptoms: urinary frequency, dyspareunia, pelvic
floor dysfunction
- Psychosocial factors: stress, anxiety, impact on daily life
- Red flag screening (bleeding, weight loss, bowel habit change,
systemic symptoms)
Explanation in Lay Terms:
“What you’re experiencing sounds like a condition we call levator ani
syndrome. It happens when the muscles around the back passage
go into spasm and cause a dull, aching pressure or discomfort, often
worse with sitting. Unlike piles or fissures, there isn’t a tear or growth
— it’s more of a muscle problem, a bit like cramp in the pelvic floor.
It can be uncomfortable and sometimes long-lasting, but it is not
dangerous.”
1. Conservative / Lifestyle:
- Reassurance (benign condition, not cancerous)
- Avoid prolonged sitting (use cushion, stand/walk breaks)
- Warm baths, heat packs to pelvic area
- Regular bowel habits, avoid constipation (hydration, fibre, stool
softeners)
2. Medical / Physical Therapy:
- Pelvic floor physiotherapy & biofeedback (most evidence-based)
- Muscle relaxants (e.g. diazepam suppositories — specialist use)
- Local anaesthetic injections or botulinum toxin (secondary care)
3. Psychological support:
- Stress management, CBT if anxiety/depression contributory
4. Safety Netting / SCA Phrases:
- “This is not harmful but can be a recurring condition.”
- “Please come back if you notice bleeding, a change in your bowel habit,
or unexplained weight loss.”
- “Physiotherapy aimed at retraining the pelvic floor muscles often helps a
lot, and I can refer you if the symptoms persist.”
- “If pain is severe and ongoing despite simple measures, we can involve a
specialist to explore other treatments.”

184- Cannabis-Induced Hyperemesis
Syndrome (CHS).
Definition: Cyclical vomiting
triggered by chronic cannabis use,
often relieved by hot showers or
baths. Usually occurs in heavy, long-
term users.
When to Suspect:
- Recurrent episodes of nausea and
vomiting with normal investigations.
- Relief with hot showers/baths.
- Chronic cannabis use (daily or near-
daily).
- Abdominal pain, dehydration,
weight loss in severe cases.
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Persistent vomiting causing
dehydration, electrolyte disturbance.
- Blood in vomit, severe abdominal
pain, fever (exclude other causes).
- Altered mental status or signs of
other substance use.
Focused History:
- Vomiting: Onset, frequency, duration, severity
- Pattern: Cyclical, recurrent episodes separated by symptom-free
intervals
- Triggers/relievers: Hot baths/showers help?
- Cannabis use: Duration, frequency, amount, type (smoking,
vaping, edibles)
- Associated symptoms: Abdominal pain, dehydration, appetite
changes, weight loss
- Red flags: blood in vomit, severe pain, fainting, fevers, bowel
changes
- Impact: work, social life, mental health
- Substance/alcohol history
- Ideas/Concerns/Expectations: Patient may not realise cannabis
is the cause; worried about serious stomach problem; wants relief.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
“From what you’re describing — the sickness, the abdominal pain,
and the fact hot showers help — it fits with something we see in
people who use cannabis regularly, called Cannabis-Induced
Hyperemesis Syndrome. It’s a bit paradoxical, because cannabis
often helps with nausea in the short term, but over time, with
repeated use, it can actually start to cause vomiting.”
Exam Tips (SCA focus):
1. Red flags for alternative diagnoses → always rule out
acute abdomen, infection, metabolic causes.
2. Emphasize cessation as definitive treatment →
temporary symptom relief (showers/anti-emetics) is
insufficient.
3. Address hydration and electrolyte risk → examiners look
for acute management awareness.
4. Patient-centered approach → discuss cannabis use non-
judgmentally, offer support for cessation.
5. Safety net clearly → severe dehydration, blood in vomit, or
confusion require urgent review.
1. Immediate / Acute Management:
- Assess hydration: offer fluids (oral or IV if admitted)
- Antiemetics (haloperidol, ondansetron sometimes used, but often
limited benefit)
- Pain relief
2. Definitive Management:
- Complete cessation of cannabis (only proven cure)
- Support with substance misuse services (drug and alcohol team referral)
- Motivational interviewing techniques: explore readiness to quit, link to
long-term health
3. Lifestyle Advice:
- Avoid cannabis “detoxing at home” without support if severe vomiting
- Encourage hydration and nutrition
- Avoid known triggers (continued cannabis use, stress, sleep deprivation)
4. Safety Netting / SCA Phrases:
- “If you’re unable to keep fluids down, or you develop dizziness, fainting,
or blood in your vomit, you need to seek urgent medical help.”
- “This condition only truly improves when cannabis is stopped. We can
support you with that process — you won’t be left alone to manage it.”
- “If symptoms return even after you quit, let us know, but most people
see a big improvement once they stop cannabis.”
- “Your vomiting is likely caused by long-term cannabis use, which is
unusual but recognised.”
- “Stopping cannabis is the most effective way to prevent further
episodes.”
- “Hot showers may temporarily relieve symptoms, but they do not treat
the underlying cause.”
- “We can support you with hydration, anti-sickness medications, and
counselling to help stop cannabis use.”
- “If you notice persistent vomiting, dizziness, fainting, blood in vomit, or
confusion, seek medical attention immediately.”

-- 211 of 437 --

185- Bilateral Giant Cell Arteritis
(GCA)
Patient Details & Red Flags
- Age >50, more common in women
- Symptoms often bilateral if both
temporal arteries are affected
Red Flags:
- New-onset headache
(temporal/occipital, often bilateral in
this case)
- Jaw claudication
- Scalp tenderness (pain when
combing hair)
- Visual symptoms: transient loss,
double vision, blurring, curtain
coming down
- Systemic: fever, weight loss,
malaise, night sweats
- Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR)
association: shoulder/hip stiffness
Focused History:
- Headache: onset, duration, location, severity, character, bilateral
or unilateral
- Visual symptoms: blurring, transient or permanent loss, double
vision
- Jaw claudication: pain when chewing
- Scalp tenderness (touching hair or pillow)
- Systemic symptoms: fatigue, fever, weight loss, night sweats
- PMR features: morning stiffness in shoulders/hips, difficulty
raising arms or standing
- Impact: daily life, fear of sight loss
- Ideas/Concerns/Expectations: patient may fear “brain tumour” or
“going blind”; may expect urgent treatment.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
“Your symptoms suggest inflammation in the blood vessels in your
head — we call this giant cell arteritis. Because it affects the arteries
that supply your eyes and brain, it’s important to treat straight away
to protect your eyesight.”
1. Emergency Action:
- If visual symptoms → immediate same-day referral to hospital
(ophthalmology/rheumatology).
- Start high-dose steroids urgently (don’t wait for tests): Prednisolone
40–60 mg daily. If acute visual loss → IV methylprednisolone may be
required in hospital.
2. Investigations:
- Bloods: ESR, CRP, FBC, LFTs
- Temporal artery biopsy (within 1 week of starting steroids)
- Consider ultrasound of temporal arteries (halo sign)
3. Symptom Control & Monitoring:
- Protect bone health: calcium + vitamin D, consider bisphosphonates,
arrange DEXA scan.
- Monitor blood pressure, glucose, weight (due to steroid side effects).
4. Safety Netting (SCA phrases):
- “If you suddenly lose vision, even for a few seconds, or develop new
severe headache or jaw pain, go to A&E immediately — it could mean the
arteries are becoming more inflamed.”
- “We’ll start treatment straight away because delay could risk permanent
sight loss.”
5. Referral:
- Urgent same-day referral to rheumatology/ophthalmology.

-- 212 of 437 --

186- Premature ejaculation
Patient Details & Red Flags
- Most common male sexual
dysfunction.
- Defined as ejaculation within ~1
min of penetration or before the
person wishes, with associated
distress.
- Red Flags / Differential to exclude:
- Erectile dysfunction
- Prostatitis / pelvic pathology
- Neurological disease
- Hypothyroidism / diabetes
Focused History:
- Onset: lifelong vs acquired
- Timing: how quickly ejaculation occurs (intravaginal ejaculation
latency time)
- Impact: on patient, partner, relationship
- Associated issues: erectile dysfunction, low libido, pelvic pain
- Psychological factors: anxiety, stress, relationship issues
- Medical history: thyroid disease, diabetes, prostate disease
- Drug history: antidepressants, recreational drugs, alcohol use
- Ideas/Concerns/Expectations: embarrassed, worried about
partner satisfaction, may expect “quick fix tablets.”
Explanation in Lay Terms:
“This is one of the most common sexual concerns in men. It happens
when climax happens sooner than you or your partner would like,
which can affect confidence and relationships. It’s not dangerous,
but I understand it can be very distressing. There are both
behavioural techniques and medicines that can help.”
1. Rule out organic causes:
- Examine if needed (genital exam if prostatitis suspected).
- Consider thyroid function, diabetes screening if indicated.
2. First-line Management:
- Education & reassurance: very common, not harmful.
- Behavioural techniques: pause–squeeze, stop–start, using thicker
condoms.
- Psychosexual therapy: referral if relationship/psychological issues.
3. Medication Options (if persistent):
- Topical anaesthetic sprays/creams (e.g., lidocaine/prilocaine).
- Off-label SSRIs (e.g., sertraline, paroxetine, dapoxetine [on-demand
SSRI not widely used in UK]) → discuss side effects (nausea, reduced
libido, erectile issues).
4. Safety Netting (SCA phrases):
- “This condition is not harmful to your health, but if you develop pain,
blood in semen, difficulty passing urine, or erectile problems, let us
know.”
- “If you and your partner feel it is causing strain or distress, we can
involve a sexual health or psychosexual counsellor.”
5. Referral:
- Psychosexual therapy if conservative/medical measures fail.
- Urology if secondary causes suspected.

-- 213 of 437 --

187- PSA request
Definition: The prostate-specific
antigen (PSA) test measures a
protein made by the prostate. It can
be raised in prostate cancer, but also
in benign conditions (BPH, prostatitis,
UTI).
Man requesting PSA test
(often well man, age >50,
asymptomatic, wants reassurance)
Red flags not to miss:
- LUTS (frequency, urgency, nocturia,
hesitancy, poor stream, incomplete
emptying)
- Haematuria (visible or non-visible)
- Bone pain, weight loss, anorexia
- History of prostate/breast cancer in
family
Focused History:
- Why patient is requesting PSA (e.g., friend had cancer, media,
family history).
- Any urinary symptoms? (frequency, nocturia, urgency, poor stream,
hesitancy, retention, incontinence).
- Red flag features: visible haematuria, weight loss, bone pain.
- FHx: prostate/breast cancer.
- PMHx: prostate enlargement, prostatitis, recent UTI, recent
ejaculation (can affect PSA).
- DHx: anticoagulants/antiplatelets (in case biopsy needed).
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “The PSA test is a blood test that looks for a protein made by the
prostate gland.”
- “It’s important to know it’s not perfect – it can be raised when there
isn’t cancer, and normal even when cancer is present in 1:4”
- “If it comes back raised, you may need further tests like MRI or
biopsy, which have risks.”
- “Sometimes, finding prostate cancer early may help, but some
cancers grow so slowly they never cause harm – and treatment can
carry side effects like incontinence or erection problems.”
- “So it’s about balancing the pros and cons and making the choice
that’s right for you.”
Management:
1. Explore motivation – “Can you tell me what made you want the test
today?”
2. Shared decision making – discuss pros and cons clearly (see above).
- Pros: may detect prostate cancer early, reassurance if normal.
- Cons: false positives → anxiety & unnecessary biopsy; false negatives →
false reassurance; possible overdiagnosis of indolent cancer.
3. Best practice approach:
- If symptomatic/red flag → refer on 2WW for suspected prostate cancer
(not just PSA).
- If asymptomatic and well → offer information leaflet (Prostate Cancer
UK / NHS PSA decision aid) and allow time to decide.
- If after informed decision he still wants PSA → arrange blood test.
- Before PSA test: Avoid ejaculation, vigorous exercise, or cycling for 48
hrs; avoid if UTI/prostatitis present.
4. Safety netting phrases:
- “If you notice new symptoms like blood in urine, new back or bone pain,
or weight loss, please come back straight away.”
- “If your PSA is raised, we may need to refer you to a specialist for further
tests.”
5. Documentation:
- Document informed consent and counselling clearly.

-- 214 of 437 --

188- LUTS in men
Male with LUTS (Lower Urinary Tract
Symptoms)
(usually >50y, often due to benign
prostatic hyperplasia [BPH])
Red flags not to miss:
- Visible or unexplained haematuria
- Recurrent UTIs
- Palpable bladder (retention)
- Hard/irregular prostate on DRE
- Weight loss, bone pain (mets)
- Neurological symptoms (cauda
equina: leg weakness, saddle
anaesthesia, incontinence)
Focused History:
- Symptoms: voiding (weak stream, hesitancy, straining, dribbling,
incomplete emptying), storage (frequency, urgency, nocturia,
incontinence).
- Duration & impact: how much does it bother daily life, sleep, work,
travel?
- Red flags: blood in urine, recurrent infections, pain, weight loss,
bone pain, retention, neuro features.
- PMHx: diabetes, prostate issues, UTIs, neurological disease.
- FHx: prostate cancer.
- DHx: medications affecting bladder/prostate (anticholinergics,
diuretics).
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “These symptoms are often caused by the prostate, a small gland
at the base of the bladder that gets bigger as men get older.”
- “This enlargement is usually benign and called BPH – benign
prostatic hyperplasia. It can narrow the water pipe and affect flow.”
- “Not all urinary problems in men are due to the prostate, so we also
check for other causes like infection, bladder issues, or rarely,
cancer.”
- “The important thing is to assess how much it affects your life and
rule out anything serious.”
1. Initial investigations & assessment:
- Urine dipstick (infection/haematuria).
- Bloods: U&Es (if renal concern).
- PSA (after counselling if appropriate).
- DRE.
- Bladder diary, frequency-volume chart.
- IPSS questionnaire
2. First-line management:
- Lifestyle advice: reduce caffeine/alcohol, avoid late fluids, double
voiding, timed voiding.
- “These small changes can make a big difference to your symptoms.”
3. Pharmacological options:
- α-blocker (tamsulosin/alfuzosin): “relaxes the muscles around the
bladder and prostate, making it easier to pass urine.”
- 5-α reductase inhibitor (finasteride): “shrinks the prostate over months
– helpful if prostate is large.”
- Combination therapy if severe.
- Antimuscarinic if overactive bladder
- Loop diuretic or desmopressin if nocturnal polyuria
4. Referral:
- Urgent 2WW if red flags.
- Routine urology if poor response to treatment, significant bother,
recurrent retention/UTI, renal impairment.
5. Safety netting phrases:
- “If you suddenly can’t pass urine, that’s an emergency – go straight to
A&E.”
- “If you notice blood in your urine, worsening infections, or new
back/bone pain, please come back urgently.”
- “We’ll review your symptoms in a few months to see if the treatment is
helping.”
6. Shared decision making:
- “We can start with lifestyle changes alone, or add in tablets depending
on how much the symptoms bother you. What feels right for you?”
- Give patient info leaflet (NICE / Prostate Cancer UK).

-- 215 of 437 --

189- Acute Prostatitis
(usually younger/middle-aged man
with acute urinary symptoms,
systemic illness)
Red flags not to miss:
- Sepsis: fever, rigors, confusion,
tachycardia
- Acute urinary retention
- Bacteraemia/urosepsis
- Perineal/rectal pain, tender boggy
prostate
- Immunocompromised patient
Differential Diagnoses:
- UTI (cystitis, pyelonephritis)
- Acute urinary retention from BPH
- STI (chlamydia, gonorrhoea
urethritis)
- Chronic prostatitis / chronic pelvic
pain syndrome
- Prostate abscess
- Prostate cancer (less likely acute)
Risk Factors:
- Recent urethral instrumentation or
catheterisation
- Recurrent UTIs
- Immunosuppression (e.g. diabetes,
HIV)
- Unprotected sexual intercourse
(STIs)
- Enlarged prostate / obstructive
uropathy
Focused History:
- Urinary symptoms: dysuria, frequency, urgency, weak stream,
incomplete emptying.
- Pain: perineal, suprapubic, rectal, lower back.
- Systemic features: fever, chills, malaise, nausea/vomiting.
- Sexual history: recent unprotected sex (consider STI – esp.
chlamydia, gonorrhoea).
- Past history: UTIs, prostate disease, instrumentation (catheter,
cystoscopy).
- Drug history / allergies: esp. antibiotics.
- Red flags: inability to pass urine, confusion, severe sepsis features.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “The prostate is a small gland just below the bladder. Sometimes
bacteria can infect it, causing pain, difficulty passing urine, and fever
– that’s called acute prostatitis.”
- “It’s different from prostate cancer. This is an infection that needs
urgent treatment with antibiotics, and you should start to feel better
in a few days.”
- “Because it can make you quite unwell, we take it seriously and
keep an eye out for sepsis.”
1. Initial Investigations:
- Urine dipstick & MSU for culture.
- Bloods: FBC, U&Es, CRP, blood cultures if febrile.
- Refer to GUM clinic for STI swabs/NAAT if risk.
- Avoid prostatic massage (risk of bacteraemia).
2. Antibiotic treatment (per NICE):
- First-line: Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD 14 days (then review, may extend to
28 days) OR
- Ofloxacin 200mg BD 14 days OR
- Trimethoprim 200mg BD 14 days (if sensitivities known).
3. Supportive management:
- Analgesia (paracetamol/NSAID if suitable).
- Encourage fluids and rest.
- Stool softeners if constipation (to reduce straining).
4. Referral / Admission if:
- Systemically unwell/septic, unable to tolerate oral antibiotics, acute
retention, immunocompromised.
5. Safety netting phrases:
- “If you develop shaking chills, confusion, vomiting, or can’t pass urine,
you must go to A&E immediately – these are signs of serious infection.”
- “We’ll check your urine and bloods, and I’d like to review you in 48 hours
to make sure you’re improving.”
- “If the symptoms don’t settle after antibiotics, we may need to refer you
to urology for further tests.”
6. Follow-up:
- Review at 48 hours and at end of course.
- Consider repeat MSU.
- Discuss STI screening if risk factors.

-- 216 of 437 --

190- Chronic Prostatitis
Chronic Prostatitis / Chronic Pelvic
Pain Syndrome
(≥3 months pelvic/prostatic pain,
often recurrent urinary symptoms,
negative cultures)
Red flags not to miss:
- Haematuria (visible or persistent
microscopic)
- Recurrent UTIs with fever/chills
(consider acute prostatitis, prostate
abscess)
- Urinary retention
- Weight loss, bone pain (exclude
malignancy)
Differential Diagnoses (DDx):
- Recurrent UTI / cystitis
- Chronic bacterial prostatitis
- Non-bacterial prostatitis (chronic
pelvic pain syndrome)
- Urethritis (STI: chlamydia,
gonorrhoea, trichomonas)
- Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)
- Bladder pathology (stones, cancer)
- Prostate cancer
Risk Factors (RFs):
- Previous acute prostatitis
- Recurrent UTIs
- Pelvic floor dysfunction
- Unprotected sexual activity / STI risk
- Enlarged prostate / obstructive
uropathy
- Psychological stress, anxiety
(exacerbates pelvic pain)
Focused History:
- Symptoms: chronic pelvic/perineal/testicular/low back pain (≥3
months), worse after sitting.
- Urinary symptoms: frequency, urgency, weak stream, hesitancy,
post-micturition dribble.
- Sexual symptoms: painful ejaculation, erectile dysfunction.
- Systemic features: usually absent (distinguishes from acute
prostatitis).
- Impact: quality of life, mood, anxiety.
- Past history: recurrent UTIs, acute prostatitis, instrumentation,
STIs.
- Drug history: any antibiotics used before, analgesia, α-blockers.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “You’ve been having symptoms for several months, and from what
you’ve described, this fits with something called chronic prostatitis.
There are two main types: one caused by repeated infection, and one
where no clear infection is found but the prostate and pelvic
muscles remain inflamed or tense – we call that chronic pelvic pain
syndrome.”
- “It’s not prostate cancer, but it can be very uncomfortable and
affect your day-to-day life.”
- “Treatment is about controlling symptoms, reducing flare-ups, and
supporting you long-term.”
1. Investigations:
- Urine dip & culture (pre- and post-prostatic massage sample if
specialist).
- STI screen if risk.
- Bloods: FBC, U&Es, CRP, PSA if indicated (not during flare, as it may be
falsely elevated).
2. Medical management:
- If chronic bacterial: offer antibiotics (fluoroquinolone e.g.,
ciprofloxacin 4–6 weeks) guided by culture.
- If non-bacterial (chronic pelvic pain syndrome): avoid unnecessary
antibiotics.
- α-blockers (e.g., tamsulosin) may help voiding symptoms.
- Analgesia: NSAIDs or paracetamol for pain relief.
3. Supportive / non-drug management:
- Pelvic floor physiotherapy, warm baths, relaxation techniques.
- Lifestyle: avoid alcohol, caffeine, spicy food (can worsen symptoms).
- Psychological support (stress/anxiety often worsen pain).
4. Referral:
- Urology if severe, persistent, or diagnostic uncertainty.
- Consider pain clinic if refractory pain.
5. Safety netting phrases:
- “If you develop fever, shaking chills, or cannot pass urine, you must seek
urgent medical help.”
- “If you notice blood in the urine or new unexplained pain, come back
promptly.”
- “We’ll review how you’re doing in 4–6 weeks and adjust treatment as
needed.”

-- 217 of 437 --

191- Infertility
Infertility
(Failure to conceive after ≥12 months
of regular unprotected intercourse)
Differential Diagnoses (DDx):
- Female: PCOS, endometriosis, tubal
factor (adhesions, blocked tubes),
ovulatory dysfunction, premature
ovarian insufficiency, thyroid
dysfunction, hyperprolactinaemia
- Male: abnormal semen parameters
(oligo/azoospermia), varicocele,
hormonal imbalance, genetic causes
- Combined male + female factor
- Unexplained infertility
Risk Factors (RFs):
- Age (female fertility declines >35)
- BMI <19 or >30 (anovulation,
reduced sperm quality)
- Smoking, alcohol, recreational
drugs (e.g., cannabis, anabolic
steroids)
- STIs, PID, tubal surgery
- Mumps orchitis, testicular trauma,
chemotherapy/radiotherapy
Focused History (ask couple if possible):
- Female partner: cycle regularity, dysmenorrhoea, dyspareunia,
past pelvic infection/surgery, contraceptive history, cervical smear
up to date, obstetric history (miscarriages, ectopics).
- Male partner: mumps, orchitis, testicular trauma, undescended
testes, surgery, erectile/ejaculatory problems.
- Both: frequency & timing of intercourse, previous fertility (children
with current/other partners).
- Lifestyle: BMI, smoking, alcohol, drugs, exercise.
- PMHx/DHx: thyroid disease, diabetes, medications (e.g.,
antipsychotics, chemo).
- Family history: premature menopause, genetic disorders.
Red Flags not to miss:
- Amenorrhoea or very irregular cycles (suggesting anovulation, e.g.,
PCOS, premature ovarian insufficiency)
- Severe dysmenorrhoea or deep dyspareunia (endometriosis)
- History of pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) or tubal surgery
(adhesions/tubal factor)
- Testicular lumps, pain, or history of undescended testes (male
factor)
- Systemic features: weight loss, night sweats, galactorrhoea
(pituitary disease)
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Infertility means not being able to get pregnant after a year of
regular unprotected sex.”
- “This doesn’t always mean you can’t have children – many couples
conceive with support.”
- “There can be several causes: sometimes it’s the woman (e.g.,
ovulation issues, blocked tubes), sometimes the man (sperm
problems), and sometimes both.”
- “We usually start with some tests for both partners to try to find the
reason.”
- “Lifestyle factors like weight, smoking, and alcohol can also play a
big role – small changes can improve fertility chances.”
Management:
1. Initial steps in primary care:
- Offer preconception advice: folic acid 400 mcg, healthy diet, exercise,
avoid smoking/alcohol/drugs.
- Address weight: “Even a 5–10% weight reduction can help ovulation and
fertility.”
2. Investigations (after ≥12 months, earlier if red flags or woman ≥36):
- Female: day 21 progesterone (to confirm ovulation if cycles 21–35 days),
thyroid function, prolactin; Chlamydia screen; consider AMH if referred +
consider FSH/LH/TSH/Prolactin
- Male: semen analysis (after 2–7 days abstinence).
3. Referral criteria:
- Refer earlier if: woman >36, history of amenorrhoea, oligo/amenorrhoea,
pelvic inflammatory disease, chemotherapy, undescended testes,
azoospermia, or abnormal semen analysis.
- Refer both partners together to fertility clinic if ≥12 months trying and no
success.
4. Secondary care management (explain to patient):
- Ovulation induction (clomifene, letrozole) if anovulatory.
- Surgical treatment for endometriosis/tubal disease.
- Assisted conception: IVF, ICSI.
5. Safety netting phrases:
- “Please come back sooner if your periods stop, if you have very painful
periods, or if your partner notices any testicular changes.”
- “We’ll arrange some initial blood and semen tests and review the results
together.”
- “If needed, we’ll get you both referred to a fertility specialist for further
help.”

-- 218 of 437 --

192- Post-orchiectomy
Causes:
- Testicular torsion (emergency)
- Testicular cancer (common in young
men, curable if caught early)
- Trauma/infection
Red Flags not to miss:
- New testicular mass or swelling in
remaining testis
- Persistent pain/swelling at surgical
site
- Weight loss, night sweats, bone pain
(cancer spread)
- Psychological distress, depression,
anxiety, concerns re: body image
Differential Diagnoses (DDx):
- Recurrence/new malignancy in
contralateral testis
- Hydrocele, epididymo-orchitis
- Post-surgical complications
(infection, haematoma, hernia)
Risk Factors (RFs):
- History of undescended testes
- Family history of testicular cancer
- Prior testicular cancer
- Infertility/subfertility
Focused History:
- Reason for orchiectomy: torsion, cancer, trauma.
- Recovery: pain, wound healing, infection signs.
- Urinary or sexual symptoms: libido, erectile function, ejaculation.
- Fertility concerns: children desired in future? sperm banking
discussed?
- Psychological impact: embarrassment, low mood, masculinity
concerns, body image.
- Family history: testicular/breast cancer.
- For torsion in children/adolescents: screen for contralateral
orchidopexy (often done at surgery).
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “The surgery removed one testicle, but the remaining one is usually
enough to produce hormones and sperm.”
- “This means fertility and sexual function are often unaffected,
though some men may notice reduced fertility.”
- “If the operation was for cancer, regular follow-up is important to
check there is no recurrence.”
- “If the operation was for torsion, the other testicle is usually fixed in
place to stop it twisting in the future.”
- “A prosthetic testicle can be fitted later if desired for appearance.”
Management:
1. Physical recovery & wound care:
- Check wound for infection, offer analgesia.
- Advise scrotal support, avoid heavy lifting initially.
2. Cancer pathway (if indication was malignancy):
- Refer to urology/oncology MDT.
- Regular surveillance with tumour markers, imaging.
- Offer fertility counselling / sperm banking if not done.
3. Fertility & hormones:
- Remaining testis usually sufficient.
- If hypogonadal symptoms (low libido, fatigue) → check testosterone, LH,
FSH.
- Consider semen analysis if fertility concerns.
4. Psychological support:
- Offer counselling/psychological support.
- Offer prosthetic testis if body image concerns.
5. Safety Netting:
- “If you notice any new lump, pain, or swelling in the remaining testicle,
come back urgently.”
- “If you develop symptoms like weight loss, persistent back pain, or night
sweats, we’ll need to check you promptly.”
SCA Phrases:
- “The good news is, the remaining testicle usually produces enough
hormones and sperm for normal function.”
- “We’ll keep you under regular follow-up to make sure everything is
healing well and there are no complications.”
- “There are options like a prosthetic testicle if appearance is worrying
you.”
- “It’s completely normal to feel anxious or low after this kind of surgery,
and support is available.”

193- Requesting a prosthetic testis
Context:
- Post-orchiectomy (torsion, cancer,
trauma, undescended testis)
- Adult or adolescent seeking
cosmetic/psychological reassurance
Red Flags not to miss:
- Symptoms in remaining testis (new
lump, swelling, pain)
- Systemic cancer symptoms (weight
loss, back pain, night sweats)
- Significant psychological distress,
depression, body image concerns
DDx:
- True prosthetic request vs
reassurance about fertility/hormones
- Hypogonadism (low testosterone
symptoms)
- Infertility concerns
Focused History:
- Reason for orchiectomy (torsion, malignancy, trauma).
- Timing since surgery and wound healing.
- Motivation for prosthesis: body image, confidence, psychological
wellbeing, partner concerns.
- Sexual function/fertility: libido, erections, fertility aspirations.
- Mood and psychosocial impact.
- Expectations: size, feel, cosmetic outcome.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “A testicular prosthesis is an artificial implant placed in the
scrotum to restore the normal appearance.”
- “It won’t replace the function of the testicle – it’s purely cosmetic –
but most men find it helps with confidence and body image.”
- “The size can usually be matched to your other testis.”
- “As with any operation, there are small risks like infection,
movement, or pain.”
- “It doesn’t affect your hormones or fertility.”
Management:
1. Explore reasons and expectations: “Can you tell me what’s made you
consider a prosthesis now?”
2. Information giving:
- Cosmetic only, no hormonal/fertility role.
- Usually silicone, comes in different sizes.
- Minor surgical procedure, usually under general anaesthesia.
- Risks: infection, extrusion, discomfort, asymmetry.
3. Options:
- Referral to urology for assessment and counselling.
- Offer psychological support if body image/low mood concerns.
4. Fertility & hormone check (if relevant):
- “Would you like us to check your testosterone or semen analysis if you’re
planning for children?”
5. Safety netting phrases:
- “If you notice new pain, swelling or lump in your remaining testicle,
please seek help urgently.”
- “If you develop fever, redness, or discharge after surgery, that could
mean infection and needs review.”
SCA phrases:
- “Many men choose a prosthesis for cosmetic reasons, and it often helps
with confidence.”
- “It won’t affect how your body works, but it will restore the look and feel.”
- “We’ll arrange referral to urology so you can discuss size, type, and
timing in more detail.”
