194- Anal fissure
Anal Fissure: It is a tear or ulcer in
the lining of the anal canal which
causes pain on defecation. Acute if <
6 weeks
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Haemorrhoids
- Perianal abscess/fistula
- Crohn’s disease (perianal)
- Anal cancer (rare)
Red Flags:
- Rectal bleeding with weight loss,
altered bowel habit, FHx of bowel
cancer
- Non-healing fissure >6 weeks
- Multiple or off-midline fissures
(think Crohn’s, HIV, TB, syphilis, anal
cancer)
- Severe pain + systemic features
(abscess, sepsis)
Risk Factors:
- Chronic constipation/straining
- Hard stools
- Low fibre diet
- Pregnancy & postpartum
- Repeated diarrhoea (IBD, IBS)
- Anal intercourse
- Younger adults (more common, due
to harder stools)
Focused History:
- Pain: sharp/stabbing pain on defecation, lasting minutes–hours.
- Bleeding: small amount of bright red blood on toilet paper/stool
surface.
- Chronicity: acute (<6 weeks) vs chronic (>6 weeks).
- Bowel habits: constipation, hard stools, straining, diarrhoea.
- Associated features: discharge, lumps, systemic (weight loss,
fever, change in bowel habit).
- PMH: IBS, IBD, previous fissures.
- Psychosocial impact: fear of defecation, dietary restriction, effect
on quality of life.
DRE is not recommended in primary care
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “This is an anal fissure, a tiny tear in the skin at the back passage.”
- “It causes pain and sometimes bright red blood when you open
your bowels.”
- “It’s common, especially if stools are hard or you’ve been
straining.”
- “Most heal with stool softening and creams, but if not, stronger
treatments are available.”
Management:
1. Conservative (first-line):
- Increase dietary fibre & fluids.
- Bulk-forming/softening laxatives (e.g., lactulose, Movicol).
- Sitz baths (warm baths after passing stool).
- Simple analgesia, topical anaesthetic gel (lidocaine).
2. Medical therapy (if persistent/chronic):
- If symptoms > 1 weeks, add GTN ointment BD for 6-8 weeks (avoid in
children, pregnancy or breastfeeding) and discuss side effects as
headache then review and refer to 2ry care if no improvement
- Topical diltiazem (unlicensed, specialist use).
3. Referral:
- non-healing fissure (>6 weeks) or red flags → colorectal referral (rule out
malignancy/IBD, EUA).
- In children refer after 2 weeks if no improvement
- If any scarring or tags – refer to 2ry care
Surgical therapy (if conservative/medical therapy fails):
- Lateral internal sphincterotomy: small cut in internal anal sphincter to
reduce resting pressure and allow healing.
- Usually outpatient, quick recovery.
- Risks: minor incontinence of flatus/liquid stool (rare), bleeding, infection.
- Very high success rate (~90–95%).
SCA phrases: “If the fissure doesn’t heal after these measures, we may
consider a small operation called a sphincterotomy.”
- “This procedure relaxes the muscle around the fissure so it can heal
more easily. Most people recover quickly.”
4. Safety netting:
- “If bleeding persists, or you notice weight loss, altered bowel habit, or
worsening pain, return promptly.”
- “If severe swelling, fever, or pus occurs, seek urgent help – this could
mean an abscess.”
SCA phrases:
- “This is very common and usually heals with simple measures.”
- “We’ll focus on softening your stools and reducing strain.”
- “If it doesn’t improve, we have ointments that help relax the muscle and
promote healing.”
- “If it’s still not healing, I’d refer you to a bowel specialist to check and
offer further treatment.”

-- 221 of 437 --

195- Haemorrhoids
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Anal fissure
- Perianal abscess/fistula
- Rectal prolapse
- Anal cancer (rare)
Red Flags:
- Rectal bleeding with weight loss,
altered bowel habit, FHx of colorectal
cancer
- Painful lump with fever (think
abscess)
- Thrombosed external haemorrhoid
with severe pain lasting >1–2 weeks
- Persistent or recurrent prolapsing
haemorrhoids
Risk Factors:
- Constipation/straining
- Low-fibre diet
- Pregnancy & postpartum
- Obesity
- Prolonged sitting
- Older age
Focused History:
- Bleeding: bright red blood on toilet paper, dripping into toilet, not
mixed with stool.
- Pain: external haemorrhoids or thrombosed haemorrhoids can be
tender; internal usually painless.
- Prolapse: internal haemorrhoid protruding from anus, may reduce
spontaneously.
- Associated symptoms: discharge, itching, swelling.
- Bowel habits: constipation, straining, diarrhoea.
- PMH: previous haemorrhoids, IBD, surgery.
- Psychosocial impact: embarrassment, avoidance of defecation,
dietary restrictions.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Haemorrhoids are like varicose veins in your back passage—they
can bleed, itch, or bulge out.”
- “Internal haemorrhoids usually aren’t painful, but external ones
can be tender, especially if a clot forms.”
- “They’re very common and often related to straining or
constipation.”
- “Most can be managed without surgery, but severe or persistent
cases may need procedures.”
1. Conservative (first-line):
- Increase fibre intake & fluids.
- Avoid straining, good toileting habits.
- Consider laxatives
- Sitz baths (warm water).
- Topical emollients or mild steroid cream for itching.
- Simple analgesia for pain.
- If related to anal sex: advice to delay anal sex until healed
2. Office-based procedures (if persistent/prolapsed):
- Rubber band ligation (internal haemorrhoids).
- Sclerotherapy or infrared coagulation.
3. Surgery (if severe/refractory):
- Haemorrhoidectomy (for grade 3–4 or thrombosed haemorrhoids).
- Stapled haemorrhoidopexy (select cases).
4. Referral:
- Red flag features → colorectal referral.
- Non-healing, recurrent, or complicated haemorrhoids.
5. Safety netting:
- “If you notice persistent bleeding, clots, severe pain, or systemic
symptoms, come back promptly.”
- “If bleeding changes character or you have weight loss or altered bowel
habit, we’ll investigate further.”
SCA phrases:
- “Most haemorrhoids improve with dietary fibre, fluid intake, and avoiding
straining.”
- “If necessary, we have simple procedures to treat persistent
haemorrhoids without major surgery.”
- “Severe cases can be treated surgically, but that’s only if other measures
fail.”
- “It’s common, and we’ll make sure to monitor you closely for any
warning signs.”

-- 222 of 437 --

196- Recurrent UTI in woman
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Fever, rigors, flank pain (possible
pyelonephritis)
- Vomiting or systemic symptoms
- Gross haematuria or persistent
microscopic haematuria
- Recurrent infections despite
antibiotics
- Known diabetes,
immunosuppression, or structural
renal/urinary tract abnormality
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Urolithiasis (stones)
- Diabetes mellitus
- Anatomical abnormalities
(vesicoureteral reflux,
postmenopausal atrophy)
- Vaginitis (Candida, bacterial
vaginosis)
- Interstitial cystitis / chronic pelvic
pain
- Catheter-associated UTI
Focused History:
- Symptoms: dysuria, frequency, urgency, haematuria, suprapubic
pain.
- Frequency: number of UTIs per year, response to antibiotics.
- Triggers: sexual activity, menstruation, hygiene, contraceptive use
(spermicides, diaphragm).
- Associated symptoms: fever, flank pain, nausea/vomiting.
- Past investigations: urine cultures, imaging.
- PMH: diabetes, renal disease, urinary tract surgery.
- Medications: recent antibiotics, immunosuppressants.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “You’re having recurrent urinary tract infections, which are
infections in the bladder or urinary tract that keep coming back.”
- “Most are caused by bacteria from the bowel entering the urinary
tract.”
- “We’ll try to find any underlying reason, and we can prevent future
infections with lifestyle advice or preventive antibiotics if needed.”
1. Investigations:
- Urine culture to confirm infection and guide antibiotics.
- Consider renal tract imaging if structural cause suspected.
- Blood glucose if diabetes not excluded.
2. Acute treatment:
- Treat symptomatic UTI with first-line antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin,
trimethoprim).
- Avoid unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotics.
3. Prevention / Lifestyle Measures:
- Increase fluid intake.
- Urinate after sexual intercourse.
- Avoid spermicides, diaphragms if possible.
- Good perineal hygiene (front-to-back wiping).
- Cranberry products / D-mannose (optional, evidence limited).
4. Antibiotic prophylaxis (if frequent, lifestyle measures fail):
- Low-dose prophylactic antibiotic for 3–6 months (e.g., nitrofurantoin at
night).
- Post-coital prophylaxis if UTIs associated with intercourse.
5. Referral:
- Persistent/recurrent UTIs despite prophylaxis.
- Structural abnormality, stone, diabetes, or complicated UTI → refer to
urology.
6. Safety Netting:
- “Return urgently if fever, flank pain, vomiting, or blood in urine
develops—could be a kidney infection.”
- “Seek help if symptoms don’t improve after antibiotics.”
SCA phrases:
- “Recurrent UTIs are common in women and often respond well to
lifestyle measures and antibiotics.”
- “We’ll try to prevent future infections with simple strategies first.”
- “If infections keep recurring, there are options like low-dose preventive
antibiotics and specialist assessment.”
- “It’s important to act promptly if symptoms suggest a kidney infection, as
this can become more serious.”

-- 223 of 437 --

197- Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
(PID)
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- High fever, chills, rigors
- Severe lower abdominal pain
- Vomiting or systemic symptoms
- Signs of peritonitis (rigidity, rebound
tenderness)
- Adnexal mass (possible tubo-
ovarian abscess)
- Pregnancy (rule out ectopic)
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Ectopic pregnancy
- Appendicitis
- Urinary tract infection /
pyelonephritis
- Ovarian cyst rupture / torsion
- Endometriosis
- Gastrointestinal causes (IBD,
diverticulitis)
Risk Factors:
- Multiple sexual partners
- Recent STI (chlamydia, gonorrhoea)
- Unprotected sex
- Previous PID
- IUCD use (especially within first 3
weeks of insertion)
Focused History:
- Symptoms: lower abdominal pain, bilateral or unilateral, often dull
or crampy.
- Vaginal discharge: purulent, malodorous.
- Fever/chills: systemic illness.
- Menstrual history: recent menses, irregular bleeding,
intermenstrual bleeding.
- Sexual history: recent unprotected intercourse, new/multiple
partners.
- Past history: prior STIs, PID, IUCD insertion.
- Associated symptoms: dysuria, dyspareunia, nausea/vomiting.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “PID is an infection of the female reproductive organs, usually
caused by sexually transmitted bacteria like chlamydia or
gonorrhoea.”
- “It can cause lower abdominal pain, fever, and unusual vaginal
discharge.”
- “If untreated, it can affect fertility or lead to abscesses, so it’s
important to treat promptly.”
1. Investigations:
- Pregnancy test to rule out ectopic.
- Vaginal/endocervical swabs for chlamydia, gonorrhoea.
- Urine analysis and culture.
- Bloods: FBC, CRP ± inflammatory markers.
- Pelvic ultrasound if abscess suspected.
2. Acute treatment:
- Outpatient (mild/moderate): oral antibiotics (e.g., doxycycline +
ceftriaxone ± metronidazole).
- Inpatient (severe/systemic, unable to tolerate PO, suspected
abscess): IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone + doxycycline ± metronidazole).
3. Partner management:
- Sexual partners in last 6 months should be tested/treated.
- Advise abstinence until treatment complete.
- Use barrier methods if sex can’t be avoided
4. Follow-up:
- Review in 48–72 hours to ensure improvement.
- Repeat testing for chlamydia/gonorrhoea after 4 weeks.
- Consider referral to gynecology if abscess, poor response, or recurrent
PID.
5. Safety Netting:
- “Return urgently if worsening pain, fever, vomiting, heavy bleeding, or
signs of abscess occur.”
- “Seek immediate help if unable to tolerate fluids or develop severe
systemic symptoms.”
- Signpost to https://www.brook.org.uk/
SCA phrases:
- “PID is usually caused by sexually transmitted bacteria and can affect
fertility if untreated.”
- “We’ll treat you promptly with antibiotics and ensure your partner is
tested and treated too.”
- “Most cases improve quickly with proper treatment, but we need to
monitor for complications like abscesses.”
- “It’s important to avoid sexual activity until both you and your partner
have completed treatment.”

-- 224 of 437 --

198- Genital Herpes Simplex Virus
(HSV)
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Severe pain, urinary retention
- Fever, systemic symptoms, malaise
- Immunocompromised patients (risk
of dissemination)
- Recurrent or non-healing ulcers >3
weeks
- Atypical lesions (consider syphilis,
chancroid, HSV resistant strains)
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Syphilis (chancre)
- Chancroid
- Lymphogranuloma venereum
- Molluscum contagiosum
- Candida infection
- Trauma / mechanical irritation
Risk Factors:
- Multiple sexual partners
- Unprotected intercourse
- Previous STI
- Immunosuppression
- Age <30 years (higher incidence)
Focused History:
- Symptoms: painful genital or perianal ulcers, dysuria, discharge.
- Prodrome: tingling, burning, flu-like symptoms before lesions.
- Onset & recurrence: first episode vs recurrent episodes,
frequency, triggers.
- Sexual history: new/multiple partners, condom use.
- Past STI history: previous HSV or other STIs.
- Systemic symptoms: fever, malaise, lymphadenopathy.
- Psychosocial impact: embarrassment, anxiety, effect on
relationships.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Genital herpes is a common viral infection caused by the herpes
simplex virus.”
- “It causes painful blisters or sores in the genital area and may recur
periodically.”
- “It is transmitted through sexual contact and can be managed with
antiviral medications.”
- “It does not affect fertility, but the virus stays in the body and can
reactivate.”
“HSV is very common and can be present for many years before
causing symptoms. It doesn’t necessarily mean recent sexual
activity outside your relationship. Let’s focus on managing your
symptoms, preventing transmission, and supporting your
relationship through open communication.”
1. Investigations:
- Clinical diagnosis is often sufficient.
- Swab for PCR or viral culture if uncertain.
- Consider serology for type-specific HSV antibodies in selected cases.
2. Acute episode treatment:
- First episode: oral antivirals (acyclovir, valacyclovir, famciclovir) for 7–10
days.
- Recurrent episodes: shorter course (2–5 days) or episodic therapy.
3. Suppressive therapy (if frequent recurrences):
- Daily oral antivirals to reduce recurrence and transmission.
- Consider in patients with ≥6 episodes/year or significant psychosocial
impact.
4. Partner management & counselling:
- Advise disclosure to sexual partners.
- Condoms reduce but do not eliminate transmission risk.
- Abstain from sexual activity during active lesions.
5. Safety netting:
- “Return if lesions do not heal, pain worsens, or systemic symptoms
develop.”
- “Seek urgent care if unable to pass urine, fever, or widespread lesions
occur.”
SCA phrases:
- “Genital herpes is common and usually manageable with antiviral
treatment.”
- “We can treat acute episodes and reduce recurrences with daily
medication if needed.”
- “It’s important to avoid sexual contact during outbreaks and inform
partners.”
- “Most people lead a normal life, and complications are rare if managed
appropriately.”

-- 225 of 437 --

199- Pruritus Vulvae / Suspected
Latex Allergy
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Severe or spreading rash
- Vesicles, erosions, ulceration
- Systemic symptoms: facial swelling,
wheeze, shortness of breath
(anaphylaxis)
- Secondary infection (discharge,
odor, crusting)
- Chronic or recurrent lesions
unresponsive to initial treatment
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Fungal infection (Candida)
- Bacterial infection (staphylococcal,
streptococcal)
- Eczema / dermatitis
- Lichen sclerosus / lichen planus
- Contact allergy (latex, soaps,
lubricants, detergents)
- STI (herpes, trichomoniasis)
Risk Factors:
- Atopy / history of eczema, asthma,
hay fever
- New personal care products,
detergents, soaps
- Condom use (latex)
- Occupation involving latex gloves
- Diabetes / immunosuppression
Focused History:
- Symptoms: itching, burning, redness, swelling, rash.
- Onset & triggers: new products, latex exposure, detergents,
lubricants, condoms.
- Associated features: vaginal discharge, sores, systemic allergy
symptoms (wheeze, lip/tongue swelling).
- Past medical history: atopy, eczema, asthma, diabetes.
- Medication / topical exposure: creams, hygiene products.
- Sexual history: new partners, STIs risk.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Pruritus vulvae means itching of the vulva, often caused by
irritation, allergy, or infection.”
- “Latex allergy can cause local irritation or, rarely, systemic allergic
reactions.”
- “Identifying and avoiding triggers is important to prevent recurrence
and complications.”
1. Identify & avoid triggers:
- Stop use of suspected irritants (latex, soaps, detergents, lubricants).
- Use non-latex condoms and gloves if latex allergy suspected.
- Avoid tight-fitting clothing, synthetic underwear.
2. Symptomatic treatment:
- Topical emollients or barrier creams.
- Mild topical corticosteroids for inflammation (short course).
- Antihistamines for severe itching.
3. Treat secondary infection if present:
- Antifungal (Clotrimazole) if Candida.
- Antibiotics if bacterial infection confirmed.
4. Investigations / Referral:
- Consider patch testing or allergy referral if latex/contact allergy
suspected.
- Gynecology referral if chronic, recurrent, or atypical lesions.
5. Safety netting:
- “Return urgently if you develop swelling of lips, tongue, face, or difficulty
breathing—this could be a serious allergic reaction.”
- “Seek help if rash worsens, spreads, or signs of infection develop.”
SCA phrases:
- “Most vulval itching is due to irritation or minor allergies and responds
well to avoiding triggers and simple creams.”
- “We’ll make sure to identify any potential latex or contact allergy.”
- “If infections develop, we can treat them effectively.”
- “Serious allergic reactions are rare, but you should seek immediate care
if symptoms like facial swelling or breathing difficulty occur.”

200- HPV Screening / Cervical
Screening
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Post-coital or intermenstrual
bleeding
- Unexplained persistent vaginal
discharge
- Pelvic pain or mass
- Previous abnormal smear / CIN 2–3
- Immunosuppression (HIV,
transplant)
Differential Considerations:
- Normal cervical cytology
- Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia
(CIN)
- Cervical cancer
- HPV infection without cytological
abnormality
- Other causes of abnormal vaginal
bleeding (polyps, fibroids,
endometrial pathology)
Risk Factors:
- Early sexual activity
- Multiple sexual partners
- Smoking
- Immunosuppression
- Previous abnormal cervical cytology
Focused History:
- Menstrual history: regularity, intermenstrual or post-coital
bleeding.
- Sexual history: number of partners, condom use, age at first
intercourse.
- Previous screening: last smear, results, follow-up.
- Vaccination history: HPV vaccination status.
- Medical history: immunosuppression, previous STIs.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “HPV screening is part of cervical screening to check for changes
that could lead to cervical cancer.”
- “HPV is a common virus, and most infections clear on their own.”
- “Screening helps detect abnormal cells early, before cancer
develops.”
- “Depending on the result, you may need repeat testing or further
investigation, like a colposcopy.”
“HPV is very common, and many people have it for years without
knowing. Its presence does not necessarily indicate recent sexual
activity outside your relationship. We can focus on managing any
symptoms, preventing transmission, and supporting each other’s
health.”
1. Screening:
- Follow national guidelines for cervical screening (age, frequency).
- Sample collected from cervix for HPV testing and cytology.
2. Follow-up based on result:
- HPV negative / normal cytology: routine recall.
- HPV positive / abnormal cytology: refer for colposcopy (a microscope
to closely examine the cervix (the lower part of the womb) for any
abnormal changes, usually after an abnormal smear test result. ± biopsy.
- Persistent high-risk HPV: monitor or treat per local protocol.
3. Prevention:
- HPV vaccination if eligible.
- Safe sexual practices (condom use, reducing partners).
4. Safety Netting:
- “Return if you notice abnormal bleeding, discharge, or pelvic pain.”
- “If you have new risk factors or immunosuppression, discuss earlier
screening.”
Signpost to Verity - PCOS Charity: https://www.verity-pcos.org.uk/
SCA phrases:
- “Cervical screening detects changes early, often before symptoms
appear.”
- “HPV is very common, and most infections resolve without treatment.”
- “Depending on your results, we may recommend repeat testing or
referral to colposcopy.”
“A colposcopy is a simple procedure where we use a special magnifying
light to look at your cervix closely. It helps us check any abnormal cells
and, if needed, take a small sample for further testing. It’s usually quick,
causes only mild discomfort, and is important for preventing cervical
cancer.”
- “Vaccination and safe sexual practices help reduce the risk of cervical
abnormalities.”

201- Nausea and Vomiting in
Pregnancy (NVP / Hyperemesis
Gravidarum)
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Inability to keep fluids down
(dehydration)
- Weight loss >5% of pre-pregnancy
weight
- Ketones in urine / electrolyte
imbalance
- Vomiting blood or bile
- Fever, abdominal pain, or jaundice
(rule out alternative pathology)
- Signs of thyroid disease or multiple
pregnancy
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Gastroenteritis
- Biliary disease / hepatitis
- Peptic ulcer or reflux
- Pancreatitis
- Hyperthyroidism
- Medication-induced nausea
Risk Factors:
- First pregnancy
- Multiple pregnancy (twins, triplets)
- History of NVP in previous
pregnancy
- Family history of severe NVP /
hyperemesis
- Migraine history
Focused History:
- Symptoms: onset, severity, frequency of nausea/vomiting, triggers.
- Fluid / food intake: ability to tolerate oral intake, dehydration
signs.
- Weight loss: quantify amount and duration.
- Associated symptoms: abdominal pain, headache, jaundice,
dysuria.
- Obstetric history: gestational age, multiple pregnancy, previous
pregnancies with NVP.
- Medication / supplements: iron, vitamins, other drugs.
- Psychosocial impact: inability to work, fatigue, effect on daily life.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Feeling sick in pregnancy is very common, especially in the first
trimester.”
- “Most women have mild nausea, but some develop more severe
vomiting that can cause dehydration or weight loss.”
- “We aim to keep you hydrated, ensure you are getting nutrients,
and prevent complications.”
1. Conservative measures (first-line, mild-moderate):
- Small frequent meals, dry crackers before rising.
- Ginger, acupressure bands, avoiding triggers.
- Adequate hydration; oral rehydration solutions if needed.
2. Pharmacological therapy:
- First-line: oral cyclizine, prochlorperazine, pyridoxine (vitamin B6) ±
doxylamine.
- Other antiemetics: metoclopramide, ondansetron (if severe, specialist
advice).
- Avoid teratogenic drugs (e.g., thalidomide, some antiemetics in early
pregnancy).
3. Severe / Hyperemesis Gravidarum:
- Admission for IV fluids, electrolyte replacement, thiamine
supplementation.
- Monitor weight, ketones, electrolytes, liver function.
- Consider nasogastric feeding if persistent inability to tolerate oral intake.
4. Referral:
- Severe dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, significant weight loss.
- Poor response to outpatient therapy.
5. Safety Netting:
- “Return if you can’t keep fluids down, have persistent vomiting,
dizziness, fainting, or abdominal pain.”
- “Seek urgent care if vomiting blood, black vomit, or signs of infection
occur.”
- Signpost to pregnancy sickness support
https://pregnancysicknesssupport.org.uk/
SCA phrases:
- “Nausea and vomiting are common in early pregnancy, and most women
respond to lifestyle measures and simple medication.”
- “Severe cases may need IV fluids to prevent dehydration and ensure your
safety.”
- “We’ll monitor your weight, hydration, and electrolytes to avoid
complications.”
- “You’re not doing anything wrong—this is a common pregnancy
symptom and we can manage it effectively.”

202- Recurrent miscarriage
Definition:
- Loss of ≥2–3 consecutive
pregnancies before 24 weeks
gestation.
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Recurrent early pregnancy loss (<12
weeks)
- Late pregnancy loss (12–24 weeks)
- Heavy bleeding, severe abdominal
pain
- Signs of infection or coagulopathy
- Underlying systemic disease (e.g.,
thyroid disorder, lupus, diabetes)
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Chromosomal abnormalities
(parental or embryonic)
- Anatomical uterine abnormalities
(septum, fibroids, adhesions)
- Endocrine disorders (thyroid,
diabetes, PCOS)
- Thrombophilias (antiphospholipid
syndrome)
- Immunological / systemic disease
- Infection (rare)
Risk Factors:
- Maternal age >35 years
- Previous miscarriages
- Smoking, alcohol, drug use
- Obesity
- Chronic medical conditions (thyroid
disease, diabetes, autoimmune)
Focused History:
- Pregnancy history: number of miscarriages, gestational age at
loss, any living children.
- Symptoms: pain, bleeding, timing of miscarriage.
- Medical history: endocrine disorders, autoimmune conditions,
thrombophilia, chronic disease.
- Medication / supplements: anticoagulants, teratogenic drugs.
- Lifestyle factors: smoking, alcohol, BMI, stress.
- Family history: recurrent miscarriage, genetic disorders.
- Previous investigations or interventions: karyotyping, imaging,
hysteroscopy, progesterone support.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Recurrent miscarriage means losing more than one pregnancy
consecutively.”
- “Often, miscarriages are due to genetic or structural problems, and
in many cases, the cause is not identifiable.”
- “There are tests and treatments that may help, and we can discuss
options for future pregnancies.”
Management:
1. Investigations: 2ry care options
- Karyotype of parents and/or products of conception.
- Screening for endocrine disorders (TSH, prolactin, diabetes).
- Screening for antiphospholipid syndrome and thrombophilias.
- Pelvic imaging: ultrasound, hysterosalpingogram, or hysteroscopy for
uterine anomalies.
2. Lifestyle advice:
- Optimize BMI, stop smoking, avoid alcohol/drugs.
- Manage chronic medical conditions (thyroid, diabetes).
3. Supportive / Medical therapy:
- Progesterone supplementation in early pregnancy (if indicated).
- Low-dose aspirin ± heparin if antiphospholipid syndrome confirmed.
4. Referral:
- Recurrent miscarriage clinic / specialist obstetrics for further evaluation
and management.
5. Psychological support:
- Counselling for grief, anxiety, or future pregnancy planning.
6. Safety Netting:
- “Return promptly if you have heavy bleeding, severe pain, fever, or signs
of infection.”
- “We will closely monitor your next pregnancy to give the best possible
support.”
Signpost to https://www.miscarriageassociation.org.uk/
SCA phrases:
- “Recurrent miscarriage is challenging, but many couples go on to have a
successful pregnancy.”
- “We can investigate underlying causes and manage risk factors to
improve outcomes.”
- “Support is available for emotional and psychological impact.”
- “Early monitoring and interventions in future pregnancies can help
maximize your chance of success.”

203- Antenatal Counselling
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- High-risk pregnancy (pre-existing
medical conditions: diabetes,
hypertension, heart disease, thyroid,
epilepsy)
- Previous obstetric complications
(preterm birth, pre-eclampsia,
miscarriage, stillbirth, congenital
anomalies)
- Advanced maternal age (>35 years)
- Multiple pregnancy
- Symptoms of current pregnancy
complications (bleeding, pain,
reduced fetal movements)
Differential Considerations / Focus
Areas:
- Normal pregnancy education and
preparation
- Screening for high-risk factors
- Lifestyle, nutrition, and vaccination
status
- Psychosocial support and birth
planning
Focused History / Counselling Topics:
- Maternal history: medical, surgical, obstetric history, medication
use, allergies.
- Lifestyle factors: smoking, alcohol, recreational drugs, exercise,
nutrition, BMI.
- Psychosocial history: support at home, mental health, domestic
issues.
- Current pregnancy: gestational age, symptoms, previous
complications.
- Screening & prevention: discuss antenatal tests, ultrasounds,
blood tests, infectious disease screening (HIV, hepatitis B, syphilis),
genetic screening if indicated.
- Vaccinations: influenza, pertussis, COVID-19 if applicable.
- Birth planning: place of delivery, pain relief preferences, mode of
delivery discussion, breastfeeding intentions.
- Medication and supplements: folic acid, vitamin D, iron,
avoidance of teratogens.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Antenatal counselling is to ensure you and your baby stay healthy
during pregnancy.”
- “We review your medical history, lifestyle, and any risk factors that
could affect pregnancy.”
- “We provide advice on diet, supplements, vaccinations, and what
to expect during labour and delivery.”
- “We also discuss tests and scans that can detect problems early.”
1. Lifestyle & Health Optimisation:
- Healthy diet, adequate fluids, folic acid (400–800 mcg), vitamin D, iron if
required.
- Avoid smoking, alcohol, recreational drugs.
- Encourage safe exercise unless contraindicated.
2. Screening & Investigations:
- Routine bloods: blood group, antibodies, Hb, infections.
- Urine analysis for protein/glucose.
- Ultrasound scans: dating, anomaly, growth scans.
- Additional screening if high-risk (gestational diabetes, pre-eclampsia).
3. Vaccination & Infection Prevention:
- Influenza, pertussis, COVID-19 if indicated.
- Advice on hygiene, food safety, avoiding toxoplasmosis/listeria.
4. Birth & Postnatal Planning:
- Discuss delivery options, pain relief, cord blood banking if relevant.
- Discuss breastfeeding, postnatal support, contraception after birth.
5. Psychological Support:
- Offer support for anxiety, depression, domestic issues.
- Signpost to antenatal classes and counselling if needed.
- Signpost to https://www.tommys.org/
6. Safety Netting:
- “Return promptly if you have vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, fluid
leakage, reduced fetal movements, fever, or any other concerning
symptoms.”
- “Contact us if you have questions or concerns about medications,
supplements, or lifestyle choices.”
SCA Phrases:
- “Antenatal counselling helps us identify any risks early and optimise your
health and your baby’s health.”
- “We’ll discuss tests, scans, and vaccinations to prevent complications.”
- “We also provide guidance on diet, lifestyle, and birth planning.”
- “You can contact the team anytime if you notice worrying symptoms or
have questions about your pregnancy.”

204- Traveller support
Moving in
• “Even if you don’t have a fixed address, we can still register you
and make sure you’re seen when you need care.”
• “I’ll explain things step by step in plain language, and we also
have leaflets and translation services if that helps.
• “We can help you and your family stay up to date with
vaccinations and routine checks, which can prevent bigger
problems later.”
• “We’ll try to give you the same doctor or nurse each time, so
you don’t have to keep repeating your story.”
• “We can arrange for a midwife or health visitor who has
experience working with the Traveller community.”
Moving out
• Encourage patient to register with a new GP practice as soon as
they know their new address.
• Remind them to bring ID and proof of address (but reassure: not
strictly required under NHS rules if difficult).
• Their medical records will be automatically transferred
electronically via NHS systems.
• Provide a sufficient supply of ongoing medication until they’re
settled with a new GP.
• Offer a repeat prescription printout so the new GP knows their
current meds.
• If under hospital care, update details with hospital so they can
transfer care to a new local service.
• Provide the patient with clinic letters / discharge summaries if
possible.
• Give written care plans (e.g. asthma, diabetes, mental health).
• Provide contact details for urgent help (e.g. NHS 111, urgent care
centres).
• Pregnancy: refer to maternity services in new area.
• Children: ensure health visitor & immunisation records transfer.
• Vulnerable patients: liaise with community teams (mental health,
social care).
205- Responding to different emotions
If feeling embarrassed
• “I can see this feels a bit difficult to talk about — that’s
completely understandable. Please don’t worry, many people
experience this, and I’m here to help, not to judge.”
• “Everything we discuss here stays confidential.”
If feeling hesitant
• “I notice you’re a little unsure about starting — that’s absolutely
fine. Take your time, and share as much or as little as you feel
comfortable with.”
• “Would it help if I asked you some gentle questions to get us
started?”
If feeling anxious
• “You seem a bit worried, and that’s very understandable. Would it
help if I explain what we’ll do today step by step?”
• “You’re in control here — we’ll go at your pace.”

206- Postmenopausal woman
requesting testosterone for low libido
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Vaginal bleeding after menopause
(possible endometrial cancer)
- Dyspareunia with
bleeding/discharge (atrophic
vaginitis, malignancy)
- Systemic symptoms: weight loss,
night sweats, bone pain
- Severe mood changes, depression
- Hirsutism / virilisation (exclude
androgen-secreting tumour)
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Hypoactive sexual desire disorder
(HSDD)
- Vaginal atrophy / dryness
(genitourinary syndrome of
menopause, GSM)
- Relationship / psychological issues
- Depression, anxiety, medication
side effects (SSRIs, beta-blockers)
- Hormonal deficiency (estrogen,
testosterone)
- Endocrine causes (thyroid
dysfunction, hyperprolactinaemia)
Risk Factors for Low Libido:
- Menopause (↓ estrogen &
androgens)
- Medications (SSRIs,
antihypertensives)
- Chronic illness, fatigue
- Relationship stress
Focused History:
- Symptoms: onset, duration, impact on quality of life, presence of
arousal/orgasm difficulties.
- Menopausal symptoms: hot flushes, vaginal dryness, sleep
disturbance.
- Psychosocial: relationship quality, stress, mood.
- Past medical history: depression, thyroid disease, cardiovascular
risks, breast cancer history.
- Medication history: SSRIs, antihypertensives, hormonal therapy.
- Sexual history: pain on intercourse, vaginal dryness, partner
factors.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Low sexual desire after menopause is common and can be caused
by hormone changes, vaginal dryness, mood, or other health
issues.”
- “Testosterone can sometimes help, but it’s only considered if other
causes are ruled out and symptoms are significant.”
- “We usually look at this holistically – hormones, vaginal health,
mood, and relationship factors all play a role.”
1. Assessment:
- Exclude reversible causes (thyroid, prolactin, medication review).
- Assess for GSM (atrophic vaginitis) → may need topical estrogen.
- Assess for depression, anxiety, relationship difficulties.
2. First-line management:
- Optimise lifestyle: sleep, exercise, stress reduction.
- Address vaginal dryness: lubricants, vaginal moisturisers, topical
estrogen.
- Review & adjust medications affecting libido.
- Counselling / psychosexual therapy if relationship or psychological
factors present.
3. Testosterone therapy (specialist-initiated):
- NICE: Can be considered in postmenopausal women with HSDD not
explained by other factors.
- Usually offered alongside HRT (unless contraindicated).
- Off-label in UK: use transdermal testosterone (e.g., Testogel in low dose,
adapted from male prep).
- Requires monitoring: baseline testosterone, repeat after 3–6 months,
watch for side effects (acne, hair growth, voice changes).
4. Safety Netting:
- “If you develop new vaginal bleeding, increasing hair growth, voice
changes, or mood changes, let us know.”
- “We’ll reassess your symptoms in a few months to see if treatment is
helping.”
SCA phrases:
- “It’s understandable you want help with this – many women experience it
after menopause.”
- “Testosterone is not a standard licensed treatment for women in the UK,
but specialists sometimes use it in selected cases.”
- “Before starting, we should rule out other causes like vaginal dryness or
mood issues.”
- “If appropriate, I can refer you to a menopause specialist to discuss a
trial of testosterone therapy safely.”

207- PMB while on HRT
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Any vaginal bleeding >12 months
after last natural period (consider
endometrial cancer until proven
otherwise)
- Bleeding after starting or changing
HRT that persists >6 months
- Heavy / persistent bleeding
- Intermenstrual or postcoital
bleeding
- Associated systemic symptoms:
weight loss, night sweats, pelvic pain
- Risk factors: obesity, PCOS,
diabetes, tamoxifen use, FHx
endometrial/ovarian/breast cancer
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Endometrial cancer / hyperplasia
- HRT-related breakthrough bleeding
(common in first 3–6 months)
- Atrophic vaginitis
- Cervical cancer / cervical pathology
- Polyps, fibroids
- Trauma, infection (STI, endometritis)
Focused History:
- Bleeding pattern: when it started, frequency, volume, relation to
starting/changing HRT, duration of HRT use.
- Type of HRT: cyclical vs continuous combined, oestrogen-only,
patches vs tablets.
- Risk factors: BMI, diabetes, PCOS, family history.
- Other symptoms: discharge, pelvic pain, weight loss, systemic
symptoms.
- Cervical screening: up to date?
- Medication history: tamoxifen, anticoagulants.
- Impact on patient: anxiety about cancer, effect on quality of life.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Some women notice bleeding in the first few months after starting
HRT – this can be normal as the womb lining adjusts.”
- “However, any bleeding after the menopause, especially if it’s
persistent or starts after being settled, needs checking to rule out
more serious causes like womb cancer.”
- “We’ll arrange some straightforward tests to be safe.”
1. Initial steps:
- Take a careful history of bleeding + type of HRT.
- Pelvic & speculum exam (look for atrophy, polyps, cervical lesions,
infection).
2. Investigations (refer via PMB pathway if appropriate
3. HRT adjustments (after malignancy excluded):
- Review type/dose/regimen of HRT (e.g., continuous vs sequential).
- Consider changing preparation if irregular bleeding continues.
4. Safety netting:
- “If bleeding continues, gets heavier, or you develop pain or unexpected
symptoms, please come back urgently.”
- “Most causes are benign, but we don’t want to miss anything serious.”
Signpost to Women health concern: https://www.womens-health-
concern.org/
SCA phrases:
- “I understand bleeding after menopause can be worrying.”
- “Some bleeding is expected in the first few months of HRT, but if it
persists or occurs later, we need to investigate further.”
- “The first step is a scan of the womb lining and sometimes a small
sample to rule out cancer.”
- “Once serious causes are excluded, we can review and adjust your HRT
to improve the bleeding pattern.”

208- Contraception in 16 years
Red Flags / Safeguarding Triggers:
- Under 13 (cannot consent – child
protection)
- 14–15 years: can consent if Fraser
competent, but always assess
exploitation risk.
- Age of partner (concerns if
significantly older, coercion,
exploitation)
- Non-consensual activity / abuse /
grooming
- STIs / recurrent emergency
contraception use
- Self-harm, neglect, domestic abuse
Differential Considerations (what
she may really need):
- Routine contraception
- Emergency contraception
- STI testing
- Safeguarding referral
Focused History:
- Sexual activity: consensual? age of partner? coercion?
- Understanding: risk of pregnancy, STIs, options available.
- Past contraception: used before? any side effects? emergency
contraception use?
- Medical history: migraines, clotting disorders, smoking, family
history.
- Menstrual history: LMP, regularity, heavy periods, dysmenorrhoea.
- Psychosocial: school, home, safety, mood.
- Safeguarding: risk of abuse, exploitation, consent issues.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “At 16, you are legally allowed to consent to contraception.”
- “Our conversations are confidential – I won’t share unless I’m
worried you or someone else is at serious risk.”
- “We’ll talk through the safest and most effective options, including
protection against pregnancy and STIs.”
Management:
1. Assess competence & safeguarding:
- Apply Fraser/Gillick principles: young person understands risks,
benefits, alternatives, and consequences.
- Document safeguarding check (age of partner, consent, coercion,
abuse).
- If <13 or safeguarding concerns → escalate appropriately.
- Offer FTF to check weight and BP
2. Contraceptive options:
- Long-acting reversible contraception (LARC): implant, injection, IUS,
IUD (most effective).
- COCP / POP: if medically suitable.
- Barrier methods: condoms for STI protection (essential in young).
3. STI prevention & screening:
- Offer condoms regardless of chosen method.
- Offer chlamydia/gonorrhoea/HIV/syphilis testing.
4. Emergency contraception (if relevant):
- Levonorgestrel pill (within 72 hrs) or ulipristal (within 120 hrs).
- Copper IUD (most effective).
5. Safety netting:
- “Come back if you have side effects, miss pills, or need advice about
pregnancy risk.”
- “If sex ever feels pressured or unsafe, you can always talk to us
confidentially.”
Signpost to contraception choices:
https://www.contraceptionchoices.org/ and
https://www.letstalkaboutit.nhs.uk/contraception
SCA phrases:
- “At your age, you can make your own decisions about contraception if
you understand the pros and cons.”
- “I’ll keep our conversation private unless I’m worried you’re at risk of
harm.”
- “The most effective options are long-acting, like an implant or coil, but
we can discuss what suits you best.”
- “Condoms are still important, as they’re the only way to protect against
infections.”

209- Contraception in Perimenopause
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Irregular or heavy bleeding (rule out
fibroids, polyps, malignancy)
- Intermenstrual or postcoital
bleeding
- Age >50 with persistent cycles (low
risk of pregnancy but still possible)
- Systemic symptoms: weight loss,
fatigue, signs of endocrine disorders
- Personal/family history of
thromboembolism or hormone-
sensitive cancers
Differential Considerations:
- Irregular cycles due to
perimenopause
- Fibroids, endometrial hyperplasia
- Polycystic ovarian syndrome (if
persistent symptoms)
- Hypothyroidism / hyperthyroidism
- Contraception side effects
Focused History:
- Menstrual pattern: cycle length, frequency, heaviness, spotting,
changes over time.
- Contraception history: previous methods, side effects,
adherence.
- Medical history: hypertension, diabetes, migraine with aura, VTE,
breast cancer, liver disease.
- Lifestyle: smoking, BMI, exercise.
- Fertility desire: still wishes to avoid pregnancy?
- Symptoms of perimenopause: hot flushes, night sweats, mood
changes, vaginal dryness.
- Medications: current medications, including HRT or supplements.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “During perimenopause, cycles can become irregular and fertility
decreases, but pregnancy is still possible until menopause is
confirmed.”
- “We can discuss safe and effective contraception options while
also considering your perimenopausal symptoms.”
- “Some contraceptives can also help with heavy periods or
menopausal symptoms.”
1. Contraceptive choice:
- Combined hormonal contraception (COCP / patch / vaginal ring): if no
contraindications and under 50; helps regulate cycles and manage
symptoms.
- Progestogen-only methods: implant, IUS, injection, POP – safe if VTE
risk or over 40–50.
- LARC preferred: if adherence is a concern.
- Barrier methods: condoms for STI protection if new partner.
2. Monitoring & investigations:
- Blood pressure, BMI, smoking status.
- Consider endometrial evaluation if abnormal bleeding persists (>6
months, heavy, intermenstrual).
- Reassess contraception method around age 50–55 as menopause is
reached.
3. Additional benefits:
- Hormonal contraception can reduce heavy bleeding, PMS, and
perimenopausal symptoms.
- IUS can reduce bleeding if fibroids or heavy periods present.
4. Safety netting:
- “Return if you have breakthrough bleeding, worsening headaches, chest
pain, shortness of breath, or leg swelling.”
- “If you think you may be pregnant, come back promptly.”
SCA phrases:
- “Even in perimenopause, pregnancy is possible until you’ve had a year
without periods.”
- “We’ll pick the safest and most effective method for you, considering
your health and lifestyle.”
- “Some options can also help manage symptoms like heavy bleeding or
hot flushes.”
- “We’ll review your method regularly and adjust as you approach
menopause.”

210- Missed pill
Missed Combined Oral
Contraceptive Pill (COCP)
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Multiple missed pills (>2 in one
cycle, especially third week)
- Vomiting/diarrhoea within 2–3 hours
of pill
- Unprotected intercourse during the
pill-free interval or after missed pill
- Signs of pregnancy
Differential Considerations:
- Pill failure (missed doses, vomiting,
interactions)
- Irregular bleeding unrelated to
missed pills
- Drug interactions (antibiotics like
rifampicin, anticonvulsants, St John’s
Wort)
Focused History:
- Number of missed pills: which week of cycle, how many
consecutive pills missed.
- Timing: when last pill taken.
- Other medications / supplements: antibiotics, anticonvulsants,
herbal remedies.
- Symptoms: vomiting, diarrhoea, abnormal bleeding.
- Sexual activity: unprotected intercourse during missed pills.
- Method used previously: adherence pattern, side effects.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Missing pills reduces the effectiveness of your contraception and
increases the risk of pregnancy.”
- “Depending on which week you missed, the advice changes
slightly.”
- “We’ll talk about how to take your pills safely and whether
emergency contraception is needed.”
1. General advice based on missed pill guidance: If 2 or more
- Week 1: take most recent missed pill immediately, continue others, use
backup contraception for 7 days, consider emergency contraception if
unprotected sex in previous 5 days.
- Week 2: take most recent missed pill immediately, continue others,
backup contraception usually not needed if pills taken correctly in
previous 7 days.
- Week 3: depends on regimen: take most recent pill immediately,
continue active pills, start next pack without pill-free interval or use
backup contraception for 7 days; consider emergency contraception if
unprotected sex in previous 5 days.
- If 1 pill missed at any time – take immediately even taking 2 in a day with
no additional measures needed
- If mini pill – condoms until 2 days of established pill use – take the
missed pill as soon as possible and continue as usual
2. Vomiting / diarrhoea: repeat pill or backup contraception as per above.
3. Safety netting:
- “If you miss multiple pills, have unprotected sex, or are worried about
pregnancy, consider emergency contraception promptly.”
- “Return if you develop unexpected bleeding or think you may be
pregnant.”
4. SCA phrases:
- “The risk of pregnancy depends on when the pills were missed and
whether you had unprotected sex.”
- “We’ll use clear instructions to cover the missed pill and maintain
protection.”
- “Emergency contraception can be offered if there’s a significant risk.”
- “Using a backup method for 7 days after a missed pill is important to
maintain effectiveness.”

211- Postpartum contraception
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Recent complications (postpartum
haemorrhage, infection,
thromboembolism)
- Breastfeeding difficulties or
contraindications
- Hypertension, liver disease,
migraine with aura (if considering
estrogen-containing contraception)
- Unprotected intercourse soon after
delivery (risk of early pregnancy)
Differential Considerations:
- Fertility can return as early as 4–6
weeks postpartum
- Choice of contraception influenced
by breastfeeding, medical
comorbidities, and personal
preference
Focused History:
- Delivery details: date, mode, complications.
- Breastfeeding status: exclusive, mixed, formula.
- Medical history: hypertension, VTE risk, migraine, liver disease,
previous thromboembolism.
- Medications: current prescriptions, supplements.
- Sexual activity: current or anticipated resumption.
- Previous contraception experience: side effects, preferences.
- Fertility desire: timing for next pregnancy.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “You can become fertile again soon after giving birth, even if
breastfeeding.”
- “We can discuss contraception that is safe for you and your baby,
taking into account breastfeeding and medical history.”
- “There are several options, and we can choose one that fits your
lifestyle and preferences.”
1. Immediate postpartum (<21 days):
- Progesterone-only methods: POP, implant, injection → safe in
breastfeeding.
- Barrier methods: condoms.
- LARC insertion: IUD/IUS may be inserted after 4–6 weeks, or earlier if
clinician skilled.
2. Combined hormonal contraception (COCP/patch/ring):
- Usually delayed until 6 weeks postpartum if breastfeeding (to avoid
affecting milk supply).
- Earlier if not breastfeeding and low VTE risk.
3. Emergency contraception:
- Levonorgestrel or copper IUD if unprotected intercourse.
4. Counselling:
- Discuss efficacy, side effects, convenience, and impact on
breastfeeding.
- Emphasise adherence with daily pills or timing of injections.
5. Safety netting:
- “Return if you have unexpected bleeding, pain, infection symptoms, or if
you think you might be pregnant.”
- “We can change methods if the current choice is unsuitable or side
effects occur.”
SCA phrases:
- “It’s important to start contraception soon after delivery to avoid an
unplanned pregnancy.”
- “Progesterone-only methods are safe while breastfeeding and effective.”
- “We can review your method at follow-up and adjust if needed.”
- “Barrier methods provide protection against pregnancy and STIs while
you decide on longer-term contraception.”

212- Perimenopausal woman
requesting “menopause blood tests”
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Irregular or heavy bleeding,
intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding
- Bleeding >12 months after last
period (postmenopausal)
- Systemic symptoms: weight loss,
fatigue, pelvic pain
- Personal/family history of hormone-
sensitive cancers (breast,
endometrial)
- Thyroid disease or other endocrine
disorders
Differential Considerations:
- Perimenopause (fluctuating
hormones, irregular cycles)
- Thyroid dysfunction (hyper- or
hypothyroidism)
- Pituitary disorders (prolactinoma)
- Early ovarian insufficiency
- Medication-related menstrual
changes
Focused History:
- Symptoms: hot flushes, night sweats, mood changes, sleep
disturbance, vaginal dryness, irregular cycles.
- Menstrual history: cycle length, frequency, heaviness, spotting,
changes over time.
- Medical history: thyroid disease, breast cancer, cardiovascular
risk factors.
- Medication history: hormonal contraception, HRT, SSRIs,
anticoagulants.
- Family history: early menopause, cancers, endocrine disorders.
- Lifestyle: smoking, alcohol, BMI, stress.
- Fertility / pregnancy desire: any concerns or current
contraception.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “At your age, it’s normal for periods to become irregular as
hormone levels fluctuate – this is perimenopause.”
- “Blood tests are usually not reliable in perimenopause because
hormone levels fluctuate day-to-day.”
- “We mainly diagnose menopause clinically based on symptoms
and cycle changes, not just blood tests.”
1. Investigations:
- Blood tests (FSH, LH, estradiol) usually not required unless symptoms
atypical, early menopause suspected (<45), or irregular bleeding needs
investigation.
- Thyroid function, prolactin if clinically indicated.
2. Symptomatic management:
- Lifestyle: exercise, sleep hygiene, stress reduction.
- Menopausal symptom relief: consider HRT if appropriate (risk/benefit
discussion, contraindications).
- Non-hormonal options: SSRIs/SNRIs for hot flushes, vaginal
moisturisers/lubricants for dryness.
3. Red flag assessment:
- Rule out abnormal uterine bleeding, thyroid disease, or other endocrine
disorders.
4. Safety netting:
- “Return if you develop heavy, persistent, or unusual bleeding, pelvic
pain, or other new symptoms.”
- “If symptoms worsen or affect quality of life, we can discuss starting
treatment like HRT or non-hormonal options.”
SCA phrases:
- “Blood tests are generally not helpful in perimenopause because
hormone levels fluctuate.”
- “We can usually make the diagnosis based on your symptoms and cycle
pattern.”
- “If your symptoms are troublesome, there are safe treatment options we
can discuss.”
- “We’ll monitor you and review any new symptoms promptly to make sure
nothing serious is missed.”

213- Amenorrhea while on POP
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Persistent amenorrhea >6 months
(evaluate for pregnancy, endocrine
disorder)
- Associated abdominal pain,
bloating, or systemic symptoms
- Unintended weight changes,
galactorrhoea, signs of thyroid
disease
- History of missed pills or drug
interactions
- Unprotected intercourse → risk of
pregnancy
Differential Considerations:
- Physiological effect of POP
(common)
- Pregnancy
- Hypothalamic-pituitary disorders
(prolactinoma, thyroid dysfunction)
- PCOS
- Premature ovarian insufficiency
Focused History:
- Medication adherence: timing of pill daily, any missed pills.
- Contraceptive history: duration on POP, prior menstrual pattern.
- Symptoms: breast tenderness, galactorrhoea, fatigue, weight
changes, acne, hirsutism.
- Sexual history: risk of pregnancy, recent unprotected intercourse.
- Medical history: thyroid disease, PCOS, pituitary disorders.
- Lifestyle factors: stress, weight changes, intense exercise.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Some women on the progestogen-only pill may notice their
periods become very light or stop altogether – this is usually normal.”
- “Other causes, like pregnancy or hormonal issues, are less
common but important to rule out.”
Management:
1. Rule out pregnancy:
- Perform urine or serum pregnancy test if sexually active.
2. Reassure if physiological:
- Amenorrhea is common on POP and not harmful.
- No need for intervention if she is otherwise well and no red flags.
3. Investigate if indicated:
- Consider thyroid function, prolactin, FSH/LH if persistent or associated
symptoms.
- Evaluate for PCOS if other features present (hirsutism, acne, irregular
cycles before POP).
4. Contraceptive advice:
- Continue POP as long as adherence is maintained.
- Discuss emergency contraception if any missed pills and unprotected
intercourse.
5. Safety netting:
- “Return if you develop abdominal pain, unexpected bleeding, systemic
symptoms, or if periods do not return after stopping POP in future.”
- “Return if you suspect pregnancy.”
SCA phrases:
- “Amenorrhea is a common side effect of the progestogen-only pill.”
- “It does not usually indicate any health problem.”
- “We’ll rule out pregnancy first to be safe.”
- “If you have other concerning symptoms, we can investigate further.”

-- 239 of 437 --

214- Premenstrual Dysphoric
Disorder (PMDD)
Definition: Severe form of
premenstrual syndrome
characterized by emotional,
behavioural, and physical symptoms
that interfere with daily functioning,
occurring in the luteal phase
(typically 1–2 weeks before
menstruation) and resolving after
menstruation.
Risk Factors: Personal or family
history of mood disorders, severe
PMS, stress, hormonal fluctuations,
age 20–40.
Differential Diagnosis (DDx): Major
depressive disorder, generalized
anxiety disorder, thyroid disease,
bipolar disorder, chronic stress, PMS.
When to Suspect:
- Recurrent, predictable emotional
and physical symptoms 1–2 weeks
before menstruation.
- Significant interference with work,
school, or relationships.
- Symptoms resolve shortly after
menstruation starts.
Red Flags: Persistent low mood or
anxiety outside luteal phase, suicidal
ideation, psychosis, severe functional
impairment—may indicate major
depressive disorder or other
psychiatric illness.
Focused History:
- Symptom diary: Ask patient to track symptoms for at least two
cycles—this confirms the luteal phase pattern and distinguishes
PMDD from other mood disorders.
- Emotional symptoms: irritability, anxiety, depressed mood,
tearfulness. Why: these are the hallmark features that differentiate
PMDD from normal PMS; severity and impact on daily functioning are
key.
- Physical symptoms: breast tenderness, bloating, headaches,
fatigue. Why: physical symptoms support the cyclical hormonal link.
- Behavioural impact: absenteeism, interpersonal conflicts,
decreased productivity. Why: PMDD is diagnosed when symptoms
interfere significantly with normal functioning.
- Lifestyle factors: diet, sleep, stress, caffeine/alcohol intake. Why:
these may exacerbate symptoms and can be modified as part of
treatment.
- Past psychiatric & family history: depression, anxiety. Why: helps
differentiate PMDD from other psychiatric disorders and identify risk
factors.
- Red flags: suicidal ideation, persistent symptoms outside luteal
phase. Why: suggests alternative or comorbid psychiatric disorder
needing urgent attention.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “PMDD is a severe type of premenstrual symptoms linked to
hormonal changes in the second half of your menstrual cycle.”
- “Symptoms are predictable and usually resolve once your period
starts.”
- “Keeping a symptom diary helps us confirm the pattern and guide
treatment.”
- “This condition is different from depression because it occurs
cyclically, not all month.”
Management:
1. Symptom tracking:
- Use daily symptom diary for ≥2 cycles to confirm timing/severity.
2. Lifestyle measures:
- Regular exercise, balanced diet, sleep hygiene, stress management.
- Limit caffeine, alcohol, and refined sugars if exacerbating symptoms.
3. Pharmacological options:
- First-line: SSRIs (e.g., fluoxetine, sertraline), either continuous or luteal
phase dosing.
- Other options: combined hormonal contraception (continuous dosing)
to suppress ovulation, GnRH analogues in refractory cases (with
specialist input).
- Adjuncts: NSAIDs for physical symptoms, magnesium or vitamin B6 in
mild cases.
4. Psychological support:
- CBT can help with coping strategies and mood regulation.
- Support groups or counselling if severe.
- Signpost to National Association for Premenstrual Syndromes (NAPS)
https://www.pms.org.uk/ or https://thepmddproject.org/
5. Safety netting:
- “Return promptly if you develop suicidal thoughts, severe mood
changes, or inability to function.”
- “Keep symptom diary to monitor response to treatment.”
SCA phrases:
- “Your symptoms occur in a predictable pattern before your period and
are severe enough to affect daily life.”
- “Tracking symptoms helps confirm the diagnosis and guide treatment.”
- “SSRIs and hormonal treatments can help reduce symptoms
effectively.”
- “Lifestyle changes and psychological support are important alongside
medical treatment.”

215- Gender dysphoria
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Severe psychological distress, self-
harm, or suicidal ideation
- Bullying, harassment, or social
isolation
- Comorbid mental health conditions:
depression, anxiety, eating disorders
- Substance misuse
Differential Considerations:
- Body dysmorphic disorder
- Depression or anxiety
- Autism spectrum disorder (may
have gender identity concerns)
- Psychotic disorders with gender-
related delusions
Focused History:
- Gender identity: how the patient identifies, duration of feelings,
consistency over time.
- Puberty / development history: any distress with secondary
sexual characteristics.
- Psychological impact: mood, anxiety, self-harm, suicidal
thoughts.
- Social context: family support, school/work environment, peer
relationships, bullying.
- Medical history: comorbid mental health conditions, medications.
- Previous interventions: counselling, hormone therapy, surgeries,
support groups.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Gender dysphoria is when someone’s experienced gender identity
does not match the sex they were assigned at birth, and it can cause
significant distress.”
- “Support is available to help you live in the gender you identify with,
including psychological support and medical options.”
- “Treatment is highly individual and usually involves a
multidisciplinary approach.”
“Chest binders can help create a flatter chest, but they need to be
used safely. Try not to wear it for more than 8–12 hours, avoid
sleeping in it, and make sure it isn’t so tight that it restricts breathing.
Take breaks, choose the right size, and let me know if you develop
pain, skin problems, or breathing difficulties.”
1. Psychological support:
- Referral to specialist gender identity clinic / multidisciplinary team.
- Counselling/psychotherapy for coping strategies and mental health
support.
2. Social support:
- Peer support groups, school/workplace adjustments. Signpost to
https://mermaidsuk.org.uk/ or https://www.stonewall.org.uk/
- Family support and education.
3. Medical interventions (if desired and appropriate):
- Puberty blockers: for adolescents to pause unwanted pubertal changes
(specialist-led).
- Cross-sex hormones: oestrogen or testosterone under specialist
supervision.
- Surgical options: gender-affirming surgeries (after multidisciplinary
assessment).
4. Mental health monitoring:
- Assess and manage depression, anxiety, self-harm risk.
- Ongoing risk assessment and safety planning.
5. Safety netting:
- “If you experience thoughts of self-harm or feel unsafe, seek urgent help
immediately.”
- “We’ll continue to support you while accessing specialist care.”
SCA phrases:
- “I want to understand your gender identity and how this affects you.”
- “Support is available, and you don’t have to go through this alone.”
- “We can involve specialist teams to guide you safely through any
medical or social interventions.”
- “Your mental health and safety are a priority while we explore the best
options for you.”

216- Transgender patient requesting
hormonal treatment
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Severe psychological distress, self-
harm, or suicidal ideation
- Bullying, discrimination, or unsafe
environment
- Comorbid mental health conditions
(depression, anxiety, eating
disorders)
- Substance misuse
- Risky attempts at unsupervised
hormone use
Differential Considerations:
- Gender dysphoria
- Body dysmorphic disorder
- Depression/anxiety
- Psychotic disorders with gender-
related delusions
Focused History:
- Gender identity: self-identification, duration, consistency.
- Psychological impact: mood, anxiety, self-harm, suicidal
thoughts.
- Social context: support systems, school/work environment, peer
and family support, bullying.
- Previous interventions: counselling, prior hormone therapy,
surgeries.
- Medical history: comorbidities, medications, contraindications to
hormone therapy.
- Risk assessment: self-harm, substance misuse, unsafe hormone
use.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “I understand you want to start hormone treatment, but there is a
long waiting list for specialist clinics.”
- “We need to make sure this is done safely and under specialist
supervision to avoid health risks.”
- “While you wait, there are ways we can support your mental health
and explore safe options.”
1. Referral and waitlist management:
- Ensure referral to specialist gender identity clinic / endocrinology.
- Provide clear explanation about expected waiting time.
2. Psychological support while waiting:
- Counselling / therapy for coping, distress management.
- Peer support groups and community resources.
- Monitor and manage anxiety, depression, or self-harm risk.
3. Risk mitigation:
- Advise against unsupervised hormone use.
- Discuss safer alternatives for symptom relief (e.g., voice training, hair
removal, social transition support).
4. Safety netting:
- “If you have thoughts of self-harm or feel unsafe, seek help immediately
(emergency services, crisis lines).”
- “Keep in touch with us or your GP if mental health worsens or you feel at
risk.”
5. SCA phrases:
- “I understand this waiting period can be frustrating, and it’s important to
stay safe while waiting.”
- “We can provide support for mental health, coping strategies, and social
transition during this time.”
- “Starting hormones without specialist supervision can be unsafe, so we
wait until appropriate assessment.”
- “We’ll keep in regular contact and monitor your wellbeing while you wait
for specialist care.”

217- Rape/ sexual assault
Red Flags / Warning Signs:
- Acute physical injury: bruises,
lacerations, bleeding, fractures
- Genital or anal trauma
- Signs of psychological trauma:
shock, dissociation, severe anxiety
- High risk of pregnancy or STI
transmission
- Vulnerable patient: under 18,
impaired capacity, intoxication
Differential Considerations:
- Sexual assault vs consensual sexual
activity with distress
- Other trauma (domestic violence,
abuse)
Focused History:
- Immediate safety: Are they in a safe environment now? Any
ongoing threat?
- Incident details: Timing, nature of assault, relationship to
perpetrator, use of force or coercion (document objectively).
- Medical history: allergies, medications, previous sexual assaults,
mental health history.
- Contraception / pregnancy risk: last menstrual period, use of
contraception.
- STI risk: potential exposure, prior infections.
- Psychosocial support: presence of family/friends, support
networks.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “I’m very sorry this happened. You are safe now, and we will
support you.”
- “We can provide medical care, prevent infections, discuss
emergency contraception, and arrange follow-up support.”
- “You don’t have to go through this alone; specialist services can
support you emotionally and legally.”
1. Immediate safety & consent:
- Ensure patient is in a safe environment.
- Obtain informed consent for examination and interventions.
2. Medical assessment & care:
- Examination: injuries, genital/anal trauma, document findings.
- Pregnancy prevention: offer emergency contraception if within window.
- STI prophylaxis: HIV, chlamydia, gonorrhoea, hepatitis B as indicated.
- Tetanus update if needed.
3. Psychological support:
- Immediate emotional support, reassure patient.
- Referral to specialist sexual assault services (SARC) for forensic
services and holistic support and counselling.
- Consider risk assessment for self-harm or suicidal ideation.
4. Legal & safeguarding:
- Explain options for reporting to police, if patient wishes.
- Safeguarding referral if minor or vulnerable adult.
- Forensic sample collection (with consent).
5. Follow-up & safety netting:
- Arrange follow-up for medical review, repeat STI screening, and mental
health support.
- “Return urgently if you develop pain, bleeding, signs of infection, or
worsening mental health.”
SCA phrases:
- “You are safe now, and we will provide support for your physical and
emotional wellbeing.”
- “Emergency contraception and STI prevention are available and most
effective if started soon.”
- “You do not have to make decisions about reporting to the police
immediately; support services are available.”
- “It is normal to feel upset, anxious, or scared, and we can arrange
counselling to help you cope.”

219- Diabulimia
Definition: Eating disorder where a
person with type 1 diabetes
deliberately reduces/omits insulin to
lose weight.
Red Flags:
- Recurrent DKA admissions or high
ketones
- Rapid weight loss or severe
underweight
- Poor glycaemic control (high HbA1c,
erratic readings)
- Severe body image concerns,
anxiety, depression
- Self-harm, suicidal ideation
- Complications of uncontrolled
diabetes: recurrent infections,
neuropathy, retinopathy
Focused History:
- Diabetes history: diagnosis age, insulin regimen, frequency of
omission, recent HbA1c, admissions.
- Eating behaviours: body image concerns, weight preoccupation,
fear of weight gain.
- Symptoms of hyperglycaemia/DKA: thirst, polyuria, tiredness,
nausea, abdominal pain.
- Mental health: depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicidal thoughts.
- Impact on life: school/work absence, relationships, social
withdrawal.
- Support system: family awareness, previous CAMHS or ED
services.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Diabulimia is when someone with diabetes leaves out or reduces
their insulin on purpose, usually because of worries about body
weight or image.”
- “It’s very serious because without insulin the body can’t use sugar
for energy – leading to dangerous complications like DKA and long-
term damage.”
- “The good news is support is available from a specialist team who
understand both diabetes and eating disorders.”
1. Immediate safety:
- If acutely unwell (DKA signs) → urgent hospital admission.
- Assess suicide risk and self-harm → escalate if high risk.
2. Multidisciplinary referral:
- Refer to specialist diabetes eating disorder service (if available).
- Involve diabetes specialist nurse, endocrinologist, dietitian, and mental
health team.
3. Psychological support:
- Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT-ED) or other eating disorder
interventions.
- Address body image concerns and coping strategies.
4. Diabetes management:
- Review insulin regimen, monitoring, and complications.
- Gradual reintroduction of insulin under supervision to avoid refeeding
complications.
5. Safety netting:
- “If you feel unwell with nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, or very thirsty
– seek urgent help as this could be life-threatening DKA.”
- “You’re not alone – we’ll involve a team to support both your diabetes
and emotional wellbeing.”
SCA phrases:
- “I can see this is really difficult for you, and it’s important you know
you’re not alone.”
- “Leaving out insulin may seem like a way to control weight, but it can
cause very serious harm.”
- “We’ll get you the right support from a team that specialises in both
diabetes and eating disorders.”
- “Your safety is my main concern – I want to make sure you’re supported
both medically and emotionally.”

220- Gynecomastia
Gynaecomastia
Definition: Benign enlargement of
male breast glandular tissue due to
imbalance between oestrogen and
androgen activity.
Red Flags:
- Unilateral, hard lump, irregular
margin, skin/nipple changes → ?male
breast cancer
- Bloody nipple discharge
- Rapid onset or progressive
enlargement
- Associated systemic features
(weight loss, testicular mass, liver
disease)
Focused History:
- Onset & progression: When noticed, any change in size,
painful/tender?
- Laterality: unilateral vs bilateral.
- Associated symptoms: nipple discharge, skin changes, weight
loss, testicular pain/swelling, reduced libido, erectile dysfunction.
- PMH: chronic liver/kidney disease, thyroid disease, testicular
disease, malnutrition.
- Drug history: spironolactone, cimetidine, ketoconazole, digoxin,
anti-androgens, anabolic steroids, cannabis, alcohol.
- FHx: breast/testicular cancer.
- Psychosocial: embarrassment, body image issues.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “This is called gynaecomastia, meaning enlargement of the breast
tissue in men.”
- “It can happen because of changes in the balance of male and
female hormones.”
- “Sometimes it’s caused by medicines, alcohol, medical conditions,
or very rarely, breast cancer in men.”
1. Investigations (to confirm cause):
- Clinical exam (distinguish from fat deposition, check for hard masses).
- Bloods: LFTs, U&Es, TFTs, testosterone, oestradiol, LH/FSH, β-hCG,
prolactin.
- Testicular exam ± USS if suspicious.
- Mammogram/US + referral if suspicious of breast cancer.
2. Reversible causes:
- Stop/switch offending drug.
- Reduce alcohol, stop anabolic steroids/cannabis.
3. Observation:
- Pubertal gynaecomastia is common, self-limiting (often resolves in 6–18
months).
4. Specialist referral:
- Endocrinology or breast clinic if concerning features or unclear cause.
- Signpost to https://kidshealth.org/en/teens/boybrst.html
5. Treatment options (if persistent / troubling):
- Tamoxifen or surgery (reduction mammoplasty) in selected cases.
Safety netting:
- “If you notice the lump becoming hard, painful, with skin changes or
nipple discharge, let us know urgently.”
SCA phrases:
- “This is quite common and often benign, but we’ll rule out anything
serious.”
- “We’ll check some blood tests and review any medicines or lifestyle
factors that could contribute.”
- “If it doesn’t settle or if there are worrying features, I’d refer you to a
specialist for further tests.”
- “Most cases resolve on their own, especially in teenagers.”
