259- ED in cardiac patient
Definition: Inability to achieve or
maintain an erection sufficient for
sexual activity, occurring in patients
with cardiovascular disease.
When to suspect / Diagnostic
features:
- Patient reports difficulty
achieving/maintaining erection
- Onset may coincide with cardiac
disease diagnosis or medications
- Risk factors: hypertension, diabetes,
hyperlipidemia, smoking, beta-
blocker use
- Red flags: recent ACS (<6 months),
unstable angina, heart failure,
hypotension, concurrent nitrate
therapy
Focused History:
- ED history: onset, duration, severity, psychological vs physical
factors, morning erections.
- Cardiac history: stable/unstable angina, MI, PCI/CABG, heart
failure, arrhythmias.
- Medication history: nitrates, beta-blockers, antihypertensives,
statins, PDE5 inhibitors.
- Associated symptoms: chest pain on exertion, dyspnea, fatigue,
dizziness.
- Lifestyle: smoking, alcohol, exercise.
- Psychosocial factors: depression, relationship stress.
Examination (SCA style):
- BP, pulse, orthostatic changes.
- Cardiovascular exam: murmurs, heart sounds, edema, signs of
heart failure.
- General: BMI, signs of metabolic syndrome.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Your difficulty achieving erections may be related to your heart
condition or medications.”
- “Some ED treatments can be unsafe if you have heart disease,
especially if you take nitrates.”
- “We can explore safe options that consider your heart health and
effectiveness for erectile function.”
SCA phrases:
- “Your ED may be related to your heart condition or medications.”
- “Because you take nitrates, PDE5 inhibitors are unsafe right now.”
- “We can explore safe alternatives like vacuum devices, injections,
or counseling.”
- “If your heart condition stabilizes and nitrates are adjusted under
cardiology review, PDE5 inhibitors may become an option.”
- “Your safety is the priority, and we will ensure any treatment does
not worsen your heart health.”
1. Cardiovascular safety assessment:
- Ensure stable cardiac status (no recent ACS, unstable angina,
decompensated heart failure).
- Review BP, medications, and comorbidities.
2. Non-pharmacological options:
- Lifestyle optimization: exercise, weight loss, smoking cessation.
- Psychosexual counseling, behavioral therapy, vacuum erection devices,
intracavernosal injections.
3. Pharmacological options (if safe):
- Concurrent use of PDE-5 inhibitors and organic nitrates (such as
isosorbide mononitrate or isosorbide dinitrate), nicorandil, or amyl nitrate
('poppers') are absolutely contraindicated due to the risk of hypotension.
If a PDE-5 inhibitor is taken and the man develops chest pain, organic
nitrates should not be used for at least 24 hours for sildenafil and possibly
vardenafil (half-life 4 hours), 48 hours for tadalafil (due to its longer half-
life of 17.5 hours), and at least 12 hours for avanafil (half-life 6–17 hours
Hypotension is more likely to occur within four hours following treatment
with an alpha-blocker. A sildenafil starting dose of 25 mg is
recommended.
Tadalafil is not recommended in men taking doxazosin.
4. Medication review / adjustment:
- Consider switching beta-blockers or other anti-anginals if contributing to
ED.
- Evaluate if nitrates can be minimized safely to allow PDE5 inhibitor use
(cardiology input required).
5. Safety netting / patient advice:
- “Never combine PDE5 inhibitors with nitrates.”
- “Report chest pain, dizziness, syncope, or palpitations immediately.”
- “Sexual activity should be approached cautiously if angina symptoms
occur.”

-- 280 of 437 --

260- Post MI care
Definition: Patient who has survived
an acute MI (STEMI/NSTEMI) now
being reviewed in primary care for
secondary prevention, rehabilitation,
and optimisation of long-term care.
When to suspect / Diagnostic
features:
- History of recent hospital admission
with confirmed MI
- Often discharged on secondary
prevention bundle (aspirin,
clopidogrel/ticagrelor, statin,
ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker)
- May have persistent chest pain,
fatigue, depression, or heart failure
symptoms post-MI
Red Flags:
- Recurrent or new chest pain
- Breathlessness, orthopnoea, ankle
swelling (heart failure)
- Palpitations, syncope (arrhythmias)
- Poor adherence to secondary
prevention medication
Focused History:
- Symptoms since discharge: recurrent chest pain, breathlessness,
palpitations, ankle swelling, dizziness, syncope.
- Functional status: exercise tolerance, return to work, activities of
daily living.
- Medication review: adherence to aspirin, dual antiplatelet
(clopidogrel/ticagrelor), beta-blocker, ACEi/ARB, statin, GTN use.
- Side effects: cough (ACEi), muscle aches (statin), dizziness/fatigue
(beta-blockers).
- Psychological health: depression, anxiety, sexual dysfunction.
- Lifestyle: smoking, alcohol, diet, physical activity.
- Social support: family/carers, access to rehabilitation.
Examination (SCA style):
- Vitals (BP, HR).
- Cardiovascular exam: murmurs, heart sounds, JVP, ankle edema.
- Respiratory exam: crepitations (HF).
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “You’ve had a heart attack, which means part of your heart muscle
was damaged due to a blocked artery.”
- “Our focus now is on preventing another heart attack and helping
you get back to your normal life safely.”
- “Medications, lifestyle changes, and cardiac rehab are all
important for recovery.”
1. Medication optimisation (secondary prevention bundle):
- Antiplatelet: Aspirin + clopidogrel/ticagrelor (duration per cardiology,
usually 12 months).
- Statin (high intensity, e.g., atorvastatin 80 mg).
- ACEi/ARB (titrate to max tolerated).
- Beta-blocker (unless contraindicated).
- GTN spray for angina.
2. Lifestyle modification:
- Smoking cessation, alcohol moderation.
- Diet: low fat, low salt, high fibre, Mediterranean style.
- Exercise: gradual, supervised (cardiac rehab).
3. Monitoring / follow-up:
- BP, lipids, HbA1c (if diabetic).
- Renal function, electrolytes (esp. on ACEi/ARB).
- Cardiac rehab referral.
- DVLA advice: usually no driving for 1 month post-MI (if no complications).
4. Psychosocial support:
- Screen for depression/anxiety.
- Discuss return to work, sexual activity (safe if patient can climb 2 flights
of stairs without symptoms).
- Erectile dysfunction: PDE5 inhibitors contraindicated with nitrates.
- Signpost to BHF
https://www.bhf.org.uk/informationsupport/support/cardiac-
rehabilitation/what-is-cardiac-rehab
5. Red flag & safety netting:
- “Seek urgent help if you develop new or worsening chest pain,
breathlessness, fainting, or palpitations.”
- “Always carry your GTN spray and know how to use it.”
6. SCA phrases:
- “The aim is to reduce your chance of another heart attack.”
- “These medications work together to protect your heart – stopping them
suddenly can be dangerous.”
- “Cardiac rehabilitation is a safe, supervised way to regain confidence
and fitness after a heart attack.”
- “You can usually return to driving and sexual activity once you’re stable,
but always check first with your healthcare team.”
- “If you ever get chest pain again, especially at rest, call 999
immediately.”

261- Familial Hypercholesterolaemia
(FH)
Definition: A genetic disorder
(autosomal dominant) leading to very
high LDL cholesterol levels, markedly
increasing risk of premature
atherosclerotic cardiovascular
disease (ASCVD).
When to suspect / Diagnostic
features:
- Adults: total cholesterol >7.5
mmol/L or LDL >4.9 mmol/L
- Children (<16): total cholesterol
>6.7 mmol/L or LDL >4.0 mmol/L
- Family history of premature CHD (MI
<60 in 1st-degree relative)
- Tendon xanthomata, corneal arcus
at young age
- Use Simon Broome criteria
(definite FH = high cholesterol +
tendon xanthomata in
patient/1st/2nd degree relative or
genetic confirmation; possible FH =
high cholesterol + family history of
premature CHD)
Red Flags:
- Very high cholesterol resistant to
standard therapy
- Premature angina/MI
- Sudden cardiac death in family
Focused History:
- Personal history: angina, chest pain, claudication, stroke/TIA.
- Family history: premature MI, sudden death, known
hyperlipidaemia.
- Medication history: statins, ezetimibe, side effects (myalgia, liver
issues).
- Lifestyle: diet (saturated fats, processed foods), exercise,
smoking, alcohol.
- Symptoms of complications: exertional chest pain,
breathlessness, TIAs, leg pain on exertion.
Examination (SCA style):
- Tendon xanthomata (Achilles, extensor tendons).
- Xanthelasma around eyes.
- Corneal arcus in <45 y/o.
- BP, BMI, cardiovascular exam.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “You have a condition where your body naturally produces very high
cholesterol due to a gene you’ve inherited.”
- “This significantly increases your risk of heart disease at a younger
age.”
- “Treatment focuses on lowering your cholesterol as much as
possible to protect your heart and blood vessels.”
• Autosomal dominant - Siblings and children of a person with FH
have a 50% risk of inheriting the condition.
• Untreated, people aged 20-39 with familial
hypercholesterolaemia (FH) have a 100-fold increased risk of
death from heart disease
SCA phrases:
- “This condition is inherited, which means your family members may
also be affected.”
- “The main aim is to lower your cholesterol to reduce your risk of
heart attack and stroke.”
- “We’ll refer you to a specialist clinic for advanced management and
family screening.”
- “Medication and lifestyle work together to protect your heart.”
- “Regular monitoring ensures we get your cholesterol down safely.”
1. Lifestyle measures:
- Diet: reduce saturated fats, red meat, fried foods; increase fibre, oily
fish, plant sterols.
- Exercise: at least 150 min moderate per week.
- Smoking cessation, reduce alcohol.
2. Pharmacological therapy (stepwise):
- If confirmed heterozygous FH + high risk (established CHD or FH or
premature CHD or > 2 RFs for CVD), refer to lipid clinic. If not high risk,
then advise about lifestyle modifications + offer medications:
o High intensity statin: atorvastatin 20mg – with target of at least
50% reduction in LDL or Ezetimibe as alternative
o 2nd line if not achieving target: titrate dose of statin to max then
add ezetimibe
o 3rd line change statin to simvastatin 80mg
o Then refer to lipid clinic or add inclisiran with ezetimibe
monotherapy
- All children with confirmed FH, should be managed by 2ry care where
they usually start medication by the age of 10 or earlier
3. Specialist referral:
- Refer all patient with clinical Dx of FH to lipid clinic for genetic testing
and cascade screening of family.
- Cardiology input if symptomatic or high risk.
4. Family screening:
- Offer lipid testing ± genetic testing to first-degree relatives.
- Children: genetic testing by age 10 if one parent affect or by age of 5 if
both
5. Monitoring:
- Baseline LFTs, CK (if statin intolerance suspected).
- Repeat lipids after 3 months of therapy, then 6–12 months.
6. Safety netting:
- “If you develop chest pain, shortness of breath, or stroke-like symptoms,
seek urgent help.”
- “If you get severe muscle aches or dark urine while on statins, contact us
immediately.”

-- 282 of 437 --

262- Satins declined for 1ry
prevention
Definition: Patient at elevated
cardiovascular risk (QRISK ≥10% over
10 years, diabetes, CKD, or other risk
factors) but declining statin therapy
for prevention of first cardiovascular
event.
When to suspect / Diagnostic
features:
- Middle-aged/older adults with
QRISK ≥10%
- Diabetic patients >40 years old
- CKD stage 3–5
- Family history of premature CVD
Red Flags:
- Very high cholesterol (TC >7.5 or
LDL >4.9 mmol/L → consider FH)
- Refusal despite strong family history
of premature MI/stroke
- Patient influenced by
misinformation (e.g. “statins always
cause muscle damage”)
Focused History:
- Cardiovascular risk profile: smoking, hypertension, diabetes,
obesity, family history.
- Past medical history: stroke, angina, TIA, peripheral vascular
disease (to rule out secondary prevention).
- Lipid profile: baseline cholesterol.
- Patient concerns: fear of side effects (myalgia, liver damage,
diabetes risk), mistrust of medication, preference for “natural”
methods.
- Lifestyle factors: diet, exercise, alcohol, stress.
- Medication history: previous statin use/intolerance.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Statins lower your cholesterol, which reduces your chance of a
heart attack or stroke in the future.”
- “For people with your risk level, statins can reduce the chance of a
serious event by about 25–30%.”
- “Side effects are uncommon, and we can monitor you closely for
any problems.”
- “Lifestyle changes are important, but for higher-risk people, tablets
give extra protection.”
1. Risk communication:
- Use QRISK score to demonstrate personalised risk.
- Show absolute benefit (e.g. “Taking a statin lowers your risk of a heart
attack from 1 in 5 to 1 in 7 over the next 10 years”).
2. Address misconceptions / side effects:
- Explain most patients tolerate statins well.
- Side effects (muscle aches, liver issues) are rare and reversible if they
occur.
- Offer trial period with monitoring.
3. Offer alternatives:
- If statins refused, consider ezetimibe (esp. in FH, diabetes).
- Emphasise lifestyle: diet (Mediterranean), weight loss, exercise, smoking
cessation, BP control.
4. Shared decision-making:
- Respect patient autonomy if they refuse after informed discussion.
- Document discussion clearly.
5. Monitoring:
- Annual cardiovascular risk review (QRISK, BP, HbA1c, lipid profile).
6. Safety netting:
- “If you change your mind in future, you can start statins at any time.”
- “If you develop chest pain, sudden weakness, or stroke-like symptoms,
seek urgent help.”
7. SCA phrases:
- “I hear your concerns about statins and understand you’d prefer not to
take them.”
- “My role is to give you clear information about the benefits and risks so
you can make an informed choice.”
- “Lifestyle measures are very important, but given your risk level,
medication offers extra protection.”
- “It’s your decision, and we will support you with whichever option you
choose.”
- “We’ll keep monitoring your health, and you can reconsider treatment at
any time.”

263- PAD
Definition: Narrowing of peripheral
arteries (commonly
femoral/popliteal) causing exertional
leg pain relieved by rest.
When to suspect / Diagnostic
features:
- Exertional calf/buttock/thigh pain
relieved by rest
- Reduced/absent foot pulses
- ABPI ≤0.9
- Risk factors: smoking, diabetes,
hypertension, dyslipidaemia, age >55,
male sex
Red Flags:
- Rest pain (night pain, relief by
dangling leg) → critical limb ischaemia
- Non-healing ulcers, gangrene,
tissue loss
- Sudden worsening pain/colour →
acute limb ischaemia
- ABI < 0.5
Focused History:
- Claudication symptoms: site, walking distance until pain, relief
with rest, progression.
- Impact on life: mobility, work, daily activities.
- Cardiovascular risk: smoking, hypertension, diabetes,
cholesterol, family history of CVD.
- Red flag symptoms: rest pain, ulceration, colour/temperature
change.
- Past medical history: CAD, stroke, CKD, AF (embolism risk).
- Medication history: antiplatelets, statins, antihypertensives.
Examination (SCA style):
- Pulse check (femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial).
- Capillary refill, skin colour/temperature, trophic skin changes.
- Measure ABPI if available.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “This pain is caused by reduced blood flow to your leg muscles
when you walk, due to narrowing of the arteries.”
- “It’s a warning sign of artery disease, not only in your legs but also
in your heart and brain.”
- “The good news is that with lifestyle changes and treatment,
symptoms often improve and your risk of heart attack or stroke can
be reduced.”
1. Lifestyle modification (cornerstone):
- Smoking cessation (biggest impact on prognosis).
- Exercise therapy: supervised exercise program - structured walking
programme (30–60 min, 3×/week for ≥3 months, walk until moderate pain
then rest and resume).
- Weight loss, Mediterranean diet, alcohol moderation.
- Signpost to https://www.circulationfoundation.org.uk/
2. Medical optimisation:
- Antiplatelet: offer clopidogrel first line (or aspirin if not tolerated).
- Statin: high-intensity (atorvastatin 80 mg).
- BP & diabetes control to target.
- Analgesia only if needed but encourage walking through discomfort to
improve collateral circulation.
3. Vascular referral:
- If lifestyle + medical therapy fails and patient remains functionally
limited.
- Urgent referral if critical limb ischaemia features.
4. Safety netting:
- “If you develop pain at rest, ulcers that won’t heal, or black toes/feet,
seek urgent help.”
- “If your walking distance suddenly drops, it may mean a clot has blocked
your artery and you should go straight to hospital.”
5. SCA phrases:
- “This is due to furring up of the arteries, similar to what can happen in the
heart.”
- “Stopping smoking is the single most effective treatment for protecting
your legs and heart.”
- “Walking through the pain may sound strange, but it helps new blood
vessels to grow around the blockage.”
- “We’ll start you on a tablet to thin the blood and a strong cholesterol-
lowering medicine to protect your heart and circulation.”
- “Most people improve with exercise and tablets, but if things don’t get
better, we can consider referral for a procedure to open up the artery.”

-- 284 of 437 --

264 – AAA
Definition: A permanent dilatation of
the abdominal aorta ≥3 cm in
diameter.
When to suspect / Diagnostic
features:
- Often asymptomatic (detected on
screening or incidental imaging).
- Symptoms if enlarging/impending
rupture: abdominal/back pain,
pulsatile abdominal mass.
- Rupture: severe abdominal/back
pain, hypotension, collapse (medical
emergency).
Red Flags (emergency referral):
- Sudden severe abdominal/back pain
- Collapse/hypotension
- Tender pulsatile mass
Focused History:
- Presentation: screening result or incidental finding? Any
symptoms? (abdominal pain, back pain, pulsatile mass).
- Risk factors: age >65, male sex, smoking, hypertension, family
history of AAA.
- Comorbidities: CAD, COPD, CKD, diabetes.
- Functional status: mobility, frailty, fitness for potential surgery.
- Medication history: BP meds, statins, antiplatelets.
- Social history: smoking, alcohol, support system.
Examination:
- Pulsatile abdominal mass (if palpable).
- Assess BP, pulses, cardiovascular exam.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “An aneurysm is a bulge in the main blood vessel in your tummy.”
- “Small ones usually don’t cause problems but can get bigger slowly
over time.”
- “If it grows too large, there is a risk it could burst, which is life-
threatening.”
- “We monitor them closely with scans, and if it gets above a certain
size, surgery is considered.”
1. Surveillance (based on size):
- 3.0–4.4 cm: ultrasound every 12 months.
- 4.5–5.4 cm: ultrasound every 3 months.
- ≥5.5 cm or rapid growth (>1 cm/year): refer to vascular surgery
(consider repair).
2. Risk factor modification:
- Stop smoking (most important).
- Control BP, diabetes, cholesterol.
- Offer statin (atorvastatin 80 mg) and antiplatelet (clopidogrel or aspirin).
3. Referral & MDT discussion:
- Elective endovascular or open repair if AAA ≥5.5 cm and patient fit.
- In frail/unfit patients → palliative approach (symptom control, advanced
care planning).
Driving: In group 1 must stop if 6.5 cm or more + must notify DVLA if 6 cm
or more. In group 2, mut notify DVLA if any AAA + must stop if >5.5 cm
4. Red flag / safety netting:
- “If you develop sudden severe tummy or back pain, dizziness, or
collapse, call 999 immediately.”
5. SCA phrases:
- “The aim of monitoring is to step in if it gets too big, before it causes
problems.”
- “Stopping smoking and controlling blood pressure are the best things
you can do to slow down growth.”
- “Surgery is only considered if it gets above a certain size, as the risks of
surgery are higher than the risks of a small aneurysm.”
- “If you ever get sudden severe pain in your tummy or back, or feel faint,
that’s an emergency and you must call 999 straight away.”

-- 285 of 437 --

265- Nausea and Vomiting in Palliative
Care
Definition: Distressing symptoms in
advanced illness, often multifactorial
(chemical, mechanical,
psychological causes).
When to suspect / Diagnostic
features:
- Reported nausea, retching, or
vomiting in advanced cancer, end-
stage organ failure, or terminal
illness.
- May be triggered by opioids,
metabolic derangements
(hypercalcaemia, uraemia), gastric
stasis, bowel obstruction, raised ICP,
or anxiety.
Red Flags:
- Coffee-ground vomit / frank
haematemesis
- Projectile vomiting / raised ICP
symptoms
- Persistent bowel obstruction with
colicky pain and distension
- Sudden uncontrolled vomiting
despite treatment
Focused History:
- Symptom profile: onset, frequency, timing (after food, at night,
continuous), volume.
- Associated symptoms: abdominal pain, bloating, constipation,
hiccups, early satiety, headache, visual changes.
- Triggers/relievers: medications (esp. opioids), bowel obstruction,
anxiety.
- Medical background: cancer type, organ failure, recent bloods
(calcium, renal function).
- Current meds: opioids, antiemetics tried, laxatives.
- Impact on quality of life: eating, sleep, distress.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Nausea and vomiting are common in advanced illness and can be
caused by many things, like the illness itself, medicines, or the gut
slowing down.”
- “Our aim is not just to find the cause but to give you relief with the
right medicines.”
- “Different anti-sickness medicines work in different ways, so we try
to match the right one to the likely cause.”
1. General principles:
- Identify likely cause (chemical, gastric stasis, obstruction, raised ICP,
psychological).
- One regular antiemetic, given continuously if needed (avoid mixing
multiple).
- Address reversible factors where possible (constipation,
hypercalcaemia).
2. Symptom control (drug options):
- Opioid-induced/chemical: haloperidol, levomepromazine.
- Gastric stasis/partial obstruction: metoclopramide or domperidone
(avoid in complete obstruction). Cyclizine if colic developed
- Complete bowel obstruction: cyclizine or hyoscine butylbromide (avoid
prokinetics).
- Raised ICP: dexamethasone + cyclizine
- Multifactorial/refractory: levomepromazine (broad spectrum).
3. Supportive measures:
- Small, frequent meals; avoid strong odours.
- Adequate hydration (oral/SC fluids depending on goals of care).
- Psychological support/reassurance.
4. MDT involvement:
- Palliative care team for syringe driver if symptoms persistent.
- District nursing for ongoing support.
5. Safety netting:
- “If you keep being sick despite the medication, or it stops you keeping
tablets down, let us know quickly so we can switch to injections or a
syringe pump.”
- “If vomiting becomes sudden, severe, or with blood, please let us know
urgently.”
6. SCA phrases:
- “I understand nausea can be just as distressing as pain, and we’ll aim to
get this under control.”
- “There are different types of anti-sickness medicines, and we’ll choose
one tailored to the cause.”
- “If tablets don’t stay down, we can give the medicine through the skin
with a small pump to keep you comfortable.”
- “The main focus is on your comfort and quality of life.”
- “We’ll review and adjust quickly until you’re more settled.”

-- 286 of 437 --

266- Nausea & Vomiting in a Palliative
Patient After Starting Morphine
Definition: Nausea and vomiting are
common side effects of opioids (up to
30%), usually within the first 1–2
weeks of treatment as the body
adapts.
When to suspect / Diagnostic
features:
- New nausea/vomiting temporally
related to opioid initiation or dose
increase
- No other obvious GI or metabolic
cause
- Typically settles after 5–7 days if
managed
Red Flags:
- Vomiting blood / coffee-ground
vomit
- Projectile vomiting, severe
headache, blurred vision (↑ ICP)
- Persistent colicky pain, abdominal
distension, constipation → bowel
obstruction
- Severe dehydration or inability to
keep fluids down
Focused History:
- Timing: onset in relation to starting/increasing morphine.
- Symptoms: nausea, vomiting, appetite loss, hydration status.
- Other side effects: constipation, drowsiness, confusion.
- Associated red flags: haematemesis, bowel obstruction
symptoms, headache/neurological symptoms.
- Medication review: morphine dose, other opioids, laxatives,
antiemetics.
- Impact: eating, sleeping, distress.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Feeling sick is a common side effect when first starting morphine.”
- “It usually settles in a week or so, as your body gets used to the
medicine.”
- “We can give you a sickness tablet that works particularly well for
morphine-related nausea.”
1. Symptom control (opioid-related nausea):
- First line: haloperidol (0.5–1.5 mg nocte, oral or SC).
- Alternatives: metoclopramide (if no obstruction), levomepromazine
(broad spectrum if refractory).
- Avoid cyclizine as first line.
2. Continue morphine:
- Reassure that nausea often settles within 5–7 days.
- Do not stop opioid if pain well-controlled unless nausea is
unmanageable.
3. Supportive care:
- Ensure co-prescribed laxatives (constipation worsens nausea).
- Encourage small, frequent meals, fluids.
- Consider syringe driver if oral route not tolerated.
4. Review & monitoring:
- Reassess in 24–48h.
- If intolerable despite antiemetics → consider opioid switch (e.g.
oxycodone, fentanyl).
5. Safety netting:
- “If the nausea doesn’t improve in a few days, or you can’t keep
medicines down, please let us know quickly so we can switch to an
injection or a continuous pump.”
- “If you vomit blood, develop sudden tummy swelling and pain, or severe
headache with vomiting, seek urgent help.”
6. SCA phrases:
- “This sickness is a common short-term effect of morphine and usually
improves.”
- “We’ll give you an anti-sickness medicine tailored to this cause.”
- “We don’t need to stop the morphine, as it’s helping your pain, but we’ll
control the nausea alongside it.”
- “If tablets are hard to keep down, we can give the medicine under the
skin to keep you comfortable.”
- “We’ll review this together and adjust quickly if it doesn’t improve.”

-- 287 of 437 --

267- Uncontrolled Pain in a Palliative
Patient on Oral Morphine
Definition: Breakthrough or
uncontrolled cancer/palliative pain
despite regular oral morphine.
When to suspect / Diagnostic
features:
- Persistent pain despite appropriate
regular morphine dose
- Frequent need for breakthrough
medication (>3–4 doses/24h)
- Escalating pain severity
- Pain interfering with sleep, mobility,
or basic care
Red Flags:
- Sudden change in pain character
(neuropathic, colicky, new site)
- Features of spinal cord compression
(weakness, sensory loss,
bladder/bowel disturbance)
- Bowel obstruction (vomiting,
distension, no bowel movement)
- Delirium/confusion due to opioid
toxicity
Focused History:
- Pain assessment: site, character (burning, stabbing, dull), severity
(0–10 scale), duration, triggers.
- Pattern: constant vs breakthrough pain, night waking, impact on
function.
- Current regimen: morphine dose (modified release + immediate
release), frequency of breakthrough use, adherence.
- Side effects: nausea, constipation, drowsiness, hallucinations.
- Other meds: adjuvants (gabapentin, NSAIDs, steroids).
- Red flag symptoms: weakness, bladder/bowel issues, new
neurological deficit, bowel obstruction.
- Patient concerns: fear of dose escalation, addiction, sedation.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Pain can sometimes break through even when you’re taking
regular morphine.”
- “We use both long-acting morphine for background pain and short-
acting for breakthrough pain.”
- “If you’re needing lots of breakthrough doses, it’s a sign your
regular dose may need adjusting.”
- “We’ll keep you comfortable by increasing your background dose
safely and reviewing closely.”
Exam pearl: Always link breakthrough use → need to adjust
background dose. Examiners look for safe opioid titration, mention
of side effect prevention, and awareness of red flags like cord
compression.
1. Optimise opioid dosing:
- Check use of breakthrough morphine (immediate release = 1/6 of total
24h dose).
- If >3–4 breakthrough doses/24h → increase background (modified
release) morphine accordingly.
- Ensure correct conversion if changing route (oral → SC via syringe driver if
swallowing difficult).
2. Manage side effects:
- Continue laxatives routinely.
- Anti-emetics (haloperidol, metoclopramide, levomepromazine) if
nausea.
3. Consider pain type & adjuvants:
- Neuropathic → gabapentin, amitriptyline.
- Bone pain → NSAIDs, steroids, bisphosphonates.
- Liver capsule/stretch pain → steroids.
4. Specialist input:
- Refer / discuss with palliative care team if complex pain or opioid
toxicity.
5. Safety netting:
- “If pain suddenly worsens, or if you notice new weakness, numbness, or
problems with bladder or bowels, please seek urgent help.”
- “If you feel very drowsy, confused, or your breathing slows, let us know –
it may be a sign your dose is too high.”
6. SCA phrases:
- “I can see you’ve been needing frequent breakthrough doses, which tells
me your background morphine needs increasing.”
- “We’ll adjust your regular dose to keep you more comfortable, while still
giving you top-up doses if needed.”
- “Sometimes, different types of pain need additional medicines alongside
morphine, and we can add these in if appropriate.”
- “We’ll review you closely and can involve the palliative care team for
extra support.”
- “My aim is to keep you comfortable, safe, and able to do the things that
matter to you.”

-- 288 of 437 --

268- Starting & Escalating Morphine in
a Palliative Patient
Definition: Morphine is the first-line
strong opioid for moderate-to-severe
cancer/palliative pain. Safe initiation
and dose titration are essential to
balance pain relief with minimising
side effects.
When to suspect / Indications:
- Moderate to severe cancer/palliative
pain not controlled by
paracetamol/NSAIDs or weak opioids
- Frequent breakthrough pain despite
step 2 analgesics (e.g. codeine,
tramadol)
- Patient expressing uncontrolled pain
interfering with sleep, mobility, or
quality of life
Red Flags (before/during opioids):
- Sudden neurological symptoms
(spinal cord compression)
- Acute bowel obstruction (colicky
pain, distension, vomiting)
- Opioid toxicity: confusion,
hallucinations, myoclonus,
drowsiness, shallow breathing
Focused History:
- Pain assessment: site, severity (0–10), character, breakthrough
episodes, effect on daily life.
- Current analgesia: paracetamol, NSAIDs, weak opioids.
- Opioid history: previous exposure, tolerance, side effects.
- Other symptoms: nausea, constipation, sedation.
- Renal/hepatic function: (dose adjustment if impaired).
- Concerns: fears about addiction, “end of life” stigma with
morphine, drowsiness.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Morphine is the standard strong painkiller we use in palliative
care.”
- “We usually start with small, regular doses, and give extra doses for
breakthrough pain.”
- “The aim is to keep you comfortable, not to make you drowsy or
shorten life.”
- “We’ll adjust the dose gradually depending on how much
breakthrough pain you need.”
Exam pearls for SCA:
• Always mention laxatives and antiemetics.
• Show safe titration using breakthrough doses.
• Address opioid fears (addiction, life-shortening).
• Know when to switch to SC syringe driver.
1. Starting morphine (opioid naïve patient):
- Start with immediate-release morphine (e.g. 5mg PO 4-hourly) + the
same dose PRN for breakthrough pain.
- Alternatively, modified-release morphine (e.g. 10mg PO 12-hourly) +
breakthrough morphine (1/6 of total daily dose).
- Always prescribe laxatives (senna + docusate, or macrogol) and
antiemetic (haloperidol/metoclopramide PRN).
2. Dose escalation:
- Review breakthrough use: if patient needs >3–4 breakthrough doses/24h
→ increase background dose.
- Calculate new 24h dose (background + breakthrough used) → set new
regular dose.
- Adjust by 30–50% increments depending on pain and side effects.
3. Alternative routes:
- If oral route not tolerated → use subcutaneous syringe driver (morphine
SC, or diamorphine if renal impairment).
4. Monitoring & side effects:
- Watch for constipation, nausea, drowsiness.
- Reassure that tolerance develops to nausea/sedation but not
constipation.
5. Safety netting:
- “If you feel very drowsy, confused, or your breathing slows, please let us
know urgently.”
- “If pain suddenly worsens in a new way, or you get leg weakness or
bladder/bowel problems, seek urgent help.”
6. SCA phrases:
- “We’ll start morphine at a low dose and adjust gradually to keep you
comfortable.”
- “You’ll also have a breakthrough dose to take if the pain flares.”
- “It’s important to always take a laxative with morphine to prevent
constipation.”
- “Needing frequent breakthrough doses tells us we should increase your
background dose.”
- “Our goal is to keep you comfortable and safe, while respecting your
wishes at every step.”

-- 289 of 437 --

269- Palliative Patient Requesting
Fentanyl Patch (not tolerating
morphine)
Definition: Fentanyl patches are
strong opioids delivered
transdermally, useful if oral opioids
are not tolerated or if swallowing is
difficult.
When to suspect / Indications:
- Intolerable side effects on oral
morphine (e.g., hallucinations, severe
nausea, intractable constipation,
confusion, opioid toxicity)
- Poor oral intake, difficulty
swallowing, unreliable absorption
(e.g., bowel obstruction, cachexia)
- Patient preference after informed
discussion
Red Flags:
- Acute uncontrolled pain needing
rapid titration (fentanyl patch
unsuitable for acute titration – onset
12–24h, steady state 48–72h)
- Features of opioid toxicity
(drowsiness, hallucinations,
myoclonus, shallow breathing)
- Misconception of patch as
“weaker/safer” than morphine
Focused History:
- Symptoms: nature of pain, current level, breakthrough frequency.
- Opioid history: morphine dose, side effects (nausea, constipation,
sedation, hallucinations).
- Function/swallowing: can patient take oral meds reliably?
- Concerns: fears about morphine (addiction, stigma, “end of life
drug”), preference for patch convenience.
- Red flags: sudden pain changes (spinal cord compression, bowel
obstruction), opioid toxicity features.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Fentanyl patches are a strong painkiller option when morphine
tablets cause problems.”
- “The patch works slowly and steadily over 3 days, so it’s not
suitable for sudden pain changes.”
- “We still need to give you small doses of a quick-acting painkiller
for breakthrough pain alongside the patch.”
- “Switching opioids is safe but needs careful dose conversion and
monitoring
1. Opioid switch (if morphine not tolerated):
- Convert total 24h oral morphine dose → equivalent fentanyl patch (use
conversion chart; e.g., 60mg oral morphine ≈ 25mcg/hr fentanyl patch).
- Prescribe short-acting oral morphine (or SC diamorphine if not
tolerated) for breakthrough pain.
- Continue laxatives (though fentanyl causes less constipation).
2. Key counselling points:
- Onset delayed (12–24h, steady state at 72h).
- Never cut patches; change every 72h.
- Avoid external heat (sauna, hot baths) → risk of overdose.
- Monitor closely in first 72h for toxicity.
3. Alternative options if fentanyl unsuitable:
- Oxycodone, alfentanil, hydromorphone (specialist advice).
- Syringe driver with SC opioids if rapid titration needed.
4. Safety netting:
- “If pain is not controlled within a couple of days, or you need lots of
breakthrough doses, please let us know quickly.”
- “If you become very drowsy, confused, or your breathing becomes
shallow, stop the patch and seek urgent help.”
5. SCA phrases:
- “I understand morphine has been difficult for you – fentanyl patches are
a good alternative in some cases.”
- “The patch gives a steady level of pain relief, but it takes a day or so to
fully kick in, so we’ll keep quick-relief tablets alongside.”
- “Switching safely requires us to calculate the right equivalent dose and
monitor you closely.”
- “We’ll keep reviewing your comfort and can adjust quickly if the patch
isn’t enough.”
- “My goal is to keep you comfortable, safe, and supported while
respecting your preferences.”
Exam pearl: Fentanyl patch = good for stable, continuous pain, not
good for acute titration. Always show safe conversion + mention
breakthrough opioid in your answer.

-- 290 of 437 --

270- Discussing Advance Care
Planning (ACP) with a Palliative
Patient
Definition: ACP is a voluntary
process where patients discuss and
record their preferences for future
care, including place of care,
treatment options, and preferred
priorities.
When to suspect / Indications:
- Palliative diagnosis with limited
prognosis (advanced cancer, organ
failure, frailty)
- Patient or family asks about “what
happens next”
- Declining function, recurrent
admissions, or loss of decision-
making capacity risk
Red Flags:
- Patient not ready / feels
overwhelmed by timing of
conversation
- Depression, hopelessness, or
existential distress (may need
psychological/spiritual support first)
- Conflict within family about
decisions
Focused History (exploratory & supportive):
- Patient understanding: “What is your understanding of your
current illness?” I’d like to talk together about what’s happening with
your health and what matters to you. Would this be okay?
- Concerns: fears about future, worries about being a burden, loss of
independence.
- Values & priorities: symptom control vs prolonging life, place of
care (home, hospice, hospital).
- Preferences: resuscitation, hospital admission, life-prolonging
treatment (IV antibiotics, NG feeding).
- Support system: family, carers, community nurses.
- Readiness: check emotional state before going deep.
- What are your biggest worries
- What are the most important goals if your health gets worse
- If, things get worse, how much would you be willing to got through
for the possibility of more time?
- What are your hopes about your health
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Advance Care Planning means thinking ahead and sharing your
wishes for future care.”
- “It’s about making sure your voice is heard, even if you’re too
unwell to speak for yourself later.”
- “It’s not about giving up; it’s about making sure care is in line with
what matters most to you.”
When asking how long will I stay
“How long have I got left, doctor?”
“That’s a very important and understandable question.”
“It sounds like you’ve been thinking a lot about the future.”
“Can I check what has prompted you to ask today?”
“It’s very hard to give an exact time – everyone’s illness can
behave differently.”
“Some people live for months, some shorter, and some
longer than we expect.”
“Doctors often get it wrong when predicting exact times.”
1. Explore patient wishes gently:
- Use open questions: “What matters most to you as we think about the
future?”
- Allow silence, validate emotions.
2. Discuss key elements of ACP:
- Preferred priorities of care: where they’d like to be cared for. Including
home, hospice, and hospital
- Treatment escalation plans
- Advance statement: general wishes/values.
- Advance decision to refuse treatment (ADRT): legally binding refusal of
specific treatments (e.g., resuscitation, ventilation).
- DNACPR: discussion in line with patient’s wishes.
- Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA): appointing someone to make
decisions if capacity lost.
- Financial support: SR1
3. Provide reassurance:
- ACP is voluntary, not legally binding (except ADRT).
- Can be changed at any time.
- Aim is comfort, dignity, and respecting wishes.
4. Offer documentation & support:
- Record in GP/palliative notes, share with out-of-hours, ambulance
service, and carers (update EPaCCS after consent
- Offer written info and time to reflect.
- Involve specialist palliative care if complex.
- Discuss available support if things getting worse both physically,
socially, emotionally, and financially
5. Safety netting:
- “You don’t need to decide everything today – we can revisit this whenever
you wish.”
- “If you change your mind, we’ll update your plan immediately.”
- “We’ll keep you supported, whatever decisions you make.”
6. SCA phrases:
- “Would it be okay if we talked about planning ahead for your care?”
- “Many people find it reassuring to know their wishes will be respected in
the future.”
- “This is not about giving up, but about making sure your care reflects
your values.”
- “You can change your mind at any time – nothing is fixed.”
- “We’ll make sure your family and healthcare team know your wishes.”

-- 291 of 437 --

ACP Tool Description Legal Status Exam Tip / How to Explain
Advance Statement General wishes, preferences, beliefs, values (e.g., “I’d like to
die at home”, “I value being comfortable and alert”). Not legally binding Must be taken into
account when making best-interest decisions.
“This helps your healthcare team understand what matters most to
you. It’s not legally binding, but it guides our decisions.”
Advance Decision to
Refuse Treatment
(ADRT)
A written statement refusing specific medical treatments in
future (e.g., “I do not want to be put on a ventilator if my
cancer worsens”).
Legally binding (if valid and applicable to the
situation).
“This is legally binding – if you’ve clearly refused a treatment in
advance, doctors must respect that decision, even if you can’t speak
for yourself at the time.”
Do Not Attempt CPR
(DNACPR)
Instruction not to attempt cardiopulmonary resuscitation. Can
be patient- or clinician-initiated.
Legally respected medical order (not a legal
document, but must be followed by clinicians unless
circumstances change).
“A DNACPR means doctors won’t try resuscitation if your heart
stops. It’s not a legal contract, but it’s a clear medical instruction
that is respected across healthcare settings.”
Lasting Power of
Attorney (LPA for Health
& Welfare)
Legal appointment of a trusted person to make decisions on
your behalf if you lose capacity. Must be registered with the
Office of the Public Guardian.
Legally binding once activated (when patient
lacks capacity).
“If you choose, you can legally appoint someone you trust to make
healthcare decisions for you. This has legal power if you’re unable
to decide for yourself.”

271- Palliative Patient with Persistent
Hiccups
Definition: Persistent hiccups
(singultus) lasting >48 hours in a
patient receiving palliative care.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Hiccups lasting >48 hours.
- Interfering with eating, drinking,
sleep, or causing distress.
- Associated with nausea, vomiting,
weight loss, or chest/abdominal
symptoms.
Red Flags:
- Sudden neurological symptoms
(stroke, raised ICP, brain mets).
- Severe vomiting / dehydration.
- Chest pain, breathlessness
(cardiac/resp causes).
- GI obstruction signs.
Focused History:
- Onset & duration: when did hiccups start, continuous vs
intermittent, duration >48 hrs.
- Impact: on eating, drinking, sleep, quality of life.
- Associated symptoms: nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, reflux,
bloating, dysphagia, headache, neurological changes.
- Medical history: cancer type/stage, recent chemo/radiotherapy,
CNS disease, GI pathology.
- Drug history: steroids, opioids, benzodiazepines (all can
contribute).
- Social context: family concerns, goals of care.
Explanation in lay terms:
- “Hiccups are caused by irritation of the nerve or muscle that
controls breathing.”
- “In advanced illness they can be due to things like reflux,
medications, or pressure from the cancer.”
- “Our aim is to reduce the hiccups and keep you comfortable.”
1. Address reversible causes (if appropriate):
- Review & adjust meds (steroids, opioids).
- Treat reflux / gastritis (PPI, metoclopramide).
- Small, soft meals, upright posture after eating.
2. Pharmacological options (stepwise):
- Metoclopramide 10 mg TDS (dopamine antagonist).
- If ineffective → Haloperidol 0.5–1.5 mg at night.
- Baclofen 5–10 mg TDS (if appropriate).
- Chlorpromazine (licensed but sedating; consider if others fail).
3. Supportive care:
- Maintain hydration, nutrition.
- Reassure patient & family that hiccups are common in advanced illness
and often treatable.
4. Safety net & follow-up:
- Monitor response & side effects.
- Escalate to specialist palliative team if refractory.
SCA phrases:
- “I can see the hiccups are making things really uncomfortable for you.”
- “There are a few possible causes, sometimes medicines or reflux, but
sometimes it’s part of the illness itself.”
- “We’ll start with simple measures like adjusting medicines for reflux and
trying a tablet that can calm the hiccups.”
- “If these don’t work, we have stronger medicines we can try in a stepwise
way.”
- “The goal is to ease the hiccups and make sure you’re able to eat, rest,
and stay comfortable.”

-- 293 of 437 --

272- Agitation / Restlessness in a
Palliative Patient
Definition: Agitation is a common
symptom in advanced illness,
including delirium, pain, hypoxia,
metabolic disturbance, or
psychological distress. It may
manifest as restlessness, aggression,
confusion, or anxiety.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- New-onset restlessness, confusion,
or aggression in a patient with
palliative illness.
- Inability to sleep, sit still, or follow
instructions.
- May be sudden or gradual.
Red Flags:
- Delirium due to infection, metabolic
disturbance, hypoxia, or CNS
pathology (stroke, metastases)
- Severe pain causing agitation
- Anxiety or existential distress
- Medication toxicity (opioids,
steroids, anticholinergics)
Focused History:
- Onset & course: sudden vs gradual, daytime vs night, continuous
or intermittent.
- Associated symptoms: pain, dyspnoea, hallucinations, confusion,
sleep disturbance.
- Medication review: opioids, steroids, anticholinergics, sedatives,
recent changes.
- Hydration/nutrition: poor intake can worsen delirium.
- Environment / psychosocial factors: noisy environment, fear,
unmet spiritual or psychological needs.
Explanation in lay terms:
- “You seem more restless and upset than usual.”
- “Sometimes in advanced illness, the body or mind can get
unsettled due to pain, medicines, or the illness itself.”
- “We can help calm you and find the cause if possible, so you feel
comfortable and safe.”
Exam pearls:
• Always screen for reversible causes first (pain,
constipation, infection, hypoxia).
• Non-pharmacological strategies are first-line if mild.
• Use low-dose antipsychotics for distressing agitation,
midazolam for severe anxiety or end-of-life restlessness.
• Always include safety, monitoring, and family
involvement.
1. Identify & treat reversible causes:
- Pain, dyspnoea, constipation, urinary retention, infection, metabolic
disturbance.
- Adjust or review medications contributing to agitation (opioids, steroids,
anticholinergics).
2. Non-pharmacological measures:
- Calm, quiet environment; familiar objects or people; reassurance.
- Encourage hydration if possible.
- Orient patient frequently (clock, window, explanations).
3. Pharmacological management (if distressing/refractory):
- Haloperidol: 0.5–1 mg SC/PO as needed, titrated cautiously.
- Levomepromazine: 6.25–12.5 mg SC/PRN or continuous if severe.
- Consider midazolam if severe anxiety or agitation not responding to
antipsychotics.
4. Safety & monitoring:
- Monitor sedation, respiratory rate, response to medication.
- Involve family in reassurance, updates, and support.
5. SCA phrases:
- “I can see you’re feeling restless and upset; we’ll try to make you more
comfortable.”
- “Sometimes this happens because of pain, medicines, or the illness
itself, and we can often help.”
- “We can use a small dose of medicine to calm you while we check for
any reversible causes.”
- “We’ll review you frequently and adjust treatment to keep you safe and
comfortable.”
- “We’ll keep your family involved so you’re supported at all times.”

-- 294 of 437 --

273- Carer Burnout / Stress in Family
Caregiver
Definition: Physical, emotional, and
psychological exhaustion
experienced by a caregiver, often
leading to reduced ability to provide
care.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Family member providing
prolonged, intensive care for patient
with chronic/palliative illness.
- Reports of exhaustion, irritability,
sleep disturbance, anxiety, low
mood, or physical symptoms.
- Declining ability to manage care
tasks or increased reliance on
support services.
Red Flags:
- Thoughts of self-harm or
hopelessness.
- Physical health deterioration (weight
loss, hypertension, recurrent illness).
- Inability to meet basic needs of
patient or themselves.
- Social isolation or lack of support
Focused History:
- Caregiving context: hours spent per day, tasks undertaken,
duration of caregiving.
- Impact: sleep, mood, physical health, social relationships, work.
- Symptoms: fatigue, anxiety, irritability, low mood, poor
concentration.
- Support systems: other family members, community/palliative
care services, respite.
- Coping mechanisms: hobbies, exercise, religion/spirituality.
- Red flags: self-neglect, self-harm, inability to safely care for
patient.
Explanation in lay terms:
- “Looking after someone full-time can be exhausting, and it’s
normal to feel stressed or overwhelmed.”
- “Burnout can affect both your health and your ability to care for
your loved one.”
- “There are ways we can support you so you don’t carry this burden
alone.”
Exam pearls:
• Always acknowledge and validate carer stress —
examiners score empathy.
• Screen for mental health issues and safety (suicidal
ideation, severe depression).
• Include practical support, respite, and community
resources.
• Document carer capacity and impact on patient care.
1. Acknowledge & validate:
- Recognise the effort and stress they are under.
- Reassure that needing help is normal and responsible.
2. Support & respite:
- Offer respite care (day care, short-term hospice stay).
- Refer to community/palliative support services (carer support groups).
- Explore practical help (home care, family/friends).
- Signpost to Age UK, Carer UK
3. Psychological support:
- GP review for anxiety or depression.
- Referral to counselling, cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), or peer
support groups.
4. Physical health check:
- Blood pressure, weight, sleep, nutrition.
- Encourage exercise, balanced diet, rest.
5. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you feel unable to cope or have thoughts of harming yourself or
others, contact us immediately.”
- Regular check-ins to review stress, mood, and caregiving capacity.
6. SCA phrases:
- “Caring for someone full-time is hard, and it’s normal to feel exhausted.”
- “We can explore ways to give you support so you can rest and look after
your health.”
- “It’s okay to accept help; it doesn’t mean you’re failing.”
- “Let’s check your own health and wellbeing, and put a plan in place to
prevent burnout.”
- “You don’t have to cope alone – there are services, respite, and support
groups available.”
“Respite care gives you a temporary break while ensuring your loved one
continues to receive safe care.”
“It’s a way to prevent caregiver burnout and support your health, without
leaving the patient unattended.”

274- Carer Requesting Sleeping Pills
for a Patient with Dementia
Definition: Sleep disturbance is
common in dementia (sundowning,
nocturnal restlessness), but hypnotic
medications carry high risk of harm
(falls, delirium, cognitive worsening).
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Difficulty falling asleep or staying
asleep, nocturnal agitation,
wandering
- Daytime sleepiness or napping
- Sleep disturbance affecting carer’s
rest and wellbeing
Red Flags:
- New or sudden change in behaviour
or sleep pattern (infection, delirium,
pain)
- Aggressive or self-harming
behaviour
- Unsafe environment (risk of falls if
sedated)
Focused History:
- Sleep pattern: onset, duration, night awakenings, daytime sleep,
naps.
- Current medications: review for sedating drugs, diuretics,
stimulants.
- Behaviour changes: agitation, wandering, aggression,
sundowning.
- Medical causes: pain, constipation, urinary frequency, infection.
- Environment: noise, lighting, bedtime routine, carer support.
- Carer concerns: exhaustion, safety, patient comfort.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Sleep problems are very common in dementia, especially at
night.”
- “Medications to help sleep can sometimes cause confusion, falls,
or make memory worse.”
- “We usually try non-drug strategies first and keep medications only
if really necessary.”
Exam pearls:
• Always explore non-pharmacological options first in
dementia.
• Highlight risk of sedation, falls, delirium with sleeping
pills.
• Include reversible causes of sleep disturbance.
• Show empathy for carer stress while prioritizing patient
safety.
1. Identify reversible causes:
- Pain, urinary frequency, constipation, infection, medication side effects.
- Adjust bedtime routine and environment (quiet, dark, comfortable,
familiar).
2. Non-pharmacological interventions:
- Consistent bedtime routine.
- Reduce daytime naps, light exposure during day.
- Avoid caffeine/alcohol.
- Reassure and orient patient frequently.
3. Pharmacological interventions (last resort, low dose, short term):
- Only if non-drug measures fail and sleep disruption is severe.
- Consider short-acting, low-dose sedative (e.g., melatonin, or short-
term zopiclone 3.75–5 mg) under careful monitoring.
- Avoid benzodiazepines where possible due to fall risk.
4. Safety & monitoring:
- Monitor for confusion, falls, excessive sedation.
- Involve carer in observation.
5. Shared decision-making & documentation:
- Explain risks and benefits clearly.
- Document discussions and rationale for any prescription.
6. SCA phrases:
- “I understand you’re worried about your mum’s sleep and how
exhausting the nights are for you.”
- “We need to be careful with medications because they can worsen
confusion or cause falls.”
- “Let’s try adjusting her routine, environment, and daytime activity first,
which often helps.”
- “If we do consider medication, it would be the lowest effective dose, for
the shortest time, and we’ll review her closely.”
- “Our aim is to improve sleep safely for your mum while also supporting
you.”
275- Dementia Sleep Disturbance

-- 296 of 437 --

Scenario / Symptom Focused History & Explanation Management Plan (with SCA phrases)
Nocturnal Agitation /
Sundowning
- Agitation, confusion, restlessness in evening/night.
- Often due to circadian disruption, fatigue, pain, unmet needs.
- Red flags: sudden delirium, infection, new medication, pain, hypoxia.
- Assess reversible causes (pain, constipation, infection).
- Non-drug: calm environment, dim lighting, familiar routines, reassurance.
- Pharmacological (last resort): low-dose antipsychotics (haloperidol) short-term.
- SCA phrases: “I can see she gets upset at night; we’ll first try gentle adjustments to
routine and environment before any medicines.”
Wandering / Night-time
Mobility
- Safety risk, falls, caregiver exhaustion.
- Red flags: delirium, incontinence, infection, new neurological
symptoms.
- Environmental safety (bed alarms, remove hazards).
- Encourage structured day activities, lighting at night.
- Only consider low-dose sedatives if severe distress and monitored.
- SCA phrases: “We’ll make her environment safe and try to keep her engaged during
the day so nights are calmer.”
Difficulty Falling Asleep /
Early Waking
- Often linked to circadian changes, daytime napping, pain, anxiety.
- Red flags: depression, untreated pain, infection.
- Non-drug: consistent bedtime, reduce naps, increase daytime light exposure,
relaxation techniques.
- Pharmacological: consider melatonin or short-acting hypnotic only if non-drug
measures fail.
- SCA phrases: “We can try adjusting her routine first, which often helps her fall asleep
more easily.”
Carer Exhaustion / Request
for Sleeping Pills
- Night-time disturbance affects carer wellbeing.
- Red flags: caregiver depression, burnout, thoughts of harm, neglect.
- Support: respite, community support, counseling.
- Education: explain risks of hypnotics in dementia.
- Pharmacological: last resort, lowest effective dose, short duration, monitor closely.
- SCA phrases: “I understand the nights are exhausting for you. We need to be careful
with medicines, but we’ll explore safe ways to help both your mum and you get rest.”
Daytime Sleepiness /
Napping
- Common in dementia; may worsen nighttime insomnia.
- Red flags: worsening cognition, sleep apnea, metabolic disturbance.
- Encourage daytime activity, structured routine, light exposure.
- Avoid long naps; short rest periods only.
- SCA phrases: “Short daytime naps can help her rest, but long naps may make night-
time sleep harder, so we’ll try a balanced routine.”
Key SCA tips for dementia + sleep:
1. Always explore reversible causes first: pain, infection, constipation, medication, anxiety.
2. Prioritize non-pharmacological strategies: routines, light exposure, reassurance, structured activities.
3. Medications are last resort: use lowest effective dose, short-term, and monitor. Avoid benzodiazepines if possible.
4. Carer support is essential: respite, counseling, education.
5. Use empathetic phrases that validate both patient and carer.

276- Dementia Patient with Multiple
Concerns: Medications, Travel,
Driving
Definition: Patient with dementia
presenting with questions about
cognitive medications (effectiveness,
side effects), travel plans, and driving
safety.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Patient or carer expresses concern
about memory, medications, and
safety.
- New symptoms or deterioration in
cognition.
- Planning activities that require
judgment, like travel or driving.
Red Flags:
- Rapid cognitive decline, sudden
confusion, falls, or new neurological
deficits.
- Medication side effects: dizziness,
sedation, hypotension.
- Unsafe driving behaviors: near-
misses, confusion while driving.
- Travel risks: inability to manage
medications, fatigue, wandering, or
medical emergencies abroad.
Focused History:
A. Medications:
- Current cognitive meds (donepezil, memantine) and adherence.
- Side effects: nausea, dizziness, confusion, fatigue.
- Patient perception: “Is it helping?”
B. Cognition & function:
- Daily tasks, memory, orientation, decision-making.
- Any new symptoms: confusion, falls, agitation.
C. Driving:
- Last time driven, incidents, confidence, reaction times.
- Legal obligations: reporting cognitive decline to DVLA (UK context).
D. Travel:
- Destination, duration, independence, support system.
- Ability to manage medications, navigation, fatigue, and
emergencies.
E. Carer input:
- Safety concerns, support, supervision needs.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “It’s common to wonder if your memory tablets are working. They
help slow decline, but don’t reverse dementia.”
- “Driving requires good judgment and memory — we need to check
it’s safe for you and others.”
- “Travel is possible, but we need to plan so you can manage
medications, rest, and any emergencies safely.”
- How medications work: “Medications like donepezil, rivastigmine,
or galantamine work by increasing chemicals in the brain that help
nerve cells communicate, which can slow memory decline.”
- “Memantine works differently, helping protect brain cells from
overactivity that can cause damage.”
- “These medicines help slow progression but do not reverse
dementia, so expectations should be realistic.”
- “Driving and travel require good memory and judgment, so we need
to plan carefully to keep you safe.”
1. Medication review:
- Assess effectiveness and side effects.
- Discuss adherence, realistic expectations.
- Consider dose adjustment or switch if tolerated poorly.
2. Cognitive function assessment:
- Brief cognitive assessment (MMSE, MOCA, or GP brief test).
- Check for reversible causes of decline (infection, meds, depression,
sleep disturbance).
3. Driving safety:
- Discuss legal responsibilities (Must inform DVLA).
- Assess recent driving ability, incidents, confidence.
- If risk identified: advise temporary suspension, formal driving
assessment, or alternatives.
4. Travel planning:
- Assess independence, support available.
- Plan for medication management, travel insurance, rest periods.
- Consider destination risks (medical support, language barriers,
emergency access).
5. Patient education & shared decision-making:
- Set realistic expectations about medications and dementia progression.
- Encourage open discussion about safety, independence, and quality of
life.
6. Safety net & follow-up:
- Arrange follow-up to review cognition, medication effects, and plans for
driving or travel.
- Encourage patient/carer to report new symptoms promptly.
7. SCA Phrases:
- “It’s understandable to wonder if your tablets are helping; let’s review
how they’re working and any side effects.”
- “Driving can become unsafe with memory problems, so we need to
check this together and consider alternatives if needed.”
- “If you plan to travel, we can plan carefully to keep you safe and make it
manageable.”
- “We’ll keep monitoring your symptoms and adjust treatment or advice as
needed.”
- “Your independence and safety are both important, so we’ll make
decisions together.”

277- Dementia and agitation
Definition: Agitation in dementia is a
common behavioural and
psychological symptom, presenting
as restlessness, aggression,
irritability, or mood disturbance.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Patient with known dementia
showing new or worsening
restlessness, verbal or physical
aggression, or disruptive behaviours.
- Interferes with daily care, sleep, or
safety.
Red Flags:
- Sudden change in behaviour
(possible delirium, infection,
metabolic disturbance).
- Self-harm or aggression risking
patient or others.
- New neurological signs (stroke,
seizure).
Focused History:
- Onset & pattern: sudden vs gradual, daytime vs night, frequency,
triggers.
- Associated symptoms: pain, constipation, urinary retention,
infection, hallucinations, sleep disturbance.
- Medications: recent changes, sedatives, opioids, anticholinergics.
- Environment: noise, lighting, new caregivers, disruptions.
- Carer perspective: stress, coping, safety concerns.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “You seem more restless or upset than usual, which can happen in
dementia.”
- “Sometimes agitation is caused by pain, infection, or changes in
the environment, not just the dementia itself.”
- “Our aim is to help you stay calm, safe, and comfortable.”
- “It can be very stressful to manage, and it’s normal to feel
exhausted or worried.”
- “There are ways to help your loved one stay calm and to support
you at the same time.”
- “It’s understandable to feel exhausted and worried when your loved
one is agitated.”
- “We can look for causes of agitation and try ways to make your
loved one calmer without medication first.”
- “If needed, there are safe medications we can use carefully.”
- “Let’s also make sure you get support, rest, and help to cope with
these challenges.”
- “We’ll review this together and adjust the plan to keep both of you
safe and comfortable.”
1. Identify & treat reversible causes:
- Pain, constipation, urinary retention, infection, medications.
- Correct environmental triggers (noise, lighting, unfamiliar surroundings).
2. Non-pharmacological interventions:
- Calm, quiet environment; familiar objects; reassurance.
- Structured daily routines, regular meals, hydration, and daytime activity.
- Carer education on handling agitation safely.
3. Pharmacological interventions (if distressing/refractory):
- Haloperidol: 0.5–1 mg SC/PO PRN, low-dose titration.
- Risperidone: 0.25 mg BD
4. Safety & monitoring:
- Monitor sedation, respiratory rate, response to medication.
- Involve family/carer in reassurance and observation.
5. Shared decision-making:
- Discuss goals of care, patient comfort, and safety.
- Document interventions, responses, and carer involvement.
6. Carer support
- Explain triggers, safe handling techniques, and strategies to reduce
agitation.
- Offer respite care, counselling, support groups, or community resources.
- Monitor carer mental and physical health.
- Signpost to https://www.alzheimers.org.uk/ OR
https://youngdementianetwork.org/
7. SCA Phrases:
- “I can see you’re feeling restless and upset; we’ll try to make you more
comfortable.”
- “Sometimes agitation is caused by pain, infection, or the environment,
so we’ll check and manage these first.”
- “If these measures aren’t enough, there are safe medications we can use
at the lowest dose to help.”
- “We’ll involve your family and keep reviewing to ensure you’re safe and
comfortable.”

278- Duty of Candour – Patient or
Family Concern Following an Incident
Definition: Duty of candour is a legal
and professional obligation for
healthcare professionals to be open
and honest with patients when
something goes wrong in their care,
causing (or potentially causing) harm.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Patient/family raises concern about
an error, adverse outcome, or
unexpected complication.
- Staff or documentation reveal an
incident or near-miss.
Red Flags:
- Serious harm to patient (e.g.,
medication error, delayed diagnosis,
surgical complication).
- Emotional distress or loss of trust
from patient/family.
- Legal or regulatory risk if duty of
candour not followed.
Focused History:
- Patient/family perspective: “What happened?” “What are you
concerned about?”
- Timeline & facts: When incident occurred, who was involved, what
was done immediately.
- Impact: Physical harm, psychological distress, ongoing symptoms.
- Communication so far: Has anyone explained what happened?
- Patient expectations: Apology, explanation, reassurance,
corrective action.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Duty of candour means we have a responsibility to be honest with
you when something goes wrong.”
- “We will explain exactly what happened, what we are doing to make
things right, and how we will prevent it happening again.”
- “Our goal is to support you, answer your questions, and maintain
trust.”
1. Immediate response:
- Acknowledge the incident promptly and express empathy.
- Apologize if appropriate: “I’m sorry this happened.”
2. Explain the facts clearly:
- Provide accurate, factual explanation of what occurred.
- Avoid speculation or assigning blame.
3. Outline remedial actions:
- Describe steps taken to mitigate harm or prevent recurrence.
- Offer follow-up care if needed.
4. Documentation:
- Record details of incident, discussion, apology, and agreed actions.
- Notify relevant internal governance or risk management teams.
5. Support for patient/family:
- Emotional support, counselling if needed.
- Information on complaints procedures or independent advocacy.
6. Safety net & follow-up:
- Arrange review or monitoring of patient outcomes.
- Update patient/family on progress of investigations or systemic changes.
7. SCA Phrases:
- “I want to be honest with you about what happened, as part of our duty of
candour.”
- “We take this seriously and will explain the facts and what we are doing
to make things right.”
- “I’m sorry this has caused distress, and we will support you through
this.”
- “We will ensure steps are taken to prevent this happening again and keep
you informed.”
- “Everything we discuss will be documented and followed up
appropriately.”

279- Polypharmacy in Elderly Patient
Definition: Polypharmacy is
commonly defined as use of five or
more medications concurrently,
which increases the risk of adverse
drug events, interactions, and
medication non-adherence.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Elderly patient (>65) taking multiple
medications for chronic conditions.
- Reports side effects, falls, dizziness,
cognitive changes, or fatigue.
- Carer or patient expresses difficulty
managing medications.
Red Flags:
- Confusion, falls, syncope,
hypotension, hypoglycemia,
bleeding.
- Drug interactions (e.g.,
anticoagulants + NSAIDs).
- Non-adherence due to complex
regimens.
Focused History:
- Medication list: all prescription, OTC, herbal supplements.
- Indication: reason for each medication, start/stop dates.
- Side effects: dizziness, falls, nausea, constipation, sedation,
cognitive changes.
- Adherence & understanding: difficulty remembering doses,
skipped medications.
- Functional impact: frailty, falls, confusion, mood changes.
- Carer input: support with medications, observations of adverse
effects.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “You are taking many medications, which can sometimes interact
and cause side effects.”
- “Polypharmacy can affect your balance, memory, and overall
wellbeing.”
- “We want to review your medicines to make sure each one is
necessary and safe.”
Examples of Medications Commonly Deprescribed in Elderly
• Benzodiazepines: sedation, falls, cognitive impairment.
• Anticholinergics: confusion, constipation, urinary
retention.
• Proton pump inhibitors: long-term use without clear
indication.
• Statins: in frail elderly or palliative patients with limited
benefit.
• NSAIDs: risk of GI bleeding, renal impairment.
• Duplicate therapies: e.g., two antihypertensives when one
is sufficient.
1. Comprehensive medication review:
- Assess indication, effectiveness, safety, and necessity of each drug.
- Check for drug interactions, duplications, and contraindications.
2. Deprescribing if appropriate:
- Consider stopping medications where harms outweigh benefits.
- Prioritize high-risk drugs (sedatives, anticholinergics, NSAIDs,
hypoglycemics).
3. Simplify regimen:
- Reduce frequency, use combination pills, align dosing times.
- Provide clear instructions, pill boxes, or blister packs.
4. Monitor & follow-up:
- Check for symptom improvement, side effects, adherence.
- Adjust medications gradually to avoid withdrawal effects.
5. Patient education:
- Explain purpose, benefits, and risks of each medication.
- Emphasize reporting side effects promptly.
6. Carer involvement:
- Support with medication administration and monitoring.
7. SCA Phrases:
- “I see you’re taking several medications, so it’s important we review
them to make sure they’re all needed and safe.”
- “Some medications can cause dizziness, fatigue, or memory issues, so
we may simplify your regimen.”
- “We’ll prioritize medicines that give the most benefit and consider
stopping others if appropriate.”
- “We can use aids like pill boxes to make it easier to manage your
medicines safely.”
- “You and your carer can help monitor for any side effects or problems
and let us know promptly.”

-- 301 of 437 --

280- Suspected asthma in adults
Definition: Chronic inflammatory
airway disease characterized by
variable airflow obstruction, airway
hyperresponsiveness, and symptoms
such as wheeze, cough, shortness
of breath, and chest tightness.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Recurrent wheeze, cough
(especially at night or early morning),
shortness of breath triggered by
allergens, exercise, cold air, or
respiratory infections.
- Symptoms improve with
bronchodilators.
Red Flags:
- Severe shortness of breath, inability
to speak full sentences.
- Saturations <92% or accessory
muscle use.
- Previous severe exacerbations or
ICU admissions.
- Failure to respond to initial reliever
inhaler.
Focused History:
A. Symptom history:
- Wheeze, cough, chest tightness, dyspnea: frequency, severity,
triggers, nocturnal symptoms.
- Pattern: seasonal, exercise-induced, occupational exposures.
B. Past medical history:
- Atopy, eczema, allergic rhinitis, previous respiratory conditions.
- Family history of asthma or atopy.
C. Medication history:
- Reliever inhalers, previous steroid courses, adherence.
D. Lifestyle & triggers:
- Smoking, pets, dust, occupational exposures, exercise.
E. Impact on daily life:
- Sleep disturbance, activity limitation, school/work absenteeism.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Asthma is when the airways in your lungs become sensitive and
narrow sometimes, causing wheeze, cough, or breathlessness.”
- “It can be triggered by things like exercise, allergies, or infections,
and it usually improves with inhalers.”
- “With proper diagnosis and treatment, we can control your
symptoms and prevent attacks.”
Explaining Spirometry
• “We can measure your lung function with a test called
spirometry.”
• “You’ll blow into a machine as hard as you can. This
measures how much air you can blow out and how
quickly.”
• “We’ll also give you a puff of a reliever inhaler to see if your
lungs improve, which helps confirm asthma.”
Explaining Peak Flow Monitoring
• “Sometimes symptoms vary from day to day, so we can use
a peak flow meter at home.”
• “It measures how fast you can blow air out of your lungs,
and we can track changes over time.”
• “Large variations in readings suggest asthma.”
1. Initial assessment & investigations:
- Physical exam: wheeze, prolonged expiration.
- Peak flow variability, spirometry if available.
- Consider FeNO or allergy testing if indicated.
2. AIR or MART therapy
- Stepwise escalation per GINA/NICE guidelines.
3. Trigger management & lifestyle:
- Smoking cessation, allergen avoidance, exercise management.
4. Safety & self-management:
- Asthma action plan: recognize early signs, when to seek help.
- Review inhaler technique at every visit.
5. Follow-up:
- Review symptoms and peak flow after 4–6 weeks.
- Adjust treatment according to control and side effects.
6. SCA Phrases:
- “Your symptoms suggest asthma, but we need to confirm with tests and
see how you respond to inhalers.”
- “Let’s review your triggers and lifestyle so we can help prevent flare-
ups.”
- “I’ll teach you how to use the inhaler correctly and make an action plan
for attacks.”
- “With proper treatment, most people can lead a normal, active life.”
FeNO measures the amount of nitric oxide in your breath, which is a
marker of airway inflammation, particularly eosinophilic inflammation
seen in asthma.
“This is a simple breath test that measures inflammation in your lungs.”
“It helps us see if your symptoms are caused by asthma and if inhalers will
be effective.”
“You just blow steadily into a device for a few seconds — it’s painless and
quick.”
“The result can help guide the right treatment and prevent attacks.”
