-- 303 of 437 --

281- Asthma Exacerbation (Acute
Attack)
Definition: Worsening of asthma
symptoms over hours to days, often
triggered by infection, allergens, or
poor adherence.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Increased wheeze, cough,
breathlessness, chest tightness.
- Symptoms worse at night or with
exertion.
- Poor response to usual reliever
inhaler.
Red Flags (Severe / Life-
Threatening):
- Inability to speak full sentences,
accessory muscle use.
- Respiratory rate >30/min, HR >120
bpm.
- Oxygen saturation <92%.
- Peak flow <50% of personal best.
- Silent chest on auscultation.
- Previous ICU admission for asthma.
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, and severity of symptoms.
- Triggers: infection, allergen, exercise, medication non-adherence.
- Current medications: inhalers (reliever/controller), adherence,
technique.
- Past exacerbations: frequency, severity, hospitalizations.
- Red flag symptoms: confusion, exhaustion, bluish lips/fingertips.
- Impact on daily activities.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Your asthma is flaring up, which means your airways are more
narrowed and inflamed than usual.”
- “This is why you are wheezing, short of breath, and having chest
tightness.”
- “We need to act promptly to relieve your symptoms and prevent
them from worsening.”
1. Assess severity (mild, moderate, severe, life-threatening):
- Based on symptoms, vital signs, oxygen saturation, and peak flow.
2. Immediate treatment:
- Mild/moderate: Increase reliever (SABA) inhaler use, oral steroids if
needed, monitor.
- Severe: High-dose SABA, oxygen if needed, oral/IV steroids, consider
hospital referral.
- Life-threatening: Call emergency services, oxygen, continuous
nebulized SABA, IV steroids, ICU if required.
3. Identify triggers & optimize baseline therapy:
- Review inhaler technique and adherence.
- Address infection, allergens, or medication non-adherence.
4. Safety netting & follow-up:
- Clear instructions on warning signs (worsening breathlessness, blue lips,
inability to speak).
- Arrange follow-up within 1–2 days after exacerbation for reassessment
and step-up therapy if needed.
5. SCA Phrases:
- “You are having an asthma flare, which narrows your airways and makes
breathing difficult.”
- “We need to use your inhaler more frequently and may start a short
course of steroids to reduce inflammation.”
- “If your symptoms worsen, you should seek help immediately or call
emergency services.”
- “We’ll review your inhaler technique and triggers to prevent future
attacks.”
- “Our goal is to relieve your symptoms quickly and prevent
complications.”

-- 304 of 437 --

282- Newly Suspected COPD
(Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary
Disease)
Definition: COPD is a chronic,
progressive disease characterized by
airflow limitation that is not fully
reversible, usually caused by
smoking or environmental
exposures.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Chronic cough, sputum production,
and exertional breathlessness.
- Age >40 years, history of smoking or
long-term exposure to irritants.
- Recurrent chest infections or
wheeze.
Red Flags:
- Severe breathlessness at rest,
cyanosis, confusion.
- Signs of acute exacerbation:
tachypnea, tachycardia, hypoxia.
- Weight loss, cachexia, or signs of
cor pulmonale.
Focused History:
- Respiratory symptoms: chronic cough, sputum (colour, volume),
wheeze, breathlessness (MRC dyspnoea scale).
- Exacerbation history: frequency, severity, hospitalizations.
- Risk factors: smoking (pack-years), occupational exposures,
biomass fuels.
- Impact on daily life: exercise tolerance, activities, sleep.
- Comorbidities: cardiovascular disease, diabetes.
- Medication history: inhalers, previous treatments.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “COPD is a long-term lung condition that makes breathing harder
over time due to narrowed airways.”
- “It is usually caused by smoking or long-term exposure to dust or
fumes.”
- “Symptoms include cough, mucus, wheeze, and shortness of
breath.”
- “We can do tests to confirm the diagnosis and start treatments to
help your breathing and quality of life.”
“Pulmonary rehab is a program to help you breathe easier and
improve your fitness safely.”
“It combines exercise, education, and techniques to manage your
symptoms better.”
“You’ll learn breathing exercises and ways to do daily activities with
less breathlessness.”
“It has been shown to reduce hospital visits and improve your
confidence in managing COPD.”
“The exercises are supervised and tailored to your abilities, so you
won’t be overexerted.”
1. Diagnosis confirmation:
- Spirometry: post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.7 confirms airflow
limitation.
- Optional: CXR, CT, or lab tests to rule out other conditions.
2. Smoking cessation:
- Offer support, pharmacotherapy (varenicline, nicotine replacement).
3. Vaccinations:
- Annual influenza, pneumococcal, COVID boosters.
4. Pharmacological management (per GOLD/NICE guidelines):
- Mild: SABA as needed.
- Moderate: Add long-acting bronchodilator (LABA or LAMA).
- Severe: Consider combination therapy (LABA+LAMA, ICS if frequent
exacerbations).
- Short courses of steroids/antibiotics for exacerbations.
5. Non-pharmacological interventions:
- Pulmonary rehabilitation: It is a program designed to help people with
lung conditions, like COPD , breathe better and improve their quality of
life. It combines exercise training, education on managing symptoms, and
breathing techniques. The goal is to strengthen your lungs and muscles,
reduce breathlessness, and help you stay active. It is usually 6–8-
week programs, outpatient or community-based. It can be repeated
annually or after exacerbations if beneficial.
- Nutritional support, oxygen assessment if hypoxic.
6. Safety net & follow-up:
- Educate about exacerbation warning signs.
- Plan review every 3–6 months for inhaler technique, symptoms, and
comorbidities.
7. SCA Phrases:
- “Your symptoms suggest COPD, a chronic lung condition, but we’ll
confirm with a breathing test called spirometry.”
- “Stopping smoking is the most important thing to slow progression.”
- “We can start inhalers to help your breathing and reduce flare-ups.”
- “Pulmonary rehab and exercise can make you feel less breathless.”
- “We’ll review you regularly and provide an action plan for flare-ups.”

283- Lung caner with persistent cough
Definition: Cough is a common
symptom in lung cancer patients,
either from the tumor itself, post-
obstructive infection, or treatment-
related changes.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Known lung cancer with ongoing or
worsening cough.
- Associated with haemoptysis, chest
pain, breathlessness, hoarse voice,
or weight loss.
- History of smoking or occupational
exposure.
Red Flags:
- Haemoptysis.
- New/worsening breathlessness or
stridor.
- Severe chest pain.
- Unexplained weight loss or systemic
decline.
- Signs of post-obstructive
pneumonia.
Focused History:
- Cough characteristics: duration, frequency, dry vs productive,
blood-streaked.
- Associated symptoms: haemoptysis, chest pain, breathlessness,
wheeze, systemic features.
- Treatment history: chemo, radiotherapy, surgery, immunotherapy
(and side effects).
- Impact on daily life: sleep disturbance, fatigue, anxiety.
- Social history: smoking, alcohol, support at home.
- Ideas, concerns, expectations: worries about worsening cancer,
fear of spread.
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “A persistent cough can happen in lung cancer because the tumor
irritates the airways or due to infections or treatment effects.”
- “It doesn’t always mean the cancer is worsening, but we should
check carefully.”
- “We can manage the cough itself to make you more comfortable
and also look for any underlying cause.”
1. Exclude red flags / acute issues:
- If haemoptysis, stridor, or severe breathlessness → urgent
hospital/specialist referral.
- If infection suspected → CXR, sputum culture, antibiotics.
2. Symptom control:
- Cough suppressants (codeine linctus, low-dose morphine if distressing).
- Nebulised saline, humidified air.
- Steroids if post-radiotherapy inflammation.
3. Ongoing cancer care:
- Liaise with oncology/palliative team regarding radiotherapy for local
control, or stenting if airway obstruction.
4. Holistic & supportive care:
- Explore patient’s understanding and anxieties.
- Offer psychosocial support, palliative care involvement if appropriate.
- Smoking cessation support if still smoking.
5. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you cough up blood, feel more breathless, or develop chest pain,
please seek urgent help.”
- Arrange review after investigations or with oncology team.
6. SCA Phrases:
- “I can see this cough is troubling you — it’s common in lung cancer, but
we’ll look at ways to ease it.”
- “Sometimes the cough is due to irritation from the cancer, other times
it’s linked to infection or treatment.”
- “We can try medicines to calm the cough and make you more
comfortable.”
- “Your cancer team and palliative care can also support with symptom
control.”
- “If your cough worsens, especially if you notice blood or severe
breathlessness, it’s important to get urgent help.”

284- Chronic Dry Cough
Definition: Cough persisting for more
than 8 weeks in adults, often due to
benign but irritating causes.
Common Causes:
- Post-viral airway hyperreactivity.
- Asthma (including cough-variant).
- Gastro-oesophageal reflux (silent
reflux).
- Post-nasal drip / upper airway cough
syndrome.
- ACE inhibitor use.
Red Flags:
- Haemoptysis.
- Unexplained weight loss, night
sweats.
- Persistent hoarse voice.
- Breathlessness / chest pain.
- Abnormal chest X-ray.
Focused History:
- Cough details: duration, day vs night, triggers, dry vs productive.
- Resp symptoms: wheeze, chest tightness, breathlessness.
- ENT symptoms: nasal drip, throat clearing, hoarseness.
- GI symptoms: heartburn, reflux, regurgitation.
- Drug history: ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker.
- Red flag screen: blood in sputum, weight loss, smoking history, TB
exposure.
- Impact: sleep disturbance, embarrassment, anxiety about cancer.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “I’m worried this might be
something serious like lung cancer.”
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “A dry cough lasting this long is often due to irritation in the airways,
reflux, or post-nasal drip.”
- “Most causes are not serious, but we do check for warning signs
carefully.”
- “Our plan is to look for reversible causes and help reduce the
cough so it doesn’t keep disturbing you.”
1. Exclude red flags / urgent issues:
- Ask about haemoptysis, chest pain, unexplained weight loss → urgent
CXR / referral if positive.
- Consider 2WW referral if suspicion of malignancy.
2. First-line investigations (Primary Care):
- CXR, spirometry with reversibility, FeNO if available.
- Review meds (stop ACE inhibitor if present).
- Trial of PPI if reflux suspected.
- Trial of nasal steroid spray if post-nasal drip suspected.
3. Symptom control:
- Simple linctus, honey, humidified air.
- Reassurance about self-limiting causes.
4. Holistic & supportive care:
- Explore anxiety about lung cancer; provide clear safety-netting.
- Lifestyle: smoking cessation, weight management (for reflux), avoid
triggers (dust, perfumes, cold air).
5. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you notice blood in your phlegm, worsening breathlessness, chest
pain, or weight loss, please come back urgently.”
- Arrange follow-up after investigations (e.g., 4–6 weeks).
6. SCA Phrases:
- “I can see this cough is really wearing you down — let’s look into
possible causes and get you some relief.”
- “The good news is that the majority of long-standing coughs are not due
to cancer, but we’ll do the right checks to be safe.”
- “Sometimes it’s linked to acid reflux, asthma, or nasal drip — things we
can test and treat.”
- “Stopping your current blood pressure tablet might help, since that can
cause cough in some people.”
- “If anything changes — especially if you cough up blood or lose weight —
please don’t wait, seek urgent review.”

285- Strong Family History of Breast
Cancer – Patient Worried
Definition: Women with multiple
relatives affected by breast cancer,
especially at younger ages, may have
an increased risk of hereditary breast
cancer (e.g., BRCA1/2 mutations).
NICE Referral Criteria for Familial
Breast Cancer: Refer to secondary
care/genetics if any of the following:
- One first-degree female relative
diagnosed with breast cancer under
the age of 40 years.
- One 1st-degree male relative
diagnosed with breast cancer at any
age.
- One first-degree relative with
bilateral breast cancer where the
first primary was diagnosed under
the age of 50 years.
- Two first-degree relatives, or one
first-degree and one second-degree
relative, diagnosed with breast
cancer at any age.
- One first-degree or second-degree
relative diagnosed with breast cancer
at any age AND one first-degree or
second-degree relative diagnosed
with ovarian cancer at any age (one
must be first-degree).
- Three first-degree or second-degree
relatives diagnosed with breast
cancer at any age.
Red Flags:
- Current breast lump, nipple
discharge, skin changes, weight loss,
bone pain
Focused History:
- Family history: who in family, age at diagnosis, bilateral/multiple
cancers, ovarian cancer, male breast cancer.
- Personal history: any breast lumps, nipple discharge, skin
dimpling, past breast biopsies, OCP/HRT use, age at
menarche/menopause, parity.
- Impact: anxiety, daily worry, avoidance of checks.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “I’m worried I’m going to get
breast cancer like my mum.” / “Should I be tested for the BRCA
gene?”
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Having relatives with breast cancer can increase your own risk.”
- “But most breast cancers are not inherited — only about 5–10% are
due to genes like BRCA.”
- “We look at your family pattern, and if it’s strong, we can refer you
to a specialist genetics service.”
- “There are also screening options we can offer earlier for women at
higher risk.”
• Seek specialist advice from secondary care if:
o Any of the following are present in the family history in
addition to breast cancers in relatives not fulfilling the
criteria for referral:
▪ Bilateral breast cancer.
▪ Male breast cancer.
▪ Ovarian cancer.
▪ Jewish ancestry.
▪ Sarcoma in a relative younger than 45 years
of age.
▪ Glioma or childhood adrenal cortical
carcinomas.
▪ Complicated patterns of multiple cancers at
a young age.
▪ Two or more relatives with breast cancer on
the father's side of the family.
o There is uncertainty about whether or not to refer.
o The person is not sufficiently reassured by the information
provided.
1. Exclude acute concern:
- If patient has current lump or symptoms → urgent 2WW breast clinic
referral.
2. Risk assessment & referral:
- Apply NICE family history criteria.
- Refer directly to a specialist genetics service if a high-risk predisposing
gene mutation (such as BRCA1, BRCA2, or TP53) has been identified.
3. Preventive options (specialist-led):
- Enhanced screening (annual mammograms/MRI).
- Chemoprevention (tamoxifen, raloxifene).
- Prophylactic mastectomy/oophorectomy (only for confirmed gene
carriers).
4. Holistic & supportive care:
- Address anxiety and provide clear written info (Breast Cancer Now,
Macmillan, Breast cancer UK, Prevent breast cancer).
- Lifestyle: maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular exercise,
stop smoking, avoid COCs
- Offer referral to counselling if anxiety overwhelming.
5. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you ever notice a lump, skin dimpling, or nipple changes, please come
back urgently.”
- Arrange follow-up after genetics/family history clinic referral.
6. SCA Phrases:
- “I can see this is really worrying you — let’s go through your family history
carefully.”
- “Most breast cancers are not inherited, but some families do carry genes
that increase risk.”
- “Based on your family history, we can refer you to a genetics service who
will assess whether testing or extra screening is right for you.”
- “There are ways to lower risk, from lifestyle changes to medical options
— but these decisions are taken together with specialists.”
- “If you ever develop any new breast changes, don’t wait for your next
check — please see us straight away.”

286- Family History of Familial
Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) –
Patient Worried
Definition: FAP is an autosomal
dominant condition caused by
mutations in the APC gene, leading to
hundreds to thousands of
adenomatous colorectal polyps, with
a near 100% risk of colorectal cancer
if untreated.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Known family history of FAP or APC
mutation.
- Multiple relatives with bowel cancer
at young age (<50).
- Relatives who had colectomy in
early adulthood.
Red Flags:
- Rectal bleeding, anaemia, change in
bowel habit, weight loss.
- Abdominal pain, obstruction
symptoms.
NICE Referral Criteria for
Suspected FAP (genetic
testing/secondary care):
- Any first-degree relative with
confirmed FAP (genetic or clinical).
- Family history strongly suggestive of
FAP (multiple early-onset colorectal
cancers/polyps).
Focused History:
- Family history: Who is affected, age at diagnosis, colectomy,
genetic testing done?
- Personal symptoms: rectal bleeding, bowel habit change,
anaemia, abdominal pain.
- Previous tests: colonoscopy, genetic testing.
- Impact: anxiety about cancer, lifestyle, family planning.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “I’m scared I’ll definitely get
bowel cancer” / “Do I need genetic testing?” / “What about my
children?”
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “It is a condition runs in families when some lumps/growth grow in
the lining in the bowel and if not treated, one of these will certainly
turn into cancer usually by age of 40.
The chance of getting this condition from dad is 1:2 (hope)
- “If someone has a close relative with FAP, we take that seriously
and usually offer genetic counselling and testing.”
- “If you carry the gene, regular bowel checks and sometimes surgery
can prevent cancer from developing.”
- “If your relative don’t carry the gene, and your relative does have
hundreds of polyps then they should be treated as they have it as it
might be caused by gene variant
1. Exclude acute issues:
- If rectal bleeding, anaemia, weight loss, or obstruction → urgent
colorectal referral (2WW).
2. Referral & investigations:
- If FH of confirmed FAP → refer to clinical genetics for counselling +
testing.
- If positive gene → enrol in surveillance colonoscopy programme
(usually from age 10–12).
- Colonoscopy every 1–2 years until colectomy.
- Consider colectomy in late teens/early adulthood if polyps develop.
3. Holistic & supportive care:
- Psychological support for anxiety.
- Discuss implications for children (50% inheritance risk).
- Fertility/pregnancy: discuss genetic counselling pre-conception.
- Lifestyle: healthy weight, avoid smoking/alcohol excess, high-fibre diet.
- Signpost to https://polyposispatient.support/
4. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you develop blood in stool, persistent diarrhoea, or weight loss,
please see us urgently.”
- Ensure referral to genetics/colorectal team.
5. SCA Phrases:
- “I can understand why you’re worried, as having a family member with
FAP can feel frightening.”
- “The good news is that knowing about it early means we can do
something — surveillance and treatment can prevent bowel cancer.”
- “We can arrange a referral to the genetics team — they’ll talk you through
testing and what it means for you.”
- “If you do carry the gene, there’s a clear programme of checks and
treatment to stop cancer developing.”
- “If you don’t carry the gene, your risk goes back to the same as anyone
else’s.”

287- Family History of Huntington’s
Disease – Patient Worried
Definition: HD is an autosomal
dominant neurodegenerative
disorder caused by a CAG repeat
expansion in the HTT gene. It typically
presents between ages 30–50 with
progressive movement disorder
(chorea), cognitive decline, and
psychiatric symptoms.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Known family history (one parent
affected → 50% chance of
inheritance).
- Progressive chorea, personality
change, memory loss.
Red Flags:
- New neurological symptoms
(chorea, unsteady gait).
- Severe psychiatric symptoms
(depression, suicide risk).
- Functional decline in work/social
life.
Referral Criteria:
- Any patient with a first-degree
relative diagnosed with HD who
requests testing → refer to clinical
genetics.
- Anyone with clinical features
suggestive of HD → refer neurology ±
genetics.
Focused History:
- Family history: which relatives affected, age of onset, genetic
confirmation.
- Personal symptoms: movement problems, mood changes,
memory issues.
- Psychiatric: depression, anxiety, suicidal thoughts.
- Impact: fear of future, relationships, family planning, employment.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “I’m terrified I’ll get what my dad
had.” / “Should I have the genetic test?” / “What about my children?”
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Huntington’s disease is an inherited condition where a change in
one gene can cause problems with movement, mood, and memory.”
- “If a parent has the condition, each child has a 1 in 2 chance of
inheriting it.”
- “There’s a genetic test, but this is only done after careful
counselling because the results can have big implications for your
future, family, and emotions.”
- “At the moment, there isn’t a cure, but there are treatments to
manage symptoms and strong support available.”
1. Exclude urgent issues:
- If suicidal thoughts or severe depression → urgent mental health referral.
- If new neuro symptoms → neurology referral.
2. Referral & investigations:
- If FH and asymptomatic → refer to clinical genetics for counselling
before any testing.
- If symptomatic → neurology + genetics.
- Testing only after at least 2 sessions of genetic counselling and with
informed consent.
3. Symptom management if positive
- Movement disorder: tetrabenazine, antipsychotics if chorea severe.
- Psychiatric: SSRIs for depression/anxiety, antipsychotics if psychosis.
- Cognitive: occupational therapy, cognitive strategies.
4. Holistic & supportive care:
- Psychological support (counselling, support groups).
- Family planning advice (options: PGD/IVF with genetic testing, adoption).
- Occupational and social support (DVLA, work adaptations).
- Involve charities (Huntington’s Disease Association UK).
4. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you ever feel low, unsafe, or develop worrying new symptoms, please
come back urgently.”
- Arrange follow-up after genetics/neurology referral.
5. SCA Phrases:
- “I can see this is weighing heavily on you — it’s completely
understandable given your family history.”
- “Each child of an affected parent has a 50% chance of inheriting the
condition.”
- “There is a test available, but it’s only done after specialist counselling,
because knowing the result can have a big impact on your life.”
- “Even though there isn’t a cure yet, there is a lot we can do to support
symptoms, mental health, and planning for the future.”
- “You don’t have to go through this alone — there are specialist services
and support groups to help you and your family.”

288- Insomnia
Definition: Chronic insomnia is
difficulty falling asleep, maintaining
sleep, or early waking, occurring at
least 3 nights/week for >3 months,
with associated daytime impairment.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Persistent poor sleep despite
adequate opportunity to sleep.
- Daytime fatigue, poor
concentration, irritability.
- No other primary sleep disorder
(e.g., OSA, restless legs, Shift work
disorder (SWD) and jet lag disorder
DDx as above +
Parasomnia: abnormal events that
happen during sleep, such as
sleepwalking, sleep talking, or sleep
terrors, or during the transition from
sleep to wakefulness.
Narcolepsy: a chronic neurological
disorder affecting the brain's ability to
regulate sleep-wake cycles, causing
excessive daytime sleepiness and
sudden attacks of sleep. Other
symptoms can include cataplexy
(muscle weakness triggered by
emotions), sleep paralysis (inability to
move or speak when waking), and
hallucinations.
Red Flags:
- Depression with suicidal ideation.
- Suspicion of obstructive sleep
apnoea (loud snoring, witnessed
apnoeas, daytime sleepiness).
- Substance misuse (alcohol, drugs).
Focused History:
- Sleep pattern: sleep onset latency, nocturnal awakenings, early
waking.
- Daytime effects: fatigue, performance, mood, concentration.
- Sleep hygiene: bedtime routine, caffeine/alcohol/nicotine use,
screen time, naps, shift work.
- Medical causes: pain, asthma, heart failure, thyroid disease.
- Psychiatric causes: depression, anxiety, PTSD.
- Medication/substances: steroids, antidepressants, stimulants,
alcohol, recreational drugs.
- Coping strategies tried: relaxation, over-the-counter remedies,
previous sleep tablets.
- Impact on life: relationships, work, mood.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “Am I going mad?”, “Do I need
long-term sleeping tablets?”
Consider tools such as Epworth score for OSA, GAD-7, PHQ-9,
Insomnia Severity Index
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Chronic insomnia means your sleep pattern has been disrupted
for several months.”
- “It’s a common problem and can be linked to stress, mood, or
habits.”
- “Sleeping tablets are not a long-term solution, but there are highly
effective treatments we can use.”
- “I can see this has been going on for a long time and is really
affecting your daily life.”
- “Let’s also check whether stress, low mood, or another medical
problem is making things worse.”
1. Exclude urgent issues:
- Screen for low mood, anxiety, suicidal thoughts.
- If OSA, parasomnia, narcolepsy suspected → refer for 2ry care .
2. Non-pharmacological first line (NICE):
- CBT for insomnia (CBT-I) is the gold standard. Sleepio is a digital CBTi
- Sleep hygiene measures:
- Consistent bedtime/wake time (even on weekends).
- Avoid caffeine, alcohol, nicotine in evenings.
- No screens before bed.
- Get out of bed if not asleep within 20 min.
- Bedroom = only for sleep/intimacy.
- Regular exercise (not close to bedtime).
- Bright light in morning to reset circadian rhythm.
- 2 weeks sleep diary
- Signpost to Mental Health Foundation: How to sleep better.
3. Pharmacological (not routine, only if severe/short-term crisis):
- Avoid long-term hypnotics (z-drugs, benzodiazepines)
- Consider melatonin (licensed in >55s) for max 13 weeks
- For adults, consider offering daridorexant, only if CBTi has been tried but
not worked, or is unavailable or unsuitable.
- Short course of hypnotics if disabling crisis, with strict review plan for < 2
weeks
4. Holistic & supportive care:
- Address comorbid depression/anxiety (CBT, SSRIs if indicated).
- Pain management if contributory.
- Stress reduction, mindfulness, relaxation.
- Refer to IAPT for psychological support.
5. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If your sleep worsens, or if you develop low mood or thoughts of
harming yourself, please seek help urgently.”
- Arrange review in 4–6 weeks to assess progress.
6. SCA Phrases:
- “The best long-term treatment is CBT for insomnia — it helps change the
thought patterns and habits that keep the problem going.”
- “Sleeping tablets are not a long-term answer because they stop working
after a while and can cause dependence.”
- “There are several non-medication approaches we can try that have very
strong evidence.”

-- 311 of 437 --

289- Narcolepsy
Definition: A chronic neurological
sleep disorder caused by
dysregulation of REM sleep, leading
to excessive daytime sleepiness and
abnormal REM phenomena.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Excessive daytime sleepiness
despite adequate sleep.
- Cataplexy (sudden loss of muscle
tone triggered by emotions).
- Sleep paralysis and hypnagogic
hallucinations.
- Automatic behaviours (doing things
without remembering).
- Onset typically in teens or young
adults.
Red Flags / Differentiate from:
- Obstructive sleep apnoea (snoring,
witnessed apnoeas).
- Depression, hypothyroidism.
- Seizure disorders.
- Medication/substance causes.
Focused History:
- Sleep symptoms: excessive daytime sleepiness, naps,
unrefreshing sleep, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hallucinations.
- Sleep hygiene: bedtime, sleep duration, shift work,
caffeine/alcohol/drugs.
- Functional impact: school/work performance, driving, accidents.
- Past medical history: thyroid, epilepsy, psychiatric disorders.
- Medication/substances: sedatives, antidepressants, stimulants,
alcohol, recreational drugs.
- Family history: narcolepsy or sleep disorders.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “Am I lazy?”, “Could this be
something serious with my brain?”, “Do I need medication?”
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Narcolepsy is a condition where the brain can’t properly regulate
sleep and wake cycles.”
- “That means you may feel suddenly very sleepy during the day, or
even lose muscle control if you laugh or get emotional.”
- “It’s not due to laziness or lack of effort — it’s a medical condition
we can manage.”
1. Confirm diagnosis:
- Refer to a specialist sleep clinic.
- Tests: polysomnography (overnight sleep study) + multiple sleep latency
test (MSLT).
2. Non-pharmacological measures:
- Regular sleep schedule.
- Planned short naps during the day.
- Good sleep hygiene.
- Avoid alcohol and sedating medicines.
- Safety advice: no driving until assessed by DVLA (UK guidance:
narcolepsy must be reported).
3. Pharmacological (specialist-led):
- Stimulants (modafinil, methylphenidate).
- Sodium oxybate for cataplexy.
- SSRIs/SNRIs for cataplexy/sleep paralysis if needed.
4. Holistic & supportive care:
- Explore impact on education, work, relationships.
- Signpost to support groups (Narcolepsy UK).
- Occupational adjustments (school/workplace).
5. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you feel your symptoms worsen, or if you have any accidents or
injuries due to sleepiness, seek help urgently.”
- Arrange review after sleep clinic assessment.
6. SCA Phrases:
- “I can see this excessive sleepiness is really affecting your life.”
- “Narcolepsy is a recognised condition, and there are effective
treatments to help manage it.”
- “We’ll arrange a referral to a specialist sleep clinic for proper tests to
confirm what’s going on.”
- “It’s important not to drive until you’ve been fully assessed, for your
safety and others’.”
- “We can work with both medical treatments and lifestyle changes to help
you feel more in control.”

290- Postnatal depression and
psychosis
Postnatal Depression & Psychosis
Definition:
- Postnatal depression (PND):
depressive episode occurring within
the first year after childbirth.
- Postnatal psychosis (puerperal
psychosis): severe psychiatric
emergency usually presenting within
2–4 weeks after delivery, with
psychotic symptoms (delusions,
hallucinations, mania, severe
confusion).
When to Suspect:
- Persistent low mood, fatigue, poor
bonding with baby, guilt,
hopelessness.
- Intrusive thoughts, thoughts of
harming self/baby.
- Psychosis: bizarre beliefs, hearing
voices, confusion, rapid mood
swings, disorganised behaviour.
Red Flags:
- Suicidal ideation or infanticidal
thoughts.
- Psychotic symptoms.
- Neglecting baby’s basic needs.
ocused History:
- Mood: low mood, loss of interest/pleasure, guilt, hopelessness.
- Sleep/appetite/energy: disrupted beyond normal newborn
pattern.
- Bonding: ability to care for and connect with baby.
- Thoughts: suicidal ideation, self-harm, intrusive or harmful
thoughts towards baby.
- Psychotic features: hallucinations, delusions, severe confusion,
mania.
- Risk assessment: safeguarding, support at home, partner/family
input.
- Past psychiatric history: depression, bipolar, schizophrenia,
previous postnatal illness.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “Am I a bad mother?”, “Is this my
fault?”, “Will I be taken away from my baby?”
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “It’s very common for new mothers to feel low, anxious, or
overwhelmed — that’s not your fault.”
- “Sometimes this develops into postnatal depression, which is more
than just the ‘baby blues’ and needs proper support.”
- “Rarely, some women can develop a severe illness with unusual
thoughts or confusion, called postnatal psychosis, which needs
urgent help to keep both you and your baby safe.”
1. Exclude urgent issues:
- If suicidal or psychotic → emergency same-day psychiatry referral /
admission (Mother & Baby Unit if available).
2. Postnatal Depression (mild–moderate):
- Refer to perinatal mental health team.
- Psychological therapy (CBT, interpersonal therapy).
- Support groups, peer support, health visitor involvement.
- Encourage rest, partner/family support.
3. Postnatal Depression (moderate–severe):
- Consider antidepressants (SSRIs — sertraline is safest in breastfeeding).
- Monitor closely.
4. Postnatal Psychosis:
- Medical emergency → urgent psychiatry referral (999 if immediate risk).
- Likely inpatient care with antipsychotics, mood stabilisers, possible
antidepressants.
5. Holistic & supportive care:
- Safeguarding for baby and mother.
- Involve partner/family in care.
- Link with health visitor, midwife, perinatal psychiatry, and GP.
- Signpost to charities (PANDAS Foundation, Mind).
6. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you ever feel like harming yourself or your baby, please seek urgent
help immediately.”
- Arrange early follow-up (within days if concerns).
7. SCA Phrases:
- “You’ve been through a huge life change, and it’s understandable that
you feel overwhelmed.”
- “This is not your fault — postnatal depression is an illness, just like
physical illnesses, and it can be treated.”
- “If thoughts of harming yourself or your baby come up, that’s an
emergency — please let someone know straight away.”
- “There are safe and effective treatments we can offer while you continue
to care for your baby.”
- “We’ll make sure you’re supported by a specialist team that looks after
mums and babies.”

291- Schizophrenia – new Dx
Definition: A severe, chronic mental
health disorder characterised by
psychotic symptoms (delusions,
hallucinations, thought disorder) and
negative symptoms (social
withdrawal, lack of motivation,
blunted affect).
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- New onset hallucinations (often
auditory).
- Delusions (paranoid, persecutory,
grandiose).
- Disorganised thoughts/behaviour.
- Social/occupational decline.
- Often onset in young adulthood.
Red Flags:
- Suicidal ideation/self-neglect.
- Risk to others (violent thoughts,
paranoia).
- Severe agitation or inability to care
for self.
Focused History:
- Presenting symptoms: hallucinations, delusions, disorganised
behaviour, social withdrawal.
- Impact: functioning at work/education, relationships, self-care.
- Risk assessment: suicidal ideation, self-neglect, harm to others.
- Past psychiatric history: previous depression, psychosis,
admissions.
- Family history: psychotic illness, bipolar disorder.
- Substance use: cannabis, alcohol, stimulants.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “Am I going mad?”, “Will this last
forever?”, “Will I need to be in hospital?”
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Schizophrenia is a condition that affects how the brain processes
information, leading to experiences like hearing voices or having
strong unusual beliefs.”
- “It doesn’t mean having a ‘split personality’ — that’s a common
myth.”
- “It can be distressing, but with the right support and treatment,
many people manage their symptoms and live fulfilling lives.”
1. Exclude urgent issues:
- If suicidal, severely psychotic, or unsafe → same-day urgent psychiatry
referral.
2. Specialist involvement:
- Referral to Early Intervention in Psychosis (EIP) team.
- Psychiatric assessment, risk assessment, and care planning.
3. Pharmacological (specialist-led):
- Antipsychotics (oral or depot) e.g., risperidone, olanzapine, aripiprazole.
- Monitor side effects (weight, metabolic, extrapyramidal).
4. Non-pharmacological support:
- Psychological therapies (CBT for psychosis, family therapy).
- Social support: housing, benefits, occupational rehabilitation.
5. Holistic care:
- Address substance misuse.
- Support for family/carers.
- Safeguarding if concerns about vulnerability.
6. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If your symptoms get worse, or if you ever feel unsafe, please seek
urgent help straight away.”
- Arrange close GP follow-up and liaison with psychiatry.
7. SCA Phrases:
- “I can see this is a frightening time for you.”
- “Schizophrenia doesn’t mean you’ll lose control of your life — with
treatment, many people recover well.”
- “We’ll link you with a specialist team that supports people with
psychosis.”
- “Treatment usually involves medication to calm down the symptoms,
alongside talking therapies and social support.”
- “We’ll make sure your family is also supported, as this can affect them
too.”

292- PTSD – Refugee from
Afghanistan
Definition: Post-traumatic stress
disorder occurs after exposure to life-
threatening or traumatic events (e.g.,
war, torture, displacement).
Symptoms include re-experiencing,
avoidance, hyperarousal, and
negative mood/cognition changes for
> 1 month)
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Refugee/asylum seeker with trauma
history.
- Nightmares, flashbacks, intrusive
memories.
- Avoidance of reminders of trauma.
- Hypervigilance, exaggerated startle.
- Numbness, guilt, detachment.
DDx: acute stress disorder within 4
weeks pf exposure to traumatic event
Red Flags:
- Suicidal ideation or severe self-
neglect.
- Risk of harm to others.
- Substance misuse.
- Severe comorbid depression or
psychosis.
Focused History:
- Trauma history: exposure to violence, war, torture, displacement
(sensitive approach, avoid forcing details).
- Re-experiencing: nightmares, intrusive memories, flashbacks.
- Avoidance: people/places/situations that trigger memories.
- Hyperarousal: insomnia, irritability, hypervigilance, startle
response.
- Mood/cognition: guilt, shame, detachment, hopelessness.
- Functional impact: family life, work, ability to integrate.
- Risk assessment: suicidal thoughts, harm to others, safeguarding
concerns (children/family).
- Cultural factors: stigma, reliance on community/spiritual support.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “I keep reliving the war,” “I can’t
sleep,” “Will I ever feel normal again?”
- The effects on work or school, relationships, social life, and quality
of life, and assess their degree of distress and functional impairment
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “After very frightening or dangerous experiences, the mind can
sometimes stay ‘on alert’, as if the danger is still there.”
- “This can cause nightmares, flashbacks, and a constant feeling of
being on edge — this is called post-traumatic stress disorder, or
PTSD.”
- “It’s not a weakness — it’s a recognised medical condition after
trauma, and there are treatments that can help.”
Explaining Trauma-Focused CBT
• “This is a type of talking therapy where you work with a
trained therapist to gently face and process the painful
memories.” Up to 12 sessions
• “It doesn’t mean you have to go through every detail
straight away — it’s done at your pace, safely.”
• “The idea is to help your mind understand that those events
are in the past, so the memories feel less overwhelming
and less like they’re happening right now.”
• “It gives you tools to manage flashbacks, nightmares, and
the constant sense of being on edge.”
1. Exclude urgent issues:
- Assess for suicidal ideation, psychosis, severe risk. If present → urgent
psychiatry referral.
2. First-line interventions:
- Refer to specialist trauma-focused psychological therapies: trauma-
focused CBT or EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing).
Use this site to find the nearest centre https://ukpts.org/
- Avoid routine debriefing or benzodiazepines.
3. Pharmacological (if severe or therapy unavailable or patient wants
medications):
- Antidepressants (venlafaxine or SSRIs e.g., sertraline, paroxetine).
- Adjuncts if required (specialist-guided).
- Do not offer drug treatments for PTSD in children and young people aged
under 18 years.
4. Holistic & supportive care:
- Link with refugee support services (housing, legal, social).
- Community groups, interpreters, cultural mediators.
- Explore spirituality/faith if important to patient.
- Safeguarding for children/family.
- Address comorbidities (substance misuse, chronic pain).
- Signpost to PTSD UK or Migrant Help
5. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you ever feel overwhelmed, or have thoughts of harming yourself,
please seek help urgently.”
- Arrange close follow-up (weeks not months).
- Coordinate with community mental health team and voluntary refugee
services.
6. SCA Phrases:
- “I can hear how much these experiences are still affecting you — I want
you to know this is a recognised condition, and it is treatable.”
- “PTSD happens because the brain keeps re-living trauma — it’s not your
fault.”
- “There are therapies, such as trauma-focused CBT and EMDR, that are
very effective.”
- “I’ll make sure you’re linked to services that understand the needs of

-- 315 of 437 --

Explaining EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitisation and
Reprocessing)
• “This is another therapy for trauma where, while thinking
about the difficult memory, the therapist uses eye
movements or sounds to help your brain process it
differently.”
• “It’s a bit like moving the memory from the ‘emergency’
part of the brain to the ‘long-term storage’ part, so it no
longer feels so frightening or vivid.”
• “Many people who’ve been through war or trauma find
EMDR very effective because it doesn’t rely only on talking
— it works with how the brain naturally heals.”
• “You remain in control the whole time, and you don’t have
to describe every detail of your trauma if that feels too
painful.”
Reassuring Phrases for Engagement
• “You’ve survived very difficult experiences — these
therapies are designed to help you feel safe again and
reduce the power those memories have over you.”
• “It’s not about erasing the past, but about helping you live
in the present without being trapped by those memories.”
• “Many refugees and survivors of trauma have found these
approaches life-changing — they can help you sleep better,
feel calmer, and reconnect with your daily life.”
people who’ve been through war and displacement.”
- “You’re not alone in this — we’ll work together to get you the right
support.”

293- Bullying
Definition: Repeated intentional
harm (physical, verbal, or online)
causing distress to a child/teen.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Child reluctant to attend school,
frequent unexplained physical
symptoms (headaches, stomach
aches).
- Changes in mood, withdrawal,
irritability.
- Falling grades, sleep disturbance.
- Parental concerns about
behavioural change.
Red Flags:
- Self-harm, suicidal thoughts.
- Severe school avoidance.
- Safeguarding concerns at home or
school.
Focused History (child/teen + parent if present):
- School life: attendance, friendships, teachers, performance.
- Bullying details: what happens, who is involved, how often, online
vs face-to-face.
- Impact: mood, sleep, appetite, somatic complaints, confidence.
- Mental health: low mood, anxiety, self-harm, suicidal thoughts.
- Support network: parents, friends, trusted adults.
- Past history: previous bullying, trauma, learning difficulties.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “Why me?”, “Will it ever stop?”,
“Do I have to change schools?”
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Bullying is when someone repeatedly hurts or picks on another
person — it’s never the victim’s fault.”
- “It can affect your mood, confidence, and even physical health.”
- “The good news is there is support, and we’ll make sure school and
services step in to help keep you safe.”
- “I can see this is really upsetting for you — no one deserves to be
treated this way.”
- “Bullying can make people feel very low and isolated, but you’re not
alone in this.”
1. Safeguarding & risk assessment:
- Ask about self-harm, suicidal thoughts, neglect/abuse at home.
- If immediate risk → urgent safeguarding/mental health referral.
2. School involvement:
- Encourage disclosure to school (teachers, safeguarding lead).
- GP can provide supportive letter if needed.
3. Supportive management:
- Referral to CAMHS if significant anxiety, depression, or self-harm.
- Offer counselling, CBT-based support.
- Online safety advice if cyberbullying.
- Empower coping strategies: trusted adults, supportive friends,
confidence-building activities.
4. Holistic care:
- Engage parents/carers.
- Explore community/faith groups for support.
- Address sleep, exercise, diet.
- Peer support and anti-bullying organisations (e.g., Childline, NSPCC,
Anti-Bullying Alliance, KOOTH, Young Minds).
5. Anger & emotional regulation strategies (important if patient talks
about hurting themselves when upset):
- Teach safe coping: walk away, squeeze a stress ball, write/draw feelings,
listen to music, do breathing exercises.
- Encourage physical outlets like sports or running instead of self-harm.
- Suggest grounding techniques (5–4–3–2–1 senses method).
- Explore referral to school counsellor/anger management workshops.
5. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you ever feel like you might harm yourself, or things get too
overwhelming, please tell an adult straight away and come back to us.” -
Arrange early review in 2–4 weeks.
6. SCA Phrases:
- “We’ll make sure the school is aware and takes action.”
- “If at any point you feel unsafe or think about harming yourself, that’s
really important — please come to us straight away.”
- “There are safe spaces and people who can support you, so you don’t
have to carry this by yourself.”

294- Workplace Bullying in a Patient
with Disability
Definition: Repeated negative
behaviours directed at an individual
with a disability in the workplace. May
include discrimination, exclusion,
harassment, or unreasonable work
demands.
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Patient reports bullying or
discrimination at work.
- Disability-related exclusion or unfair
treatment.
- Emotional impact: stress, anxiety,
depression, low self-esteem.
- Physical symptoms: poor sleep,
somatic complaints.
- Work absence, sickness
certification requests.
Red Flags:
- Suicidal thoughts or self-harm.
- Severe anxiety or depression.
- Safeguarding concerns (e.g.
exploitation, harassment).
Focused History:
- Nature of bullying: who is involved (colleagues, managers), type
(verbal, exclusion, unreasonable workload), duration.
- Impact on work: attendance, performance, risk of job loss.
- Impact on health: mood, anxiety, sleep, appetite, physical
symptoms.
- Mental health risk: self-harm, suicidal thoughts.
- Disability details: type, impact, whether reasonable adjustments
are being made.
- Support network: family, friends, union, HR.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “Am I being punished because of
my disability?”, “Will I lose my job?”, “Do I have any rights?”
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- “Bullying at work is when someone is repeatedly treated unfairly or
made to feel small.”
- “Having a disability should never put you at a disadvantage — the
law protects your right to fair treatment.”
- “We need to look after both your health and your work situation.”
1. Safeguarding & risk assessment:
- Ask about suicidal thoughts or self-harm.
- Urgent referral if high risk.
2. Occupational & legal support:
- Signpost to Occupational Health for workplace adjustments.
- Inform about Equality Act 2010 protections (UK): employer must make
reasonable adjustments and cannot discriminate based on disability.
- Advise HR/line manager involvement, or union support.
- Provide Fit Note if sickness absence needed.
3. Mental health support:
- Consider referral to counselling/CBT via IAPT or secondary care if severe.
- Explore stress management, sleep hygiene, relaxation techniques.
- Medication (e.g. antidepressants) if indicated.
4. Holistic & community support:
- Disability support organisations (e.g. Scope, Mind, ACAS for employment
advice).
- Encourage trusted family/friends for emotional support.
- Discuss financial support if unable to work (PIP, ESA, etc.).
5. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you ever feel overwhelmed, or think about harming yourself, please
reach out urgently.”
- Early follow-up in 2–4 weeks, sooner if mood deteriorates.
6. SCA Phrases:
- “I’m really sorry you’re going through this — no one should be treated
unfairly at work, especially because of a disability.”
- “You do have rights, and the law protects you from discrimination.”
- “We can link you with occupational health and HR to make sure
reasonable adjustments are in place.”
- “Your wellbeing is my priority — we can look at counselling or other
support if this has affected your mood.”
- “If you ever feel unsafe, or think about harming yourself, please come
back straight away or seek urgent help.”

295- Disability support
Area Support Available Details / Examples
Medical & Psychological GP & Secondary Care Ongoing management, referrals, prescriptions, equipment.
Mental Health Services (IAPT / CMHT) Counselling, CBT, crisis support.
Specialist Rehabilitation Services Physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy.
Workplace Support Occupational Health Assessment, workplace adjustments (reduced hours, modified duties).
HR / Line Manager Workplace policies, grievance procedures.
Access to Work Funds equipment, job coaching, transport support, interpreters.
Trade Unions Advocacy for workplace discrimination or bullying.
Legal Rights Equality Act 2010 Employers & service providers must make “reasonable adjustments.”
ACAS Free advice on workplace rights and mediation.
Financial Support Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) Paid by employer (up to 28 weeks).
Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) For those unable to work due to disability/illness.
Personal Independence Payment (PIP) Extra help with daily living & mobility costs.
Universal Credit Includes extra disability element if unable to work.
Disabled Facilities Grant Home adaptations (e.g. ramps, stairlifts, accessible bathrooms).
Practical Everyday Support Blue Badge Scheme (Disabled Parking) Allows parking closer to destinations; helps with mobility.
Disabled Persons Railcard / Bus Pass Reduced travel costs, free local bus travel in many areas.
Motability Scheme Use mobility allowance to lease a car, scooter, or powered wheelchair.
Carer’s Allowance For people looking after someone with significant needs.
Social Care Support (Local Council) Carer assessments, respite care, home help, equipment.
Priority Services Register Extra support from utility companies (e.g. during power cuts).
Housing Support Adapted social housing, help from housing associations.
Community & Voluntary Charities (Scope, Mencap, RNIB, MS Society, Mind) Advocacy, peer support, grants, advice.
Citizen’s Advice Help with benefits, debt, employment rights, housing.
Peer & Faith-based Groups Social support and reducing isolation.

296- Anxiety with Panic Attacks
Definition: Anxiety disorder
characterised by recurrent panic
attacks (sudden, intense fear with
physical symptoms).
When to Suspect / Diagnostic
pointers:
- Recurrent episodes of palpitations,
breathlessness, chest tightness,
dizziness, sweating.
- Often mistaken for
cardiac/respiratory disease.
- Fear of dying or losing control.
- Avoidance behaviours (e.g., won’t go
out alone).
Red Flags:
- Suicidal thoughts.
- Alcohol or drug misuse.
- Severe functional impairment (not
leaving house, not working).
Focused History:
- Symptoms: onset, duration, triggers, frequency of panic attacks,
typical features (palpitations, shortness of breath, chest pain,
dizziness).
- Impact: work, relationships, social life.
- Associated symptoms: worry, poor sleep, irritability, fatigue.
- Mental health risk: suicidal thoughts, self-harm, substance
misuse.
- Medical exclusion: past cardiac, thyroid, respiratory history.
- Coping: avoidance, reassurance-seeking, safety behaviours.
- Ideas, Concerns, Expectations: “Am I having a heart attack?”,
“Am I going mad?”, “Can this be cured?”
Explanation in Lay Terms:
- Generalised anxiety: “This is when your mind is constantly on
edge, worrying about many things most of the time, even when there
isn’t a clear reason. It often comes with poor sleep, feeling tense,
and being unable to switch off.”
- “What you’re experiencing are panic attacks — a surge of
adrenaline that makes the body feel like it’s in danger, even when
you’re safe.”
- “It’s very common, and although the physical feelings are
frightening, they are not dangerous.”
- “With the right support and strategies, panic attacks can be
controlled and often improve significantly.”
- “Panic attacks happen when the brain sets off the body’s alarm
system by mistake, flooding it with adrenaline.”
- “That’s why you feel chest tightness, breathless, shaky — but these
attacks, though frightening, are not dangerous.”
1. Rule out physical causes:
- ECG, thyroid function, medication review if indicated.
2. First-line management:
- Self-help & psychoeducation: explain panic cycle.
- Breathing retraining, relaxation techniques.
- Encourage exercise, sleep hygiene, reduced caffeine/alcohol.
3. Psychological therapy (NICE first-line):
- Refer to IAPT (CBT most effective for panic disorder).
- Online CBT options (NHS-supported):
• NHS Talking Therapies (IAPT) online programmes
• SilverCloud (digital CBT for anxiety & panic)
• Living Life to the Full (self-directed CBT modules)
• FearFighter (NHS digital CBT programme specifically for panic/phobias).
4. Medication (if severe or not responding):
- SSRI (sertraline first-line).
- SNRI or TCA second-line.
- Benzodiazepines only short-term / crisis use.
- Propranolol: useful for physical symptoms of anxiety (palpitations,
tremor, sweating), particularly situational anxiety or GAD with marked
somatic symptoms.
5. Safety net & follow-up:
- “If you feel like things are getting unbearable, or you ever feel you might
harm yourself, please seek urgent help.”
- Arrange review in 2–4 weeks to check progress.
6. SCA Phrases:
- “These symptoms feel frightening, but I want to reassure you they are not
dangerous.”
- “Panic attacks happen because the brain triggers the body’s alarm
system, even when there’s no real threat.”
- “We can look at practical strategies to calm the body during attacks —
like controlled breathing.”
- “CBT is a very effective talking therapy that helps people break the cycle
of panic and worry.”
- “Medication can also be an option if symptoms are severe or don’t
improve with therapy.”
