- “These don’t cure psoriasis, but they reduce flares and make skin feel
more comfortable.”
- “I’ll show you how to use them — the right amount and the right way.”
3. Lifestyle advice:
- “Moisturise daily, avoid harsh soaps, and use gentle shampoos.”
- “Cutting down smoking and alcohol helps reduce flare-ups.”
- “Stress is a common trigger — things like relaxation, exercise, or CBT can
help.”
- “Try not to scratch, as this can worsen patches or cause infection.”
4. Referral:
- Dermatology if severe, widespread, or not responding.
- Rheumatology if joint symptoms.
5. Safety Netting (SCA phrases):
- “If your skin suddenly becomes red all over or you feel feverish and
unwell, seek urgent help — that can be a rare but serious complication.”
- “If your joints become swollen, stiff, or painful, please let me know
quickly — that can be part of psoriasis too.”
- “If creams don’t help enough, there are stronger hospital treatments,
including light therapy or tablets.”
- “If it’s affecting your confidence or mood, that’s really important —
please tell me so we can support you.”
- “We’ll review your skin regularly to make sure things are under control.”

-- 375 of 437 --

345- Impetigo in a Child
Definition: Common, highly
contagious superficial bacterial skin
infection (usually Staphylococcus
aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes).
When to Suspect (CKS):
- Honey-coloured crusted lesions,
usually on the face or hands.
- Sometimes bullous lesions.
- Mild itch or discomfort.
Common DDx: Cold sores, eczema
with secondary infection, insect
bites, scabies, contact dermatitis.
Red Flags – urgent review:
- Spreading cellulitis (red, hot,
swollen skin).
- Fever, child unwell.
- Periorbital involvement.
- Recurrent infections (consider
underlying condition).
Focused History:
- Onset and spread of rash.
- Any fever or child generally unwell.
- Itching, pain, or discharge.
- Other family members with similar rash (contagion).
- Past skin conditions (eczema).
- Any recent antibiotic use or allergies.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “This looks like impetigo, a common skin infection in children.”
- “It’s caused by bacteria that live harmlessly on the skin but can
cause infection if they get in through a scratch or irritation.”
- “It spreads easily through touch, towels, or toys, so we’ll need to
take precautions at home.”
- “It usually clears up quickly with the right treatment.”
- “It is not usually serious, but we do keep an eye out for spreading
infection or if your child seems generally unwell.”
Management (CKS):
- Localised, non-bullous: topical fusidic acid (first-line, 5–7 days).
- Widespread/bullous/systemic symptoms: oral flucloxacillin
(clarithromycin if penicillin allergy).
- Do not prescribe topical + oral antibiotics together.
- Keep child at home until lesions are crusted/healed or for at least 48
hours after starting antibiotics (to reduce spread).
- Good hygiene: wash hands regularly, trim nails, avoid scratching, wash
towels/bedding separately, don’t share flannels or toys.
- Cover lesions loosely if possible.
- Wash hands regularly, especially after touching the area.
- Keep nails short to reduce scratching.
- Discourage scratching/picking as this worsens spread and scarring.
- Use separate towels, bedding, and flannels — wash on a hot cycle.
- Clean toys/surfaces the child uses.
- Cover lesions with loose clothing or gauze if possible (but avoid tight
dressings).
- Bathe daily with mild soap and water, gently pat dry.
- Encourage good general skin hygiene (moisturise dry skin, treat
underlying eczema if present).
SCA Phrases:
- “The cream/antibiotics will clear the infection in about a week.”
- “Keep your child at home from school for 48 hours after starting
treatment, as it’s contagious.”
- “Wash towels and bedding separately to avoid spreading it.”
- “If the redness spreads, the skin becomes hot/swollen, your child gets a
fever, or looks unwell, seek urgent medical advice.”
- “If this keeps happening, we may need to check for underlying skin
conditions like eczema or if anyone else in the family is a carrier.”

-- 376 of 437 --

346- Acanthosis Nigricans
Definition: Hyperpigmented, velvety
thickened skin, usually in skin folds
(neck, axillae, groin). Caused by
insulin resistance, endocrine
disorders, medications, or rarely
malignancy.
When to Suspect:
- Gradually developing dark,
thickened patches in folds.
- Often seen in obesity, PCOS, type 2
diabetes.
Common DDx: Post-inflammatory
hyperpigmentation, Addison’s
disease, tinea corporis, drug-induced
pigmentation.
Red Flags (urgent review / referral):
- Sudden onset, rapidly progressing,
extensive lesions → consider
underlying malignancy (esp. gastric
adenocarcinoma).
- Associated systemic symptoms:
unexplained weight loss, night
sweats, anorexia.
Focused History:
- When did the skin changes start? Rapid or gradual?
- Any associated itch, pain, or spread?
- Symptoms of diabetes: thirst, frequent urination, tiredness.
- Risk factors: obesity, family history of diabetes, PCOS.
- Medications (steroids, hormonal treatments, nicotinic acid).
- Red flag symptoms: weight loss, persistent abdominal pain, night
sweats.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “This looks like a skin change called acanthosis nigricans. It
happens when the skin cells grow a bit more than usual, making the
area darker and thicker.”
- “The most common reason is high insulin levels in the body, which
we often see with weight gain or diabetes.”
- “It’s not contagious and not dangerous by itself, but it can be a sign
of an underlying health issue we should check.”
- “In rare cases, if it comes on very suddenly, it can be linked to
internal problems like certain cancers — but in most people, it’s due
to insulin resistance or weight.”
Investigations (if needed):
- Blood tests: fasting glucose, HbA1c (diabetes screen), fasting insulin if
metabolic syndrome suspected.
- Lipid profile (if metabolic syndrome risk).
- Hormonal tests in women: testosterone, FSH/LH if PCOS suspected.
- Consider thyroid function tests, adrenal function if endocrine cause
suspected.
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Address underlying cause: weight management, diet, physical activity.
- Education about insulin resistance and diabetes risk.
- Skin care: gentle cleansing, emollients, avoid friction.
- Cosmetic options: camouflaging creams, keratolytics (urea, salicylic
acid), but effect limited.
Pharmacological / Specialist Options:
- Treat underlying metabolic issue (e.g., metformin for insulin
resistance/diabetes).
- Consider dermatology referral if diagnosis unclear or rapid onset.
- Oncology referral if suspicious for malignancy.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “This skin change itself is not harmful, but it is your body’s way of telling
us we should check your general health, especially for diabetes.”
- “The main treatment is addressing the underlying cause — often
improving weight and sugar control helps the skin improve too.”
- “If the patches spread quickly, become very thick, or you develop
unexplained weight loss or sweats, please come back urgently.”
- “We’ll arrange some blood tests to check for diabetes and other possible
causes, so we can treat the root problem.”

-- 377 of 437 --

347- Melasma
Definition: Acquired
hyperpigmentation, usually
symmetrical, on sun-exposed areas
of the face (cheeks, forehead, upper
lip). Common in women, often
hormonally influenced.
When to Suspect:
- Brown or gray-brown patches,
gradual onset.
- Associated with pregnancy, oral
contraceptives, hormone therapy, or
sun exposure.
Common DDx: Post-inflammatory
hyperpigmentation, lentigines, tinea
versicolor, drug-induced
pigmentation, Addison’s disease.
Red Flags:
- Rapidly changing pigmentation.
- Asymmetrical, irregular borders,
ulceration → consider skin cancer.
Focused History:
- Onset and duration of patches.
- Symmetry and progression.
- Exposure to sun, tanning beds.
- Hormonal factors: pregnancy, contraceptive use, hormone therapy.
- Skin care products used (bleaches, retinoids).
- Previous skin conditions, family history.
- Psychological impact: embarrassment, self-esteem.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “This looks like melasma, which is darker patches on the skin that
usually appear on the face.”
- “It’s caused by increased pigment production, often triggered by
sun, hormones, or pregnancy.”
- “It’s not contagious, not cancer, and does not indicate internal
disease in most cases.”
- “It can sometimes fade after triggers are removed, but it often
comes and goes.”
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Strict sun protection: broad-spectrum sunscreen, hats, avoid peak sun
hours.
- Gentle skin care: avoid harsh scrubs or irritating chemicals.
- Camouflage make-up if desired.
Pharmacological / Specialist Options (usually dermatology):
- Topical agents: hydroquinone, azelaic acid, tretinoin (alone or
combination).
- Chemical peels or laser therapy for resistant cases (specialist only).
Investigations:
- Usually clinical diagnosis; rarely Wood’s lamp or biopsy if uncertain.
- Review medications or hormonal factors contributing.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “Melasma is not harmful, but it can be distressing — treatments aim to
lighten the patches over time.”
- “Sun protection is the most important step; creams help prevent
worsening.”
- “Avoid harsh skin products which can worsen pigmentation.”
- “If you notice rapid changes, asymmetry, bleeding, ulceration, or other
new spots, come back urgently.”
- “We can refer you to a dermatologist if creams or sun protection aren’t
enough, or for specialist treatment options.”

-- 378 of 437 --

348- Actinic Keratosis (AK)
Definition: Pre-cancerous skin
lesions caused by chronic sun
exposure, usually on sun-exposed
areas (face, scalp, hands).
When to Suspect:
- Rough, scaly, pink or brown patch,
often asymptomatic.
- Slow-growing, commonly in fair-
skinned adults >40 years.
Common DDx: Seborrhoeic
keratosis, psoriasis, eczema, basal
cell carcinoma, squamous cell
carcinoma in situ.
Red Flags – urgent review / referral:
- Rapidly growing, ulcerated,
bleeding, or painful lesion.
- Pigmented, asymmetric, irregular
borders → consider malignant
transformation.
- Lesions that do not respond to usual
treatments or change appearance
quickly.
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, and progression of the lesion.
- Symptoms: pain, itching, bleeding.
- Sun exposure history, use of tanning beds, outdoor occupation.
- Previous skin cancers, immunosuppression (transplant, steroids).
- Family history of skin cancer.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “This is called an actinic keratosis. It’s a rough patch of skin caused
by long-term sun exposure.”
- “It is not cancer yet, but if left untreated, a small number can
develop into skin cancer over time.”
- “It’s common in adults who spend a lot of time in the sun,
especially fair-skinned people.”
- “Treatment reduces the risk of these lesions turning into skin
cancer.”
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Strict sun protection: broad-spectrum sunscreen, protective clothing,
hats.
- Avoid sunbeds and excessive UV exposure.
- Regular self-skin checks for new or changing lesions.
Pharmacological / Specialist Options:
- Cryotherapy (first-line for isolated lesions).
- Topical treatments for multiple lesions: 5-fluorouracil, imiquimod,
diclofenac gel.
- Photodynamic therapy for field treatment of multiple lesions.
Investigations:
- Usually clinical diagnosis.
- Biopsy if lesion suspicious for squamous cell carcinoma (rapid growth,
ulceration, bleeding).
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “Actinic keratosis is pre-cancerous, so treatment helps prevent
progression to skin cancer.”
- “Use sunscreen every day and avoid peak sun hours.”
- “Check your skin regularly for new or changing spots.”
- “If any lesion grows rapidly, bleeds, or changes color, seek urgent
review.”
- “We may refer you to dermatology for treatment or biopsy if needed.”

-- 379 of 437 --

349- Seborrhoeic Eczema
Definition: Chronic, relapsing
inflammatory skin condition affecting
sebaceous (oil-rich) areas: scalp,
face (nasolabial folds), chest, and
sometimes ears. Characterized by
erythema, scaling, and itching.
When to Suspect:
- Red, scaly, itchy patches in typical
distribution.
- Often worse in winter or during
stress.
Common DDx: Psoriasis, atopic
eczema, tinea capitis, contact
dermatitis, rosacea (for facial
lesions).
Red Flags:
- Signs of secondary bacterial
infection: pustules, spreading
redness, pus.
- Unusual distribution or rapidly
worsening lesions → consider
immunodeficiency or other
dermatologic conditions.
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, and pattern of flare-ups.
- Symptoms: itch, soreness, dandruff.
- Triggers: stress, cold weather, harsh soaps/shampoos.
- Past treatments tried (shampoos, creams, steroids).
- Family history of eczema, psoriasis, or other skin conditions.
- Associated systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss — rare).
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “This is seborrhoeic eczema, a common, non-contagious skin
condition.”
- “It happens because certain areas of your skin that produce more
oil can react to a harmless yeast (Malassezia) that normally lives on
the skin, or due to inflammation triggered by stress or irritation.”
- “The redness and scaling are caused by your skin reacting to these
factors — it’s not caused by infection from other people.”
- “It can flare up with stress, cold weather, or harsh
soaps/shampoos.”
- “It’s not dangerous, but it can be itchy, uncomfortable, and
sometimes cosmetically concerning.”
- “It often comes and goes, but with the right care we can control it
and reduce flare-ups.”
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Gentle, non-soap cleansers.
- Regular shampooing with antifungal or medicated shampoos
(ketoconazole, selenium sulfide) for scalp involvement.
- Avoid scratching and harsh scrubbing.
- Moisturise dry areas to reduce flaking and irritation.
- Identify and avoid triggers: stress, harsh soaps, cold/dry weather.
- Camouflage creams for cosmetic reassurance if needed.
Pharmacological / Specialist Options:
- Topical antifungal creams/ointments (ketoconazole, ciclopirox) for
inflamed areas.
- Low-potency topical corticosteroids for short periods during flares.
- Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) for sensitive
areas (face, eyelids).
Investigations:
- Usually clinical diagnosis.
- Consider skin swab or scrapings if secondary infection suspected.
- Biopsy only if atypical presentation or treatment-resistant.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “Seborrhoeic eczema is not contagious and usually responds well to
creams and medicated shampoos.”
- “Regular gentle skin care and avoiding triggers help prevent flare-ups.”
- “If you notice spreading redness, pus, or fever, seek urgent review as it
may be infected.”
- “Flare-ups are common, but we can control symptoms and keep the skin
comfortable.”
- “We can refer to dermatology if it doesn’t respond to usual treatments or
for persistent, troublesome flares.”

-- 380 of 437 --

350- Lichen Planus (LP)
Definition: Chronic inflammatory
skin and mucosal disorder. Classic
skin lesions are polygonal, flat-
topped, purple papules, often itchy.
Can affect wrists, ankles, oral
mucosa, genitalia, scalp (lichen
planopilaris).
When to Suspect:
- Purple, itchy, polygonal papules,
often in wrists, ankles, lower back.
- White, lacy patches in the mouth
(oral LP).
- Nail changes: thinning, ridging, or
loss.
Common DDx: Psoriasis, eczema,
drug-induced lichenoid reactions,
pityriasis rosea, chronic candidiasis
(oral LP).
Red Flags:
- Rapidly spreading lesions,
ulceration, persistent oral lesions →
consider squamous cell carcinoma
transformation (rare).
- Severe pain, secondary infection, or
functional impairment (oral intake,
vision if eyelids involved).
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, and pattern of lesions.
- Itchiness, pain, burning sensation, especially oral lesions.
- Medication history (recent drugs can trigger lichenoid reactions).
- Family or personal history of autoimmune disease.
- Oral habits: smoking, alcohol, dental trauma.
- Systemic symptoms: weight loss, fevers (rare).
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Lichen Planus is a skin and sometimes mouth condition where
your immune system reacts to your skin and mucosa, causing small,
flat, purplish bumps or lacy white patches.”
- “It’s not contagious and not caused by infection or poor hygiene.”
- “We don’t always know the exact cause, but sometimes
medications, stress, or autoimmune tendencies trigger it.”
- “Most cases improve over time, but it can come and go. Oral
lesions may persist longer.”
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Avoid skin trauma (Koebner phenomenon can trigger new lesions).
- Gentle skin care and moisturizers for itchy areas.
- Soft diet if oral lesions cause discomfort.
- Avoid irritants: spicy foods, alcohol, tobacco (for oral LP).
Pharmacological / Specialist Options:
- Topical corticosteroids (high-potency for skin; mid-potency for oral
mucosa).
- Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) for oral or
sensitive areas.
- Systemic therapy (oral steroids, retinoids, or immunosuppressants) for
severe or widespread disease — specialist-led.
- Treat secondary infections if present.
Investigations:
- Usually clinical diagnosis.
- Biopsy if diagnosis uncertain, rapidly changing lesions, or persistent oral
lesions to rule out malignancy.
- Consider hepatitis C testing (association with LP in some populations).
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “Lichen Planus is not infectious, but it can be uncomfortable or itchy.”
- “Most skin lesions improve with treatment; oral lesions may need longer-
term management.”
- “Avoid trauma to affected areas, and we’ll provide creams to reduce
inflammation and itch.”
- “If lesions ulcerate, persist, or oral lesions do not heal, we need to review
urgently to exclude rare complications.”
- “We can refer to dermatology or oral medicine for specialist
management if needed.”

-- 381 of 437 --

351- Pityriasis Alba
Definition: Common, benign, self-
limiting hypopigmented patches,
often on the face, usually in children
and adolescents. Mild scaling may be
present.
When to Suspect:
- Pale, slightly scaly patches, often on
cheeks, forehead, or upper arms.
- Typically occurs in children aged 3–
16.
- Often worse in summer or after sun
exposure (hypopigmentation more
noticeable).
Common DDx: Vitiligo, tinea
versicolor, post-inflammatory
hypopigmentation, atopic eczema.
Red Flags:
- Rapidly spreading depigmented
lesions.
- Complete loss of pigment or sharply
demarcated patches → consider
vitiligo.
- Scaling, erythema, or itch
suggesting fungal infection or
eczema.
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, progression of patches.
- Symptoms: itch, redness, scaling.
- Personal/family history of eczema, atopy, or skin conditions.
- Sun exposure, recent skin irritation, or inflammation.
- Impact on self-esteem or social concerns.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “These pale patches are called pityriasis alba, which is very
common in children and teenagers.”
- “They are usually harmless and often associated with mild eczema
or dry skin.”
- “The skin sometimes looks lighter after sun exposure because
surrounding skin tans more.”
- “It’s not contagious, not caused by infection, and not a sign of
serious illness.”
- “The patches usually improve gradually over months, but they can
take time to completely fade.”
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Emollients/moisturizers to reduce dryness.
- Gentle skin care, avoid harsh soaps and scrubbing.
- Sun protection: sunscreen and protective clothing.
- Reassure parents/child that it is cosmetic and self-limiting.
Pharmacological / Specialist Options:
- Mild topical corticosteroids if significant dryness, inflammation, or
eczema present.
- Topical calcineurin inhibitors for sensitive areas if needed.
Investigations:
- Usually clinical diagnosis.
- Consider KOH prep if fungal infection suspected.
- Referral if rapidly spreading, atypical, or diagnostic uncertainty exists.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “This is a harmless skin condition that improves over time.”
- “Regular moisturisers and gentle skin care help the patches fade faster.”
- “It’s not contagious and does not indicate serious illness.”
- “If the patches spread quickly, become scaly, itchy, or there’s complete
loss of pigment, come back for review.”
- “We can refer to dermatology if the appearance is distressing or
diagnosis is unclear.”

-- 382 of 437 --

352- Pityriasis Rosea (PR)
Definition: Acute, self-limiting
papulosquamous skin eruption, likely
viral (HHV-6/7). Typically starts with a
“herald patch” followed by a
generalized Christmas-tree
distribution rash on the trunk.
When to Suspect:
- Single initial herald patch (which is
usually a single, oval, slightly pink or
red patch with a fine, scaly edge,
often 2–10 cm in size. This patch
appears first and is sometimes
slightly itchy) followed by multiple
smaller oval patches along skin lines.
- Mild itch possible, otherwise well.
- Common in children and young
adults.
Common DDx: Tinea corporis,
secondary syphilis, guttate psoriasis,
drug eruption, eczema.
Red Flags:
- Systemic symptoms: fever, malaise,
lymphadenopathy.
- Rash on palms/soles → consider
secondary syphilis.
- Rapidly spreading, painful, or
blistering lesions.
- Rash persisting >12 weeks or
atypical distribution.
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, and sequence (herald patch first, then generalized
rash).
- Symptoms: itch, discomfort, pain.
- Recent viral illness or prodrome (common).
- Medications (rule out drug-induced eruption).
- Sexual history if palms/soles involved (rule out secondary syphilis).
- Family history of psoriasis or eczema.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “This is pityriasis rosea, a common skin rash that usually happens
after a mild viral infection.”
- “It often starts with a herald patch, which is a single, slightly larger
pink or red oval patch that appears first and signals the start of the
rash. Think of it as a ‘warning spot’ before the rest of the rash
appears.”
- “After a few days to a week, smaller patches develop, usually on
the trunk in a characteristic pattern.”
- “The rash may be slightly itchy, but it is not contagious and does
not indicate serious illness.”
- “The exact cause isn’t fully known, but it seems related to viral
reactivation, similar to a cold.”
- “Most rashes improve on their own within 6–8 weeks, though mild
skin marks may persist for longer.”
- “You don’t need to avoid school, work, or contact with others — it’s
safe.”
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Gentle skin care and moisturisers to reduce dryness and scaling.
- Avoid harsh soaps, scrubbing, or prolonged hot showers.
- Wear loose, breathable clothing.
- Cool compresses or oatmeal baths for itch relief.
- Avoid scratching to prevent secondary infection.
Pharmacological / Specialist Options:
- Topical corticosteroids for localized itch.
- Oral antihistamines for symptomatic relief.
- UVB therapy rarely used in persistent or widespread cases
(dermatology).
Investigations:
- Usually clinical diagnosis.
- Consider VDRL/RPR if palms/soles involved or sexual history suggests
secondary syphilis.
- Biopsy only if atypical presentation.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “Pityriasis rosea is harmless, self-limiting, and usually clears within 6–8
weeks.”
- “The herald patch is the first spot and helps us recognise the condition
early. It is oval, slightly pink or red, with a fine scaly border.”
- “It’s not contagious and not caused by poor hygiene.”
- “Use moisturisers and avoid scratching to reduce discomfort.”
- “Return if the rash spreads to palms/soles, is painful, blistering, or does
not improve after a few months.”
- “We can refer to dermatology if diagnosis is uncertain or symptoms are
severe or persistent.”

-- 383 of 437 --

353- Pityriasis Versicolor (Tinea
Versicolor)
Definition: Superficial fungal
infection caused by Malassezia
species, leading to hypo- or
hyperpigmented scaly patches, often
on the trunk, neck, and upper arms.
When to Suspect:
- Patches that are lighter or darker
than surrounding skin.
- Fine scaling, sometimes mildly
itchy.
- More noticeable after sun exposure.
- Recurrent or persistent despite
general hygiene.
Common DDx: Vitiligo, pityriasis
alba, seborrhoeic dermatitis,
eczema.
Red Flags:
- Rapidly spreading or inflamed
lesions → consider secondary
infection.
- Systemic symptoms (fever, malaise)
— rare, suggest other conditions.
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, progression of lesions.
- Symptoms: itch, scaling.
- Previous episodes or treatment.
- Sun exposure (hypopigmentation more noticeable).
- Skin care products used.
- Family history of skin conditions.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “This is pityriasis versicolor, a common fungal infection of the skin
caused by a yeast called Malassezia.”
- “It causes patches that may be lighter or darker than your normal
skin and may have fine scaling.”
- “It is not dangerous and not contagious, but it can recur,
especially in hot, humid weather or if the skin is oily.”
- “The color changes can take several weeks to months to fully return
to normal, even after treatment.”
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Daily bathing and gentle skin cleansing.
- Avoid excessive sweating and occlusive clothing when possible.
- Sun protection: avoid tanning, as contrast makes patches more
noticeable.
- Keep skin dry and use breathable fabrics.
Pharmacological / Specialist Options:
- Topical antifungals: ketoconazole, selenium sulfide, terbinafine applied
to affected areas.
- Oral antifungals (fluconazole, itraconazole) in widespread or recurrent
cases — specialist-led.
Investigations:
- Usually clinical diagnosis.
- Wood’s lamp: yellow-green fluorescence may help.
- KOH prep: spaghetti-and-meatballs appearance confirms Malassezia.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “This skin condition is harmless and can be treated with antifungal
creams or shampoos.”
- “It is not contagious, and good hygiene helps prevent recurrence.”
- “Color changes may persist for weeks after treatment, but the yeast is
cleared.”
- “Return if the rash spreads rapidly, becomes inflamed, itchy, or fails to
improve with treatment.”
- “Recurrent cases may need specialist review for oral antifungal therapy.”

-- 384 of 437 --

354- Erythema Multiforme (EM)
Definition: Acute, immune-mediated
skin reaction, often triggered by
infections (commonly HSV) or
medications. Characteristic target
(“bullseye”) lesions, mainly on
extremities; mucosal involvement in
EM major.
When to Suspect:
- Acute onset of target lesions on
hands, feet, or forearms.
- Mild itch or burning.
- Sometimes preceded by infection
(cold sore/URI) or recent drug
exposure.
Common DDx: Urticaria, Stevens-
Johnson syndrome, drug eruptions,
viral exanthems.
Red Flags:
- Widespread blistering or mucosal
ulceration → suspect Stevens-
Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal
necrolysis.
- Fever, systemic illness, or pain.
- Rapid progression of lesions.
Focused History:
- Onset and progression of rash.
- Recent infections (HSV, mycoplasma, URIs).
- Medication history (antibiotics, NSAIDs, anticonvulsants).
- Mucosal involvement: mouth, eyes, genitals.
- Systemic symptoms: fever, malaise, joint pain.
- Previous episodes.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Erythema multiforme is a skin reaction, usually triggered by a viral
infection or sometimes a medication. It is not contagious and does
not mean serious illness in most cases.”
- “It causes round, target-shaped or bullseye lesions, usually starting
on the hands, feet, or arms, and sometimes appearing on the trunk.”
- “Mouth or other mucosal lesions can occur, which can be
uncomfortable but are usually self-limiting.”
- “The body’s immune system reacts to the infection or medication,
leading to these characteristic spots.”
- “Most rashes resolve on their own within 2–6 weeks, but recurrent
episodes can occur in some patients, especially if linked to cold sore
infections.”
- “It is important to identify triggers, so we can prevent future
episodes and reassure you that it usually does not cause long-term
problems.”
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Gentle skin care and moisturisers to reduce dryness and scaling.
- Avoid scratching to prevent secondary infection.
- Lukewarm baths, loose breathable clothing, cool compresses for
comfort.
- Analgesic mouthwash if oral lesions present.
Pharmacological / Specialist Options:
- Topical corticosteroids for mild skin lesions.
- Systemic corticosteroids may be considered for severe EM major
(specialist-led).
- Antiviral therapy (acyclovir) if recurrent EM linked to HSV.
Investigations:
- Usually clinical diagnosis.
- Consider swabs or serology if HSV suspected.
- Blood tests if systemic symptoms: FBC, LFTs, renal function.
- Biopsy only if atypical or severe presentation.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “Most cases of EM are mild and resolve on their own.”
- “Avoid scratching and keep skin clean to reduce discomfort and prevent
infection.”
- “Return urgently if rash spreads rapidly, blisters extensively, or you
develop mouth, eye, or genital ulcers or pain.”
- “We will review medications and infections that might trigger it to prevent
recurrence.”
- “Recurrent EM may benefit from antiviral prophylaxis if linked to cold
sore outbreaks.”

-- 385 of 437 --

355- Erythema Nodosum (EN)
Definition: Acute, inflammatory
reaction of the subcutaneous fat
(panniculitis), presenting as tender,
red or purple nodules, usually on the
anterior shins. Often associated with
infections, medications, or systemic
conditions.
When to Suspect:
- Acute onset of tender, raised, red-
purple nodules, usually on the shins.
- Fever, malaise, or arthralgia may be
present.
- Recent infection (streptococcal
pharyngitis, TB), medication exposure
(sulfonamides, oral contraceptives),
or systemic disease (IBD,
sarcoidosis).
Common DDx: Cellulitis, vasculitis,
deep vein thrombosis, panniculitis
from other causes.
Red Flags:
- Severe systemic symptoms: high
fever, hypotension.
- Rapidly spreading lesions,
blistering, or ulceration.
- Signs of deep vein thrombosis or
severe infection.
Focused History:
- Onset, progression, and location of nodules.
- Recent infections: sore throat, respiratory infections.
- Medication history (recent antibiotics, oral contraceptives).
- Associated symptoms: fever, malaise, joint aches, cough, GI
symptoms.
- Past medical history: IBD, sarcoidosis, autoimmune conditions.
- Family history of autoimmune disease.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Erythema nodosum is a reaction in the fat layer under the skin that
causes tender, red or purple lumps, usually on the front of the
legs.”
- “It happens because your immune system reacts to an infection,
medication, or sometimes another underlying condition, leading
to inflammation in the skin.”
- “The lumps themselves are not dangerous and do not spread to
other people.”
- “You may also feel generally unwell, have mild fever, or joint aches
— this is part of the body’s reaction.”
- “It usually improves over 2–6 weeks, especially once the underlying
cause is addressed.”
- “Recurrence can happen if triggers occur again, which is why
identifying infections or medications that caused it is important.”
- “The redness and swelling can be painful, but the condition itself is
self-limiting and not life-threatening.”
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Rest and leg elevation to reduce discomfort and swelling.
- Cool compresses for pain and redness.
- Avoid prolonged standing or pressure on affected legs.
- Comfortable, supportive footwear.
Pharmacological / Specialist Options:
- NSAIDs for pain and inflammation (if no contraindications).
- Treat underlying cause: antibiotics for streptococcal infection,
management of IBD, sarcoidosis, or adjustment of triggering medications.
- Corticosteroids rarely for severe or persistent cases (specialist-led).
Investigations:
- FBC, ESR/CRP to assess inflammation.
- Throat swab or streptococcal serology if recent sore throat.
- Chest X-ray if sarcoidosis suspected.
- TB screening if risk factors.
- Additional tests guided by suspected underlying cause.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “Erythema nodosum is usually self-limiting and improves over a few
weeks.”
- “The lumps are tender but harmless and not contagious.”
- “Elevate your legs, rest, and use pain relief as needed.”
- “Return urgently if you develop severe pain, spreading redness,
blistering, fever, or swelling suggestive of deep vein thrombosis or
infection.”
- “We will investigate for underlying causes to prevent recurrence and
manage triggers appropriately.”

-- 386 of 437 --

356- Bullous Pemphigoid (BP)
Definition: Chronic autoimmune
blistering skin disorder, mainly in
older adults, caused by
autoantibodies against
hemidesmosomal proteins (BP180,
BP230), leading to subepidermal
blisters.
When to Suspect:
- Older adult with tense blisters on
normal or erythematous skin,
commonly on trunk, limbs, and
flexural areas.
- Itching may precede blisters.
- Chronic or relapsing course.
Common DDx: Pemphigus vulgaris,
dermatitis herpetiformis, bullous
drug eruptions, chronic eczema.
Red Flags:
- Extensive blistering or mucosal
involvement (pemphigus overlap).
- Signs of infection: pus, fever.
- Rapidly worsening or painful lesions.
Focused History:
- Onset and progression of blisters.
- Itch severity and location.
- Previous skin disorders or autoimmune conditions.
- Medication history (recent drugs that may trigger bullous
eruptions).
- Systemic symptoms: fever, malaise.
- Past dermatology review or biopsy results.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Bullous pemphigoid is a condition where your body’s immune
system mistakenly attacks the skin, causing tense blisters.”
- “These blisters usually appear on the trunk, arms, or legs and may
be preceded by itching.”
- “It is not contagious and does not result from poor hygiene.”
- “The condition can be chronic or relapsing but is usually
manageable with treatment.”
- “Blisters can be painful or uncomfortable, and scratching can lead
to infection, so careful skin care is important.”
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Gentle skin care: avoid trauma, scratching, or harsh soaps.
- Cool compresses for discomfort.
- Maintain hydration and nutrition.
- Dressings for ruptured blisters to prevent secondary infection.
Pharmacological / Specialist Options:
- Topical high-potency corticosteroids for limited disease.
- Systemic corticosteroids for widespread disease.
- Immunosuppressants (azathioprine, mycophenolate) or biologics
(rituximab) for severe/refractory disease — specialist-led.
- Antibiotics only if secondary infection occurs.
Referral Criteria:
- All suspected cases for urgent dermatology referral.
- Widespread disease or mucosal involvement.
- Refractory to topical treatment.
- Rapid progression, severe pruritus, or secondary infection.
- Need for systemic immunosuppressive therapy.
Investigations:
- Skin biopsy for histology and direct immunofluorescence.
- Blood tests: FBC, renal, liver function before systemic therapy.
- Consider antibody titres (BP180/BP230) if needed.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “This is an autoimmune condition causing blisters but is not
contagious.”
- “Careful skin care and avoiding scratching reduce complications.”
- “Most cases respond to topical or systemic treatment under
dermatology guidance.”
- “Return urgently if you develop signs of infection, rapidly spreading
blisters, fever, or mouth lesions.”
- “We will monitor response to treatment and adjust medications to
minimize side effects.”

-- 387 of 437 --

357- Stevens-Johnson Syndrome
(SJS) / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis
(TEN)
Definition: Severe, life-threatening
mucocutaneous reactions, usually
drug-induced, characterized by
widespread epidermal necrosis and
detachment.
- SJS: <10% body surface area (BSA)
detachment.
- TEN: >30% BSA detachment.
When to Suspect:
- Rapid onset of painful, red or
purplish rash with blisters.
- Mucosal involvement: mouth, eyes,
genitals.
- Often preceded by drug exposure
(antibiotics, anticonvulsants,
NSAIDs) within 1–3 weeks.
- Prodrome: fever, malaise, flu-like
symptoms.
Common DDx: Bullous pemphigoid,
pemphigus vulgaris, staphylococcal
scalded skin syndrome, severe drug
eruptions.
Red Flags:
- Extensive skin detachment,
blistering, or erosions.
- High fever, hypotension, sepsis.
- Eye involvement → risk of permanent
damage.
- Rapid deterioration.
Focused History:
- Recent drug exposure within 1–3 weeks.
- Onset, progression, and distribution of rash/blisters.
- Mucosal involvement: mouth, eyes, genitals.
- Prodromal symptoms: fever, malaise, arthralgia.
- Past drug reactions or allergies.
- Systemic symptoms: shortness of breath, confusion, dehydration.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “You have a severe skin reaction, often caused by a medication,
where the top layer of the skin and mucous membranes are
damaged.”
- “This can be painful and may affect the mouth, eyes, and genitals.”
- “It is serious and potentially life-threatening, which is why urgent
hospital care is needed.”
- “It is not contagious and not caused by an infection alone.”
mmediate Management / Pre-Hospital:
- Stop suspected causative drug immediately.
- Provide supportive care: fluids, analgesia, wound protection.
- Monitor for signs of sepsis, dehydration, or electrolyte imbalance.
- Avoid topical irritants.
Hospital / Specialist Management:
- Urgent admission, ideally to a burn unit or intensive care unit.
- Multidisciplinary team: dermatology, ophthalmology, ICU, nutrition.
- Supportive care: fluid/electrolyte replacement, pain control, wound
care.
- Systemic therapy (corticosteroids, IVIG, ciclosporin) per specialist
guidance.
- Monitor for secondary infection and organ involvement.
Investigations:
- Bloods: FBC, U&E, LFTs, CRP.
- Cultures if infection suspected.
- Skin biopsy for confirmation.
Referral / Urgent Action:
- Immediate hospital admission – SJS/TEN is a medical emergency.
- Notify dermatology, burn unit, ICU as appropriate.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “This is a medical emergency; we need to admit you urgently to prevent
complications.”
- “We have stopped the suspected medication, and the hospital team will
monitor your skin, fluids, and vital organs closely.”
- “Do not take any new medications without checking with a doctor.”
- “Return immediately if you develop worsening skin, mouth, eye
problems, fever, or confusion.”

-- 388 of 437 --

358- PUPPP (Pruritic Urticarial
Papules and Plaques of Pregnancy)
Definition: Common, benign,
pregnancy-related dermatosis
characterized by itchy papules and
plaques, typically starting in the
abdominal striae during the third
trimester. Resolves spontaneously
postpartum.
When to Suspect:
- Third trimester of first pregnancy
(primigravida most common).
- Rash starting within abdominal
stretch marks, may spread to thighs,
buttocks, arms.
- Severe pruritus but no systemic
symptoms.
Common DDx: Atopic eruption of
pregnancy, pemphigoid gestationis,
contact dermatitis, viral exanthems.
Red Flags:
- Lesions before third trimester,
severe systemic symptoms, mucosal
involvement → consider pemphigoid
gestationis or other serious
dermatoses.
Focused History:
- Onset, location, and progression of rash.
- Severity of itching and sleep disturbance.
- Personal or family history of atopy.
- Medication history.
- Obstetric history: gestation, multiple pregnancy.
- Systemic symptoms: fever, malaise, blisters.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “This is a common pregnancy-related rash that causes itchy red
bumps or plaques, usually starting on your belly in the stretch
marks.”
- “It is not dangerous for you or your baby and usually resolves
within a few days to weeks after delivery.”
- “The exact cause is unclear but is thought to be related to
stretching of the skin and pregnancy-related immune changes.”
- “It is not contagious and does not indicate an allergy or infection.”
Non-Pharmacological / Supportive:
- Emollients and moisturizers to soothe skin.
- Cool baths or compresses to reduce itching.
- Loose, breathable clothing.
- Avoid scratching to prevent secondary infection.
Pharmacological / Specialist Options:
- Oral or topical antihistamines (e.g., chlorphenamine) for itch relief.
- Topical mild to moderate corticosteroids for limited areas if severe.
- Systemic corticosteroids rarely needed (specialist-led) for extensive
pruritus.
Investigations:
- Usually clinical diagnosis.
- Consider blood tests or biopsy only if atypical presentation or early-
onset rash to rule out pemphigoid gestationis.
Referral Criteria:
- Unusual onset (early pregnancy), mucosal involvement,
severe/extensive rash, poor response to standard treatment →
dermatology referral.
- Any suspicion of pemphigoid gestationis, which carries fetal risk.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “This rash is common in pregnancy and usually resolves after delivery.”
- “Moisturizers and antihistamines help relieve itching.”
- “Return promptly if blisters develop, the rash spreads rapidly, or you
develop fever or mucosal lesions.”
- “We will monitor to ensure it does not indicate a more serious
pregnancy-related skin condition.”

359- Rashes in pregnancy
Condition / Scenario Focused History & Layperson-Friendly Explanation (SCA phrasing) Management Plan (with SCA phrases, Non-
Pharmacological, Safety Netting & Referral Criteria)
PUPPP (Pruritic Urticarial Papules
& Plaques of Pregnancy)
History: Third trimester, rash starting in abdominal striae, may spread to thighs/arms.
Severe itching, no systemic symptoms. Personal/family atopy history.
Lay Explanation: “Common pregnancy rash, itchy bumps on belly stretch marks, not
harmful to baby, usually resolves after delivery.”
Management:
- Emollients, cool baths, loose clothing.
- Antihistamines for itch relief.
- Mild/moderate topical corticosteroids for limited areas.
Referral: Dermatology if early onset, mucosal involvement,
severe/extensive rash.
Safety Netting: Return if blisters, rapid spread, fever, or
mucosal lesions.
Pemphigoid Gestationis History: Rare autoimmune blistering, usually second/third trimester. Severe pruritus,
blisters, may start around umbilicus, can extend.
Lay Explanation: “Immune system reacts against your skin during pregnancy, causing
blisters; can affect baby slightly, requires careful monitoring.”
Management:
- Specialist-led: topical or systemic corticosteroids.
- Antihistamines for itch.
Referral: Urgent dermatology/obstetric review.
Safety Netting: Return for worsening blisters, infection, or
fetal concerns.
Prurigo of Pregnancy (Papular
Urticaria of Pregnancy)
History: Mid-late pregnancy, intensely itchy papules on limbs, usually without
systemic symptoms.
Lay Explanation: “Small itchy bumps due to pregnancy changes in skin, harmless to
baby, usually resolves after birth.
Management:
- Emollients, topical corticosteroids for itch.
- Oral antihistamines if needed.
Referral: Atypical lesions, severe itch, or poor response.
Safety Netting: Return if blisters, infection, or rash spreads
rapidly.
Intrahepatic Cholestasis of
Pregnancy (ICP) – with pruritus
History: Severe generalized pruritus, worse on palms/soles, often late second/third
trimester. No primary rash.
Lay Explanation: “Liver changes in pregnancy can cause intense itch; this does not
harm your skin but may affect baby, so monitoring is important.”
Management:
- Ursodeoxycholic acid per obstetric guidance.
- Topical emollients for comfort.
Referral: Obstetric review for liver function and fetal
monitoring.
Safety Netting: Return if jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, or
worsening itch.

360- Chickenpox in Child – Concern:
Pregnant Mother Exposure
Definition: Acute viral infection
caused by varicella-zoster virus (VZV),
with pruritic vesicular rash, fever,
malaise.
When to Suspect:
- Typical vesicular rash in crops
starting on trunk/face, spreading to
limbs.
- Fever, malaise, irritability.
- Known exposure to other cases or
outbreaks.
Common DDx: Hand-foot-mouth
disease, insect bites, impetigo,
shingles (rare in child).
Red Flags:
- High fever, lethargy, poor oral
intake, secondary bacterial infection.
- Immunocompromised child.
- Pregnant mother exposed and non-
immune.
Focused History:
- Rash onset, distribution, and progression.
- Associated fever, malaise, irritability.
- Exposure history: contact with chickenpox/shingles.
- Past infection or vaccination (child & mother).
- Any underlying chronic illness or immunocompromise.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Your child has chickenpox, which causes itchy blisters and fever.
It is common in children and usually mild.”
- “Chickenpox is contagious, so we need to consider if anyone at
home is at risk.”
- “Since you are pregnant, we need to check your immunity because
chickenpox in pregnancy can rarely affect the baby.”
- “Most healthy children recover fully with supportive care.”
Management:
- Supportive care for child: paracetamol for fever/pain, cool baths,
emollients, antihistamines for itch.
- Avoid NSAIDs in chickenpox due to risk of complications.
- Maintain hydration and nutrition.
Investigations / Actions for Mother:
- Advise that the most infectious period is from 24 hours before the rash
appears, but infectivity continues until all the lesions have crusted over
(commonly about 5 days after the onset of the rash)
- If the woman has a definite history of chickenpox or shingles or two
doses of a varicella containing vaccine, and is not immunocompromised =
reassurance
- If not and significant exposure: test for VZ IgG within 48 hours: if positive
= reassure and if negative = urgently discuss with a specialist the need for
prophylaxis (antiviral treatment or human varicella-zoster immunoglobulin
[VZIG]).
- If started to have rash, seek immediate advice + offer symptomatic
treatment + avoid contact with other pregnant ladies or
immunosuppressed
- Red flags: respiratory symptoms/ neurological symptoms/ persistent
fever or cropping of the rash continue after 6 days = send for urgent
hospital assessment
Referral / Urgent Advice:
- Pregnant mother non-immune → urgent obstetric referral.
- Child with severe disease, high fever, immunocompromised, or
secondary infection → hospital referral.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “We will check your immunity and take steps to protect your baby.”
- “Return immediately if you develop a rash, fever, or feel unwell.”
- “Most children recover well, but we monitor the mother closely to
prevent complications.”

-- 391 of 437 --

361- Hand-Foot-and-Mouth Disease
(HFM)
Definition: Common viral illness
(usually coxsackie A16 or
enterovirus 71) causing oral ulcers
and vesicular rash on hands, feet,
and sometimes buttocks.
When to Suspect:
- Child with fever, malaise, reduced
appetite.
- Painful oral ulcers / vesicles.
- Vesicular rash on palms, soles,
fingers, toes, buttocks.
- Usually <10 years old.
Common DDx: Chickenpox,
herpangina, eczema herpeticum,
varicella, hand dermatitis.
Red Flags:
- Signs of dehydration (poor oral
intake, lethargy).
- High persistent fever.
- Neurological symptoms
(encephalitis, seizures) → rare but
serious.
- Immunocompromised child.
Focused History:
- Onset, progression, distribution of rash and oral lesions.
- Associated fever, irritability, sore throat, reduced feeding.
- Exposure history (other children at nursery/school).
- Past medical history, immunization status, immunocompromise.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Your child has a common viral illness that causes a mild fever,
mouth ulcers, and a rash on the hands and feet.”
- “It is not serious in healthy children and usually resolves in 7–10
days.”
- “It is contagious, so avoid contact with vulnerable people,
especially pregnant women who are non-immune to chickenpox.”
- “Most children recover fully with supportive care.”
Management / Supportive Care:
- Symptomatic treatment: paracetamol for fever/pain, topical anesthetic
gels for oral ulcers.
- Maintain hydration and nutrition; soft foods if oral ulcers painful.
- Hand hygiene to prevent spread.
- Avoid NSAIDs in children under certain ages as per guidance.
Investigations:
- Usually clinical diagnosis; no routine labs required.
- Consider swabs only if atypical presentation or severe disease.
Referral / Urgent Advice:
- Severe dehydration, persistent high fever, lethargy → hospital review.
- Neurological symptoms (seizures, altered consciousness) → urgent
referral.
- Immunocompromised child with HFM → specialist advice.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “This illness is usually mild and self-limiting.”
- “Encourage fluids and soft foods; keep your child comfortable.”
- “Return if your child cannot drink, has persistent fever, unusual
drowsiness, or seizures.”
- “Practice good hand hygiene to prevent spread to other children or
vulnerable adults.”

-- 392 of 437 --

362- Molluscum Contagiosum
Definition: Common viral skin
infection caused by poxvirus, leading
to small, firm, dome-shaped, flesh-
colored papules with central
umbilication. Self-limiting in most
cases.
When to Suspect:
- Children 1–10 years old (most
common), or sexually active adults.
- Lesions: small, shiny, pearly
papules with central dimple,
scattered or grouped.
- Often asymptomatic but can be
itchy or inflamed.
Common DDx: Warts, acne, skin
tags, basal cell carcinoma (adults),
chickenpox (vesicular stage),
folliculitis.
Red Flags:
- Rapidly spreading lesions,
ulceration, signs of secondary
bacterial infection.
- Immunocompromised patients with
widespread disease.
- Adult with genital lesions → consider
STI screen.
Focused History:
- Onset, number, and distribution of lesions.
- Symptoms: itching, redness, irritation.
- Recent exposure to affected children or adults (playgroups,
schools, sexual contact).
- Past medical history: immunodeficiency, eczema, chronic illness.
- Any prior treatments or topical medications.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Your child / you have a mild viral skin infection that causes small,
firm bumps on the skin.”
- “It is contagious, but usually harmless and resolves on its own
within several months.”
- “Scratching can spread the virus to other parts of the body or to
other people.”
- “In children, it is not sexually transmitted. In adults, genital lesions
may require STI discussion.”
Management / Supportive Care:
- Observation: Most cases self-resolve in 6–12 months.
- Topical or procedural treatments if symptomatic, cosmetically
concerning, or persistent: cryotherapy, curettage, topical agents (e.g.,
potassium hydroxide, cantharidin).
- Skin hygiene: avoid scratching, keep nails short, wash hands regularly.
- Prevent transmission: avoid sharing towels, clothing, and close skin
contact with non-infected people.
Investigations:
- Usually clinical diagnosis.
- Biopsy or swab rarely needed if atypical or immunocompromised.
Referral / Urgent Advice:
- Immunocompromised patients with widespread or atypical lesions →
dermatology referral.
- Adult with genital lesions → STI screening / sexual health referral.
- Secondary bacterial infection → treat with appropriate antibiotics.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “This condition is usually self-limiting and harmless.”
- “Avoid scratching to reduce spread and infection.”
- “Return if lesions become painful, inflamed, or widespread, or if there
are concerns about immune problems.”
- “We can discuss treatments if the bumps do not resolve after several
months or are causing discomfort.”

-- 393 of 437 --

363- Scabies
Definition: Contagious skin
infestation caused by the Sarcoptes
scabiei mite, leading to intense
pruritus, especially at night, and
characteristic skin lesions.
When to Suspect:
- Intense itching, worse at night.
- Burrows or papules in web spaces
of fingers, wrists, waistline,
genitalia, axillae.
- Family members or close contacts
with similar symptoms.
- Infants: widespread vesicles,
palms/soles affected.
Common DDx: Eczema, impetigo,
dermatitis herpetiformis, insect bites,
fungal infections.
Red Flags:
- Secondary bacterial infection
(impetigo, cellulitis).
- Crusted scabies
(immunocompromised, elderly).
- Extensive lesions with systemic
illness.
Focused History:
- Onset and severity of itching.
- Distribution of rash and burrows.
- Household or close contact symptoms.
- Previous scabies treatments or skin conditions.
- Immunocompromised status (HIV, transplant, elderly).
- Travel history or institutional exposure (schools, care homes).
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Scabies is caused by tiny mites that burrow into the skin, causing
intense itching, especially at night.”
- “It is contagious, so other household members may also be
affected.”
- “It is not a sign of poor hygiene — anyone can get scabies.”
- “Treatment is effective, but itching may continue for a few weeks
even after mites are gone.”
Management:
- First-line: topical permethrin 5% cream (whole body from neck down,
leave 8–14 hours, repeat after 7 days).
- Alternative: oral ivermectin in selected cases (specialist guidance).
- Non-pharmacological: wash clothing, bedding, towels in hot water;
vacuum furniture; avoid close contact until treated.
- Treat all household contacts simultaneously.
Investigations:
- Usually clinical; dermoscopy or skin scraping to identify mites in
uncertain cases.
Referral / Urgent Advice:
- Crusted scabies → dermatology / infectious diseases referral.
- Immunocompromised patients with severe disease.
- Secondary bacterial infection → treat with antibiotics.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “Itching may persist for 2–4 weeks after treatment — this is normal.”
- “Reapply cream as directed and ensure all household contacts are
treated.”
- “Return if rash worsens, spreads, or you notice signs of infection
(redness, pus, fever).”
- “Avoid close skin-to-skin contact until treatment is complete.

-- 394 of 437 --

364- Refractory / Persistent Napkin
Dermatitis
Definition: Persistent erythema,
irritation, or rash in the diaper area
not responding to standard
emollients/barrier creams.
When to Suspect:
- Rash >1–2 weeks despite regular
barrier creams.
- Involvement beyond typical diaper
area (thighs, abdomen, groin folds).
- Severe erythema, erosions,
pustules, or bleeding.
Common DDx:
- Candida (yeast) infection.
- Bacterial infection (staph, strep).
- Eczema / atopic dermatitis.
- Psoriasis (napkin variant).
- Contact dermatitis (wipes,
detergents).
Red Flags:
- Ulceration, pus, systemic signs
(fever, lethargy).
- Suspected immunodeficiency.
- Failure to thrive.
Focused History:
- Duration, progression, and previous treatments.
- Frequency of nappy changes, type of wipes/diapers.
- Associated symptoms: pain, bleeding, fever, systemic signs.
- Family history: atopy, psoriasis, recurrent infections.
- Previous episodes or recurrent rash.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Your child has a rash in the nappy area that hasn’t improved with
usual creams.”
- “There are several possible causes — yeast infection, bacterial
infection, eczema, or irritation from products.”
- “With the right treatment, most rashes heal quickly. We just need to
find the exact cause.”
Management:
- First-line / review: Ensure frequent nappy changes, air exposure, mild
cleansers, barrier creams.
- If suspected Candida (yeast): topical antifungal (e.g., nystatin or
clotrimazole) ± mild steroid for inflammation.
- If suspected bacterial infection: consider topical antibiotic if localised
(e.g., fusidic acid) or systemic if severe.
- Atopic dermatitis / eczema: emollients, short-course low-potency
topical steroid if inflamed.
- Avoid harsh soaps, wipes with fragrances, and tight-fitting nappies.
Investigations:
- Skin swab for bacteria/fungal culture if refractory.
- Consider referral if atypical distribution, recurrent or severe rash.
Referral / Urgent Advice:
- Suspected immunodeficiency, systemic infection, ulceration, or failure
to thrive → paediatric/dermatology referral.
- Refractory rash despite 1–2 weeks of appropriate topical therapy.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “We will adjust treatment according to the cause — yeast, bacteria, or
eczema.”
- “Keep the area clean, dry, and well-aired.”
- “Return urgently if fever, pus, bleeding, or spreading rash occurs.”
- “Most rashes improve quickly once the right treatment is started.”

-- 395 of 437 --

365- Plantar Wart (Verruca Plantaris)
Definition: Hyperkeratotic lesion on
the plantar surface caused by human
papillomavirus (HPV). Can be
painful, especially when weight-
bearing.
When to Suspect:
- Thickened, rough, hyperkeratotic
papule or plaque on the sole of the
foot.
- May have small black dots (“seed
warts”) representing thrombosed
capillaries.
- Pain on pressure, particularly
vertical pressure (pinch test
negative).
- Often in children, adolescents, or
immunocompromised adults.
Common DDx: Callus/corn, foreign
body, plantar fibroma, molluscum
contagiosum (rare on sole).
Red Flags:
- Rapidly growing, ulcerated, or
bleeding lesion.
- Immunocompromised patient.
- Lesions not responding to standard
treatments.
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, and progression of lesion(s).
- Pain severity, impact on walking/activities.
- Previous treatments (cryotherapy, salicylic acid).
- Exposure history (swimming pools, communal showers).
- Past medical history: immunosuppression, diabetes.
- Family history: tendency for warts.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Your child / you have a wart on the sole of the foot caused by a
virus.”
- “It is not dangerous, but can be painful when walking or standing.”
- “Warts are contagious through direct contact, so avoid sharing
towels or shoes.”
- “Most warts improve with time, but treatment can speed this up.
- “The virus can spread through direct contact, such as wet surfaces
in communal areas like swimming pools.”
- “Using protective footwear (flip-flops) and keeping feet clean and
dry reduces the risk of spreading to others.”
Management:
- First-line: Salicylic acid daily (after soaking and gentle removal of thick
skin).
- Non-pharmacological: Keep feet clean and dry, wear socks/shoes,
avoid walking barefoot in communal areas.
- Swimming advice:
- Wear waterproof flip-flops in pool areas and showers.
- Cover wart with waterproof plaster if needed.
- Avoid swimming if the wart is bleeding or inflamed.
- Second-line / GP-led: Cryotherapy, cantharidin, laser if
painful/persistent.
- Investigations: Usually clinical; biopsy if atypical/ulcerated.
- Referral / Urgent Advice: Immunocompromised, rapidly growing,
ulcerated, or painful lesions.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “The wart is harmless but contagious; good hygiene helps prevent
spread.”
- “Apply treatment daily and keep the area covered and clean.”
- “Return if lesion bleeds, grows rapidly, or does not improve after several
weeks.”
- “Protect feet in communal areas, like pools and showers, to prevent
spreading to others.”

-- 396 of 437 --

366- Freckle (Ephelides)
Definition: Small, flat, brown
macules on sun-exposed skin caused
by increased melanin production,
often genetically determined.
When to Suspect:
- Multiple small, light-brown macules,
usually on face, arms, shoulders.
- More apparent in fair-skinned
individuals, especially children.
- Pigmentation darkens in summer,
fades in winter.
Common DDx: Lentigines, solar
lentigo, café-au-lait spots, nevus,
early melanoma (rare in children).
Red Flags:
- Rapid change in size, shape, or
color.
- Irregular borders, multiple colors,
bleeding, ulceration.
- Family history of melanoma.
Focused History:
- Onset and progression.
- Sun exposure history, use of sunscreen.
- Family history of freckles, moles, or skin cancer.
- Any associated lesions elsewhere on the body.
- History of skin changes, bleeding, or pain.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Freckles are small, harmless spots caused by sun exposure
and genetics.”
- “They are not dangerous and usually appear on fair-skinned people
in the sun.”
- “Freckles may darken in summer and lighten in winter.”
- “They are different from moles or melanoma, which may need
closer attention.”
Management / Advice:
- No treatment needed; cosmetic treatment optional if desired.
- Sun protection: Use sunscreen, wear hats and protective clothing.
- Monitoring: Observe for any new, changing, or irregular lesions.
Referral / Urgent Advice:
- Dermatology referral if lesions are atypical, rapidly changing, bleeding, or
suspicious for melanoma.
- Education on skin self-examination and sun safety.
SCA Phrases & Safety Netting:
- “Freckles are normal and harmless, especially in fair-skinned children.”
- “Use sunscreen and avoid excessive sun exposure.”
- “Return if you notice any sudden changes in size, color, or shape of a
spot.”
- “Dermatology review is only needed for atypical or suspicious lesions.”

-- 397 of 437 --

367- Varicose veins
Definition: Dilated, tortuous
superficial veins in the legs due to
valve incompetence causing venous
reflux.
Dx Criteria / When to Suspect:
- Visible, bulging superficial veins.
- Symptoms: aching, heaviness,
swelling, itching, cramps, cosmetic
concern.
Risk Factors:
- Increasing age.
- Female sex.
- Family history.
- Pregnancy.
- Obesity.
- Prolonged standing/sedentary
lifestyle.
- Previous DVT or leg trauma.
Red Flags:
- Skin changes (eczema,
pigmentation, lipodermatosclerosis).
- Venous ulceration (esp. around
ankle).
- Sudden bleeding from a vein.
- Superficial vein thrombosis.
- Sudden severe swelling (rule out
DVT).
Focused History:
- Symptom burden: pain, heaviness, swelling, itching, cosmetic
concerns.
- Duration, triggers (standing, heat, pregnancy).
- Effect on mobility, work, daily life.
- History of DVT, surgery, trauma, pregnancies.
- Red flags: ulcers, bleeding, sudden swelling, skin changes.
Lay Explanation:
- “Varicose veins are swollen, twisted veins you can see under the
skin, usually in the legs.”
- “They happen because the valves in the veins don’t work
properly, so blood flows backwards and pools.”
- “This makes them bulge, and can cause aching, heaviness, or
itching.”
- “They are more likely with age, pregnancy, family history, or if you
stand a lot.”
- “Most are not dangerous, but if you get skin changes, ulcers, or
bleeding, that’s a warning sign.”
Management / Plan:
- Conservative (first-line):
• Stay active, regular walking, avoid prolonged sitting/standing.
• Elevate legs when resting.
• Weight management if overweight.
• Avoid tight clothing around thighs/waist.
• Good skin care: moisturise, avoid scratching.
• Compression stockings only if unsuitable for intervention (specialist
advice). Exclude arterial insufficiency first)
- Referral (NICE): Vascular surgery if:
• Symptomatic (pain, swelling, heaviness, itching).
• Skin changes due to chronic venous insufficiency.
• Superficial vein thrombosis with varicose veins.
• Bleeding from varicose veins (emergency if active).
- Ulcer not healing in 2 weeks or venous ulcers
- • Don’t refer for just cosmetic reasons or in case of pregnant
ladies
- Specialist options: Endothermal ablation (laser, radiofrequency), foam
sclerotherapy, surgery if others unsuitable.
- Safety Netting (SCA phrases):
- “If a vein suddenly bleeds, press firmly and call 999.”
- “Return if you develop skin ulcers, sudden swelling, or severe pain.”
- “We’ll refer you as your veins are causing symptoms and concern.”

-- 398 of 437 --

368- Osgood–Schlatter Disease
Definition: A common cause of
anterior knee pain in adolescents,
due to inflammation at the tibial
tuberosity (where the patellar
tendon attaches) from repetitive
stress.
Dx Criteria / When to Suspect:
- Pain and swelling over the tibial
tubercle.
- Worse with running, jumping, or
kneeling.
- Typically in active adolescents (10–
15 yrs), more in boys.
- May be unilateral or bilateral.
- Self-limiting, resolves with skeletal
maturity.
Risk Factors:
- Rapid growth spurt.
- Repetitive sports (football,
basketball, athletics).
- Male sex (though also seen in girls).
- Tight quadriceps/hamstrings.
Red Flags (not typical OSD — think
alternative Dx):
- Rest/night pain.
- Systemic symptoms (fever, weight
loss).
- Severe swelling, redness, inability to
bear weight.
- Locking or giving way of the knee.
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, triggers (sports, kneeling).
- Impact on activities/school sports.
- Any swelling, redness, locking, systemic symptoms.
- Past trauma/injuries.
- Growth/development stage (recent growth spurt).
Lay Explanation:
- “This is a common and harmless cause of knee pain in growing
teenagers.”
- “It happens because the bone just below the knee is still
developing, and the tendon that attaches there gets irritated with
running, jumping or kneeling.”
- “It’s more common during growth spurts and in young people who
do a lot of sport.”
- “It can be sore, especially after activity, but it usually settles once
growth finishes.”
Management / Plan:
- Conservative:
• Activity modification (reduce high-impact sports if painful, but don’t
need to stop all activity).
• Ice after exercise for 15 -20 minutes 3 times a day
• Stretching/strengthening (hamstrings, quadriceps).
• Simple analgesia (paracetamol, NSAIDs short-term).
• Knee pads for kneeling.
- Education: self-limiting condition, resolves with growth (may take
months).
Usually settle in few weeks to months
- Referral: Orthopaedics if:
• Severe/persistent pain despite conservative measures.
• Diagnostic uncertainty.
• Red flag features (night pain, systemic signs, locking).
- Safety Netting (SCA phrasing):
- “You can keep being active, just ease off activities that really aggravate
the knee.”
- “Come back if the pain becomes severe, constant, or if the knee locks,
swells a lot, or you get fever.”
- “This usually improves by itself once the bones finish growing.”

-- 399 of 437 --

369- Renal Colic
Definition: Severe, colicky flank pain
due to urinary tract obstruction by a
stone.
Dx Criteria / When to Suspect:
- Sudden onset, severe loin-to-groin
pain, often colicky.
- Restlessness (can’t get
comfortable).
- +/- hematuria.
- Nausea, vomiting common.
- May radiate to scrotum, labia, or
inner thigh.
Risk Factors:
- Male sex.
- Dehydration, hot climate.
- High protein/salt diet.
- Family history of stones.
- Obesity, metabolic syndrome.
- Conditions: hyperparathyroidism,
gout, recurrent UTIs.
Red Flags (urgent referral / admit):
- Fever, rigors (suggests infected
obstructed kidney = urological
emergency).
- Anuria / bilateral obstruction / single
kidney.
- Uncontrolled pain or vomiting.
- Acute kidney injury.
Focused History:
- Pain: onset, site, radiation (loin to groin), severity.
- Previous episodes or stones.
- Associated: nausea, vomiting, hematuria, LUTS.
- Red flag symptoms: fever, rigors, anuria, severe vomiting.
- Risk factors: hydration, diet, family history.
Lay Explanation:
- “Renal colic is caused by a stone blocking the flow of urine in the
kidney or ureter.”
- “It causes very severe pain that comes in waves, often from the
back or side spreading to the groin.”
- “The pain happens because the kidney is trying to push urine past
the blockage.”
- “Most stones are small and will pass by themselves, but
sometimes treatment is needed.”
- “We get concerned if there’s an infection or if both kidneys are
blocked — that can be serious and needs urgent hospital care.”
- “Drink fluids and take pain relief as prescribed.”
- “Most small stones pass by themselves, but we’ll monitor to make
sure your kidneys stay safe.”
Management / Plan:
- Referral (Urology): Emergency admissions if
• Infection + obstruction (fever, rigors). • Anuria, bilateral obstruction,
single kidney. • Severe uncontrolled pain/vomiting. • Rising
creatinine/suspected AKI (CKD, solitary kidney, transplant kidney,
bilateral obstruction) - The person is dehydrated and cannot tolerate oral
fluids due to nausea and/or vomiting.
For all other people with suspected renal or ureteric colic:
Arrange urgent (within 24 hours of presentation) imaging to confirm the
diagnosis and assess the likelihood of spontaneous stone passage. (non-
contrast CT KUB gold standard for adults, USS in pregnancy/children).
- Immediate in primary care:
• Strong analgesia (NSAIDs first-line, opioids if needed).
• Anti-emetics if vomiting.
- Do not offer antispasmodics to people with suspected renal or ureteric
colic.
- Investigations: Urine dip to exclude infection
Discuss management options in 2ry care:
- Conservative: Many stones <5 mm pass spontaneously — advise fluids,
analgesia, strain urine to catch stone.
Management options include watchful waiting, medical expulsive therapy,
and surgical treatment.
- Elective management (specialist): lithotripsy, ureteroscopy, or surgery
if persistent/large stones.
For prevention of recurrence:
To increase fluid intake to 3 L a day of water + To add fresh lemon juice to
drinking water and avoid carbonated drinks + To reduce salt intake +
advise adults to maintain a normal calcium intake of 700–1200 mg + To
eat a balanced diet, including plenty of fruits and vegetables, and
maintain a healthy weight.
Based on serum calcium and stone analysis – may add thiazides in adults
and potassium citrate in children in case of calcium stones
- Safety Netting (SCA phrasing):
- “Go to hospital urgently if you get fever, rigors, can’t pass urine, or the
pain is unbearable.”

-- 400 of 437 --

370- Acute scrotal/ testicular Pain
Definition: Pain in one or both
testicles, caused by conditions
ranging from benign self-limiting to
surgical emergencies.
Dx Criteria / When to Suspect:
- Torsion (emergency): sudden
severe unilateral pain,
nausea/vomiting, high-riding tender
testis, absent cremasteric reflex.
- Epididymo-orchitis: gradual onset,
dysuria, fever, tender/swollen
epididymis.
- Other causes: trauma, hernia,
hydrocele, tumour.
Risk Factors:
- Adolescents & young men (torsion
risk ↑ at puberty).
- Recent sexual activity / STI exposure
(epididymo-orchitis).
- Heavy lifting/straining (hernia).
- Previous torsion or undescended
testis.
Red Flags (urgent referral):
- Suspected torsion (sudden severe
pain, vomiting).
- Severe swelling, redness, systemic
unwell.
- Inability to feel testis (possible
tumour/hernia).
- Trauma with ongoing pain/swelling.
Focused History:
- Onset and duration of pain (sudden vs gradual).
- Associated: nausea/vomiting, fever, urinary symptoms, trauma.
- Past history: previous torsion, undescended testes, STIs.
- Sexual history (partner, contraception, recent infections).
- Red flag check: sudden onset, systemic symptoms, swelling.
Lay Explanation:
- “Testicular pain can have different causes — some harmless,
some emergencies.”
- “One urgent cause is torsion, where the testicle twists, cutting off
its blood supply. This causes sudden, very severe pain and needs
emergency surgery to save the testicle.”
- “Another cause is infection (epididymo-orchitis), which comes on
more gradually and may be linked to a urine or sexually transmitted
infection.”
- “Sometimes it can be due to a hernia, trauma, or swelling, but we
must always rule out torsion quickly.”
Management / Plan:
- Suspected torsion = emergency surgical referral (A&E immediately)
— do not delay for imaging.
- If epididymo-orchitis likely:
- If symptoms are severe, the person is systemically unwell, or there is
a suspected serious complication, arrange emergency hospital
admission.
- Otherwise, urine dip/culture, STI swabs, antibiotics (per local
guidelines), analgesia, scrotal support.
- If an STI is the most likely cause, advise urgent referral to a local
specialist sexual health clinic for STI testing, treatment, and possible
contact tracing.
- Consider treating empirically with oral ofloxacin 200 mg twice daily
for 14 days
- Advise on rest, scrotal support (such as with supportive underwear),
and the use of analgesia such as paracetamol and/or nonsteroidal
anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) until symptoms improve.
- If symptoms worsen, or are not improving after 3 days of antibiotic
treatment, advise the person to seek urgent medical review
- If suspected STI, advice sexual abstinence and ensure that partner
notification is complete
- Review after 3 days and after 2 weeks. Reassure that scrotal swelling
completely resolves in more than 80% of people by 3 months after
antibiotic treatment. If persistent, arrange USS
- Other causes:
• Trauma → scrotal USS if severe.
• Hernia → urgent surgical referral if strangulated.
• Hydrocele/tumour → routine urology referral.
- Safety Netting (SCA phrasing):
- “If the pain comes on suddenly and very severely, treat it as an
emergency and go straight to hospital.”
- “Come back urgently if you develop fever, swelling, difficulty passing
urine, or worsening pain.”
- “For mild causes, we’ll support with pain relief and treatment, but we’ll
always act fast if we’re worried about torsion.”

-- 401 of 437 --

371- Lactating mastitis
Definition: Inflammation of the
breast tissue, usually due to milk
stasis and sometimes secondary
bacterial infection (commonly
Staphylococcus aureus).
Diagnostic Features:
- Breast pain, redness, swelling,
warmth.
- Fever, flu-like symptoms.
- Usually unilateral, localised.
Risk Factors:
- Infrequent or missed feeds.
- Poor latch or positioning.
- Cracked nipples.
- Tight clothing or pressure on breast.
Red Flags:
- Breast abscess (fluctuant lump,
severe pain, not settling).
- Severe systemic infection (rigors,
sepsis signs).
- Inflammatory breast cancer
(persistent erythema not resolving,
peau d’orange).
Focused History:
- Onset and duration of pain/swelling.
- Fever, systemic unwellness.
- Feeding history (frequency, latch, side preference).
- Nipple trauma, cracks, discharge.
- Previous episodes of mastitis.
- Past medical history (immunosuppression, diabetes).
Lay Explanation:
- “This is inflammation of your breast while breastfeeding. It
usually happens when some milk gets trapped in the breast and
causes irritation.”
- “Sometimes bacteria can get in through tiny cracks in the nipple,
leading to an infection.”
- “It causes pain, swelling, and sometimes fever, but the good news
is that with frequent feeding, rest, and antibiotics if needed, it
usually settles quickly.”
- “It’s safe – and actually helpful – to continue breastfeeding through
this.”
Explain that good breastfeeding technique is necessary to
prevent mastitis.
• Advise the woman to:
o Make sure the infant is attached to the breast
correctly.
o Feed on demand, both in terms of frequency and
duration.
o Avoid missed feeds, especially when the infant
starts to sleep through the night.
o Finish the first breast before offering the other.
o Breastfeed exclusively for 4–6 months, if possible.
o Avoid the use of a dummy, which may result in
poor attachment to the breast.
Arrange hospital admission if:
There are signs of sepsis (such as tachycardia, fever, and chills).
The infection progresses rapidly.
The woman is haemodynamically unstable or immunocompromised
- Continue breastfeeding / expressing milk: encourages drainage. Start
feeds on affected side first.
- Support with latch and positioning (refer to breastfeeding support
services, lactation consultant, HV). Explain that good breastfeeding
technique is necessary to prevent mastitis.
- Self-care: Rest, fluids, warm compress before feeds, cold compress
after feeds, simple analgesia (paracetamol/ibuprofen, safe in
breastfeeding).
- Prescribe an oral antibiotic if the woman has a nipple fissure that is
infected, symptoms have not improved (or are worsening) after 12–24
hours despite effective milk removal, and/or breast milk culture is
positive. Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS 10–14 days (or
erythromycin/clarithromycin if penicillin allergic). Safe in breastfeeding.
- If abscess suspected: Arrange urgent breast clinic / surgical referral for
ultrasound and drainage.
If recurrence: Identify and manage any predisposing factors, such as:
Nipple damage or Staphylococcus aureus carriage — send nasal swabs
from both the woman and the infant to identify nasal carriage of S. aureus.
If the woman does not wish to continue breastfeeding, advise:
Not stop breastfeeding abruptly.
Support the breasts with a comfortable bra.
Express enough milk to keep the breasts comfortable.
Take paracetamol or ibuprofen if pain occurs.
- Safety Netting (SCA phrasing):
- “Please come back urgently if the swelling becomes a lump that feels
like it has pus, or if you feel very unwell with high fever or chills.”
- “If it doesn’t start improving in 24–48 hours, despite feeding and
supportive care, we may need to start antibiotics.”
- “If antibiotics don’t help or a lump develops, we’ll arrange a specialist
review to rule out an abscess.”

-- 402 of 437 --

372- Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP)
Definition: Use of antiretroviral
medication (tenofovir + emtricitabine)
by HIV-negative individuals to
prevent HIV infection.
When to Offer (Eligibility):
- HIV-negative person at ongoing high
risk of HIV:
• Men who have sex with men (MSM)
with condomless anal sex.
• Heterosexuals with HIV-positive
partner not on effective treatment.
• People with multiple partners and
inconsistent condom use.
• People who inject drugs and share
needles.
- Trans or non-binary person and are
regularly having condomless sex
- Exchange sex for money, drugs,
shelter or another reason
Red Flags:
- Already HIV-positive (PrEP is not
treatment).
- Symptoms suggestive of acute HIV
(fever, rash, lymphadenopathy after
high-risk exposure).
- Significant renal impairment.
Focused History:
- Sexual history (partners, condom use, anal/vaginal/oral sex).
- Past STI history.
- Partner’s HIV status and treatment.
- Needle use/sharing.
- Hepatitis B status/vaccination.
- Last HIV test and any recent symptoms.
- Drug use (esp. chemsex).
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “PrEP is a tablet you take regularly to stop HIV from taking hold
in your body if you’re exposed.”
- “It works very well if taken correctly, but it doesn’t protect against
other STIs or pregnancy, so condoms are still important.”
- “We need to do some tests before starting – including HIV, kidney
function, and hepatitis – to make sure it’s safe for you.”
- “PrEP is different from PEP, which is emergency treatment after a
possible exposure.”
- PrEP should not be prescribed to anyone below the age of 12 years.
- PrEP does not affect any type of contraception
Management / Plan:
- Signpost to a sexual health clinic people who may be eligible for PrEP
to discuss the following
- Pre-treatment checks: 4th gen HIV test (must be negative), renal
function (U&Es, eGFR >60), Hep B/C status, STI screen.
- PrEP comes as tablets that contain 2 medicines called tenofovir
disoproxil and emtricitabine. Both medicines are often also used to treat
HIV.
- Follow-up: HIV test every 3 months, renal function every 6–12 months,
STI screening.
- Health promotion:
- Condom use, vaccinations (Hep A/B, HPV).
- PrEP does not protect you against other sexually transmitted infections
(STIs).
Most people can take PrEP every day but some people can take it on
demand (just before and after sex).
- If you take PrEP daily for vaginal/frontal sex, it takes 7 days to give
maximum protection. If you take it only when you need it for anal sex,
you'll be protected about 2 hours after your first double dose.
- HIV women – better to avoid combined contraception pills or patches
- For emergency contraception: Cu-IUD is the best or double dose of
progesterone only EC
- In pregnancy, risk of transmission can decreased to < 1% by taking the
ART and avoidance of breastfeeding
- Referral: To GUM/sexual health clinic if complex case or unclear
eligibility.
- Safety Netting (SCA phrasing):
- “If you develop flu-like illness, rash, or swollen glands while on PrEP,
stop taking it and come back urgently – it could mean new HIV infection.”
- “Keep up with regular HIV and kidney checks to make sure the
medication stays safe and effective.”
- “If you miss doses, it won’t work as well, so let’s talk through a plan that
fits your routine.”

-- 403 of 437 --

373- Post exposure prophylaxis (PEP)
Definition: A short course of
antiretroviral medicines to reduce
the risk of HIV infection after a high-
risk exposure. Must be started as
