soon as possible, ideally within 24
hrs, up to 72 hrs.
Indications:
- Sexual exposure: Unprotected
receptive anal or vaginal sex with
partner known/suspected HIV-
positive and not on effective ART.
- Community/occupational
exposure: Needlestick injuries,
mucosal splash, bites from high-risk
source.
Risk Factors:
- High-prevalence area or partner.
- MSM with condomless anal sex.
- Blood exposure in
healthcare/community setting.
- Shared needles/IDU.
Red Flags:
- Presentation after >72 hrs (PEP not
effective).
- Already HIV-positive (needs
treatment, not PEP).
- Severe renal impairment (adjust
regimen).
Focused History:
- Exposure details: What happened? When? (timing crucial).
- Type of exposure: sexual (anal/vaginal/oral), occupational, sharps
injury, bite.
- Source risk: Known HIV status of partner/source, on treatment or
not, viral load.
- Past HIV test status of patient.
- Other risks: Hepatitis B/C vaccination/exposure.
- Drug history, allergies, renal issues.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “PEP is a course of HIV tablets taken for 28 days after a high-risk
contact to reduce the chance of infection.”
- “It’s most effective if started quickly – within 24 hours – and won’t
work if started after 3 days.”
- “It’s not 100%, so we’ll also need to do blood tests now and
follow-up HIV tests.”
- “It doesn’t protect against other STIs or pregnancy – we may need
to test and advise on those too.”
Management / Plan: Refer to sexual health or A&E occupational health
depending on setting.
• Post exposure prophylaxis if occupational exposure is a medical
emergency
o Clean the site thoroughly with soap and water without
squeezing
o Puncture wounds should be encouraged to bleed freely and not
to be sucked + Report to Health and safety executive
o Go to A&E for PEP
• Post exposure prophylaxis if sexual exposure is a medical emergency
o PEPSE can be initiated if the person presents within 72 hours of
exposure and should be given as early as possible (ideally within
24 hours of exposure).
o Follow up should be arranged by the prescribing team — HIV
testing is recommended 8–12 weeks after exposure.
- Baseline tests: HIV, Hep B/C, renal function, pregnancy test if relevant.
- Start PEP: 28 days of Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate + Emtricitabine +
Dolutegravir (first-line in UK).
- Timing: Start ASAP, ideally <24h, max 72h.
- Additional care: Emergency contraception if indicated; Hepatitis B
vaccination/Ig if not immune; full STI screen.
- Follow-up: HIV test at baseline, 4 weeks, 12 weeks, 24 weeks. Monitor
renal/liver function.
- Safety Netting (SCA phrasing):
- “If you feel unwell with flu-like symptoms, rash, swollen glands in the
coming weeks, come back urgently as this could be early HIV.”
- “It’s really important to complete the full 28-day course, even if you feel
well.”
- “We’ll arrange follow-up blood tests at specific times to confirm you
remain HIV negative.”

-- 404 of 437 --

374- Male Infertility
Definition: Inability to conceive after
12 months of regular, unprotected
intercourse, due to male factors
(sperm quality, quantity, or delivery).
Dx Criteria / When to Suspect:
- No conception after 12 months (6
months if female partner >35).
- Abnormal semen analysis
(oligospermia, azoospermia,
teratospermia, asthenospermia).
Risk Factors:
- History of cryptorchidism, mumps
orchitis, testicular trauma, torsion.
- Varicocele.
- Chronic illnesses (diabetes,
liver/kidney disease).
- Medications: testosterone, chemo,
anabolic steroids.
- Lifestyle: smoking, alcohol,
recreational drugs, obesity, heat
exposure.
- Genetic conditions (Klinefelter, Y-
chromosome microdeletion).
Red Flags:
- Testicular atrophy, small testes.
- History of undescended testes or
orchitis.
- Gynecomastia or other signs of
endocrine disorder.
- Erectile dysfunction, ejaculatory
disorders.
Focused History:
- Duration of trying to conceive.
- Sexual history (libido, erectile function, ejaculation, frequency).
- Past medical/surgical history: testicular disorders, mumps,
trauma, torsion, hernia surgery.
- Medications, recreational drug use, occupational exposures.
- Family history of infertility/genetic disorders.
- Lifestyle: smoking, alcohol, obesity, heat exposure.
Lay Explanation:
- “Infertility in men usually means the sperm is not able to fertilise
the egg properly.”
- “This can be due to problems with sperm number, shape, or
movement, or blockages preventing sperm from being delivered.”
- “It’s not usually your fault, and often we can find a cause and offer
treatments.”
- “Some causes are temporary or lifestyle-related, while others may
need specialist treatment.”
Management / Plan:
- Initial tests (GP / fertility clinic):
• Semen analysis (2 separate samples). Collected by masturbation/
clean nontoxic container/ stored in temp from 20-37 degrees (keep close
under the clothes during transport). Collected after a minimum of 2 days
and a maximum of 7 days of sexual abstinence. Deliver within 20 minutes
after collection. If the result of the first semen sample is abnormal, order a
repeat test after 3 months or within 2-4 weeks if azoospermia or severe
anxiety
• Hormonal profile: FSH, LH, testosterone, prolactin if abnormal semen or
small testes.
• Scrotal ultrasound if varicocele suspected.
- Lifestyle advice:
• Stop smoking, limit alcohol, avoid recreational drugs.
• Maintain healthy weight and diet.
• Avoid heat exposure (hot baths, laptops on lap).
- Specialist referral:
• Abnormal semen analysis, hormonal abnormalities, suspected
obstruction, genetic concerns.
• For assisted reproductive techniques (IVF/ICSI) if required.
• Consider earlier referral if the following factors are present:
o Previous genital pathology or urogenital surgery / STI/ varicocele
o Significant systemic illness/ Abnormal genital examination.
o Two abnormal semen examination results.
o Known reason for infertility (for example, cancer).
- Safety Netting / SCA phrases:
- “If you notice testicular swelling, pain, lumps, erectile dysfunction, or
changes in libido, come back promptly.”
- “Some causes of infertility can be treated or assisted with specialist
techniques, so we’ll work with you and your partner.”
- “Early referral to a fertility clinic helps identify causes and improves
outcomes.”

-- 405 of 437 --

375- IVF and ICSI
Feature IVF (In Vitro Fertilisation) ICSI (Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection)
Definition Fertility treatment where eggs are collected, fertilised with sperm in the lab, and resulting embryos
transferred to the uterus.
A specialised IVF technique where a single sperm is
injected directly into an egg before embryo transfer.
Eligibility / Indications Medical / clinical indications:
- Female infertility: tubal blockage, ovulatory problems, endometriosis.
- Male factor mild/moderate.
- Unexplained infertility >12 months.
- Failed previous fertility treatments (IUI).
NHS criteria (England, examples):
- Woman aged under 40 at start of treatment.
- BMI 19–30.
- No children together (couple) or in household.
- Typically 3 IVF cycles funded if eligible.
- Couple has been trying >2 years, or 12 months with risk factors.
Indications:
- Severe male factor infertility (low count, motility,
morphology, azoospermia with sperm retrieval).
- Previous failed IVF cycles.
- Fertilisation failure with standard IVF.
- Genetic considerations (preimplantation testing).
NHS eligibility:
- Same female criteria as IVF.
- Male factor infertility confirmed.
- Usually offered when standard IVF unlikely to succeed.
Red Flags /
Contraindications
- Untreated uterine abnormalities (fibroids, adhesions)./ - Active infection (STI, pelvic infection)./ - Poor
ovarian reserve. / - Severe medical comorbidity making pregnancy high risk.
Same as IVF, plus:
- Male partner must have at least some viable sperm
(ejaculated or retrieved).
Focused History (SCA
style)
- Duration of infertility, previous investigations./ - Menstrual history, ovulation, gynaecological history./
- Male partner semen analysis./ - Lifestyle: smoking, alcohol, BMI./ - Expectations and understanding.
Same as IVF, plus:
- Previous IVF outcomes.
- Detailed male fertility assessment.
- Surgical history (vasectomy, testicular biopsy).
Lay Explanation - “IVF is bringing eggs and sperm together in a lab to create embryos, which are then placed into the
uterus to try to achieve pregnancy.”
- “It is usually considered when natural conception is difficult due to tubal, ovulatory, or unexplained
issues.”
- “ICSI is a more advanced version of IVF where a single
sperm is injected into each egg.”
- “It is mainly used when sperm quality is low or previous
IVF attempts failed.”
- “Success rates can be higher in these situations.”
Process (Step-by-Step) 1. Ovarian stimulation: Hormones to produce multiple eggs.
2. Monitoring: Ultrasound & blood tests to track follicle growth.
3. Egg retrieval: Minor procedure under sedation.
4. Fertilisation: Eggs mixed with sperm in lab.
5. Embryo culture: 3–5 days until suitable stage.
6. Embryo transfer: Into uterus via catheter.
7. Support & follow-up: Progesterone support, pregnancy test ~2 weeks later.
1. Ovarian stimulation & monitoring: Same as IVF.
2. Egg retrieval: Same as IVF.
3. Sperm preparation: Selected single sperm per egg.
4. Injection: Sperm injected directly into egg in lab.
5. Embryo culture & assessment: 3–5 days.
6. Embryo transfer: Into uterus.
7. Support & follow-up: Progesterone, pregnancy test ~2
weeks later.
Management / Plan
(SCA style)
Pre-treatment: Hormonal & fertility tests, lifestyle optimisation, counselling.
Treatment: Steps above.
Follow-up: Monitor for pregnancy, OHSS, multiple pregnancy, complications.
Same as IVF, plus:
- Ensure male factor cause addressed and sperm collection
method planned.
- Genetic testing if indicated.
Safety Netting / SCA
Phrases
- “Report severe abdominal pain, bloating, vomiting, or shortness of breath (OHSS warning).”
- “Seek help if vaginal bleeding or unusual symptoms occur post-transfer.”
- “Success is not guaranteed; multiple cycles may be needed.”
Same as IVF, plus:
- “If sperm retrieval or injection causes pain, swelling, or
infection, seek urgent advice.”

-- 406 of 437 --

376- Faecal incontinence
Definition: Accidental leakage of
stool due to muscle weakness,
nerve dysfunction, or bowel
disturbance.
When to Suspect / Consider:
- Leakage of stool or mucus affecting
daily life.
- Symptoms related to urgency,
constipation, diarrhoea, or obstetric
history.
Risk Factors:
- Constipation or diarrhoea.
- Pregnancy, obstetric injury, rectal
prolapse.
- Diabetes, obesity, learning
disability.
- Medications: nitrates, CCBs, beta-
blockers, topical GTN, laxatives,
orlistat, opioids, benzodiazepines,
SSRIs, sildenafil.
- Excess alcohol or spicy food.
Red Flags:
- Neurological: cauda equina
syndrome, stroke, multiple sclerosis.
- Malignancy: bowel or anal cancer.
- Inflammatory: IBD.
- Advanced haemorrhoids or trauma.
Focused History:
- Duration, frequency, and severity of incontinence.
- Stool consistency and relation to bowel habits.
- Obstetric history (perineal trauma, vaginal delivery).
- Neurological symptoms: weakness, numbness, saddle
anaesthesia.
- Medication and alcohol history.
- Impact on daily life, emotional and social wellbeing.
Lay Explanation:
- “Faecal incontinence means you may pass stool or mucus
without meaning to.”
- “It can happen due to weak muscles, nerve problems,
constipation, diarrhoea, medications, or past injuries.”
- “Most causes can be managed or treated to improve control and
quality of life.”
Management / Plan:
- Assessment / Investigations:
• Face-to-face review, perineal/rectal exam.
• FIT test and bloods to exclude malignancy or IBD.
- Address modifiable risk factors:
• Treat constipation or diarrhoea.
• Adjust medications where possible.
• Lifestyle advice: reduce alcohol, manage diet.
- Supportive measures:
• Continence products, pelvic floor exercises, psychological support.
- Referral:
• Urgent if neurological signs, red flags, or suspected malignancy.
• Routine referral to gastroenterology/colorectal surgery if persistent or
severe symptoms.
- Safety Netting (SCA phrasing):
• “Return urgently if you notice sudden worsening, new neurological
symptoms, bleeding, or weight loss.”
• “We can support you with continence products and lifestyle advice
while investigations are ongoing.”

-- 407 of 437 --

377- Gilbert Syndrome
Definition: A benign hereditary
(usually autosomal recessive)
condition causing mild, intermittent
unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia
due to reduced activity of the enzyme
UDP-glucuronosyltransferase.
Diagnosis / Criteria:
- Typically mild indirect
hyperbilirubinemia on blood tests.
- Normal liver enzymes, FBC, and liver
function otherwise.
- Exclusion of hemolysis or liver
disease.
- Often diagnosed in adolescence or
early adulthood.
- Recurrent episodes of self-resolving
jaundice (especially after heavy
physical exertion, fasting, sleep
deprivation, alcohol, dehydration,
surgery, or an intercurrent illness).
Risk Factors / Triggers:
- Fasting, dehydration, illness, stress,
menstruation, strenuous exercise.
- Male sex slightly more common.
Red Flags:
- Jaundice with abnormal LFTs (ALT,
AST, ALP).
- Symptoms of liver disease:
abdominal pain, dark urine, pale
stools, fatigue.
- Family history of serious liver
disease.
Focused History:
- Onset and pattern of jaundice or yellowing of eyes/skin.
- Triggering factors (fasting, stress, illness).
- Associated symptoms: fatigue, abdominal pain, dark urine, pale
stools.
- Past medical and family history of liver disease.
- Medication and alcohol history.
Lay Explanation:
- “Gilbert syndrome is a harmless inherited condition where your
liver processes bilirubin a little more slowly than usual.”
- “This can cause mild yellowing of your skin or eyes, often during
stress, fasting, illness, or heavy exercise.”
- “It is not dangerous, does not damage your liver, and does not
shorten life expectancy.”
Management / Plan:
- Reassurance: Condition is benign; no treatment required.
- Lifestyle advice: Avoid prolonged fasting, maintain hydration, manage
stress, advise the person to drink alcohol within recommended limits.
- Investigations: Usually diagnosed on routine blood tests; exclude
hemolysis or liver disease if atypical features present.
- Referral: Only if LFTs abnormal, persistent jaundice, or additional
symptoms suggestive of liver pathology.
- Safety Netting (SCA phrasing):
• “Return if you notice persistent jaundice, dark urine, pale stools,
abdominal pain, or fatigue.”
• “Episodes are usually mild and temporary, triggered by stress or
fasting.”
People with Gilbert's syndrome should be advised that:
• No treatment or regular monitoring is required.
• It cannot progress or cause chronic liver disease.
• Any episode of jaundice should be self-limiting and should resolve
within a few days, is not a sign of serious illness, and is not infectious.
They should seek medical advice if any episode of jaundice is more
severe or persistent than usual, as this may indicate an alternative
diagnosis, which may co-exist with Gilbert's syndrome.
• Statin — people with Gilbert's syndrome may have an increased risk
of statin intolerance and should be monitored more closely while
taking them.

-- 408 of 437 --

378- Jaundice in adults
Type / Definition /
Key Features
Clinical Clues / Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing) Management / SCA Tips
Prehepatic Jaundice Caused by increased bilirubin production before it reaches the liver.-
Common causes: haemolytic anaemia, Gilbert’s syndrome, certain drugs.-
Usually unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with normal LFTs.
- Investigations: FBC, reticulocyte count, blood film, haemolysis screen, bilirubin
fractionation.- Advice: Reassure if Gilbert’s syndrome (benign, triggered by fasting,
exercise, stress).- Referral: Only if atypical features or haemolytic disease suspected.
Intrahepatic
(Hepatocellular)
Jaundice
Due to liver cell injury or dysfunction.- Causes: viral hepatitis, alcohol,
NAFLD, autoimmune hepatitis, drugs, haemochromatosis, malignancy.- Lab
pattern: Raised ALT/AST (AST>ALT in alcohol, ALT>AST in viral).
- Investigations: LFTs, viral hepatitis screen, autoimmune markers, imaging (US/CT)
if needed.- Advice: Avoid alcohol, review hepatotoxic drugs, maintain hydration and
nutrition.- Referral: Urgent if red flags (confusion, coagulopathy, systemic
symptoms).
Post-hepatic
(Cholestatic) Jaundice
Due to obstruction of bile flow.- Causes: gallstones, pancreatic or biliary
malignancy, strictures.- Lab pattern: Raised ALP > ALT/AST, conjugated
bilirubin.
- Investigations: LFTs, ultrasound/CT/MRCP, consider ERCP if obstruction
suspected.- Referral: Urgent secondary care or gastroenterology for imaging and
management.- Advice: Monitor for cholangitis (fever + RUQ pain + jaundice).
Gilbert Syndrome
(Benign, Prehepatic)
Autosomal recessive enzyme defect → reduced bilirubin conjugation.- Mild
intermittent jaundice, unconjugated bilirubin <85 μmol/L.- Triggers: fasting,
exercise, stress, dehydration, alcohol, illness, lack of sleep.- Liver enzymes
normal.
- Reassure: No treatment needed.- Lifestyle advice: Avoid prolonged fasting, stay
hydrated, manage stress.- Safety net: Return if jaundice persists, bilirubin rises >100
μmol/L, or other liver symptoms develop.
Red Flags / Urgent
Features
- Weight loss, haematemesis, melena, abdominal pain, abdominal swelling.-
Confusion, seizures, bruising.- Fever + RUQ pain + jaundice (cholangitis).-
Acute epigastric pain (pancreatitis).- HELLP in pregnancy.- Bilirubin >100
μmol/L.
- Immediate action: Admit or urgent referral to secondary care.- Investigations: Full
bloods, imaging as appropriate.- Referral: Age >40 with new jaundice → 2-week
urgent referral to exclude pancreatic or biliary malignancy.

-- 409 of 437 --

379- Varicocele
Definition: Enlargement of the veins
(pampiniform plexus) within the
scrotum, usually on the left side.
Diagnosis / Key Features:
- Painless, soft scrotal swelling that
may feel like a “bag of worms.”
- Often left-sided (90%), occasionally
bilateral (10%).
- May become more prominent when
standing or straining and reduce
when lying down.
- Graded: 1 (palpable with Valsalva),
2 (visible with Valsalva), 3 (visible at
rest).
Risk Factors:
- Tall, lean body habitus, low BMI.
- Family history of varicocele or
fertility issues.
Red Flags:
- Sudden painful swelling.
- Varicocele not reducing when lying
down.
- Blood in urine, abdominal pain,
unexplained weight loss (consider
renal/retroperitoneal pathology).
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, side, and progression of swelling.
- Associated pain, tenderness, or heaviness.
- History of infertility or difficulty conceiving.
- Family history of varicocele or male infertility.
- Systemic symptoms: haematuria, weight loss, abdominal pain.
Lay Explanation:
- “A varicocele is enlarged veins near the testicle, usually on the
left side.”
- “It is often painless and common, especially in taller, thinner
men.”
- “Most men do not have problems, but some may have fertility
issues or discomfort.”
Although varicoceles may be associated with fertility problems,
nearly two-thirds of men who have a varicocele have no difficulty in
fathering children.
Management / Plan:
- Investigations:
• Scrotal US with Doppler to confirm diagnosis.
• Semen analysis if fertility concerns.
• Consider FSH, testosterone and semen analysis
• For adolescents with:
o Subclinical or grade I varicocele — no treatment is necessary.
▪ Provide advice and reassurance.
o Grade II or III varicocele and symmetrical testes — observe
with annual examinations. ▪ The primary indication for surgery is
testicular growth arrest.
o Grade II or III and asymmetrical testes — refer to a urologist for
possible surgery.
• For men with:
o Sub-clinical or grade I varicocele — no treatment is necessary.
▪ Offer semen analysis if fertility is a concern.
o Grade II or III asymptomatic varicocele and normal semen
parameters — consider observing with semen analysis every 1–2
years if clinically appropriate.
o Grade II or III symptomatic varicocele, or with abnormal semen
parameters — refer to a urologist for possible surgery.
- Safety Netting (SCA phrasing):
• “Return if swelling suddenly worsens, becomes painful, does not
reduce when lying down, or you notice blood in urine or abdominal pain.”
• “Surgery is only considered if there are fertility concerns or significant
discomfort.”

-- 410 of 437 --

380- Undescended testicles
Undescended Testicles
(Cryptorchidism)
Definition: Incomplete descent of
one or both testes from the abdomen
through the inguinal canal, resulting
in absence from the scrotum.
Diagnosis / Key Features:
- Testis not palpable in scrotum or
inguinal canal.
- May be palpable during a warm
bath (suggests retractile testis).
- Usually unilateral; bilateral in ~10%.
Risk Factors:
- Family history of undescended
testes.
- Low birth weight / small for
gestational age.
- Maternal smoking.
- Congenital adrenal hyperplasia.
Red Flags:
- Ambiguous genitalia.
- Bilateral undescended testes.
- Testicular torsion.
- History or suspicion of cancer
Focused History:
- Check which testis/ testes are undescended.
- Prenatal, birth, and perinatal history.
- Family history of undescended testes or testicular cancer.
- Any associated genital abnormalities.
- Past examinations and growth milestones.
Lay Explanation:
- “Sometimes a baby’s testicle does not move down into the
scrotum before birth.”
- “This is quite common, especially in premature or small babies,
and usually affects one testicle.”
- “Most will descend spontaneously in the first few months.”
- “Early referral can prevent future problems such as fertility issues
or increased cancer risk.”
• “Most babies’ testes descend naturally within the first few
months.”
• “Even if the testicle remains undescended, surgery can usually
correct it safely.”
• “Fertility is often normal, and the risk of testicular cancer remains
low.”
- If mum asks about ultrasound: “In most cases, an ultrasound is
not needed because a careful physical examination is usually
sufficient to assess the testicle.”
Management / Plan:
- Examination: Face-to-face, warm environment; palpate scrotum and
inguinal canal.
- Observation:
• At term, 50% complete descent by 12 weeks.
• Unilateral: re-examine at 6–8 weeks, then at 4–5 months.
- Referral:
• Urgent (within 24h) if ambiguous genitalia or bilateral undescended
testes at birth.
• Suspected bilateral at 6–8 weeks → urgent referral to paediatric surgery
within 2 weeks.
• Unilateral persistent undescended testis at 4–5 months → referral to
paediatric surgery.
- Investigations: USS not needed in primary care.
- Reassurance:
• 83% of men with unilateral undescended testis have normal sperm
count; paternity 89% vs 94% general population.
• Lifetime risk of testicular cancer ~2–3%.
- Safety Netting:
• Return if testis becomes painful, enlarged, or if new genital
abnormalities appear.
• Educate parents on monitoring the scrotum during early infancy.

-- 411 of 437 --

381- Malaria Prophylaxis in Women
Definition: Use of preventive
medication to reduce the risk of
malaria infection during travel to
endemic areas.
Malaria Overview:
- Caused by Plasmodium parasites
transmitted via the bite of an infected
female Anopheles mosquito.
- Symptoms include fever, chills,
headache, malaise, and fatigue.
- Severe malaria can cause anaemia,
organ failure, cerebral malaria, and
death if untreated.
Indications for Prophylaxis:
- Travel to malaria-endemic regions
(Africa, Asia, South America).
- High-risk groups: pregnant women,
immunocompromised individuals,
children, elderly.
Risk Factors:
- Travel without prophylaxis.
- Non-adherence to protective
measures (mosquito nets,
repellents).
Red Flags:
- Fever during or after travel.
- Severe malaise, jaundice,
confusion, or signs of severe malaria.
- Pregnancy (requires safe drug
choice).
Focused History:
- Planned travel: destination, duration, urban vs rural.
- Medical history: pregnancy, chronic conditions, liver/kidney
disease.
- Drug allergies or previous adverse reactions to prophylaxis.
- Current medications (to avoid drug interactions).
- Knowledge and use of mosquito bite prevention (nets, repellents,
clothing).
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Malaria is an infection caused by parasites transmitted by
mosquito bites in certain countries.”
- “It can cause fever, chills, fatigue, and serious illness if
untreated.”
- “We can reduce your risk by taking medication before, during,
and after travel.”
- “You should also use protective measures like mosquito nets,
repellents, and covering clothing.”
- “Some medicines are preferred in pregnancy or if you have
liver/kidney issues.”
Management / Plan:
- Advise avoidance of travel to malarious areas if the person is at high risk
of severe or fatal malaria (for example pregnant women, children, the
elderly [aged over 70 years] and those with complex comorbidities or
immunosuppression).
- Choice of Prophylaxis (based on destination and patient factors):
Consulting the Public Health England or NaTHNaC website
• Atovaquone-proguanil (safe in non-pregnant adults; avoid in severe renal
impairment).
• Mefloquine (safe in pregnancy after first trimester; contraindicated in
seizure disorders).
• Doxycycline (not for pregnant women or <8 years old).
- Timing: Start 1–2 weeks before travel (depending on drug), continue
during travel, and after leaving endemic area (usually 1–4 weeks).
- Adjunct Measures:
Physical barriers
- Full-length, loose-fitting, light-coloured clothing with long sleeves,
long trousers and socks if out of doors after sunset.
- Bed nets
Chemical barriers
- Advise the person to use a 50% DEET (N,N-Diethyl-meta-toluamide)
based insect repellent first line on exposed areas of skin.
Avoidance measures — advise people to:
- Plan activities at times when there is reduced risk (for example,
during the daytime).
- Dusk and dawn should be avoided.
- Safety Netting (SCA phrases):
• “Seek urgent medical attention if you develop fever, chills, malaise, or
jaundice during or after travel.”
• “Take your prophylaxis exactly as prescribed; missing doses reduces
protection.”
• “Continue protective measures even while on medication.”
- Referral: Travel or sexual health clinic if complex medical history,
pregnancy, or multiple drug interactions.
- Consider the need for standby emergency medication if the person is
visiting a remote area and likely to be more than 24 hours away from
medical care.
- Signpost to The TravelHealthPro website

-- 412 of 437 --

382- Hot flashes – tamoxifen induced
Definition: Vasomotor symptoms
(sudden feeling of heat, sweating,
flushing, sometimes palpitations)
triggered by tamoxifen, a selective
oestrogen receptor modulator (SERM)
used in breast cancer treatment,
prevention of breast cancer and
treatment of anovulatory infertility
Why it happens: Tamoxifen blocks
oestrogen effects in breast tissue but
can also interfere with hypothalamic
thermoregulation, mimicking
menopause.
Red Flags While on Tamoxifen
• Vaginal bleeding (risk of
endometrial
hyperplasia/cancer).
• Calf pain, swelling, chest
pain, breathlessness → risk
of DVT/PE.
• New neurological
symptoms (sudden
weakness, seizures).
• Severe abdominal pain,
jaundice, or abnormal
bleeding → rare but serious
complications.
Clinical Clues:
- On tamoxifen (often for breast cancer, post-surgery or adjuvant
therapy).
- Symptoms: sudden heat, sweating, flushing, night sweats, sleep
disturbance.
- No vaginal bleeding (if present → red flag).
- May be distressing, affecting quality of life.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “The medication you are on works by blocking female hormones to
protect you from breast cancer.”
- “One of the common side effects is hot flushes and sweats,
similar to menopausal symptoms.”
- “It can be uncomfortable, but the treatment remains very important
for your long-term health.”
Management:
- First line: Lifestyle measures: avoid caffeine, alcohol, spicy food, hot
drinks, stop smoking, light clothing, good sleep hygiene, exercise.
- Non-hormonal medications (if severe and persistent):
• SSRIs (e.g., venlafaxine, citalopram – avoid paroxetine/fluoxetine as they
reduce tamoxifen effectiveness).
• Gabapentin or clonidine (less common).
- Avoid HRT (contraindicated in breast cancer).
- Consider CBT or mindfulness for coping.
- Safety netting (SCA phrases):
• “If you notice new vaginal bleeding, abdominal swelling, or severe mood
changes, please come back urgently.”
• “If the symptoms become unbearable despite changes, we can look at
safe medical options together.”
- Tamoxifen may need to be stopped at least 6 weeks before elective
surgery.
- For premenopausal women taking tamoxifen, advise that tamoxifen has
been associated with reduced bone density.
- Signpost to https://breastcancernow.org/
Contraception Advice
• Tamoxifen is teratogenic → pregnancy must be avoided.
• Avoid hormonal contraception (oestrogen-containing, ↑ clot risk).
• Safer options: copper IUD, hormone-free methods, or barrier
contraception.
• Need for effective contraception during treatment with tamoxifen and
for 2 months after stopping treatment.
• If hormonal needed, progestogen-only pill, injection, or implant
can be considered (individualised, liaise with oncology).
• SCA phrasing:
o “It’s very important not to get pregnant while on tamoxifen,
as it can harm a baby.”
o “The safest options are non-hormonal methods like the
copper coil or condoms.”
o “We would avoid the combined pill because it increases the
risk of blood clots with tamoxifen.”

-- 413 of 437 --

383- Gastroenteritis = Missed pills
Definition: Acute diarrhoea and/or
vomiting may reduce absorption of
the combined oral contraceptive pill
(COCP). If this happens, it can be
considered as “missed pills.”
Risk Factors: Recent travel,
contaminated food/water, sick
contacts, poor hydration.
Red Flags: Blood in stool, severe
dehydration, persistent
vomiting/diarrhoea >48 hrs, severe
abdominal pain, pregnancy risk from
missed/ineffective pill use.
Focused History:
- Onset & duration of diarrhoea/vomiting.
- Ability to keep fluids down.
- How many pills have been taken/missed.
- Where in the pill pack illness occurred (week 1, 2, or 3).
- Sexual activity in last 7 days and contraception back-up.
- Travel, food, contacts.
Lay Explanation:
- “If you are sick or have diarrhoea, the pill may not be absorbed and
it’s as if you missed a pill.”
- “If you were unwell for less than 24 hours, you’re covered — just
carry on.”
- “If it lasts more than 24 hours, you’ll need to use condoms until
you’ve taken 7 pills in a row after you’ve recovered.”
- “What we do next depends on where in your cycle this happens.”
Management Plan:
General care: Fluids, rest, avoid school/work for 48 hrs after last episode.
Pill-specific guidance:
- <24 hrs illness → Continue as normal.
- >24 hrs illness (like missing a pill):
• Week 1 of pack: Risk of pregnancy is highest. Use condoms for 7 days. If
unprotected sex occurred in pill free period in the previous 7 days,
emergency contraception may be needed.
• Week 2 of pack: Carry on taking pills, use condoms for 7 days, risk is
lower if the last 7 pills before illness were taken correctly. No indication
for EC if pills in the preceding 7 days have been taken consistently
• Week 3 of pack: Take pills as normal, finish the pack, and start the next
one immediately (no pill-free break).
- If 2+ pills missed at any stage: Risk increases — consider emergency
contraception and continue pills with back-up. Condoms should be used
or sex avoided until seven consecutive active pills have been taken
- The most recent pill missed should be taken as soon as possible.
If mini pills:
- Additional contraceptive precautions (condoms or avoidance of sex)
are advised for 2 days (48 hours) after restarting the POP.
- Emergency contraception is indicated if unprotected sexual
intercourse occurred after the missed pill and within 48 hours of
restarting the POP.
Safety Netting (SCA phrasing):
- “If your illness carries on, or you vomit blood, can’t drink enough, or feel
faint, seek urgent help.”
- “If you’ve had unprotected sex in the last week and missed pills, let us
know straight away — you may need emergency contraception.”
- “I’ll give you a leaflet so you know exactly what to do if this happens
again.”
- “We can also talk about other contraceptive options that aren’t affected
by vomiting or diarrhoea, like the injection, implant, or coil.”

-- 414 of 437 --

384- Dupuytren’s Disease
(Contracture)
Definition: A fibroproliferative
disorder of the palmar fascia causing
progressive thickening and
contractures, usually affecting the
4th and 5th fingers.
Risk Factors: Family history
(autosomal dominant), male >40,
smoking, alcohol, diabetes, epilepsy
(esp. with long-term
anticonvulsants).
Red Flags: Rapid progression, severe
functional impairment, associated
systemic signs (rare), suspicion of
alternative diagnosis if painful or
associated with systemic illness.
Focused History:
- Onset and progression of finger stiffness or curling.
- Hand dominance and occupational impact.
- Pain (usually painless).
- Functional impact: can they place hand flat on table? (tabletop
test).
- FH of similar problem.
- Comorbidities (diabetes, epilepsy, alcohol).
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “This is a condition where the tissue under the skin of the palm
thickens and gradually pulls the fingers inwards.”
- “It’s not cancer and usually isn’t painful, but it can make it harder to
straighten your fingers.”
- “It tends to progress slowly and sometimes runs in families.”
Management Plan:
Conservative:
- Reassure: benign, usually painless, often slow-growing.
- Hand exercises & stretches: gently open/close the hand, stretching
fingers out on a flat surface to maintain mobility.
- Hand protection: avoid trauma/pressure to palms (e.g. padded gloves if
cycling, DIY, or manual work).
- General health: maintain healthy weight, avoid smoking & excess
alcohol (both increase progression risk).
- Monitor: keep an eye on function—can you still put your hand flat on a
table?
- Do bloods for RFs: HbA1C/lipid profile
Referral / Intervention:
- Refer to hand surgery/orthopaedics if:
• Inability to place palm flat (positive tabletop test).
• Progressive contracture causing functional limitation.
- Options include collagenase injections, needle fasciotomy, or surgical
fasciectomy.
Safety Netting (SCA phrases):
- “If you notice your fingers bending more or affecting your daily activities
like gripping or writing, please let us know early.”
- “Sometimes, surgery is needed if the hand function becomes limited.”
- “If you notice pain, swelling, or sudden worsening, please come back to
rule out other causes.”

-- 415 of 437 --

385- Pruritus Ani
Definition: Persistent or recurrent
itching around the anal area, often
worse at night. Usually benign but
can significantly impact quality of life.
Risk Factors: Poor hygiene,
excessive cleaning/wiping, moisture
(sweating, incontinence), irritants
(soaps, detergents), dietary triggers
(spicy food, caffeine, alcohol),
haemorrhoids, dermatological
conditions (eczema, psoriasis, fungal
infection), diabetes, obesity.
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Haemorrhoids
- Anal fissures
- Fungal or bacterial infections
(candida, pinworm, STI)
- Eczema, psoriasis, lichen simplex
chronicus, lichen planus
- Inflammatory bowel disease
- Anal carcinoma
- Dermatological conditions: scabies,
contact dermatitis
Red Flags: Bleeding, anal pain,
lumps/masses, ulceration, persistent
discharge, weight loss, systemic
symptoms (fever, night sweats),
failed conservative treatment—may
indicate infection, malignancy, or
inflammatory bowel disease.
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, frequency, and timing of itch (worse at night?).
- Aggravating/alleviating factors: hygiene practices, diet, sweating,
clothing, soaps, detergents.
- Bowel habits: constipation, diarrhoea, urgency, straining.
- Local symptoms: pain, bleeding, lumps, discharge, rash, skin
changes (thickening, redness, fissures).
- Previous anal conditions: haemorrhoids, fissures, abscesses.
- Systemic symptoms: weight loss, fever, night sweats (to rule out
malignancy/IBD).
- Medications: antibiotics, laxatives, steroids.
- Family history: dermatological or gastrointestinal disease.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “This is irritation and itching around your back passage. It’s usually
not serious.”
- “It can happen because the area gets damp, irritated, or comes into
contact with soaps or tight clothing.”
- “Certain foods like spicy dishes, coffee, or alcohol can make it
worse.”
- “Sometimes it’s caused by skin conditions or haemorrhoids.”
- “Most people improve with simple hygiene measures and avoiding
triggers, but we may investigate further if symptoms persist or there
are worrying signs.”
Management Plan:
Investigations (if indicated):
- Stool sample / microscopy: for parasites or infection (pinworm,
candida).
- Swab / cultures: if discharge or suspected infection.
- Anoscopy / proctoscopy: if persistent symptoms, bleeding, or suspicion
of haemorrhoids/fissure.
- Skin biopsy: if persistent dermatological lesion not responding to
treatment.
- Blood tests: FBC, CRP, glucose if systemic illness or uncontrolled
diabetes suspected.
Conservative / Self-care:
- Hygiene: wash gently with water, avoid harsh soaps or excessive wiping.
Pat dry.
- Topical treatment: barrier creams (zinc oxide, petroleum jelly), short-
term mild steroid cream (max 1–2 weeks).
- Avoid triggers: spicy foods, caffeine, alcohol, tight clothing.
- Lifestyle: keep area dry, wear cotton underwear, manage
constipation/diarrhoea.
- Skin care: treat coexisting eczema, fungal infection, or haemorrhoids if
present.
Specific Treatments:
- Threadworms: mebendazole 100 mg stat, repeat after 2 weeks if re-
infection.
- Anal fissure: topical agents (GTN cream) + treat constipation (laxatives,
stool softeners).
- Haemorrhoids (piles): topical analgesia, avoid constipation, consider
referral if 3rd degree or more.
Referral / Investigation:
- Refer to colorectal surgery / dermatology if:
• Persistent symptoms despite conservative measures.
• Red flags: bleeding, anal lump, ulceration, persistent discharge.
• Suspected secondary causes: IBD, malignancy, infection.
Safety Netting (SCA phrases):
- “If the itch persists after trying these measures for a few weeks, or if you
notice bleeding, lumps, or pain, please come back for review.”
- “Do not use strong steroid creams for long periods without advice, as
they can damage the skin.”

-- 416 of 437 --

386- Threadworm (Enterobius
vermicularis) in children
Definition: A common intestinal
parasitic infection in children causing
perianal itching, especially at night.
Usually mild and self-limiting but can
disrupt sleep and irritability.
Risk Factors: Children 5–10 years
old, close contact in
schools/nurseries, poor hand
hygiene, scratching nails,
contaminated clothing/bed linen.
Red Flags: Persistent abdominal
pain, vomiting, weight loss, rectal
bleeding, or systemic symptoms—
may suggest other gastrointestinal
pathology.
Differential Diagnosis (DDx):
- Fungal or bacterial perianal
infection
- Eczema or dermatitis
- Haemorrhoids or anal fissure
- Pinworm mimic infections (rare
parasites)
Focused History:
- Onset and duration of perianal itching (often worse at night).
- Sleep disturbance, irritability.
- Scratch marks or excoriation around perianal skin.
- Family/household members with similar symptoms.
- Recent attendance at school or nursery.
- Bowel habits: constipation, diarrhoea, abdominal pain.
- Hygiene practices: nail biting, hand washing, shared bedding or
towels.
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “Your child has a small intestinal worm called threadworm that
commonly causes itching around the bottom, especially at night.”
- “It’s very common in children and not usually serious.”
- “Other family members may also get it because the eggs spread
easily via hands, bedding, and clothing.”
- “With simple treatment and hygiene measures, it usually clears up
quickly.”
Management Plan:
Investigations (if needed):
- Scotch tape / perianal swab early morning to detect eggs (rarely
required if typical presentation).
- Stool sample usually not necessary.
Treatment:
- Mebendazole 100 mg stat, repeat after 2 weeks to prevent reinfection.
- Treat all household members simultaneously.
- Children under the age of 6 months and pregnant or breastfeeding
women should be treated with hygiene measures alone for 6 weeks.
Hygiene Measures:
- Wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water after using the toilet,
changing nappies, and before handling food. Cut fingernails regularly,
avoid biting nails and scratching around the anus.
- Shower each morning, including the perianal area, to remove eggs from
the skin.
- Change bed linen and nightwear daily for several days after treatment.
- Do not shake out items as this may distribute eggs around the room.
- Washing/drying in a hot cycle will kill threadworm eggs.
- Thoroughly dust and vacuum (including vacuuming mattresses) and
clean the bathroom by 'damp-dusting' surfaces, washing the cloth
frequently in hot water
Referral / Red Flags:
- Refer to paediatrics or GP if:
• Severe abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, or systemic symptoms.
• Persistent infection despite treatment and hygiene measures.
Safety Netting (SCA phrases):
- “If the itching continues after two doses of treatment or if your child
develops abdominal pain, blood in stool, or vomiting, please seek review.”
- “Maintaining strict hygiene is key to preventing reinfection.”

-- 417 of 437 --

387- Reactive Arthritis
Definition: An acute, sterile,
inflammatory arthritis that develops
1–4 weeks after an infection
(commonly gastrointestinal or
genitourinary). Often involves
asymmetric oligoarthritis, typically
of the lower limbs. May include
conjunctivitis, urethritis,
mucocutaneous lesions (classic
triad).
Risk Factors: Recent infection
(Chlamydia, Salmonella, Shigella,
Campylobacter, Yersinia), HLA-B27
positivity, male sex, age 20–40.
Differential Diagnosis (DDx): Septic
arthritis, RA, psoriatic arthritis,
ankylosing spondylitis,
gout/pseudogout, viral arthritis.
.
Red Flags: Severe joint destruction,
systemic symptoms (fever, weight
loss), septic arthritis, persistent
ocular symptoms (uveitis),
neuropathy, or atypical features—
may indicate alternative diagnosis
Focused History:
- Onset, duration, and pattern of joint pain/swelling (often
asymmetric, lower limb).
- Recent infections: gastrointestinal (diarrhoea, vomiting) or
genitourinary (dysuria, urethral discharge).
- Concomitant ocular symptoms: redness, pain, photophobia.
- Skin/mucous changes: mouth ulcers, keratoderma
blennorrhagicum, balanitis.
- Morning stiffness, fatigue.
- Family history of autoimmune disease or HLA-B27–associated
conditions.
When to Suspect:
- Young adult (20–40) with acute asymmetric joint pain in lower
limbs.
- History of diarrhoea or urethritis 1–4 weeks prior.
- Conjunctivitis, urethral discharge, or skin lesions accompanying
arthritis.
- No prior history of chronic arthritis
Lay Explanation (SCA phrasing):
- “This is a type of joint inflammation that usually happens after an
infection, often in the gut or urinary tract.”
- “It can cause pain and swelling in your knees, ankles, or feet, and
sometimes affects your eyes or skin.”
- “Most people get better with treatment, but some may have
ongoing joint problems.”
Why: your immune system reacts to the infection and causes
temporary inflammation in joints and other sites.
Management Plan:
Acute / Symptomatic:
- NSAIDs for pain and inflammation.
- Analgesia: paracetamol if needed.
- Rest & physiotherapy to maintain joint function.
Treat underlying infection (if still present):
- Antibiotics for ongoing bacterial infection (e.g., Chlamydia).
Chronic / Resistant cases:
- Consider DMARDs (sulfasalazine, methotrexate) if persistent arthritis >6
months.
- Ophthalmology referral if conjunctivitis or uveitis.
- Urology / dermatology referral for urethritis or skin lesions if severe.
Investigations:
- Bloods: FBC, ESR, CRP, renal & liver function.
- HLA-B27 testing (supports diagnosis, not definitive).
- Joint aspiration if monoarthritis to exclude septic arthritis (MC&S).
- Urine / swabs for Chlamydia or other STIs if urethritis suspected.
- Stool cultures if preceding diarrhoeal illness.
- Imaging: X-ray for joint changes (rare in acute phase), MRI if sacroiliitis
suspected.
Safety Netting / Referral:
- “Seek urgent review if you develop severe joint pain, swelling in one joint
that worsens quickly, fever, eye pain/redness, or urinary problems.”
- “Follow-up is important to monitor for chronic arthritis.”
- “Referral to rheumatology, ophthalmology, or urology may be needed
depending on symptoms.”

Special notes Explanation
Options for
patient not using
her spacer
• A BAI sprays the medicine automatically when you breathe in and there’s no need to press any button or time your inhalation which is easier that the current
MDI (Metered Dose Inhaler) that need more coordination. DPI is an option, but you need strong lung effort to pull the powder into your lungs.
HIV
confidentiality
• No legal obligations to inform partners as long as taking precaution to prevent transmitting it, but it strongly recommended to inform them. If not taking
precautions, this is criminal offence. Doctors has the rights to disclose information to patient at risk if patient with HIV putting others at risk after telling
patient with HIV as the last resort
• You can also opt out of having your information uploaded onto the Summary Care Records. You can also ask your GP to turn off online access. You can
request to restrict access to specific doctors.
• All information is kept confidential under the Data Protection Act
Dengue fever • It is an illness caused by a virus spread through the bite of infected mosquitoes. It can cause symptoms like high fever, severe headaches, joint and muscle
pain, skin rash, and tiredness. Most people recover fully, but can be severe in rare cases
Need for carer • Taking care of yourself is important for you and your partner to be able to be more effective in your caregiving role. What happen if you become unwell? Respite
care is an option to take a break from caring, while the person you care for is looked after by someone else.
Bulimia nervosa • It is an eating disorder where a person binge-eats large amounts of food in a short period, then tries to prevent weight gain by purging (e.g., through vomiting,
using laxatives, or excessive exercise).
Stress-related
symptoms
• Stress can cause your body to release chemical that makes it in fight or flight response for longer period cause body exhaustion and physical symptoms
OCD • A condition when a person has certain thoughts repeatedly (obsessions) and feels the need to do certain actions (compulsions) over and over.
Hoarding • It is the difficulty to throw away possessions regardless of actual value and it is a treatable mental health problem
Rotator Cuff
Disorders
• It refer to problems affecting the group of muscles and tendons around the shoulder joint, which help lift and rotate the arm. These issues can include
tendonitis (inflammation), tears (partial or full-thickness), or impingement (when tendons are pinched).
Sciatica • It is pain that radiates along the path of the sciatic nerve, which runs from the lower back through the hips, buttocks, and down each leg. It happens when the
nerve is irritated or compressed, often due to a herniated disc or bone spur.
Slipped disc • The inside part of the disc which is soft bulges out through the harder outside part and can press down the nerve that runs past it which give sciatica pain.
Reassure patient
about
Whistleblowing
• If you ever have concerns about safeguarding issues, such as abuse or neglect, you have the right to raise them without fear of retaliation. This is called
whistleblowing, and the law protects you from being treated unfairly or losing your job if you report something you believe to be wrong or unsafe. You can raise
your concerns with your employer or directly with external organizations like the Care Quality Commission or Healthwatch. Your identity is kept confidential,
and the matter will be investigated to ensure the safety of those involved.
Ehlers-Danlos
Syndrome (EDS)
• It is a group of genetic conditions that affect the connective tissues in the body, which provide structure and support to skin, joints, and blood vessels.
Picky eating • Children are often fussy with food at this age as they have growing sense of independence and worry about new things including food. As long they are healthy
and growing well, there’s no need to worry
Optic neuritis • It is inflammation of the optic nerve, which carries messages from the eye to the brain. It can cause sudden vision loss, blurry vision, or pain, especially when
moving the eye. It can be recurrent after recovery periods of at least one month affecting different parts of the body, and it can be lined to MS
Multiple
Sclerosis (MS)
• It is a condition where the immune system mistakenly attacks the protective coating around nerves (myelin) in the brain and spinal cord. This causes
communication problems between the brain and body.

-- 419 of 437 --

Parkinson's
disease
• It is a condition that affects the brain and causes problems with movement. Symptoms may include shaking (tremor), stiff muscles, slow movements, and
balance issues.
Transient global
amnesia
• A poorly understood condition when you lose your short-term memory for up to 24 hours with no risk of ministroke or stroke.
Lewy Body
Dementia (LBD)
• It is a type of dementia caused by abnormal protein deposits (Lewy bodies) in the brain. It affects thinking, memory, movement, and mood. Symptoms may
include visual hallucinations, difficulty with attention, sleep disturbances, and movement problems similar to Parkinson’s disease.
Lichenoid drug
reaction
• Iti is skin rash that happen as a reaction to certain medications causing this red purple patches which is usually worse with UV rays which explain why it is
more in the sun exposed areas. Can start few weeks to months after starting the medications. Stopping meds helps the rash to gradually clear up
Skin Picking
Disorder
(Excoriation
Disorder)
• It is a mental health condition where a person has a strong urge to pick at their skin, often leading to sores, scars, or bleeding. This can be triggered by stress,
anxiety, or simply as a habit.
Epidermoid cyst • It is a common benign lump that forms under the skin due to skin growing inward instead of shedding a way.
Acute Uveitis • It is sudden inflammation of the middle layer of the eye (the uvea), which can cause redness, eye pain, sensitivity to light, and blurred vision. It often affects
one eye and may be linked to infections or autoimmune conditions
ARMD • It is an eye condition that affects the central part of your vision due to degeneration of the cells in the centre of the retina with unknown reason by linked to
some RFs as above. It doesn’t cause total blindness but affect reading/ recognising faces/ daily activities
Leukaemia • It is a type of blood cancer that affect specific type pf white blood cell called lymphocytes which help to fight infections
Sick cell disease • It is a blood problem is affected the red blood cells which carry oxygen around the body and can cause such pains
Warfarin and St
John’s Wort
• INR is a measure of how well warfarin is thinning your blood to prevent stroke. A possible reason for low INR is interaction with St John’s Wort which make
warfarin doesn’t stay in your system long enough to work properly
Cystic fibrosis • It is a serious inherited genetic disorder that mainly affect the lungs lead to inability to clear the mucus causing repeated chest infection, and also can affect
other parts such as the gut affecting digestion causing poor growth.
Down's
Syndrome
• It is a genetic condition caused by an extra copy of chromosome 21. It can affect a person’s physical development and learning abilities, often leading to
distinctive facial features and potential health challenges. With the right support, individuals with Down's Syndrome can live healthy, fulfilling lives.
Huntington’s
syndrome
• It is an inherited genetic condition affecting the brain and causes changes in movement, thinking, behaviour with symptoms appear between 30-50 years with
progressive course and no cure. There is 50% chance to pass it to your children
Conn's disease,
also known as
primary
aldosteronism
• It is a condition where the adrenal glands produce too much of the hormone aldosterone. Aldosterone helps regulate the balance of salt and water in the
body, which controls blood pressure. When too much aldosterone is produced, it can lead to high blood pressure and low potassium levels.
Serotonin
syndrome
• It is a potentially life-threatening condition that occurs when there is too much serotonin, a chemical in the brain that helps regulate mood, sleep, and other
functions. This is due serotonergic medications (citalopram/ duloxetine/ venlafaxine/ pregabalin/ tramadol)
Postpartum
thyroiditis
• It is a condition where the thyroid becomes inflamed within the first year after giving birth. It can cause thyroid hormone levels to fluctuate, leading to
temporary hyperthyroidism (too much thyroid hormone) followed by hypothyroidism (too little thyroid hormone).
DKA • It is a serious condition that can happen in people with diabetes where a lack of insulin causes harmful substances called ketones to build up in the blood. It
can be 1st presentation of DM in 40% of cases
Anticholinergic
Burden
• It refers to the cumulative effect of taking one or more medications with anticholinergic properties, which block a brain chemical called acetylcholine. This
chemical is important for functions like memory, thinking, and muscle control.

-- 420 of 437 --

Challenging concerns/expectations
Requesting
blood to Dx
menopause
• Because it’s typically diagnosed based on typical symptoms If age > 45 years
• Hormone levels can fluctuate during this time, making blood results unreliable.
Fidelity with
genital herpes
• First episode may not necessarily indicate recent infection, and transmission can occur from an asymptomatic partner years into a monogamous relationship.
• In addition, herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) infection may have spread from elsewhere on the body, such as the lips (cold sores) or fingers
Fidelity with
other STI
• It is an STI, but this episode may not necessarily indicate recent infection, cab be dormant in the body for years
Febrile seizures
risk
• Risk of epilepsy Is low 4% which is double the general population
• Risk of recurrence about 1/3 will have recurrence in the 1st year
• No lasting effect on the intellectual function
Risk of
transmission of
meningitis at
school
• Explain steps in managing suspected meningitis (NOTIFIABLE disease managed by public health team). Even it is confirmed, t is not routinely to offer
antibiotics to contacts at school/ nursery/ friends/ work/ food or drink sharing/ travelling in the same transport. If needed, antibiotics 1st choice is
ciprofloxacin 250 -500mg stat
• Reassure about low risk of transmission at 1ry school (1/18000)
Sleeping pills for
insomnia
• Sleeping pills can be tempting for quick relief from insomnia, but they aren't the best long-term solution. They might help you fall asleep at first, but they don’t
address the underlying causes of insomnia. Over time, you may become dependent on them, and they can lead to side effects like drowsiness the next day, or
tolerance, where you need higher doses to achieve the same effect.
Diazepam for
anxiety
• Diazepam may seem helpful for anxiety in the short term, but it has significant long-term problems. It can be highly addictive, leading to dependence and
requiring higher doses over time to have the same effect. Stopping it suddenly can cause severe withdrawal symptoms, including worse anxiety or even
seizures. It only masks anxiety rather than addressing its root cause. Common side effects include drowsiness, poor concentration, and memory problems,
which can impact daily activities like driving. We need to set a plan together to gradually decrease it by 1mg per month with giving other options as SSRI and
CBT
Back pain due to
Scheuermann's
Disease
• It is a condition where the spine develops a rounded or hunched posture (kyphosis) during adolescence due to the uneven growth of the vertebrae.
Increasing dose
of opioids for
chronic pain (> 3
months)
• When we have pain for longer time, our pain system works in different way than when we have acute pain when our nervous become very sensitive even
without further damage and resting doesn’t help the pain. NSAIDs/ paracetamol/opioids/ steroids injection don’t work with chronic pain. Consider CBT/
physio or acupuncture or off licensed antidepressant e.g, duloxetine/ citalopram/ fluoxetine as alternatives
Requesting just
in case
antibiotics (apart
from COPD
rescue packs)
• Deny the request and explain risk of resistance, impact on the immunity, masking underlying serious problems, delaying presentations to the hospital and
advice to seek urgent care if any red flags
Requesting fit
note for court
hearing
• GPs cannot issue Fit Notes for court purposes. Fit Notes are specifically for work-related sickness absence. However, GPs can provide medical reports or
letters if required by the court, detailing a patient's health condition and its impact on their ability to attend court. These are usually private services that may
incur a fee.

-- 421 of 437 --

Steroids and
Bell’s palsy
• Explain that guide recommend considering steroids to be given if presented within 72 hours for 10 days and it is effective more in mild cases.
Doesn’t want to
stop driving
• It's important to stop driving for your safety and the safety of others. The law requires you to inform the DVLA because seizures can make driving dangerous.
Reporting it doesn't always mean you lose your license forever; they will assess your case and guide you. Taking this step now shows you're being responsible
and helps ensure you're safe to get back on the road when ready. Not doing that could lead to legal trouble, including fines and insurance issues and we might
need to break confidentiality and inform DVLA to ensure public safety.
Deal with
challenging
behaviour in
dementia patient
• Address underlying causes like thirst or need to go to toilet or pain + if no better to move her to a safe low stimulation environment and use of de-escalation
techniques such as active listening, verbal responding or pictures + don’t get upset + take a break if possible and safe + try soothing her with music or
distraction
Need urgent
referral for BCC
• BCC is the most common and slow-growing type of skin cancer. It's very unlikely to spread or become life-threatening. I understand why you're worried, but
BCCs typically grow over months or even years, so they don't require urgent attention. This approach allows us to prioritize faster-growing or more concerning
cancers while still ensuring you receive the care you need. Offer F2F for dermoscopy
Herpes simplex
and kissing
• Cold sores are viral skin infection causing small painful blister around the mouth. and it is unlikely to be related to kissing as no other signs of abuse and it is
not the only way for spread (can spread by sharing utensils/ lip balm/ direct contact with infected saliva or herpes lesions from other kids and also can be
transmitted from asymptomatic patients
Concerned about
social services
• Social services are here to support families, not to take children away unless there's a serious safety concern. Their main goal is to help you overcome any
challenges, provide extra support, and ensure your children are happy, healthy, and safe. Involving them simply means connecting you with helpful resources,
advice, or services to make things easier for your family.
Travel and 2 WW
referral
• Advise delay the travel and provide letter for the insurance or the airway for refund + explain that travelling without letting her insurance know make it invalid
• Explain delaying the process might potentially allowing a condition to worsen and make treatment less effective
Impact of illness
on work
• The Equality Act 2010 protects you at work if you have a health condition that affects your daily life. Your employer is required by law to make reasonable
adjustments to help you do your job, such as flexible working hours or changes to your duties.
Eye watering
while dry eye
• Dry eye is a condition when your eyes don’t make enough oily tears which results in more evaporation of the tears causing dry eye and overproduction of
watery tears causing watery eye. As the tear film is formed of 3 layers.
Homeless
support
• Offer options to make it easy for him like change his registered address to the practice or friend address + 3 months prescriptions at a time or repeat
dispensing + ensure the patient has fast access to GP with flexible appointment + note that this patient has no registered address and to avoid letters or text
messages
Why PE not chest
infection
• Explain PE as a blood clot that develops in a blood vessel in the lung which block the blood supply part of the lung causing these symptoms. It is unlikely to be infection due to the
pain which is worse with breathing and started before cough and also no other symptoms as fever or phlegm. PE can sometimes present with similar symptoms to a chest
infection that’s why might be misleading to my colleague. As GPs we have different scope and exposure, and it is not uncommon to have different opinions.
New medication
for CF
• Kaftrio improve the prognosis and prolong life expectancy to 82.5 if initiated between 12 and 17 years old.
SC or sublingual
immunotherapy
• Exposing person to increasing amounts of an allergen to induce tolerance ( weekly doses then 4 weekly for 3 years).
Poor asthma
control due to
poor Inhaler
compliance
• It is likely due to poor compliance given frequent symptoms especially at night or early morning + frequent use of blue inhaler + worsening of the peak flow
readings. It is unlikely due to exposure to dogs as her symptoms are not worse after it.

Worried about
bisphosphonates
• As pt worried about ris of osteonecrosis of the jaw or atypical femoral fractures with the treatment. Explain rare incidence of these as 1/1000 – 1/10000)
• To take them properly, swallow (not chew or crush) the tablet first thing in the morning on an empty stomach with a full glass of water (at least 200ml). Avoid
using tea, coffee, or juice. Stay upright (sitting or standing) for at least 30 minutes to prevent irritation to your throat or stomach.
Busy to cook
healthy food
• Give options for healthy food: cooking for the week and store in the freezer/ healthy meals options/ free meals on wheels for eligible patients
Statins and side
effects
• Address concerns about side effects as being rare about 5 excess cases/10,000 over 5 years. Statin actually decrease the risk of dementia by 15%
Cholesterol
medication by
SC injection
• Inclisiran = Leqvio is used only for 2ry prevention if CVD+ acting by inhibition of production of PCSK9 in the liver + high LDL level 2.6 mmol or more despite max
tolerated lipid lowering agents. Dose is 284mg SC then 2nd dose after 3 months then once every 6 months
DNACPR • DNACPR means if your heart or breathing stops your healthcare team will not try to restart it. Explain that DNACPR decision can sometimes made by
healthcare on behalf of a patient if they are too unwell to participate in this decision when CPR is unlikely to prevent their death for more peaceful and
dignified passing. Explain that DNACPR doesn’t affect other treatment or personal care
Red flags
H&N • Nasal: Unilateral symptoms, bloody discharge, recurrent epistaxis and pain
• Throat or mouth: persistent hoarseness of voice or ulceration in mouth > 3 weeks/ Unexplained neck lumps > 3 weeks, weight loss, night sweating
• Ear: Pulsatile tinnitus/ sudden hearing loss over periods of 3 days in the last 30 days / numbness of face/ visual disturbance/ disturbed balance/ head trauma/
suspected stroke/ high risk of suicide/ immunosuppressed with otalgia not responding to antibiotics
Women with
abdominal or
urinary or vaginal
symptoms
• Persistent bloating, abdominal pain, early satiety, urinary symptoms without infection, new onset IBS >50 years, FH, weight loss, Post coital bleeding/ IMB/
vaginal discharge/ blood in urine
Breast • Breast pain/ lumps/ discharge/ bleeding/ eczematous changes,
• FH (1st degree with (breast cancer <40/ male breast cancer/ bilateral breast cancer), 1st/ 2nd degree with breast cancer + 1st/ 2nd degree with ovarian
cancer, Three 1st or 2nd degree with breast cancer at any age, BRCA1/2 gene mutation
• 1st degree = Parents/ siblings/ children
• 2nd degree = grandparents/ aunts/ uncles/ half-siblings/ nieces/ nephews
CHC
contraception
• UKMEC 4:
o Postpartum < 6 weeks
o Smoking > 15/ day + age > 35 (UKMEC 3 if stopped < 1 year or smoking < 15 and will be UKMEC 2 if age < 35 or stopped > 1 year)
o Uncontrolled HTN/ Migraine with aura
o Current or Hx of stroke/TIA/ MI/ IHD / VTE/AF/ Heart or vascular diseases
o Breast cancer (all hormonal contraception are contraindicated)
o SLE with antiphospholipid abs
• UKMEC 3:
o BMI > 35 / Controlled HTN / FH of VTE
o Migraine without aura / Severe liver diseases
o Hx of breast cancer or breast mass or strong FH pf breast cancer

pregnancy

• Dehydration and electrolyte imbalance.

urinating))/ Numbness or tingling in the "saddle" area (around the buttocks, inner thighs, and groin)/ lax anal sphincter/ Sexual dysfunction)
CD/ UC • Severe diarrhoea >6 times/ fever/ dehydration/ tachycardia/ hypotension/ severe abdominal pain/ persistent vomiting/ raised CRP/ unintentional weight loss/
perianal abscess
Suspected stoke • F: Face: Sudden drooping or weakness on one side of the face
• A: Arms: Unable to lift one arm or weakness in one arm.
• S: Speech: Slurred or difficult speech.
• T: T - Time: Call 999 immediately if any of these symptoms appear.
• Other symptoms: dizziness/ headache/ double vision/ confusion
Urinary problem • Blood in urine/ not passing urine/ frothy urine/ fever with rigors/ weight loss/ back pain or incontinence with CES symptoms/ trauma/ SOB due to oedema/
confusion
Important notes
Age < 16 • Gillick competence: ask about understanding of the situation with both benefits and risks + reasons behind the decision + long term impact of this decision +
understanding of alternatives including the possibility to wait until age of 16
Diary for triggers • Allergy (Asthma / Allergy / CMPA/ Eczema/ urticaria/ angioedema/ food allergy)
• Headache or migraine / Anxiety or panic attacks/ IBS
• Trigeminal neuralgia
Women Hx • 4 Ps (period/ pills/ partner/ pregnancy) + cervical screening if (25 -64 years)
Child Hx • BINDS (Birth, Immunisations, Neonatal, Developmental, Social)
Sexual Hx • Discuss contraception / condoms/ STI/ partner notification
Avoid sex • Genital herpes: Avoid sex until lesions are cleared
• Chlamydia: Sexual intercourse (including oral sex) should be avoided until they and their partner(s) have completed treatment
• Gonorrhoea: Advise the person to abstain from sex until 7 days after they and their partner(s) have completed treatment

-- 424 of 437 --

• PID: Advise to abstain from all sexual activity until both the woman and any sexual partner(s) have completed antibiotic treatment, are symptom-free, and
have had a 'test of cure'.
Notifiable
disease
• Suspected TB / Malaria / Meningitis / Dengue fever
• Measles / Scarlet fever/ HAV
Teams to use • Admission avoidance team: when patient is palliative or elderly with complex needs to avoid any unnecessary hospital admissions
• OT/Physio: if patient has falls or disability or not coping at home
• Health improvement practitioner: to speak about lifestyle changes
• Pharmacy: to arrange blister packs/ medications review
• District nurses: wound care/ EOL medications/ catheter care
• Podiatry: assess PAD/ care for foot/nail problems
• Health visitor: to monitor children at home/ safeguarding concerns
• Social prescriber: carers, financial, housing, domestic abuse, loneliness, support groups, SR-1 forms
Avoid NSAIDs • Dyspepsia/ peptic ulcer disease/ diverticulosis/ IBD
• Patient on anticoagulants or antiplatelet
• ITP/ PGN/ Pregnancy/ Dengue fever
Travel related • Dengue fever/ Malaria/ HAV / TB/ DVT/ Barotrauma
Avoid contact
sports
• PCKD/ Infectious mononucleosis (for 3 weeks after symptoms onset)/ ITP/ EDS
Avoid contact
with others
• HAV: Avoid school/ nursery/ work (especially food handling)/ unprotected sex until not long infectious (7 days post jaundice or symptom onset
• Chicken pox/ herpes zoster: avoid contact with immunosuppressed/ pregnant/ elderly until all lesion healed
• Parvovirus: Avoid contact with other pregnant ladies (>15 mins) until results of immunity available. Can be infectious for 10 days before the rash appear. No
exclusion from school once rash appeared
Avoid alcohol • HAV/ HCV/ HBV/Haemochromatosis/ Liver cirrhosis
Dependant
people/ pets in
emergencies
• For elderly: Home care from a paid carer / Short stay in home care/ Day care/ short term respite care / Other family members or friends
• For children: day care childcare - friends or family – temporary foster care
• For Pets: animal shelters and rescue organizations such as RSPCA
CBT types • Depression and anxiety: CBT: Focuses on identifying and changing negative thought patterns and behaviors to improve mental health.
• OCD: Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP): Gradually exposing patients to triggers while preventing compulsive responses.
• PTSD: Trauma-Focused CBT (TF-CBT)/ EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing): Helps reframe traumatic memories and reduce distressing
symptoms.
• Phobia: Systematic Desensitization: Gradual exposure combined with relaxation techniques.
• Eating Disorders: Eating disorder focused CBT: Focuses on addressing body image issues, self-monitoring eating habits, and challenging distorted thoughts.
• Health anxiety: CBT for Health Anxiety: Helps reduce fear and preoccupation with illness.
• Insomnia: CBT-I : Addresses negative thoughts and behaviors around sleep.
• Panic attacks: Interoceptive Exposure: Exposure to physical sensations that trigger panic attacks to desensitize the response.
• Borderline Personality Disorder/ premenstrual dysmorphic disorder: Dialectical Behavioral Therapy (DBT): A type of CBT that teaches skills like emotional
regulation and mindfulness
Exercise • Planter fasciitis: stretching exercises

-- 425 of 437 --

• Achilles tendinopathy: weight bearing exercises and eccentric loading
• Fragility fracture/ osteoporosis: weight bearing exercises
• Nerve problems: gentle stretching and nerve glides exercises
• For all others advise stretching and strengthening exercise
• Avoid in the 1st 48 hours in meniscal injury
MSK referral • Meniscal injury: after 6 weeks of conservative management to physio
• Achilles tendinopathy: refer after 7 days of conservative for physio
• Rotator cuff disorders and osteoarthritis: refer after 6 weeks of conservative and physio to ortho team
• Sciatica: refer after 2 weeks of conservative to physio
Steroids
injection in MSK
• Risk > benefits: planter fasciitis (high risk of recurrence) and rotator cuff disorders (mask the pain not treating the problem but helps to engage with physio)
• Avoid in Achilles tendinopathy (risk of rupture)
• Good for CTS/ De -Quervain’s tenosynovitis/ OA
• Consider in Tennis elbow/ Golfer elbow/ wrist tendinitis
Complaint
process timeline
in England
• If oral (not official), should be dealt with by the end of next day, but If written:
o Complaints must be made within 12 months of the event
o Acknowledge via phone or letter within 3 days
o No time limits set for responding to a complaint (usual within 6 months)
Autosomal
dominant
• Familial adenomatous polyposis/ PCKD/ VWD/ Huntington’s syndrome
• HOCM/ Familial hypercholesteremia
Autosomal recessive • Cystic fibrosis / Haemochromatosis
QRISK • NAFLD/ Obesity/ DM/ Dyslipidaemia/ CVD/ hypothyroidism/ PCOS

Right to choose • Gender Identity clinic - ADHD clinic

students
