Family Screening in GP
Condition Genetics / Risk + When/How to Test SCA Explanation (condition, risk, prognosis, test & management)
Familial
Hypercholesterolaemia (FH)
Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test: lipid
profile at age 10 (earlier if high risk); genetic
test if available.
“This is a genetic condition where cholesterol is very high from birth. Each child has a 1 in 2 chance. If
untreated, it increases heart disease risk from a young age, but with diet, lifestyle, and often statins, people do
very well. We test children with a cholesterol blood test at age 10. If positive, we’d involve a specialist clinic and
start treatment early to prevent heart problems.”
Hypertrophic
Cardiomyopathy (HCM)
Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test: ECG +
Echo every 1–2 years from age 10–12 to
early 20s, then every 3–5 yrs.
“This is a heart muscle thickening condition that can run in families. Each child has a 1 in 2 risk. Some people
live normally, but in others it can cause rhythm problems or heart failure. We arrange heart scans and ECGs from
around age 10. If positive, a cardiologist will monitor regularly, advise on lifestyle, and sometimes use
medication or devices like a defibrillator.”
Dilated Cardiomyopathy
(familial)
Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test: ECG +
Echo every 1–3 years from age 10–12.
“This condition causes the heart muscle to weaken and enlarge. Children have a 50–50 risk. Prognosis varies —
some remain well, others develop symptoms later. We start heart scans from age 10. If positive, a cardiologist
follows closely, managing with medicines and lifestyle advice.”
Inherited Arrhythmias (Long
QT, Brugada, CPVT)
Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test: ECG
from infancy, genetic testing if family
mutation known.
“These are rhythm conditions that can run in families. Each child has a 50% risk. Some are mild, but they can
cause dangerous rhythms. We check with ECGs in childhood. If positive, children are followed by cardiology —
sometimes with medication (like beta-blockers), lifestyle adjustments, and occasionally devices to protect the
heart.”
Bicuspid Aortic Valve (BAV) Familial tendency (~9% in children). Test:
Echo once in childhood (age 5–10).
“This means the main heart valve has 2 flaps instead of 3. It sometimes runs in families — about 1 in 10 children
may inherit it. Many live normal lives, though some develop narrowing or leakage later. We arrange a one-off
heart scan for children around age 5–10. If positive, they’ll have regular heart checks to see if any treatment is
needed.”
Marfan Syndrome Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
genetic test any age; heart echo + eye checks
from infancy, repeat every 1–2 yrs.
“This affects the body’s connective tissue (tall, long limbs, eye and heart issues). Each child has a 1 in 2 chance.
Prognosis is good with monitoring but serious if missed, due to risk of aortic rupture. We test in childhood with
genetics, heart and eye checks. If positive, children are followed closely with lifestyle advice and sometimes
surgery if changes develop.”
Ehlers–Danlos (vascular type) Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
genetic testing any age; vascular imaging in
adolescence if positive.
“This rare connective tissue condition affects blood vessels, making them fragile. Each child has a 50% chance.
Prognosis can be serious due to risk of ruptures, so early diagnosis is important. We’d test with genetics and
monitor with scans if positive. Care is through a specialist genetics team.”
FTAAD (thoracic aortic
aneurysm/dissection)
Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
genetic test; Echo/MRI from age 10–12,
repeat every 1–2 yrs.
“This condition causes the main artery to stretch and tear. Children have a 50% risk. Prognosis is good with
monitoring but dangerous if missed. We test from age 10 with scans. If positive, children are seen regularly by
specialists and may need surgery later.”
ADPKD Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test: renal
USS at ≥18 (earlier if family requests).
“This condition causes kidney cysts. Each child has a 50% chance. Many live well until adulthood, though some
develop kidney failure later in life. We usually test with a kidney scan at age 18. If positive, we’d monitor blood
pressure and kidney function regularly.”
Haemochromatosis Autosomal recessive; only if both parents
carry gene. Test: genetic test/ferritin at 18+.
“This makes the body store too much iron. Children only at risk if both parents carry the gene. It develops in
adulthood. If positive, it’s very treatable with regular blood donations.”
Wilson’s Disease Autosomal recessive. Test: copper
studies/genetic test from age 4–5.
“This rare condition makes copper build up in the liver and brain. If untreated it can cause serious illness, but
with lifelong treatment, people stay well. Children can be tested from school age. If positive, medicines can
remove excess copper.”

-- 436 of 437 --

Alpha-1 Antitrypsin
Deficiency
Autosomal recessive. Test: genetic test from
infancy if family mutation known.
“This condition affects lungs and liver. If both parents carry the gene, children may be affected. Prognosis varies:
some never develop illness, others get lung or liver problems. We can test from birth. If positive, children are
monitored and advised on avoiding smoking and liver toxins.”
FAP (Familial Adenomatous
Polyposis)
Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
genetic test in childhood (10–12);
colonoscopy every 1–2 yrs if positive.
“This condition causes hundreds of bowel polyps that almost always turn into cancer if untreated. Each child has
a 50% chance. Prognosis is excellent if found early — bowel checks start at age 10 and surgery can prevent
cancer.”
Lynch Syndrome Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
genetic testing from 18+; colonoscopy from
25.
“This raises bowel and womb cancer risk. Children have a 1 in 2 chance. It doesn’t affect childhood, so we test
when they’re adults. If positive, bowel checks start from 25, and women may be offered womb checks or
surgery.”
BRCA1/2 Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
genetic testing from 18+.
“This gene increases breast and ovarian cancer risk. Each child has a 1 in 2 chance. It doesn’t affect childhood,
so testing is offered at 18+. If positive, people get extra screening (mammograms/MRIs) and may choose surgery
to reduce risk.”
Retinoblastoma Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test: eye
checks from birth, every few months in
infancy.
“This is a rare eye cancer in babies and children. Each child has a 50% chance. Prognosis is excellent if caught
early. Babies are checked from birth by eye doctors. If positive, treatment is usually successful.”
MEN (Multiple Endocrine
Neoplasia)
Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
genetic test from age 5 (MEN2) or age 10
(MEN1); endocrine bloods annually.
“This affects hormone glands (thyroid, adrenals, pancreas). Each child has a 1 in 2 chance. Prognosis depends on
early detection. Children are tested from age 5–10. If positive, they have yearly hormone tests and sometimes
early surgery.”
Von Hippel–Lindau (VHL) Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
genetic test any age; MRI/eye exams from
5–10 yrs.
“This causes tumours and cysts in brain, eyes, and kidneys. Each child has a 50% chance. Prognosis is much
better if monitored. Screening starts around age 5 with scans and eye exams.”
Neurofibromatosis (NF1/2) Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
clinical monitoring from birth.
“This causes skin marks and sometimes tumours. Each child has a 1 in 2 risk. Prognosis varies: many have mild
symptoms, others more severe. Monitoring starts from birth with skin, eye, and growth checks.”
Tuberous Sclerosis Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
clinical/genetic checks from infancy;
MRI/EEG as guided.
“This causes skin changes and sometimes seizures. Each child has a 50% chance. Prognosis varies but early
treatment improves outcomes. We test in infancy, and children have brain scans and monitoring if positive.”
Li–Fraumeni Syndrome Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
genetic test any age; whole-body MRI and
screening from childhood.
“This strongly increases cancer risk. Each child has a 1 in 2 chance. Prognosis is better with strict monitoring.
Screening includes yearly whole-body MRI scans from childhood.”
Huntington’s Disease Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test:
predictive test only at 18+ (unless
symptoms).
“This causes progressive brain changes, usually starting in mid-life. Each child has a 1 in 2 chance. Prognosis is
progressive but varies. Testing is only offered in adulthood so children can decide for themselves. If positive,
care focuses on support and symptom control.”
Sickle Cell / Thalassaemia Autosomal recessive; 25% chance of disease
if both parents carriers. Test: UK newborn
heel prick; antenatal testing in pregnancy.
“These are inherited blood conditions. If both parents carry the gene, each child has a 1 in 4 risk of the disease.
Prognosis depends on severity but can be managed with medication and sometimes transfusions. In the UK,
babies are already tested at birth.”
Hereditary Spherocytosis Autosomal dominant; 50% risk. Test: blood
film if symptoms, possible test from
infancy.
“This affects red blood cells, sometimes causing anaemia and jaundice. Each child has a 1 in 2 chance. Many
cases are mild, others need surgery. We test children with a blood test if they develop symptoms.”
Cystic Fibrosis Autosomal recessive; 25% if both parents
carriers. Test: UK newborn heel prick at
birth.
“This affects the lungs and digestion. Each child has a 1 in 4 chance if both parents carry the gene. Prognosis has
improved with modern treatment. In the UK, all babies are screened at birth. If positive, children are referred to a
specialist CF centre.”
